title: Fluid Aerial Networks: UAV Rotation for Inter-Cell Interference Mitigation
authors: Enzhi Zhou, Yue Xiao, Ziyue Liu, Sotiris A. Tegos, Panagiotis D. Diamantoulakis, George K. Karagiannidis
publish: IEEE Transactions on Vehicular Technology
abstract: With the rapid development of aerial infrastructure, unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) that function as aerial base stations (ABSs) extend terrestrial network services into the sky, enabling on-demand connectivity and enhancing emergency communication capabilities in cellular networks by leveraging the flexibility and mobility of UAVs. In such a UAV-assisted network, this paper investigates position-based beamforming between ABSs and ground users (GUs). To mitigate inter-cell interference, we propose a novel fluid aerial network that leverages ABS rotation to increase multi-cell capacity and overall network efficiency. Specifically, in the line-of-sight channel model, the spatial beamforming weights are determined by the positions of GUs. In this direction, we examine the beamforming gain of a two-dimensional multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) array at various ground positions, revealing that ABS rotation significantly affects multi-user channel correlation and inter-cell interference. Based on these findings, we propose an alternative low-complexity algorithm to design the optimal rotation angle for ABSs, aiming to reduce inter-cell interference and thus maximize the sum rate of multi-cell systems. In simulations, exhaustive search serves as a benchmark to validate the optimization performance of the proposed sequential ABS rotation scheme. Moreover, simulation results demonstrate that, in interference-limited regions, the proposed ABS rotation paradigm can significantly reduce inter-cell interference in terrestrial networks and improve the multi-cell sum rate by approximately 10% compared to fixed-direction ABSs without rotation.

title: Silicon Controlled Rectifier (SCR)-Embedded Identification of Field Winding, Damper Bar, and Eccentricity Faults in Wound Field Synchronous Machines
authors: Sungwoo Choe, Muhammad Faizan Shaikh, Namhyuk Byun, Kyunghoon Kim, Kyeongyeol Kim, Anyuan Chen, Arne Nysveen, Hossein Ehya, Sang Bin Lee
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industry Applications
abstract: It is crucial to detect faults in the rotor of wound field synchronous machines (WFSM) such as shorted field winding turns, broken damper bars, and rotor eccentricity, before they escalate into failures that cause forced outage of the machine. The off-line test methods available for detection of these faults require machine disassembly and/or physical access to the machine, which lead to high maintenance costs. On-line monitoring options require installation of sensors on the stator bore or on the machine frame and are not sensitive to the above-mentioned rotor faults. In this paper, a new test concept for remote and automated detection of WFSM rotor faults is proposed as a solution. The silicon-controlled rectifier (SCR) of the static field excitation system is used for injecting test signals into the rotor winding for testing, whenever the WFSM is de-energized. It is shown that the equivalent rotor impedance at 6 times the 50/60 Hz supply frequency at varying saturation levels of the core can be used for reliable detection and classification of shorted field winding turns, broken damper bars, and eccentricity. An experimental study on 20, 30, and 100 kVA WFSMs under the 3 fault conditions is given to validate the effectiveness of the proposed test method.

title: Open-Circuit Fault Diagnosis for Power Converters Under Variable Operational Conditions: From a Source-Free Domain Adaptation Perspective
authors: Jiusi Zhang, Kai Chen, Fan Wu, Gen Qiu, Yuhua Cheng, Shen Yin
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Electronics
abstract: As the core component of modern power electronic systems, power converters have widespread applications in renewable energy generation, smart grids, and aerospace. The open-circuit fault of power converters is highly concealed and easily derived from secondary faults, whose diagnosis technology has become a key research direction to ensure safe operation. Considering that power converters usually need to operate under different load conditions and complex environments, it is of great significance to achieve efficient open-circuit fault diagnosis while protecting data privacy. Therefore, this article proposes an open-circuit fault diagnosis approach for power converters from the perspective of source-free domain adaptation. In detail, this article proposes an attention-guided power converter fault diagnosis model to enhance the ability to capture key features. Without the need for source domain data to participate in joint training, this article proposes a dynamic clustering loss to achieve pseudolabel generation and iterative self-training, so that the model can make full use of target domain data. Furthermore, this article proposes uncertainty entropy loss and diversity entropy loss to decrease the uncertainty of fault diagnosis output while preventing the model from being over-confident. To demonstrate the reliability of the open-circuit fault diagnosis methodology, this research implements a functional power conversion system.

title: EdgeGuard: Blockchain-Enhanced Secure Data Circulation via Aggregatable Distributed Key Generation
authors: Boyang Liao, Jianwei Liu, Xinxin Xing, Jian Cui, Qianhong Wu, Willy Susilo, Robert H. Deng, Yizhong Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing
abstract: Data has become a critical driver of innovation in artificial intelligence and the evolution of 6G technologies. The explosive growth of data volume accelerates the convergence of cloud and edge computing, while simultaneously posing heightened challenges to data security and privacy. The emerging cloud-edge-device collaborative paradigm enables dynamic and large-scale data circulation across heterogeneous entities, exposing systems to complex threats such as malicious edge nodes and eavesdropping over untrusted communication channels. In response to these issues, we propose EdgeGuard, a secure and decentralized framework for cloud-edge-device data circulation. EdgeGuard is specifically designed for highly dynamic environments and ensures robust data confidentiality, integrity, traceability, and resilience against both malicious external attackers and compromised edge servers. To underpin its cryptographic foundation, we develop two core primitives. Specifically, we introduce an Aggregatable Publicly Verifiable Secret Sharing (APVSS) scheme that enables efficient sharing of field elements while supporting aggregation and public verifiability. Furthermore, we construct AggDKG, a distributed key generation (DKG) protocol. AggDKG achieves public verifiability and bias resistance with an expected total communication cost of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">${\mathcal {O}}(\kappa n^{3})$</tex-math></inline-formula> effectively overcoming the scalability limitations inherent in traditional complaint-based protocols. Collectively, these components form a comprehensive framework that strengthens secure and efficient data circulation in cloud-edge-device systems. Experimental data show that AggDKG delivers clear performance gains: across all tested scales, its total running time is only about 8%–65% of that of the DKG of Gurkan et al., and at <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$n=256$</tex-math></inline-formula>, it reduces per-node runtime by approximately 27% compared with the DKG of Gennaro et al. These results highlight EdgeGuard's superior scalability, lower latency, and stronger Byzantine resilience for secure large-scale deployments.

title: Development and Modeling of a Soft Array Actuator for Elderly Assistive Robotic Exoskeleton
authors: Yinan Jin, Yixiao Zheng, Junhao Lin, Zetong Li, Shibo Cai, Guanjun Bao, Prashant Jamwal, Shahid Hussain
publish: IEEE Transactions on Human-Machine Systems
abstract: The global elderly population is increasing rapidly, and more and more countries are stepping into the aging society. Because of deterioration in gait-related functions, such as muscle activity, most elderly people have difficulty walking, which is one of the most basic activities of daily living. A wearable lower limb robot exoskeleton offers a viable solution to this issue. Rigid exoskeletons usually have fewer degrees of freedom and require a quite big power supply for actuation, resulting in difficulties during wearing and motion. Soft exoskeletons could be an alternative choice for elderly assistance, due to their advantages of lighter weight, simpler mechanism, and compliance. Therefore, this research proposed a soft array actuator for a lower limb assistive exoskeleton for elderly people. The fully compliant actuation mechanism was designed using flexible Thermoplastic Polyurethane fabric to power the soft exoskeleton, making it compact, light, and comfortable to wear. The proposed array actuator was modeled on kinematics, contacting force, output torque, and dynamics. Mechanical performance of the compliant actuation mechanism during inflation/deflation at different air pressures and during static force testing has been evaluated. The internal air pressure of the soft array actuator reaches 100 kPa in 0.2 s and fully deflates in 0.48 s. It can generate 53.7–55.7 N to output 8.1–11.2 N·m of torque at the endpoint, which is 13.5% –18.7% of the maximum human hip joint torque during natural walking. The proposed soft actuation mechanism, with good wearability and performance, could be a good fit for a robot exoskeleton that improves natural gait and reduces human metabolic consumption during walking among the elderly. Its lightweight design, good invisibility, and high power-to-weight ratio enable the proposed soft array actuator to provide compliant, continuous, and consistent gait assistance without imposing a psychological burden on the elderly.

title: Focus on Finding Deepfakes: A Robust Proactive Detection Method Based on Orthogonal Moment Watermarking
authors: Chunpeng Wang, Wenlong Ma, Shanshan Zhang, Jie Gui, Qi Li, Yunan Liu, Zhiqiu Xia
publish: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing
abstract: Deepfake detection remains a challenging research topic, especially when the quality of forged images degrades, leading to unreliable detection results. In this paper, we propose a watermarking-based proactive method for robust proactive deepfake detection. First, we embed a watermark into the Fractional-order Quaternion Exponent Moments (FrQEMs) space of the host face image, achieving a balance between imperceptibility and robustness of the watermarking algorithm. Then, we introduce the Frequency Mamba (FreMamba) block to enhance feature extraction by leveraging correlations between frequency-domain subbands, thereby enabling the extraction of more discriminative feature representations. Finally, at the detection stage, we construct a dual-branch framework comprising a watermark extractor and a forgery discriminator. Through knowledge distillation, the watermark extractor guides the forgery discriminator to perceive forgery traces. Specifically, the integrity of the extracted watermark is compromised only when the host image is subjected to a deepfake attack, while conventional attacks do not affect the integrity. Experimental results on benchmark datasets demonstrate that the proposed method achieves superior deepfake detection accuracy. In particular, when images are subjected to conventional attacks, our method surpasses state-of-the-art approaches by more than 5.3% in terms of ACC.

title: Novel Energy-Centric Fossa Optimization-Based Energy-Aware Clustering and Routing in IoTenabled Wireless Sensor Networks
authors: T. Kiruthika, E. Karthikeyan
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: The rapid expansion of Internet of Things (IoT)enabled Wireless Sensor Networks (WSN) demands efficient energy utilization and delivery of data in constrained resources. Traditional cluster-based routing schemes, such as LEACH and HEED, often suffer from unbalanced energy consumption (EC) and unstable routing due to static cluster formation and inefficient cluster-head (CH) selection. To overcome the challenges, this paper develops a Novel Energy-Centric Fossa Optimization (NECFO)-Based Clustering and Routing framework for IoT-WSN. The NECFO method used a lightweight Energy-Distance-based clustering mechanism to balance node energy and proximity to the base station (BS) for optimal cluster formation. After clustering, the Fossa Optimization Algorithm (FOA) is applied to fine-tune the cluster heads selection by evaluating factors, such as residual energy, intra-cluster distance and communication overhead, and to find the efficient multi-hop paths between CH and the sink. The NECFO decreased transmission EC, routing delays and network lifetime. Simulation results show that NECFO provides better energy efficiency, higher packet delivery rates and increased network stability compared with traditional methods and FOA-only approaches. These make it a strong candidate for large-scale IoT monitoring systems.

title: Image-Based Anomaly Detection for Industrial Applications
authors: C Muruganantham, Narasimhan K
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: Ensuring consistent product quality in industrial manufacturing requires precise detection of visual anomalies. Traditional manual inspection is time consuming, error-prone and unsuitable for large-scale operations, while existing automated methods often struggle with robustness, generalization, and computational efficiency. This study systematically evaluates four state-of-the-art deep learning model Patch Distribution Modeling (PaDiM), PatchCore, Reverse Distillation, and Reverse Distillation++, for image-based anomaly detection using the MVTec Anomaly Detection (MVTec-AD) dataset comprising 5,354 high-resolution industrial images. To simulate practical deployment scenarios, models were trained under limited computational resources using transfer learning with pretrained convolutional backbones. Performance was assessed using the Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic (AUROC) metric, achieving average scores between 0.93 and 0.99, with Reverse Distillation++ consistently outperforming others in both detection accuracy and localization precision. These findings highlight the effectiveness of advanced deep learning-based anomaly detection frameworks in enhancing industrial quality control, enabling predictive maintenance, and minimizing production costs. The study further establishes a foundation for future research in multimodal fusion, real-time inspection, and lightweight model optimization for edge deployment.

title: Prediction of Human Animal Conflict Through Machine Learning Based HAR Algorithm
authors: Preethi D, Valarmathi R S, Bhuvaneshwari V, Prithivirajan V, Gnanaprakash V, Vaishnavi P
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: Conflicts and interactions that arise when human wants and activities directly or indirectly collide with those of wild animals are referred to as human-animal conflicts. This conflict can occur in a variety of locations, including rural, urban, and natural settings, and it can take many different forms. Crop raiding, livestock predation, human attacks, and disease transfer are a few frequent interactions with animals. Therefore, it is crucial to keep an eye on animal behaviour and movement patterns, undertake study on it, and be aware of better management techniques and early conflict detection systems. Machine learning algorithms are used to observe and prevent the hunting of endangered species for purposes other than self-defence because it has been seen that human attacks on these animals are growing. The human action recognition (HAR) refers to the comprehension of actions made by a human in a video. Actions modelling is mapping an action to a label that clearly defines a particular occurrence of that activity from various angles and at various speeds. In addition, there are a number of difficulties with newline completely automated HAR systems, including background clutter, newline occlusion, scale, and look. Cost-effective equipment makes it simpler to collect 3D data. As a result, numerous objects and people in a scene can provide depth information in the form of depth pictures. An updated learning method called Self-newline adaptable Differential Evolution-Extreme Machine Learning (SaD-EELM) for action classification is used to overcome problems and challenges of HAR, such as background clutter. Single Layer Feed- forward Network (SLFN) with Depth Motion Maps-Local Newline Binary Pattern (DMM-LBP) is used to improve action classification utilizing depth-based input. Furthermore, newline misclassification is decreased by using the Kernel-based Extreme Learning Machine (KELM) classifier.

title: Dual-Discriminator Adversarial Learning for 2D-to-3D Motion Style Transfer with Remote Information Sharing and Collaboration
authors: Xu Yaoyao
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: This study proposes a 2D to 3D motion style transfer framework based on dual-discriminator adversarial learning. It also combines the remote information sharing and collaborative training mechanisms as the communication optimization to improve cross-node consistency. The proposed method introduces topological regularization on the generator side and, at the algorithmic level, constrains local and global geometric consistency on high-dimensional motion manifolds through Chern-Simons functionals. The discriminator side features a dual-discriminator structure to record global motion style information and local joint motion features, respectively. Additionally, this model presents a geometric field averaging remote collaboration technique for multi-node distributed training settings. Effective consistency optimization and crossnode feature sharing are made possible by this tactic. The experimental design of this work made use of a multi-source motion capture dataset from several labs. The information includes 3D motion annotations for 2D skeleton video clips. Comparative experiments included direct regression (DR), single-discriminator generative adversarial network (SD-GAN), and federated averaging (FedAvg) methods. Experimental results show that the proposed Dual+Remote method achieves the lowest motion reconstruction accuracy (MPJPE) of 18.6, the lowest degree of naturalness of motion generation (FID-M) of 12.4, a smoothness of 0.72, and a motion energy deviation of only 3.1. Furthermore, in the style consistency (SCI) test, the proposed model achieved scores of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{0. 8 1}-\mathbf{0. 8 5}$</tex>. In the remote collaboration experiment, communication overhead was reduced by 92% and it achieves a collaboration efficiency (CE) of 0.61. At the algorithm training level, only 101 training epochs were required, and crossnode consistency reached 0.81.

title: Multi-Temporal Machine Learning-Based Land Use and Land Cover Change Analysis Using Landsat Imagery (1994-2024)
authors: Manasa Kolusu, Suneetha Manne, Sreekar Movva
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: The temporal and spatial effects of urbanization, industrialization and agricultural expansions on the ecosystems of the regions can only be understood by monitoring Land Use and Land Cover (LULC) changes. This paper examines the three decades of LULC changes between 1994 and 2024 in Tamil nadu, India, based on the multi-temporal Landsat imagery and machine learning on the Google Earth engine platform. Cloud masking, atmospheric correction and clipping to state boundary were done in order to make Landsat 5, 7 and 8 datasets consistent. Two supervised algorithms Support Vector Machine (SVM) and Random Forest (RF) were used to create the classified LULC maps that identified the six categories of the land surface which included Water, Forest, Built-up, Agriculture, Fallow Land and Mines. Comparative accuracy Testing showed that the RF classifier had a greater overall accuracy and kappa value than that of SVM especially in spectrally mixed areas. As indicated by the time analysis, the build-up and fallow land areas have increased significantly with a sharp reduction in the forest and agricultural lands. These results indicate the high rate of urbanization and resource overexploitation in Tamil Nadu with the necessity to apply sustainable land use and environmental planning based on evidence-based data.

title: Balancing Transparency and Privacy with Explainable AI and Federated Learning in Fraud Detection
authors: P. Phanindra Kumar Reddy, Fahimuddin Shaik, T. Pavani, B. Vijaya Durga, M. Sai, B. Sai Gowtham Reddy
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: The recenx't approaches supplemented with explainable AI (XAI) and Federated Learning (FL) include transparency and privacy particularly to the high-stakes financial fraud detection. In our project, we are working with the Paysim1 data of Kaggle in order to prepare a model that will not only be in a position to predict the accuracy of the bank transactions successfully, but also be generalized to identify the presence of a fraudulent transaction with the high degree of accuracy. Though the ensuing outcomes of the conventional fraud detecting models such as Deep Neural Networks (DNN), Recurrent Neural Networks (RNN), Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) are handy, they may be considered as problematic in terms of interpretability and privacy since they assume a centralized data processing. To address these weaknesses, the alternative machine learning models will be utilized in the proposed system, which are more understandable, and can address more intricate patterns of fraud the Decision Trees, Random Forests and Gradient Boosting Machines (GBMs). Second, Federated Learning (FL) is also applied to ensure that the models would be trained locally without the need to transmit sensitive data to comply with privacy. Not only will this ensure privacy of the information of the users, but also achieve collaborative learning that can be engaged further to perfect the model of detecting the fraud. The project will additionally be useful in creating a more ethical and effective version of financial fraud identification, the precision of the model with regards to privacy and transparency which incorporates XAI and F.

title: Design and Simulation of the Metamaterial Based Antenna Array to Reduce Mutual Coupling
authors: Shruthi M, Rajini V Honnungar, Deekshitha S, Harshitha MD, Roopa N, Shwetha C
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: In this paper, a systematic procedure is presented to streamline and simplify the design of metamaterial structures using Ansys HFSS simulation tools. A Mutual coupling is a critical limitation in closely spaced micro strip antenna arrays, particularly when the inter element distance is reduced below <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.5 \lambda$</tex>. Such coupling degrades return loss, gain and affecting overall system performance. In this work a single 10 GHz micro strip patch antenna is designed and extend into a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1 \times 2$</tex> array to study coupling behavior under <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\lambda / 2$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$<\lambda / 2$</tex> spacing conditions. To mitigate this, Split Ring Resonator metamaterial unit cell is developed and arranged into a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2 \times 6$</tex> periodic structure. The metamaterial exhibits negative permittivity and permeability, verified through S-parameter retrieval, enabling suppression of surface-wave propagation. When integrated between array elements, enhancing return loss to −36 dB while maintaining stable gain around <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\sim 7.7 \text{dB}$</tex>. the proposed metamaterial-assisted array provides and effective and compact solution for mutual coupling reduction in high frequency antenna system.

title: LFRNet: A Large Language Model-guided Feature Reweighting Network for Lightweight Intrusion Detection
authors: Dalong Zhang, Wenjie Tian, Zhiqing Tang, Yuncong Lu, Wenbo Hu
publish: 2025 International Conference on Cyber Resilience and Endogenous Safety &amp; Security (CRESS)
abstract: Intrusion detection systems in complex network traffic scenarios face critical limitations: conventional statistical methods fail to capture non-linear inter-feature dependencies of system-level features like session duration and TCP handshake delay, while existing learning-based approaches either lack interpretability for security auditing or incur high computational overhead, leading to an "explainability-adaptability trade-off" that undermines detection accuracy, generalization to emerging attacks, and decision traceability. To address these issues, this study proposes Large Language Model-guided Feature Reweighting Network (LFRNet), a lightweight IDS framework integrating data-driven statistical relevance estimation and Large Language Models (LLMs)-derived semantic knowledge. Its core design includes a statistical module quantifying feature-attack relevance via Mutual Information and Kullback–Leibler divergence to filter key features, a knowledge-guided encoder distilling LLM knowledge to assign interpretable semantic weights by correlating feature descriptions with attack-stage templates, and an adaptive fusion module synthesizing dual scores into ordered representations for sequential classification using compact GRU or Tiny-Transformer. Extensive experiments on the UNSW-NB15 dataset—with over 2.5 million traffic records and nine attack categories—show LFRNet outperforms state-of-the-art methods like NSGA2-LR, GA, and MI-Boruta in accuracy, F1-score, and false positive rate, while maintaining robust interpretability and efficiency under resource constraints, validating that LLM-guided system-level feature reweighting effectively enhances the resilience and explainability of cyber defense systems.

title: Evolutionary AI-Driven Android Malware Detection Using Genetic Algorithm-Optimized Feature Selection and Hybrid Machine Learning
authors: M. Sri Lakshmi, Mallireddy Varun Kumar Reddy, Pathan Moiz Ather Khan, Shaik Mohammed Feroz
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: The explosion in growth of Android malware has driven the need for accurate, efficient and interpretable Android malware detection frameworks. This paper introduces EvoMal-Net, an evolutionary artificial intelligence-driven Android malware detection system that combines the genetic algorithm (GA)-based optimization with hybrid ensemble learning. The proposed framework uses a two-stage GA process where optimal feature subsets are determined in the first stage followed by optimal ensemble voting weight optimization in the second stage to get maximum classification performance. EvoMal-Net integrates Light Gradient Boosting Machine (LightGBM) and Deep Neural Network (DNN) classifiers and adds a SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) based explainability layer to the model for improving model transparency. Features extracted from static, dynamic, and contextual data sources are fused to increase the robustness of the detection. Experimental evaluation using a synthetically constructed dataset of Android malware shows that EvoMal-Net achieves 96% accuracy with a 0.95 F1-score and a 0.97 AUC while having a low false positive rate and outperforms conventional baseline models. The results confirm the effectiveness of evolutionary optimization and hybrid learning in enhancing the detection of Android malware with enhanced interpretability.

title: Calibration of an Omnidirectional Vision System and Obstacle Distance Measurements
authors: Ivan Yu. Kholodilin, Yuilya S. Kuznetsova, Kristina M. Saranceva, Ksenia A. Egorova, Tao Zhou, Zihan Zhang
publish: 2026 ElCon Conference of Young Researchers in Computing &amp; Processing, and Information Security (ElCon-CP)
abstract: This paper introduces an open-source, Python-native framework for fisheye camera calibration and single-image obstacle distance estimation using camera and laser emitter. The pipeline performs intrinsic calibration of a fisheye camera, combines robust laser-stripe detection with sub-pixel centerline extraction, and computes geometrically metric ranges from a single omnidirectional capture. Our primary contribution is replacing a prior MATLAB pipeline with a transparent, reproducible Python implementation that supports widely adopted open-source libraries. This shift improves accessibility and portability across platforms. We evaluate the system on both synthetic and real datasets under diverse scenes and illumination conditions, demonstrating accurate calibration and reliable single-shot ranging. The full codebase, experiment scripts, and comprehensive documentation are publicly available. Together, these contributions provide a flexible, reproducible, and readily deployable platform for omnidirectional perception with structured laser illumination. The paper code and supporting materials are available online: https://github.com/kholodilinivan/CVSystemPython.

title: Industry 4.0 Design Principles in Continuous Production Processes: A Case Study
authors: Aline M. Culchesk, Vagner L. Gava, Ana L. F. Facin, Rodrigo F. Gonçalves, Mauro de M. Spinola
publish: IEEE Engineering Management Review
abstract: This study investigates the application of Industry 4.0 design principles in a continuous production environment, specifically in an iron grinding plant. The research aims to understand how principles such as interconnection, information transparency, technical assistance, decentralized decisions, and sustainability, are implemented in the mining sector, and to identify the associated challenges. A case study was conducted in a plant that received financial support to adapt to Industry 4.0 technologies, supported by interviews with industry experts. The results revealed a focus on interconnection and sustainability, which were considered key to optimizing mining processes. Technological implications include the need to move beyond traditional automation by integrating smart sensors, IoT platforms, AI tools, and sustainability-focused solutions. Regarding process type, the study highlights continuous processes, typical in mining, facing greater challenges for Industry 4.0 implementation compared to discrete ones, due to their rigidity and complexity. Managerial implications include the analysis of implementation costs, scalability of technologies, modularization feasibility, and economic viability, especially for small and medium-sized enterprises. These findings provide valuable insights into the practical application of Industry 4.0 in mining and emphasize the need for continuous innovation and strategic adaptation to overcome barriers and fully benefit from digital transformation in continuous production industries.

title: Deep Reinforcement Learning Enabled Joint Task Offloading and Trajectory Optimization in Multi-UAV Systems
authors: Ju Li, Fengjun Shang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Consumer Electronics
abstract: UAV-assisted multi-access edge computing (MEC) enables flexible task offloading in emergency communication networks, but joint trajectory planning and task offloading optimization remain challenging due to UAV mobility and dynamic network conditions. This paper proposes a multi-agent deep deterministic policy gradient (MADDPG)-based framework to jointly optimize UAV trajectory planning and task offloading decisions. The proposed approach follows a centralized training with decentralized execution (CTDE) paradigm, enabling UAVs to learn coordinated computation and communication strategies in dynamic environments. Each UAV is modeled as an autonomous agent, and an actor–critic architecture is designed, where distributed actors generate offloading and movement policies, while a centralized critic evaluates global state–action values to guide cooperative learning. To improve training stability and convergence efficiency, experience replay and soft update mechanisms are incorporated. Extensive simulation results demonstrate that the proposed MADDPG framework significantly reduces task offloading latency and computational overhead while improving system throughput, outperforming conventional optimization methods, and validating its effectiveness in emergency communication networks.

title: From List-Decodability to Proximity Gaps
authors: Yiwen Gao, Dongliang Cai, Yang Xu, Haibin Kan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Information Theory
abstract: Proximity testing for linear codes is a fundamental problem in coding theory with critical applications in cryptographic protocols, blockchain, and distributed storage systems. This work addresses the proximity gaps for linear codes, a crucial aspect for efficiently verifying whether a batch of words is close to a given code. We present a general framework for deriving proximity gaps from the list-decodability properties of the underlying linear code. Our main result show that for any (p,L)-list-decodable code C ⊆ Fnq with minimum relative distance ΔC > p, the probability that a random combination of a batch of t words containing a δ-far word (for δ ≤ 1 − √ 1 − p + ε) remains δ-far from C is bounded by O( tL2pn/q + t/εq ). This result also establishes a form of (mutual) correlated agreement for linear codes, which can be used to strengthen soundness analyses in protocols that rely on proximity testing, thereby reducing query complexity and enabling practical instantiations over smaller finite fields. In particular, we apply our main result to randomly punctured Reed–Solomon codes and folded Reed–Solomon codes—both of which are known to achieve list-decodability up to capacity—and derive linear proximity gaps for these families of codes under the Johnson bound.

title: Integrated On-Board Chargers for Electric Vehicles: Topologies, Control Strategies, Challenges, and Future Research Directions
authors: Malik Qamar Abbas, Lassi Aarniovuori, Pasi Peltoniemi, Michele Mengoni, Luca Zarri
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: The rapid adoption of electric vehicles (EVs) is increasing the demand for charging solutions that are compact, cost-effective, and grid-friendly. Conventional off-board charging stations face challenges such as high infrastructure cost, distribution-grid stress from high-power charging, and limited flexibility for RE integration. Integrated on-board chargers (IOBCs) offer a promising alternative by reusing the traction inverter and motor for charging, thereby reducing system size, weight, and cost, while improving hardware utilization. Despite growing research interest, a consolidated and critical assessment of IOBC architectures and control strategies remains limited. This paper presents a comprehensive review of IOBC technologies, covering key topologies and control approaches for charging, traction, and vehicle-to-grid (V2G) operation. It critically examines major implementation challenges, including unintended charging torque and torque ripple, harmonic current distortion and grid-code compliance, thermal stress due to high power density, and safety and isolation constraints. Finally, future research directions are discussed, with an emphasis on advanced converter topologies, wide-bandgap devices, enhanced thermal management, cybersecurity, and data-driven resilience for secure and reliable EV charging systems.

title: A Quantum Internet Protocol Suite Beyond Layering
authors: Angela Sara Cacciapuoti, Marcello Caleffi
publish: IEEE Transactions on Network Science and Engineering
abstract: Layering, the organization principle of the network protocols underpinning the classical Internet, is ill-suited to the Quantum Internet, built around entanglement, which is non-local and stateful. This paper proposes a quantum-native organizational principle based on dynamic composition, which replaces static layering with a distributed orchestration fabric driven by the node local state and “in-band” control. Each node runs a Dynamic Kernel that (i) constructs a local Plan of Actions (PoA) of candidate steps to advance a given service intent, and (ii) executes the PoA by composing atomic micro-protocols (MPs) into context-aware procedures, namely the meta-protocols. Quantum packets carry an in-band control-field – the meta-header – containing the service intent and an append-only list of action-commit records, termed as stamps. Successive nodes exploit this minimal, authoritative history to construct their local PoAs. As quantum packets progress, these local commits collectively induce a network-wide, direct acyclic graph that certifies end-to-end service fulfillment, without requiring global synchronization or an external controller. In contrast to classical encapsulation, which prescribes a fixed vertical processing order, the proposed suite enforces order by certification: dependency-aware local scheduling decides what may run at a certain node, stamps certify what did run and constrain subsequent planning. By embedding procedural control within the quantum packet itself, the design ensures coherence and consistency between entanglement-state evolution and control-flow, preventing divergence between resource state ad protocol logic, while remaining MP-agnostic and implementation-decoupled. The resulting suite is modular, adaptable to entanglement dynamics, and scalable. It operates correctly with or without optional control-plane hints. Indeed, when present, hints can steer QoS policies, without changing semantics. We argue that dynamic composition is the organizing principle required for a truly quantum-native Internet.

title: Simultaneous Localization and Synchronization in Distributed MIMO-OFDM Systems
authors: Boyu Teng, Xiaojun Yuan, Rui Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Wireless Communications
abstract: Time-of-arrival (ToA)-based user localization typically requires precise clock synchronization between base stations and user equipments, making the localization and synchronization problems tightly coupled with each other. This paper considers a distributed multi-input multi-output (MIMO) orthogonal frequency-division multiplexing (OFDM) system and addresses joint multi-user localization and clock synchronization within an integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) framework. Unlike existing works that only consider clock bias, we account for the impacts of both clock bias and clock skew on MIMO-OFDM signals. Specifically, clock bias introduces a constant offset in path delays, whereas clock skew causes a mismatch in the OFDM symbol durations between the transmitter and the receiver, resulting in linearly varying delays across OFDM symbols. We formulate the joint localization and synchronization problem within a Bayesian framework. Based on variational message passing and the sum-product rule, we propose a message passing algorithm, termed Bayesian Localization and Clock Synchronization (BLACS), which jointly estimates the positions, clock parameters, and velocities of multiple users. Simulation results show that accounting for clock skew significantly improves localization accuracy compared to the baseline methods. Moreover, the proposed BLACS algorithm achieves performance close to the Bayesian Cramér–Rao Bound, demonstrating its effectiveness and near-optimality.

title: Dynamic Spatiotemporal Measurement Uncertainty-Aware Detection for Robust Power System State Estimation Against Stealthy FDIA
authors: Shutan Wu, Qi Wang, Jianxiong Hu, Yujian Ye, Yi Tang, Wei He
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: In cyber-physical power systems, state estimation (SE) serves as a critical function for operational monitoring but is increasingly vulnerable to functional failures induced by stealthy external attacks. To overcome the limitations of traditional attack detection methods for SE, particularly their heavy reliance on prior attack models and static measurement infrastructures, this paper proposes a robust SE model that proactively introduces dynamic spatiotemporal measurement uncertainty (DSTMU) to detect stealthy false data injection attacks (FDIAs) without requiring an explicit prior attack model, thus improving the resilience and security of power system operations. First, to disrupt the attacker's static perception of the measurement configuration, a robust SE model with structural uncertainty and adversarial resilience is constructed by dynamically reconfiguring the measurement sampling pattern via spatial selection and temporal weighting matrices, leveraging the inherent redundancy of hybrid SCADA and WAMS measurements. Subsequently, a consistency-based detection index, the random measurement selection consistency index (RMSCI), is designed to quantify the deviations in SE results caused by varying measurement configurations. The statistical characteristics of RMSCI are analytically derived under normal and adversarial conditions, verifying its sensitivity to stealthy attacks. Furthermore, an offline robust detection-oriented optimization model is formulated, jointly optimizing the measurement selection strategies and detection thresholds. A Benders decomposition-based algorithm is employed to achieve efficient offline solution convergence for the detection-oriented deployment problem. Finally, simulation results demonstrate that the proposed method can effectively detect FDIAs of varying intensities without requiring additional hardware or external detection modules.

title: MicroSDF: Microfacet-Driven Hybrid Neural SDFs for Mixed-Reflectance Surface Reconstruction
authors: Lejia Ye, Yuhua Xu, Yulan Guo, Lian Xu, Xiaohu Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing
abstract: Accurate 3D reconstruction in real-world environments remains a significant challenge due to the coexistence of reflective and non-reflective surfaces, which pose distinct modeling demands. Existing methods often treat these surface types separately, limiting their generalizability and physical plausibility. To bridge this gap, we propose MicroSDF, a novel neural implicit framework that facilitates geometry and reflectance modeling through microfacet theory. Our approach incorporates three core innovations: (i) a microfacet-guided geometry model that extracts multi-scale surface normals (macroscopic and microfacet) from a signed distance field (SDF), regularized by a proposed microfacet normal consistency loss to enforce physically plausible surface orientations; (ii) an enhanced dual-branch color model, where the specular branch leverages the microfacet normals to model high-frequency reflectance, and the vanilla branch, unlike prior works, uses reflection direction (instead of viewing direction) to better model diffuse and low-frequency specular components; and (iii) a detection-guided color blending strategy that adaptively fuses the color outputs based on reflection priors, providing more physically intuitive blending than implicitly learned blending weights. Combined with a tailored multi-stage optimization scheme, the proposed MicroSDF achieves robust and high-fidelity reconstruction across reflective and non-reflective surfaces. Extensive experiments on DTU, Shiny Blender, Ref-NeRF, and DeepVoxels datasets demonstrate state-of-the-art performance, establishing a new direction for physically grounded neural reconstruction.

title: Score-Based Conditional Flow Models for MIMO Receiver Design with Superimposed Pilots
authors: Ruhao Zhang, Yupeng Li, Yitong Liu, Shijian Gao, Jing Jin, Hongwen Yang, Jiangzhou Wang
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Communications Society
abstract: Accurate channel state information (CSI) is vital for multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) systems. However, superimposed pilots (SIP), which reduce overhead, introduce severe pilot contamination and data interference, complicating joint channel estimation and data detection. This paper proposes a conditional flow matching receiver (CFM-Rx), an unsupervised generative framework that learns directly from received signals, eliminating the need for labeled data and improving adaptability across diverse system settings. By leveraging flow-based generative modeling, CFM-Rx enables deterministic, low-latency inference and exploits model invertibility to capture the bidirectional nature of signal propagation. This framework unifies flow matching with score-based diffusion modeling via a moment-consistent ordinary differential equation (ODE), replacing stochastic differential equation (SDE) sampling with a deterministic and efficient process. Furthermore, it integrates receiver-side priors to ensure stable, data-consistent inference. Extensive simulation results across various MIMO configurations demonstrate that CFM-Rx consistently outperforms conventional estimators and state-of-the-art data-driven receivers, achieving notable gains in channel estimation accuracy and symbol detection robustness, particularly under severe pilot contamination.

title: BCEDAS: A Blockchain and Chameleon Hash-Based Efficient Decentralized Authentication Scheme for Internet of Vehicles With Multi-Scenario_supp1-3670901.pdf
authors: Ruizhong Du
publish: N/A
abstract: The Internet of Vehicles (IoV) is a crucial component of Intelligent Transportation Systems, enhancing the intelligence and connectivity of transportation networks through comprehensive data support and communication capabilities. However, since each communication entity within the IoV operates in an open network environment, it is vulnerable to various threats, including user data leakage and vehicle operational security issues. Moreover, the IoV is highly time-sensitive, necessitating the design of a secure and efficient authenticated key agreement (AKA) protocol in IoV. Unfortunately, most current solutions only achieve one-to-one authentication and lack scalability for one-to-many or cross-domain scenarios. As a result, their efficiency remains limited, while alternative AKA schemes that support these use cases tend to introduce linearly increasing costs and computational overhead. To address these challenges simultaneously, we propose BCEDAS that utilizes chameleon hash function, physical unclonable functions and blockchain technology, which minimizes reliance on centralized trusted party while meeting essential security features, including unlinkability, non-repudiation, and side-channel attack mitigation. It also enables cross-domain authentication and multi-vehicle authentication across diverse scenarios. We conducted a comprehensive security deployment analysis by employing widely recognized Real-Or-Random model, along with the ProVerif tool. Furthermore, it exhibits competitive performance metrics in terms of computational efficiency, communication overhead, energy consumption, and average packet loss rate, which demonstrate the potential usability of BCEDAS in various environments.

title: Monocular Optical-Flow Method for Gait Event Detection and Foot-Strike pattern Evaluation in Parkinsonian Gait
authors: Luca Ceriola, Diego Torricelli, Francisco Molina-Rueda, Fabrizio Patanè, Marco Donati, Ilaria Mileti
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: Gait event detection and foot strike pattern (FSP) evaluation are critical in clinical, rehabilitation applications, particularly in monitoring pathologies such as Parkinson's disease (PD). While traditional gait analysis methods such as force platforms and optoelectronic systems offer high accuracy, they are limited by cost, setup complexity, and environmental constraints. This paper presents a novel, low-cost, vision-based system that uses a single sagittal-view RGB camera and neural network modules to identify and track subjects, segment the foot closest to the camera and estimate optical flow. The proposed modular pipeline, named Foot Otsu Optical Flow (FOOF), can automatically detect initial contact and toe-off events, as well as classify foot strike patterns (heel, midfoot, and toe strikes) while walking. The method was validated with respect to an optoelectronic system involving 25 participants, 15 with PD and 10 controls, walking on flat terrain and on irregular terrain. The results demonstrate high sensitivity 0.99 ± 0.04, and specificity 1.00 ± 0.00, and temporal errors up to 0.02 ± 0.01 s for gait event detection. Foot strike pattern analysis on flat terrain indicated excellent heel strike identification (sensitivity: 0.93, precision: 1.00). However, midfoot and toe strike detection presented greater challenges due to misclassifications, particularly in complex walking scenarios and among participants with PD. Heel strike identification remained robust on irregular terrain (sensitivity: 0.83, precision: 0.96), though midfoot (sensitivity: 0.95, precision: 0.67) and toe strikes (sensitivity: 0.68, precision 0.59) were less reliably detected. This study demonstrates a new method for accurately detecting gait events and evaluating FSP with a single camera across different terrains in multiple people environments.

title: FD-SCU: Frequency Decomposition-based Spectrum Collaborative Upsampling for Point Cloud Color Attribute
authors: Hao Liu, Wenchao Wang, Hui Yuan, Raouf Hamzaoui, Weiqing Yan, Junhui Hou
publish: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing
abstract: Existing point cloud color upsampling methods typically treat color upsampling as an interpolation problem within a local color or implicit feature domain. This largely overlooks the ability of the frequency domain to capture color correlations in local point sets. To address this limitation, we propose a spectrum collaborative strategy that uses frequency decomposition on voxel blocks (VBs) to enhance point cloud color reconstruction. We first voxelize the low-resolution (LR) color point cloud to generate multiple VBs and introduce a virtual filling strategy that adaptively assigns colors to empty voxels in each VB, ensuring that the irregularly distributed color information fully occupies the VB. We then apply the discrete cosine transform, known for its strong frequency-domain representation of locally smooth signals, to each color-filled VB to obtain frequency coefficients. These frequency coefficients are separated into high-frequency (HF) and low-frequency (LF) components. The LF coefficients, together with the LR color point cloud, are fed into a multi-scale cross-domain feature extraction module to capture deep features. Next, a Gaussian perturbation-based feature expansion generates upsampled color features, which are used to regress a coarse upsampled color point cloud. Finally, a high-frequency-guided residual refinement module uses the HF coefficients to refine the coarse upsampled result and produce a high-fidelity color point cloud. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method achieves superior performance compared to state-of-the-art methods. Our code will be publicly available at https://github.com/wangwenchaoxx/FD-SCU.

title: Machine Learning Adversarial Attacks using Partial Sinkhorn Optimization
authors: André Bertolace, Konstantinos Gatsis, Kostas Margellos
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Control Systems
abstract: Adversarial attacks are often modeled as pointwise perturbations of individual samples, which can miss structured distributional effects and may waste perturbation budget on examples that are already misclassified. We study a data-driven Wasserstein attack model in which the adversary shifts the empirical distribution under a label-preserving transport budget. Starting from this formulation, we derive a finite-dimensional transport surrogate and an equivalent lifting that makes the role of transport couplings explicit. We then introduce an entropic regularization, obtaining a difference-of-convex formulation that penalizes attacks which mainly amplify the loss of already misclassified samples. This leads to Partial Sinkhorn, an iterative algorithm that combines convex-concave linearization with Sinkhorn-type updates, such that any limit point of a convergent subsequence is a KKT stationary point of the penalized problem. Experiments on synthetic and MNIST tasks show that the proposed method generates stronger attacks than FGSM under comparable perturbation budgets, particularly in the low-distortion regime. The framework also highlights links between adversarial attack, optimal transport, and distributionally robust control.

title: The Role of AI in Climate Change Prediction: Analysing Machine Learning Models for Extreme Weather Forecasting
authors: Nanda Gopal Parise
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: ML and AI are relevant in climate change prediction, more so in extremal weather conditions observation. In terms of ability of the technologies are being applicable in spatial and temporal analysis, estimation, and climatic data. AI models, especially LSTM and YConvLSTM deep learning structures, outperform numerical weather prediction methods. These models are more effective in identifying fragile climate parameters and crossectional patterns. Nonetheless, the LSTM (Long ShortTearm Memory) technology can come in handy in identifying severe weather occasions because of its temporal endurance. YConvLSTM contributes to the enhancement of the regressionbased modeling for the purpose of minimizing the prediction errors, hence enhancing the predictability of the model. The fact is that YConvLSTM helps improve decent predictability of dependability predictions. They help in development of efficient disaster response plans, distribution of resources and even in policy formulation by producing good forecasts. However, rising computational costs, data integrity concerns, and ethical concerns remain. Climate research using artificial intelligence can handle the increasing level of complexity of rising temperatures and extreme weather occurrences. This makes it vital to climate resiliency measures and disaster recovery planning to make people aware of the climate.

title: Four Families of Binary Sequences with Low Ambiguity Function Properties
authors: Furong Bao, Zhengchun Zhou, Yang Yang, Ping Deng
publish: 2025 11th International Workshop on Signal Design and Its Applications in Communications (IWSDA)
abstract: Binary sequence families with low ambiguity function properties serve as fundamental building blocks in advanced wireless communication and radar systems. In this paper, we present a systematic characterization of the ambiguity function properties of four classes of binary sequence families. First, we adopt the convention that the quadratic character <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\eta$</tex> satisfies <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\eta(0)=1$</tex> or −1, which provides a unified representation for binary sequences. Under this definition, we refine the Weil bound for multiplicative character sums and establish their equivalence to the ambiguity functions of sequences constructed via quadratic characters. Through rigorous mathematical analysis of specific exponential sum structures, we theoretically demonstrate that all four sequence classes exhibit excellent low ambiguity function properties.

title: A Resilient LLM-driven Agent Framework for Multi-UAV Mission Planning and Control
authors: Haoyang Chen, Shaoxun Liu, Jinfeng Zhang, Yupeng Zhang, Shiyu Wang, Chenchen Liu, Peng Fu, Yuezhong Zhang, Dongchen Ji
publish: 2025 International Conference on Cyber Resilience and Endogenous Safety &amp; Security (CRESS)
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) offer a new paradigm for unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) mission planning and control. However, their inherent probabilistic nature and hallucination tendencies present significant challenges for safety- critical applications. Despite efforts to mitigate these issues through prompt engineering and post-generation validation, existing open-loop and code-oriented frameworks remain inherently unsafe. To address these limitations, we introduce Decomposer-Executor-Replanner (DER), a closed-loop agent framework designed to enhance the resilience and interpretability of LLM-driven UAV control. The Decomposer injects dynamic UAV states to transform high-level objectives into executable subtasks under explicit safety invariants. The Executor performs sandboxed tool invocations with runtime guards and feedback-aware parameter adjustments, ensuring bounded and interpretable execution. The Replanner monitors mission states, detects anomalies, and triggers recursive replanning based on contextual reasoning and mission memory. Together, these modules form a resilient and verifiable lifecycle for LLM-based UAV autonomy. Experiments conducted in Gazebo with PX4-ROS and up to 30 UAVs demonstrate that DER achieves superior safety, interpretability, and recovery capability compared to monolithic LLM and ablated variants. While current results are simulation-based, DER establishes a foundation for extending LLM-driven UAV control toward real-time deployments.

title: Accident Prone Hotspot Detection Using YOLOv8 Integrated with HDFS
authors: Dheeraj Chandra, Rashi Bagrecha, Ishita Aryasi Karwariya, Gouthaman P, T. Preethiya
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: Emergencies often occur in rapidly developing urban areas. However, delays in identifying and responding to these emergencies increase the fatality rate and worsen the outcome. This may be prevented if there exists an appropriate execution framework concerning the situation. It takes too long to respond to road accidents since crucial data is distributed over various sources such as CCTV cameras and sensors along with manual reports. This work aims to give a solution to the problem by combining the information from surveillance camera footage and IoT sensors to spot critical events and respond to the issue more effectively and accurately. By utilizing YOLOv8n, which supports the methodology to locate accidents in real time. Images from the CCTV camera and IoT sensors provide additional details, like how severe the wreckage is along with determining whether it is an accident or not. To evaluate and prioritize the incidents, machine learning models are utilized for concluding how critical the condition is and which incident requires help at the earliest so that a response can be provided. The system furthermore discovers accident prone regions using hotspots and processes real time details using multi source data. It helps emergency teams to act faster and make better decisions which helps save lives by giving them clear and timely information and reducing the response time.

title: Modeling of Ventilation and Air Conditioning Control Systems for Processing Plants
authors: Ludmila G. Musta, Yuriy K. Kozhubaev, Valeria O. Lozhkina, Yin Changwen, Abbas Ali
publish: 2026 ElCon Conference of Young Researchers in Computing &amp; Processing, and Information Security (ElCon-CP)
abstract: This article is devoted to the modeling of ventilation and air conditioning control systems for processing plants. A study of automated ventilation and air conditioning systems for processing plants is conducted.For this study, an automatic control system suitable for processing plants and enterprises was modeled. Equipment control algorithms were implemented.The following software was used to achieve this result: SMLogix, SMLogix SMH2010, and MASTER-SCADA.

title: TransGate-RiceNet: Attention-Fused Transformer-Convolutional Model for Early and High-Precision Detection of Rice Leaf Pathologies
authors: M. Rajeswari, R. Jeena, P. Sangeetha, G. Mary Valantina, P. Muneeshwari
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: Rice is an important cereal crop in the world and its production is usually influenced by diverse leaf pathologies causing immense agricultural and economic losses. These diseases are very important in crop health and sustainable food production, since early and accurate detection is of great importance. TransGate-RiceNet, which is presented in this paper, is a new hybrid deep learning model that combines the advantages of transformer encoders with gated convolutional neural networks (GCNNs) to identify and classify rice leaf diseases at fine grain. The model takes advantage of a dual stream feature extraction pipeline; the transformer branch extracts the long-range contextual dependencies whereas the gated CNN branch highlights the localization of lesion patterns. An attention fusion module multi-head is used to fuse both global and local representations that boost discriminative features learning in various lighting and environmental conditions. According to experimental assessment of the RiceLeaf-2025 benchmark data, the classification accuracy of the model is 98.84, which is even better than the state-of-theart CNN-only and transformer-only models. Furthermore, the suggested model has strong generalization on untested test sets, and it has high sensitivity and specificity in the detection of diseases at the early phase. The findings show that TransGate-RiceNet can be an effective diagnostic device to support precision agriculture based on AI that ensures that interventions are implemented on time and can be used to maximize yields.

title: A Gradient-Based Causal Discovery Framework With Applications to Complex Industrial Processes_supp1-3674543.pdf
authors: Zhiwen Zhao
publish: N/A
abstract: With the rapid development of deep learning, a wide range of neural network-based causal discovery frameworks have emerged. Although these methods have achieved significant advancements, they still face several limitations when deployed in real-world industrial processes. Many existing models follow the component-wise modeling design, where an individual model must be built for each variable. This leads to significant computational overhead, especially in high-dimensional industrial systems. Moreover, imposing sparsity constraints on the first-layer weights of neural networks to discover causal relationships limits their ability to capture complex and nonlinear interactions among variables. To address these challenges, we propose a novel lightweight causal discovery framework, termed gradient-based causal discovery (GCD). Different from conventional component-wise models, GCD only employs a single multilayer perceptron for time-series prediction and leverages <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\ell _{1}$</tex-math></inline-formula> regularization on the neural network’s input–output gradient to infer causal relationships. Numerical simulations on the Lorenz-96 and CausalTime show that GCD consistently achieves the state-of-the-art performance. Moreover, evaluations across four industrial processes, including Tennessee-Eastman, ultra-processed food, debutanizer, and gas turbine power generation, demonstrate that GCD significantly reduces computational overhead while maintaining high causal discovery accuracy, highlighting its applicability to complex industrial processes.
