title: Quantifying Fluorine Implantation Effects: Modulation of Barrier Height and Electron Effective Mass in Post-Poly-Si Gate PMOS Devices
authors: Lee Sai Link, Mohamed Fauzi Packeer Mohamed, Tan Chan Lik
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: This study explores that additional Fluorine implantation following BF₂ increases the barrier height (Φ<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">B</sub>) and reduces the electron effective mass (m*) in PMOS structures. Two methodologies are used to extract Φ<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">B</sub>, Method I calculates both barrier height (Φ<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">B1</sub>) and m* from the Fowler-Nordheim (FN) plot simultaneously, while Method II calculates the barrier height (Φ<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">B2</sub>) by assuming a fixed effective mass ratio (m*/m<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">e</sub>) = 0.5. The increase in Φ<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">B</sub> with high energy and high dose of Fluorine implantation is proposed due to the neutralization of oxide traps and passivation of interface defects at the SiO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>/Si interface. Furthermore, the reduction in m* is explained by the relaxation of the Si-O-Si network and enhanced orbital overlap due to Fluorine incorporation, evidenced by the increased Si-O-Si bond angle in FTIR analysis. The barrier height is reduced over the stress time due to increased charge trapping at the interface. This paper provides key microscopic insights into how Fluorine co-implantation effectively optimizes gate oxide performance in advanced MOS devices.

title: Quantitative Identification of Strong-Interaction Vehicles in Autonomous Driving
authors: Jinghua Wang, Guangquan Lu, Ailing Yang, Zhao Zhang, Miaomiao Liu, Jinhao Liang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: Understanding vehicle-to-vehicle interactions is critical for safe and efficient autonomous driving in complex traffic scenarios. However, most existing behavior prediction and planning models implicitly account for interactions through soft weighting or metrics based on distance and risk, without a clear or quantitative definition of vehicle interaction. This paper proposes a metric called Vehicle Interaction Level (VIL) to quantify the degree to which a vehicle’s behavior is constrained by another vehicle. By applying a VIL threshold, strong-interaction vehicles are distinguished from weak ones. Then, the Feature Difference Level (FDL) is proposed to analyze feature-level behavioral differences across interaction categories, which is quantified using the Random Forest (RF) and the Gini Index (GI). Based on real-world vehicle trajectory pairs, an optimal VIL threshold of 0.2 is determined by maximizing FDL to identify strong-interaction vehicles, providing a more discriminative and broadly applicable interaction characterization than distance-based and risk-based metrics. Furthermore, the application of this strong-interaction vehicle identification method to trajectory prediction achieves consistently better performance than the baseline across multiple evaluation metrics, demonstrating its practical value for downstream autonomous driving tasks. Owing to its lightweight and model-agnostic formulation, the VIL-based method can be integrated into existing autonomous driving systems operating in highly interactive traffic scenarios.

title: A Proactive Triple-Mutation MTD Architecture Enhancing AI-Driven Intrusion Detection against Intelligent Adversaries in CIoT Networks_supp1-3679035.pdf
authors: Hao Hao
publish: N/A
abstract: With the widespread deployment of intelligent consumer-electronics devices and the rapid expansion of consumer Internet-of-Things (CIoT) ecosystems that support next-generation intelligent systems—including smart homes, edge-AI gateways, and wearable networks—the volume and heterogeneity of device-generated network traffic have surged. To mitigate the limitations of traditional static AI-driven network intrusion detection systems (NIDS), proactive defence paradigms such as Network Moving Target Defence (NMTD) have been incorporated into device–edge–cloud architectures. Unfortunately, sophisticated adversaries may infer defence strategies and circumvent these mechanisms—leading to what we term the drug resistance of intelligent adversarial attacks. To counter this, we propose a reinforcement learning–based proactive Triple-Mutation Moving Target Defense (TM-MTD) framework that simultaneously mutates access proxies, IP addresses, and security strategies. The lightweight edge-level salience optimization ensures accurate detection in highly dynamic traffic environments, while the global policy differential optimization strengthens the defense system’s long-term resilience. Experimental results demonstrate that TM-MTD significantly enhances security performance against a variety of intelligent attack models, providing a robust defense framework for next-generation intelligent consumer-electronics systems.

title: A DC Voltage Robustness Control Strategy of Three-Phase PWM Rectifier Based on Active Disturbance Rejection Technology
authors: Gaoxiang Li, Pengpeng Huang, Xiao Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Electronics
abstract: Under load disturbance, the DC-side voltage of the three-phase pulse-width modulated (PWM) rectifier is prone to fluctuation. To improve the robustness of the DC-side voltage, a single-loop voltage direct control strategy of the three-phase PWM rectifier is proposed based on the active disturbance rejection technology, which has no current loop. In addition, by building the transfer function model, the performance of the three-phase PWM rectifier under the linear disturbance and nonlinear disturbance are analyzed and compared. Compared with the voltage-current double-loop progressive control architecture, the proposed voltage single-loop control strategy can directly regulate the DC-side voltage with using the current information, which can enhance the dynamic performance of the three-phase PWM rectifier. Meanwhile, due to the application of active disturbance rejection control technology, the steady-state performance of the PWM rectifier is also significantly improved. Finally, experimental results verify the feasibility and effectiveness of the proposed control strategy.

title: Cost-Aware Charging Scheduling for Large-Scale Electric Vehicles Based on Hybrid Reward Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
authors: Ying Zhang, Qidong Wang, Haigen Min, Xin Cheng, Yingjie Zhang, Jinchao Chen, Chenglie Du
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Electronics
abstract: The charging cost of large-scale plug-in electric vehicles (EVs) is primarily affected by charging behaviors. In this article, a cost-aware charging scheduling strategy is proposed based on a hybrid reward multi-agent reinforcement learning (HR-MARL) algorithm for multifunctional charging stations that integrate charging, storage, and discharging functions. First, the multicharging constraints are constructed, and the cost-aware charging scheduling problem is formulated. Then, the HR-MARL algorithm is designed to schedule the charging behaviors of large-scale plug-in EVs, considering electricity price fluctuations, users' requirements, and constraints from EVs, charging piles, and charging stations. The main novelties of the HR-MARL include a hybrid reward learning strategy (HRLS) and a local-global information-based replay buffer (LGI-RB). The HRLS helps ensure the target consistency of all agents, while the LGI-RB enhances the learning efficiency of the multiagent system. Finally, the proposed HR-MARL-based charging scheduling strategy is validated through a real-world charging station. The validation results demonstrate that the proposed HR-MARL outperforms several state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods in reducing charging costs for large-scale plug-in EVs.

title: High-utility Sequential Rule Mining Utilizing Segmentation Guided by Confidence
authors: Chunkai Zhang, Jiarui Deng, Maohua Lyu, Wensheng Gan, Philip S. Yu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Knowledge and Data Engineering
abstract: Within the domain of data mining, one critical objective is the discovery of sequential rules with high utility. The goal is to discover sequential rules that exhibit both high utility and strong confidence, which are valuable in real-world applications. However, existing high-utility sequential rule mining algorithms suffer from redundant utility computations, as different rules may consist of the same sequence of items. When these items can form multiple distinct rules, additional utility calculations are required. To address this issue, this study proposes a sequential rule mining algorithm that utilizes segmentation guided by confidence (RSC), which employs confidence-guided segmentation to reduce redundant utility computation. It adopts a method that precomputes the confidence of segmented rules by leveraging the support of candidate subsequences in advance. Once the segmentation point is determined, all rules with different antecedents and consequents are generated simultaneously. RSC uses a utility-linked table to accelerate candidate sequence generation and introduces a stricter utility upper bound, called the reduced remaining utility of a sequence, to address sequences with duplicate items. Finally, the proposed RSC method was evaluated on multiple datasets, and the results demonstrate improvements over state-of-the-art approaches.

title: Distribution-Aware Prompt Learning for Vision-Language Models with Dynamic Boundary Prototype
authors: Xi Yang, Xinyue Zhong, Nannan Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing
abstract: Prompt learning has emerged as an effective strategy for adapting vision-language models (VLMs) which injects learnable semantic prompts into VLMs to guide the alignment between visual and textual representations. Although existing methods have shown strong performance across various tasks, they usually focus on the representative class-level samples and overlook the atypical and hard samples in visual feature space, which hinders generalization of VLMs. To address this issue, we propose the concept of dynamic boundary prototype, which highlights ambiguous samples that are far from the class centroid and is updated at each epoch. Accordingly, we propose a Distribution-Aware Prompt Learning (DAPL) framework to calibrate the distribution of visual feature space via the definition, optimization, and updating of dynamic boundary prototypes. Firstly, we introduce Boundary-Centroid Pulling to optimize the intra-class distribution by progressively reducing the distance between boundary and centroid prototypes, thereby enhancing structural consistency within each class. Secondly, to further enhance inter-class separability, a distance-weighted contrastive loss that places greater emphasis on distinguishing adjacent classes is designed, facilitating more effective fine-grained discrimination. Thirdly, we apply Low-Rank Adaptation Fine-Tuning to adapt the vision encoder through targeted modifications to its self-attention layers. Additionally, we adopt a progressive training strategy for stable optimization. DAPL is compatible with mainstream prompt learning methods such as CoOp, CoCoOp and PromptKD, and consistently improves their average performance across 11 benchmark datasets.

title: Learning to Pursue AC Optimal Power Flow Solutions with Feasibility Guarantees
authors: Damola Ajeyemi, Yiting Chen, Antonin Colot, Jorge Cortés, Emiliano Dall'Anese
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Control Systems
abstract: This paper focuses on an AC optimal power flow (OPF) problem for distribution feeders equipped with controllable distributed energy resources (DERs). We consider a solution method that is based on a continuous approximation of the projected gradient flow – referred to as the safe gradient flow – that incorporates voltage and current information obtained either through real-time measurements or power flow computations. These two setups enable both feedback-based online and offline implementations. The safe gradient flow involves the solution of convex quadratic programs (QPs). To enhance computational efficiency, we propose a novel framework that employs a neural network approximation of the optimal solution map of the QP. The resulting method has two key features: (a) it ensures that the DERs' setpoints are practically feasible, even for an online implementation or when an offline algorithm has an early termination; (b) it ensures convergence to a neighborhood of a strict local optimizer of the AC OPF. The proposed method is tested on a 93-node distribution system with realistic loads and renewable generation. The test shows that our method successfully regulates voltages within limits during periods with high renewable generation.

title: Dynamic Heterogeneous Regularized Physics-Informed Neural Network for Accurate MRI Breast Tumor Classification
authors: Jeba Malar M, N. Kanimozhi
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: Breast cancer is the second most common cause of mortality for women, after lung cancer. Early identification of breast cancer can reduce the death rate for women. Due to the lengthy procedure of traditional breast cancer detection, an automated technique is important for early cancer detection. To overcome these challenges, this research proposes a novel Dynamic Heterogeneous Regularized Physics-Informed Neural Network with Duck Swarm Algorithm (DHRPINN-DSA) approach to improve the classification accuracy of breast tumors. Initially, MRI images are collected from the DCE-MRI dataset. Subsequently, the Adaptive Side Window Joint Bilateral Filter (ASWJBF) ensures excellent visual clarity by efficiently reducing noise while maintaining crucial structural boundaries. After that, Quantum K-Means Clustering (QKMC) technique encodes image features into quantum states and uses swap tests to compare similarity, which enables accurate clustering of questionable areas. Then, Clifford Fourier Mellin Transform (CFMT), which guarantees invariance to translation, rotation, and scaling, is used for feature extraction. Thereafter, extracted regions are passed through a Dynamic Heterogeneous Regularized PhysicsInformed Neural Network (DHRPINN) to enhance tumor classification precision. Finally, the Duck Swarm Optimization Algorithm (DSA) optimizes the DHRPINN weight parameter by mimicking the natural foraging behavior of ducks. This enhances model performance, and the fitness function increases efficiency and accuracy by lowering errors in classification. A model is proposed with an accuracy of 99.87 %, precision of 99.69 %, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{F 1}$</tex>-score of 99.78 % which is able to classify breast tumors with high accuracy for medical diagnosis while minimizing the number of false positives.

title: Automatic Legal Text Summarization: A Survey on Techniques, Rhetorical Roles, Challenges and Future Scope
authors: Rubini A, Golda Dilip
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: Text summarization is a branch of Natural Language Processing (NLP) that aims to automatically produce concise, coherent, and informative summaries from lengthy textual content. It contributes significantly in managing the exponential growth of digital information, especially in complex and domain-specific areas such as legal texts. Legal documents, including judgments, statutes, and petitions, are often lengthy, linguistically intricate, and require deep domain expertise for interpretation. This survey explores various extractive, abstractive, and hybrid techniques applied in the legal domain, along with emerging frameworks incorporating rhetorical role labeling for enhancing logical and coherent summary generation. The study also reviews datasets, data augmentation techniques, and evaluation metrics for both quantitative and qualitative. Furthermore, it highlights current challenges such as handling lengthy documents, dataset scarcity, and the complexity of legal language—;and outlines future research directions toward more explainable and domainadaptive summarization models.

title: A Federated GAN-Augmented Explainable Deep Learning Framework for IoT Intrusion Detection
authors: S. P. Ramya, K. V. Kanimozhi, S. Sreesubha, Moorthy Agoramoorthy, Loganayagi S
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: The increasing number of Internet of Things (IoT) devices has broadened the area vulnerable to attack, making networks more susceptible to advanced cyber threats. A major obstacle to creating strong Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS) is the significant imbalance in real-world network data. In this data, normal traffic greatly exceeds attack instances, which leads to poor detection rates for less common attacks. This paper suggests a new approach that combines Federated Learning (FL), Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs), and Explainable AI (XAI) to overcome these challenges. A Wasserstein GAN with Gradient Penalty (WGAN-GP), improved using Genetic Algorithms, is used to create realistic synthetic attack data in a privacy-focused, collaborative manner across various IoT devices. This enhanced data is then used to train a combined deep learning model, using both Convolutional and Long Short-Term Memory networks (CNN-LSTM), to identify spatial and temporal patterns in network activity. Additionally, Local Interpretable Model-agnostic Explanations (LIME) are used to provide clear, understandable explanations for each prediction. When tested on several standard datasets (UNSWNB15, IoT-23, CIC-IDS2018), the proposed approach shows a significant improvement in performance, achieving over 99% accuracy and better detection of uncommon attacks, while ensuring data privacy and clear explanations for security analysts.

title: A Mobilenet-Driven Transfer Learning Model for Papaya Fruit Disease Classification
authors: Anly Antony M, R Satheeshkumar
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: Papaya is an important fruit crop that is grown in many places and is a big part of the agricultural economy. But diseases like Anthracnose, Black Spot, Phytophthora, Powdery Mildew, and Ring Spot often hurt papaya production. These diseases cause big losses in yield and quality of the fruit. Timely and accurate identification of these diseases is therefore essential to reduce economic losses and promote sustainable papaya cultivation. In this study, a transfer learning-based disease classification workflow using the MobileNet architecture is proposed to address this challenge. Owing to its lightweight design and computational efficiency, MobileNet is well suited for agricultural applications with limited resources. The proposed approach leverages pre-trained MobileNet models and adapts them to a papaya disease dataset, enabling effective feature extraction while significantly reducing training time. The workflow involves image preprocessing, data augmentation, fine-tuning of selected MobileNet layers, and performance evaluation using standard classification metrics. Experimental results indicate that the proposed model successfully distinguishes between multiple papaya disease categories, achieving classification accuracies of 90.78% for Anthracnose, 91.2% for Black Spot, 90.23% for Phytophthora, 92.1% for Powdery Mildew, 89.6% for Ring Spot, and 98.98% for healthy samples. These results demonstrate the feasibility of the model for implementation in mobile and resource-constrained contexts, enabling farmers to undertake early disease detection in real-world conditions. In essence, this work proposes a realistic and scalable deep learning approach for papaya disease surveillance, with interesting implications for smart agriculture and better food security.

title: AI-Enhanced Anomaly Detection for Adaptive Nearline Data Processing at FRIB
authors: Pathan Sardar Khan, Sudhir Kumar Sangula, Chunduru PavanKumar, Alluri Veema Rao, Nara. Kalyani, Ch.C.Ravi Hemanth Kumar
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: The Facility for Rare Isotope Beams (FRIB) generates large volumes of experimental data that must be processed nearline to support timely analysis and operational decisions. Existing nearline pipelines are optimized for throughput but provide limited capability for detecting anomalies or adapting to changing experimental conditions in real time. In this work, we present an AI-enhanced anomaly detection framework integrated into the FRIB nearline data processing environment. The proposed approach embeds unsupervised machine learning models, including DBSCAN clustering and Isolation Forest, directly within the FRIBDAQ event-editing workflow to continuously monitor detector responses, particle identification outputs, and processing behavior. The system was evaluated using more than 15 million trace events, corresponding to approximately 8.7 TB of experimental data from a recent FRIB run. The AI module achieved an anomaly detection rate above 99% while introducing only a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1. 9 {\%}}$</tex> reduction in processing throughput. In addition, the adaptive feedback mechanism provided parameter adjustment recommendations that were accepted by operators in most cases, leading to improved anomaly response times. These results show that AI-enhanced nearline monitoring can improve operational reliability while preserving the high-throughput performance required for large-scale nuclear physics experiments.

title: Sat-Based Model for Verifying Access Policies with Graph Zone Decomposition in Critical Information Infrastructure Networks
authors: Damir R. Musin, Alexandr V. Dushkin
publish: 2026 ElCon Conference of Young Researchers in Computing &amp; Processing, and Information Security (ElCon-CP)
abstract: Industrial firewall rule sets in ICS/CII networks often contain thousands of entries with prefix overlaps, nested groups, negations, and vendor-specific service abstractions. These configurations must be validated offline due to air-gapped architectures and regulatory requirements (IEC 62443, NIST SP 800-82), yet existing verification tools depend on network topology, controller state, or simplified rule semantics. This paper introduces a topology-independent verification framework based on atomic predicate normalization and a communication-induced partition of the address space. We formally prove the correctness of the resulting atomic partition, the completeness of the equivalence-class decomposition, and the SAT-preserving properties of predicate slicing. Experiments on nine mixed real and synthetic ICS datasets (340–6150 rules) show a speedup of 2.4 to 3.1 times over direct SAT, full agreement with SMT/BDD baselines, and stable scaling for industrial rule structures, making the method suitable for offline auditing of critical infrastructure firewalls.

title: Cyber Resilience Quantification: A method for Resistance and Recovery Capabilities Measurement
authors: Dalong Zhang, Zhilong Han, Shuguang Ding, Gangtao Han, Zhiqing Tang
publish: 2025 International Conference on Cyber Resilience and Endogenous Safety &amp; Security (CRESS)
abstract: With the increasing complexity of cyberspace and the evolving nature of cyber threats, cyber resilience has become a key focus of cybersecurity research. Quantitative assessment of cyber resilience is a pivotal aspect of this field. This study proposes a mathematical model that captures system performance degradation and recovery dynamics under adversarial attacks or systemic perturbations. Based on this model, we develop an objective quantitative method for measuring cyber resilience, with a primary focus on the system’s resistance and recovery capabilities in the face of cyber threats. The proposed method not only provides system administrators with more precise evaluation results but also establishes a scientific foundation for designing targeted improvement strategies.

title: When Less is More: A Comparative Study of Transformer Models for Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis
authors: Shailendra K Mishra, Karan M, Tanisha Shinthray
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis (ABSA) provides an in-depth insight into user feelings by associating emotions with specific attributes, a process that is crucial for appraising feedback in contexts like restaurant reviews. While Transformer-based models lead in this area, there is often a lack of direct comparisons showing how various architectural improvements influence performance within a single, controlled framework. To address this gap, this paper introduces a thorough benchmark of three different Transformer architectures: a basic model (BERT_BASE_ATEPC), an enhanced version with a Local Context Focus mechanism (FAST_LCF_ATEPC), and a more intricate variant that also incorporates Sentence-level features (LCFS_ATEPC). The models were evaluated using the standard SemEval-2014 Task 4 restaurant dataset within the PyABSA library to ensure a fair and replicable assessment. The results indicate that the FAST_LCF_ATEPC model delivers the highest performance, while the added complexity of the LCFS_ATEPC model did not lead to further improvements. This finding suggests that while unique mechanisms like Local Context Focus are extremely advantageous, merely enhancing architectural complexity does not ensure optimal performance, underscoring the importance of targeted model development in ABSA.

title: Retrieval-Confused Generation: An Effective Provider-Side Defense Against Privacy Violation Attacks in Large Language Models
authors: Wanli Peng, Xin Chen, Hang Fu, Xinyu He, Juan Wen, Yiming Xue
publish: 2025 International Conference on Cyber Resilience and Endogenous Safety &amp; Security (CRESS)
abstract: The capacity of Large Language Models (LLMs) to infer sensitive personal information from seemingly innocuous user data presents a significant privacy risk, known as a Privacy Violation Attack (PVA). While client-side defenses exist, they often introduce substantial latency by requiring iterative API calls for anonymization. This paper introduces Retrieval-Confused Generation (RCG), a novel provider-side defense mechanism designed to counteract PVA. RCG operates by intentionally misleading the LLM’s inference process for queries identified as potential attacks. Our method first constructs a "disturbed" database by using an LLM to paraphrase a corpus of user comments. Then, for a given query, we employ a most-irrelevant retrieval strategy to select a semantically dissimilar entry from this database. This retrieved entry replaces the original user data in the context provided to the LLM. This substitution causes the LLM to generate responses based on incorrect contextual cues, leading it to infer wrong personal attributes and thus degrading the attack’s effectiveness. Extensive experiments on two datasets and eight prominent LLMs demonstrate that RCG significantly reduces the success rate of PVA, showcasing its potential as an efficient, inference-time countermeasure.

title: Interpretable Machine Learning Approaches for Resilient DDoS Detection in Software-Defined Networks
authors: Phanindra Kumar Reddy Poli, Sasidhar Reddy Kudumula, G Homeshwar, A Madhuri Yasaswini, P Nithya Nandeep Kumar, R Vinesh Reddy
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: SDNs are dynamic, scalable and may be susceptible to Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) because of its centralized control. In this paper, hybrid machine learning methodology has been introduced to detect DDoS in SDN. The suggested approach helps to use unsupervised algorithms (K-Means clustering, Principal Component Analysis (PCA), and K-Best feature selection) with supervised classifiers (Decision Trees, Random Forests, and ensemble classifiers including Stacking and Voting). The unsupervised methods are used to decrease dimensionality and create features that are significant, therefore, maximizing the accuracy of classification. The findings show that all models had an accuracy of 96 per cent, which shows that the hybrid model is effective in separating between normal and malicious traffic. In addition, ensemble techniques especially, Stacking and Voting classifiers were also found to be robust and gave the most reliable result. This framework will be used to improve the security of SDN by offering an effective DDoS detection mechanism. The next generation of SDN can see the addition of deep learning models and explainable AI in order to enhance the functionality and interpretability of SDN.

title: Flexible Federated Learning in Machinery Fault Diagnostics with Light Communication
authors: Xiang Li, Weipeng Fan, Shaojie Yang, Wei Zhang, Xu Li
publish: IEEE/CAA Journal of Automatica Sinica
abstract: While data-driven fault diagnosis methods have been successfully developed in the past years, large amounts of high-quality condition monitoring data are generally required to ensure model performance. Due to the high economic and labor costs in data collection, it is difficult for a single user to build an effective database, and exploring data of multiple users for better training becomes a promising solution. However, data privacy is of great importance in the real industries due to conflicts of interest, and direct data aggregation from different users is hardly feasible. To address this issue, a flexible federated learning method is proposed in this paper. Different from most existing methods with identical models under the federation, different customized individual deep neural network models can be used at different clients. Public data are exploited for knowledge transfer. Only the scores on public data are communicated between clients and server, rather than the whole model parameters. That significantly reduces the communication and computational burden. Experiments are carried out on two real-world machinery fault diagnosis datasets, and the results show the proposed method is promising for data privacy-preserving federated learning with flexible models and light communications.

title: Data-Driven Weather Forecasting and Climate Modeling Using Hybrid Modeling Techniques
authors: Mekala Varshith Reddy, M Nagaraju, G. Srinivas, Swobhagya Sahoo
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: Traditional meteorological models tend not to be updated to accommodate nonlinear and dynamic climate evolution, restricting their applicability in high-stakes areas. This paper introduces a hybrid, parallelized forecasting system that combines Auto Regressive Integrated Moving Average (ARIMA), Random Forest, and Long Short-term Memory (LSTM) models. Guided by logic gate-inspired pitch estimation algorithms, the model adapts adaptive selection with weighted variance penalties for unweighted model inclusion. A Self-Organizing Map (SOM) supports the system's computational efficiency through data dimensionality management. Measured on actual data, the model outperforms conventional and hybrid baselines consistently, providing better accuracy, generalization, and robustness to environmental variations. The new hybrid model outperformed in terms of accuracy, with Random Forest having the maximum <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{R}^{\mathrm{2}}$</tex> value (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{0. 9 9 3 4}$</tex>) followed by LSTM (0.9292) and detecting seasonal trends but losing out in multivariate treatment. Even with such improvements, difficulties such as high computational expenses, real-time adaptation, and explainability continue. Real-time dynamic adaptation of the model, implementation of Explainable AI, and investigation into using quantum computing for scalable weather prediction solutions are topics of interest in future research.

title: Multi-Objective Optimization for Electric Vehicle Charging Infrastructure
authors: Deepshikha Raghuvanshi, Nishant Kumar
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: There has been heightened demand for Electric Vehicles recently which brings with itself, the need of a wellplanned EV Infrastructure that can balance service quality, economic feasibility, and grid stability to promote EV adoption. This paper presents a comparative analysis of classical optimization methods applied to the strategic deployment of Electric Vehicle Supply Equipment (EVSE). The problem is formulated as an unconstrained non-linear minimization task designed to find an optimal mix of Level <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$3\left(x_{1}\right)$</tex> and Level 2 chargers <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\left(x_{2}\right)$</tex> by taking power capacity (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$x_{3}$</tex>) and daily service hours (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$x_{4}$</tex>) into consideration. The objective function minimizes the daily cost which is impacted by the choice of chargers, capital investments and customer waiting time. Four classical optimization algorithms - Steepest Descent, Nonlinear Conjugate Gradient, Quasi-Newton BFGS and Modified Newton - are evaluated across four diverse real-world scenarios: Highway Rest Stop, Urban Shopping Mall, Office Complex and Residential Area. All four scenarios have different volumes of traffic and different requirements. Experimental results consistently show that QuasiNewton is the most robust and efficient method for this problem. Modified Newton performs well when curvature information is well behaved, whereas Steepest Descent and Conjugate Gradient show noticeable sensitivity to scaling and constraint stiffness. First order methods are found to be inadequate for non-linear problems like this due to slow convergence rate.

title: Research on a Hybrid Cooling Topology Based on a Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor with Tooth-Tip Span Angle Modulation Configuration
authors: Tiange Shi, Ke Zhang, Zuosheng Yin, Xudong Huang
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: As aircraft increasingly transition toward more-electric/all-electric architectures, the demand for higher power density in aviation motor systems continues to grow. However, prolonged high-power-den-sity operation significantly intensifies the challenges of motor thermal management. To address this issue, this paper proposes a hybrid cooling topology based on a Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor with Tooth-tip Span Angle Modulation Configuration (TSAM-PMSM), which inherently exhibits both high power density and high reliability. For the stator, cooling copper tubes using aviation kerosene as the coolant are integrated into the inter-phase air gaps and double-layer winding slots of the TSAM-PMSM. This enables long-term safe operation at high power density without requiring a separate heat exchanger, thereby further improving the system power density and simplifying the cooling system. By adding cooling slot structures around the copper tubes, low-reluctance magnetic paths are provided for the flux linked with the tubes, greatly suppressing eddy current losses in the copper tubes. For the rotor, a titanium-alloy hollow shaft with copper thermal bridges is proposed to address the heat dissipation challenge of the thin, high-heat-density copper shielding layer. This enhances the rotor’s thermal stability and further reduces the temperature rise of the permanent magnets. A Lumped Parameter Thermal Network (LPTN) is established for both the stator and rotor, enabling rapid analytical calculation of the steady-state temperature rise of key components in the TSAM-PMSM. FEA results demonstrate that the proposed hybrid cooling topology allows the TSAM-PMSM to operate continuously at a current density of 12 A/mm² while meeting the temperature rise requirement.

title: An Improved Multifrequency Model of RC Based on Three-dimensional Framework
authors: Tao Zhang, Hui Wang, Jingshuo Yan, Jiawen Wang, Bowen Han, Teng Cui, Ruyan Liu, Yifei Wang, Bai Guo, Ruslan A. Ufa
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: This article presents a novel multifrequency modeling framework for resonant converters with pulse frequency modulation (PFM) and diode modulation (DM). The cross-coupling effect of the two modulation stages are derived quantitatively from the three-dimensional (3D) perspective. A 3D metope z(t, y) enveloped by modulation signal and a cylinder y2 spanned by the carrier is constructed within 3D system. Mapping the crossline from the intersection of both geometries onto plane toz retrieves switching functions of PFM and DM, i.e., sw= z(t, y2). Applying Taylor approximation, equal aera principle, and geometrical projection relation to the expression provides the disturbed switching functions. They assume the form of time-domain multiplication between two signals, i.e., the impulse sequences and the input signal of modulation stages. The cross-coupling effect from input signal to switching function is derived accordingly through frequency convolution. By integrating the cross-coupling effect, the multifrequency model of resonant converter is constructed following a systematic workflow. A matched critical parameters derivation approach is presented to solve the multifrequency model. A novel dynamic phenomenon termed “generalized beat frequency” is introduced through proposed framework, which is the extension of well-known “beat frequency dynamics”. Modeling simplification is done to facilitate the controller parameters design. Series resonant converter and LCL-T resonant converter are taken as paradigm to validate the effectiveness in terms of high-fidelity modeling and predicting the generalized beat frequency. The four interested transfer functions namely, control to output, input to output, input impedance, and output impedance are derived to compare with existing modeling approaches. Simulation and experiment demonstrate the accuracy of proposed multifrequency modeling framework.

title: A Single-Phase Three-Switch DC–AC Inverter with Wide Gain and Continuous Input-Output Current
authors: Hafiz Furqan Ahmed, Alireza Lahooti Eshkevari, Iman Abdoli, Mohsin Jamil
publish: IEEE Transactions on Power Electronics
abstract: This paper presents a new single-phase three-switch DC-AC inverter with a built-in high-frequency voltage isolation transformer (HFT). Compared to the counterpart topologies, the proposed inverter uses just three switches and no diodes. This is the minimum number of switching devices reported for DC-AC inverters. This feature also reduces switching and conduction losses, resulting in higher efficiency. The proposed inverter offers both continuous input and output currents, which significantly improves both input and output power quality. The presence of HFT isolation not only meets grid connection requirements but also effectively suppresses the common-mode voltage and leakage current problems commonly found in traditional transformerless DC-AC inverters. This also allows for two degrees of freedom in adjusting the voltage gain: the turns ratio of the HFT and the duty ratio of the power switches. Additionally, the suggested topology is able to offer wide buck-boost voltage inversion, making it well-suited for connecting to renewable energy sources that experience significant voltage fluctuations. Detailed comparisons exhibit that the inverter achieves lower total switch voltage, lower total switch current, and switching device power (SDP) compared to various isolated inverters. To show the performance of this inverter, a 400VA experimental prototype has been designed and comprehensively evaluated.

title: Immersion and Invariance Adaptive Integral Sliding Mode-Based Second-Order Prescribed Performance Control of Flexible Joint Manipulators
authors: Youngwook Jung, Junseok Boo, Kangmin Jo, Dongkyoung Chwa
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: This paper proposes an immersion and invariance (I&I) adaptive integral sliding mode-based second-order prescribed performance control (ISM-SOPPC) framework for flexible joint manipulators (FJMs) subject to dynamic uncertainties and external disturbances. Unlike existing approaches that primarily focus on fast subsystem vibration suppression without explicitly shaping the transient response of the slow subsystem, the proposed method attenuates vibrations in both fast and slow subsystems while simultaneously enforcing prescribed transient tracking performance. To this end, the high-order FJM dynamics are decomposed via singular perturbation theory, and controllers for the resulting fast and slow subsystems are designed within a dual-time-scale control framework. Within this framework, a funnel-type performance boundary derived from a second-order canonical step response is introduced for the slow subsystem to explicitly shape the desired transient behavior while mitigating potential singularity issues. In addition, by combining an integral sliding mode strategy, the resulting ISM-SOPPC eliminates the reaching phase and thereby supports the enforcement of the prescribed performance constraints from the initial time instant. Moreover, an I&I adaptive ISM-SOPPC method with a noncertainty-equivalent adaptive structure is developed to enhance the estimation performance of dynamic uncertainties and external disturbances. The resulting controller establishes robust closed-loop stability while satisfying the prescribed performance constraints. Simulation and experimental results on a 2-degree-of-freedom FJM validate the effectiveness and robustness of the proposed framework.

title: A Real-Time App-Controlled Robotic Navigation System with Gated recurrent unit(GRU)-Based User Destination Prediction
authors: Heonjong Yoo, Wan-Young Chung
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Autonomous navigation in structured environments, such as solar power panels, plays a vital role in improving operational efficiency and reducing human dependency. This study focuses on the trajectory tracking and control of a 4-wheel mobile platform (Ranger Mini V2) for autonomous navigation between designated solar panels areas. The brief report contributes to the control of the mobile platform 4 on the solar panel location based on the determination of the app. The state flow method facilitates the 4 wheel mobile platform to recognize its current location information and heading angle meanwhile the platform is moving at the solar panel location. The trajectory tracking method is an important technique that facilitates the driving performance of a 4 mini wheel ranger without human intervention. Ubuntu ROS and the ROS trajectory tracking program laptop are connected through robot operating system network. Firstly, the previous line following method is incorporated into the switch block in ROS trajectory tracking program to divide various line generation to the solar 1, 2, 3 panels. Additionally, the Gated recurrent unit(GRU) and app determine the destination number, then send the number to ROS trajectory tracking program during experimentation. The gain parameter in the block is automatically changed to the destination number during experimentation. This setup enables automatic gain adjustment based on AI predictions and user input from the app. Lastly, the experiment is carried out through the ranger mini version 2 mobile platform on the place.

title: Seeking Flat Minima Over Diverse Surrogates for Improved Adversarial Transferability: A Theoretical Framework and Algorithmic Instantiation
authors: Meixi Zheng, Kehan Wu, Yanbo Fan, Rui Huang, Baoyuan Wu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
abstract: The transfer-based black-box adversarial attack setting poses the challenge of crafting an adversarial example (AE) on known surrogate models that remains effective against unseen target models. Due to the practical importance of this task, numerous methods have been proposed to address this challenge. However, most previous methods are heuristically designed and intuitively justified, lacking a theoretical foundation. To bridge this gap, we derive a novel transferability bound that offers provable guarantees for adversarial transferability. Our theoretical results demonstrate that optimizing AEs toward flat minima over the surrogate model set, while controlling the surrogate-target model shift measured by the adversarial model discrepancy, yields a comprehensive guarantee for AE transferability. The results further lead to a general transfer-based attack framework, within which we observe that previous methods consider only partial factors contributing to the transferability. Algorithmically, inspired by our theoretical results, we first elaborately construct the surrogate model set in which models exhibit diverse adversarial vulnerabilities with respect to AEs to narrow the instantiated adversarial model discrepancy. Then, a model-Diversity-compatible Reverse Adversarial Perturbation (DRAP) is generated to effectively promote the flatness of AEs over diverse surrogate models to improve transferability. Extensive experiments on NIPS2017 and CIFAR-10 datasets against various target models demonstrate the effectiveness of our proposed attack.

title: Heterogeneous Multiplayer Reach-Avoid Differential Game Under Partial Information
authors: Zihao Ma, Hao Liu, Guanghui Wen, Frank L. Lewis
publish: IEEE/ASME Transactions on Mechatronics
abstract: This article investigates a defense strategy for the pursuit team under partial information, to minimize the number of evaders reaching the target region. The differential game characterized by players having heterogeneous speeds and sensing radii is decomposed into a collection of subgames, and the defense strategy is divided into a deployment strategy and a task assignment strategy. The deployment strategy is proposed by solving the constrained optimization problem associated with the barrier. The multiplayer task assignment is formulated as a maximum weight bipartite matching problem, and addressed by the sequential constrained integer programming. The effectiveness of the defense strategy is demonstrated through theoretical analysis and experimental validation.

title: A Hybrid Robust Time-Delay Control Framework With Integrated Disturbance Observer and Adaptive Sliding Mode Control for Robot Manipulators
authors: Jinsuk Choi, Wookyong Kwon
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Electronics
abstract: This article proposes a hybrid robust time-delay control (HRTDC) framework that incorporates a disturbance observer (DOB) and adaptive sliding mode control (ASMC) within a time-delay control scheme. The DOB mitigates time-delay estimation (TDE) errors and effectively suppresses continuous low-frequency disturbances, while the ASMC rapidly compensates for abrupt high-frequency disturbances by adaptively tuning switching gains based on error dynamics. By leveraging the complementary strengths, the proposed HRTDC achieves robust compensation for both persistent and sudden nonlinear effects, including Coulomb friction, stiction, and discrete external disturbances. To the best of the authors’ knowledge, this work presents the first integrated HRTDC framework addressing both long-term and short-term TDE error compensation. The effectiveness and robustness of the proposed approach are validated through simulations and experiments conducted on robotic manipulators, demonstrating reduced overshoot, suppressed chattering, and significantly improved tracking accuracy compared to existing methods.

title: Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor Stator Current AM–FM Model and Demodulation Analysis for Demagnetization Fault Diagnosis
authors: Jinduo Zhang, Haoran Sun, Tianyu Han, Xi Shi
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Electronics
abstract: The reliable operation of permanent magnet synchronous motors (PMSMs) is critical in modern drive systems, while demagnetization of permanent magnets (PMs) remains one of the major faults threatening their performance and safety. Considering the coupled mechanical, magnetic, and electrical interactions, an analytical model of the stator current under demagnetization conditions is developed. The model reveals that demagnetization introduces complex amplitude modulation and frequency modulation (AM–FM) in the current signal, elucidating the underlying fault mechanism. The validity of the proposed model is verified through Fourier spectrum analysis, amplitude demodulation, and frequency demodulation. In the frequency demodulation process, the current signal is first denoised via solving the Laplace norm minimization problem and then adaptively decomposed using the direct fast iterative filtering (dFIF) algorithm to accurately extract individual instantaneous frequency (IF) components and identify FM characteristics. Finally, analysis of simulated and experimental signals validates the effectiveness of the proposed method, confirming that demodulation analysis notably enhances the reliability and sensitivity of the diagnosis.

title: Residual Modeling and Experimental Validation of Motion Noise Compensation for a Rotating Accelerometer Gravity Gradiometer_supp1-3677991.docx
authors: Ji FAN
publish: N/A
abstract: Airborne gravity gradiometers play a vital role in geophysical exploration, navigation, and resource detection. However, motion-induced noise on moving platforms can exceed true anomalies by several orders of magnitude. The rotating accelerometer gravity gradiometer (RAGG) employs an orthogonal antisymmetric configuration to suppress common-mode accelerations, while frequency modulation suppresses low-frequency noise, yet nonideal factors still introduce residual acceleration noise. To address this, a motion noise compensation method formulated in the rotating reference frame and based on transfer coefficient calibration is developed. The method eliminates the need for demodulation and remodulation, thereby simplifying motion term modeling while maintaining compensation accuracy. The experimental results demonstrated the feasibility of this approach, with the combined output noise reduced to a level approaching that of RAGG self-noise in static measurements. Furthermore, a residual model is proposed to predict the suppression capability and residual noise after compensation. Its validity is confirmed by experiments in a three-axis shipborne environment, where noise is suppressed from 4×10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">-4</sup> g/Hz<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1/2</sup> to 5×10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">-8</sup> g/Hz<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1/2</sup>, corresponding to a suppression factor of approximately 9×10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup>. This is consistent with residual model predictions and confirms the validity of the proposed method. The proposed framework thus offers a quantitative basis for evaluating compensation performance and guiding the advancement of gravity gradiometers.

title: Special Issue on AI-Driven Optimization for Autonomous Vehicle Electronics [From the Guest Editors]
authors: Sousso Kelouwani, Bai Li, Basilio Lenzo, Vo-Duy Thanh
publish: IEEE Vehicular Technology Magazine

title: Re-evaluating Virtual Reality Manipulation Techniques for Precise Alignment of Complex 3D Objects
authors: Cherelle Connor, Alexander Giovannelli, Leonardo Pavanatto, Francielly Rodrigues, Haichao Miao, Vuthea Chheang, Brian Giera, Peer-Timo Bremer, Doug A. Bowman
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: Prior research has developed a number of manipulation techniques that can achieve precise object placement in virtual reality, but studies of these techniques typically use simple objects. We conducted a study comparing two existing techniques, (AMP-IT and WISDOM), during alignment of objects with complex geometry to evaluate the potential influence of geometric complexity on performance, usability, workload and preference. Our findings indicate that participants had faster completion times and higher trial completion rates with AMP-IT on high-precision alignment tasks, contrary to earlier findings that used simple objects. Yet WISDOM is still preferred and considered more usable, despite increased workload and poorer performance, exposing participants' willingness to trade objective performance for comfort during use.

title: Virtual City Traffic Simulator with AI-Driven Agents
authors: Jeba Malar M, Jaffron Shalom Kennedy R, Harish M
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: These testing environments are usually costly and time-consuming to deploy safely in real-world conditions. This paper describes the Virtual City Traffic Simulator with AIDriven Agents, an interactive 2D environment written in Python for modeling complex traffic dynamics. The developed simulator allows users to create a customizable urban layout design comprising roads, junctions, roundabouts, flyovers, and diversions, and visualizes how traffic flow would evolve in real time. Vehicle and pedestrian agents utilize a combination of rule-based behavior, pathfinding, and reinforcement learning to provide adaptive routing, congestion avoidance, and smart signal control. The overall architecture also provides mechanisms for the simulation of emergencies, accidents, and infrastructure modifications, enabling users to test various traffic management policies within a safe, virtual environment. Experimental evaluations show high accuracy regarding collision avoidance, congestion reduction, and stability in traffic flow on diverse scenarios. The proposed virtual testing facility offers a digital platform to researchers, planners, and policymakers for gaining insights about urban mobility and pretesting traffic interventions before physical deployment.

title: Fomo Based Brinjal Quality Assessment Using Edge Ai Model for Smart Agricultural Automation
authors: Jyothi M Patil, Meenaxi M Raikar, Channabasappa Muttal, Pooja B Patil
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: Automated quality assessment of agricultural produce is required for reducing the post-harvest losses and enhance sorting efficiency. In the proposed system low-power and real-time defect detection system for brinjal (Solanum melongena L.) using an edge AI model deployed on a Raspberry Pi 5 platform is presented. The proposed model uses the FOMO (Faster Objects, More Objects) object detection framework optimized for embedded hardware. The model takes preprocessed RGB images of brinjals as input, extracts discriminative features through convolutional layers, and outputs predictions identifying defective and non-defective samples. The servo-based actuation mechanism allows automatic physical sorting based on inference results. The system achieves high accuracy with minimal latency, demonstrating its suitability for on-field and post-harvest sorting applications. Experimental results confirm that the proposed model offers an efficient, scalable, and energy-conscious solution for automated quality assessment in small-scale agricultural supply chains.

title: Human Pose Estimation for Intelligent Automation and Vision Systems
authors: Sneha Giridharan, S. Jeba Priya, D.Sujitha Juliet
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: Human pose estimation has undergone significant advancements due to breakthroughs in deep learning, enhanced computational power, and innovative methodologies. This paper explores the latest techniques in pose estimation, particularly focusing on developments in 3D reconstruction, marker-less tracking, real-time processing, and applications across robotics, healthcare, and augmented reality. Recent advancements in technology have fueled progress in the HPE (Human Pose Estimation) domain. The advancement in the field has revolutionized machine perception; retrieving the pose of a person allows computers to make much more intelligent decisions, especially concerning human-machine interaction, understanding of behavioral intent, proxemics, and the state of the person, informing actions to be carried out by the intelligent machine. On review of notable approaches such as lightweight neural networks, hybrid frameworks, and ensemble methods are also discussed, highlighting their contributions to improving accuracy, robustness, and computational efficiency.

title: A Redundant-Channel Switching Method for Secure and Resilient Control of Industrial Systems under FDI Attacks
authors: Xinmei Li, Xuan Li, Xianghui Cao
publish: 2025 International Conference on Cyber Resilience and Endogenous Safety &amp; Security (CRESS)
abstract: The deep integration of computation, communication, and control has driven the rapid evolution of industrial control systems (ICSs), improving automation, efficiency, and decision-making. However, this interconnectivity also exposes ICSs to cyber–physical vulnerabilities, among which false data injection (FDI) attacks are particularly threatening due to their stealth and ability to manipulate sensing or actuation signals without triggering conventional alarms. Such attacks can compromise state estimation and disrupt closed-loop control, leading to severe operational and safety risks. This paper investigates the problem of secure state estimation and resilient control for continuous-time ICSs subjected to coordinated FDI attacks on both sensors and actuators. To address this challenge, a redundant-channel switching framework is proposed to autonomously isolate compromised communication paths, while a generalized pairwise switching sliding mode observer (GP-SMO) is developed to simultaneously reconstruct attack signals and estimate system states. Using Lyapunov-based analysis, sufficient stability conditions are derived to ensure bounded estimation errors under bounded disturbances. Simulation results validate its capability to exclude corrupted channels, achieve accurate estimation, and enhance the overall resilience of industrial processes under sophisticated cyber threats.

title: ReGLeN: Resilient Critical Node detection in Cyber-Physical Systems via Self-Supervised Graph Representation Learning
authors: Yancheng Mu, Junjie Zhang, Lixin Ji, Shuxin Liu, Lan Wu, Qi Ouyang
publish: 2025 International Conference on Cyber Resilience and Endogenous Safety &amp; Security (CRESS)
abstract: Ensuring the resilience of Cyber-Physical Systems against targeted attacks necessitates proactive protection of critical nodes, a task hampered by the reliance of existing methods on labeled data or incomplete structural metrics. This paper introduces a self-supervised graph representation learning framework ReGLeN for resilient critical node detection, which autonomously learns robust node embeddings by integrating network topology, node attributes, and semantic edge features derived from textual data using GPT-2. Key results show our method achieves a high AUC score for identifying critical nodes and has a lower ANC value compared with centrality-based and machine learning baselines. The derived node embeddings enhance classifier precision, demonstrating an ability to capture structural patterns inaccessible to traditional features. This work establishes a practical, unlabeled paradigm for critical node identification, directly contributing to enhanced protection strategies and improved resilience in complex CPS infrastructures.

title: Hybrid IoT-ML Framework for Metro Safety: Crowd Density Forecasting with Onboard Health Surveillance
authors: R. Surendar, T. Hariprasath, S. Karthick
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: The way people commute by metro in towns poses a desperate situation with regards to safety of passengers during rush hours and emergency cases. In this paper, the authors introduce an IoT-based smart metro safety system, which integrates the real-time monitoring of the crowd density with passenger health by applying machine learning algorithms. The suggested system utilizes an embedded platform of Arduino Uno which is connected to various sensors such as heart rate monitors, blood pressure sensors, and temperature sensors to individual health tracking and the computer vision techniques to estimate the crowd density. Random Forest classifier was found to be 94 percent accurate <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\text{R 2}=\text{0. 9 4}$</tex>, MAE <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=\text{2. 8 3})$</tex> in forecasting patterns of crowd density in varying intervals of time. The comparative analysis of three ML algorithms, including Those that are Random Forest, Support Vector Machine (SVM) and Neural Networks, has shown that the former produces the best results, namely 79 percent accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score of the crowd classification tasks. A Neural Network model was able to achieve 66.7 percent accuracy in classifying alerts by classifying them into overcrowding, equipment failure, suspicious behavior and temperature deviations. The system has real-time data display by LCD displays, GSM-based alerts and UART communication protocols. Experimental outcomes prove the ability of the system to ensure the safety of passengers due to the provision of early warnings in case the crowd density is over the safe or critical parameters (90 people/unit area). It is a holistic solution because it provides metro operators with practical solution to proactive safety management and coordination of emergency response.

title:
The reliable preassigned-time consensus control of the second-order multi-agent systems: An event-triggered sliding-mode control approach
                    <sup>*</sup>
authors: Feida Song, Leimin Wang, Zhi-Wei Liu
publish: 2025 International Conference on Cyber Resilience and Endogenous Safety &amp; Security (CRESS)
abstract: For the fast consensus control of unmanned systems, preventing the infinite gain of the controller is crucial for ensuring the safe operation of the controlled system. Firstly, by reasonably relaxing the convergence requirements of the system, a new practical preassigned-time (PPAT) stabilization theory is proposed. The significant advantage of this lemma is that it avoids the singularity problem that may arise in the design of preassigned-time controllers. Based on this lemma, a novel practical sliding-mode control (SMC) is designed and applied to the PPAT leader-following consensus control of second-order multiagent systems (MASs). Unlike traditional SMC, the design of this sliding surface does not require the sliding variable to be zero at all times for the stability of the system. The sliding variable only needs to converge to a small neighborhood around the ideal sliding manifold to ensure the system stability, which is more in line with the digital controller in networked environments. Furthermore, a corresponding event-triggered scheme (ETS) is designed to intelligently decide the triggering moments, reducing the communication frequency. Finally, some experiments are given to validate the safety and effectiveness of this algorithm in ensuring the rapid convergence of unmanned systems.

title: Graph-Theoretic and Deep Learning Hybrid Framework for Optimized Healthcare Diagnosis and Treatment Prediction
authors: Nirmala B, Sindhusaranya Balraj, Nongmaithem Aristotle Singh, Ramu Kuchipudi, A. Kaliappan, Vishal Ratansing Patil
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: In healthcare, where it is indispensable for diagnosis, treatment, and prediction, AI has sparked a revolution among many other industries. Medical imaging tests like X-rays, CT scans, and MRIs can be interpreted very accurately by AI-driven systems, particularly by deep learning algorithms, which can spot anomalies and disorders. By quickly analysing massive volumes of clinical data, these innovations allow doctors to make accurate diagnoses and educated treatment decisions. With an emphasis on data preprocessing, feature extraction, and classification, this study delves into the integration of AI approaches for healthcare diagnosis and therapy prediction. Conventional methods of feature extraction based on projections frequently remove unnecessary data but do not always provide the best results. To get over these restrictions, they offer a new graphtheoretic approach to object detection that can use detection sub-windows of any shape. A graph cut method finds the global best solution by combining local feature responses with boundary information, while the LLC approach finds the local feature replies. The suggested Graph-Theoretic LLC model achieved a 96.12% success rate in diagnostic prediction tasks, according to the experimental data. This shows that it has the ability to improve the accuracy of disease detection and provide more reliable healthcare treatment prediction.

title: Practical EM Side-Channel Security Evaluation of Low-Cost IoT Microcontrollers Using Raw Traces
authors: Ylicia Godinez, Jaya Dofe
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: The increasing deployment of low-cost Internet of Things (IoT) devices in cyber-physical systems raises significant security concerns for embedded cryptographic implementations. While prior side-channel studies focus on lab-targeted platforms with dedicated measurement points, real-world, mass-market devices are less understood. This study systematically investigates the vulnerabilities of Advanced Encryption Standard (AES) to electromagnetic (EM) side-channel attacks on both XMEGA boards and Arduino Uno boards. Using correlation electromagnetic analysis (CEMA) and differential electromagnetic analysis (DEMA), we quantify attack success with metrics such as probability of guessing the key (PGE) and the differential coefficient. Our results show that while XMEGA platforms allow full key recovery with relatively few traces, attacks on Arduino Uno achieve only partial success, even after tens of thousands of traces, highlighting the effects of trace variability, environmental noise, and component differences. These findings provide a realistic assessment of EM attack feasibility on low-cost embedded devices and offer guidance for practical security evaluations beyond labtargeted boards.

title: Two Novel Constructions of Mutually Orthogonal Complementary Sets
authors: Yu Wang, Dejiao Cai, Yongqiu Chen, Zilong Liu, Chunlei Li, Xiuping Peng
publish: 2025 11th International Workshop on Signal Design and Its Applications in Communications (IWSDA)
abstract: This paper presents two new constructions of mutually orthogonal complementary sets (MOCSs), which have found wide applications in engineering. The first is a direct method utilizing extended generalized Boolean functions (EGBFs) to generate MOCSs with flexible, non-power-of-two lengths. This method can also produce complete complementary codes (CCCs) and is well-suited for hardware implementation. The second is an indirect method that combines existing complementary sequence sets (CSSs) and MOCSs to create larger sets. To further expand the existence space of MOCSs, we also propose a novel direct construction method for CSSs.

title: Real-Time Multimodal Anaemia Detection Using Deep Learning
authors: Snehal Rathi, Sneha Katole, Kunal Thakare, Soham Tawari, Rahul Solanke
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: Anaemia continues to be a widespread health issue, especially in regions where basic diagnostic facilities are not readily available. Conventional screening depends on laboratory blood tests, which are accurate but not always feasible in field settings or community-level programs. In this work, we present a portable screening approach that evaluates anaemia without the need for invasive procedures. The system observes visual signs of pallor from images of the fingernails, conjunctiva, and palm while simultaneously recording physiological information such as blood-oxygen saturation and heart rate through inexpensive sensor modules. A compact convolutional model analyses subtle colour and texture variations in the images, and these visual cues are combined with sensor-given output to provide a more complete prediction. This multimodal design helps reduce the errors typically seen in image-only methods, particularly when lighting, skin tone, or device quality varies. Initial testing shows that integrating both visual and physiological signals leads to more reliable results, making the system suitable for rapid assessments in community health programs and other low-resource settings.

title: Lightweight Techniques for Federated Anomaly Detection in Log Data
authors: Anastasia Pustozerova, André García Gómez, Max Landauer, Markus Wurzenberger, Florian Skopik, Edgar Weippl, Rudolf Mayer, Andreas Ekelhart
publish: IEEE Transactions on Reliability
abstract: Accurately and efficiently identifying anomalies within log data is crucial for maintaining the reliability, availability, and security of modern computing systems. In interconnected environments, log data often come from distributed sources such as Internet of Things (IoT) devices, industrial networks, or smart grids. Centralizing these logs for anomaly detection can be challenging due to strict confidentiality requirements, regulatory constraints, and the limited bandwidth of edge networks. Federated learning (FL) offers an alternative by enabling local model training on site, while aggregating only models instead of sensitive data, thereby preserving data confidentiality and reducing data transfer. This paper develops and evaluates a federated log-anomaly detection pipeline and analyzes its components. We adapt lightweight anomaly detection techniques in a federated setting, comparing them with deep learning (DL) methods, assessing detection capabilities, computational efficiency, memory requirements, and inference time. We explore the residual privacy risks in FL within the proposed pipeline, develop a threat model and suggest mitigation strategies. Our findings indicate that lightweight anomaly detection methods can match the effectiveness of DL techniques in the FL framework, while often providing improved computational and communication efficiency. Furthermore, these techniques show better resilience to non-IID data distributions, a typical FL challenge that can severely hinder the effectiveness of traditional machine learning models. However, the memory footprint of lightweight models varies with dataset characteristics and, in some cases, may exceed that of DL models.

title: Human-in-the-Loop Pareto Optimization: Trade-off Characterization for Assist-as-Needed Training and Performance Evaluation
authors: Harun Tolasa, Volkan Patoglu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Haptics
abstract: During human motor skill training and physical rehabilitation, there exists an inherent trade-off between task difficulty and user performance. Characterizing this trade-off is crucial for evaluating user performance, designing assist-as needed (AAN) protocols, and assessing the efficacy of training protocols. In this study, we propose a novel human-in-the loop (HiL) Pareto optimization approach to characterize the trade off between the performance and the perceived challenge level of motor learning or rehabilitation tasks. We adapt Bayesian multi criteria optimization to systematically and efficiently perform HiL Pareto characterizations. Our HiL optimization employs a hybrid model measuring performance with a quantitative metric, while the perceived challenge level is captured with a qualitative metric derived from preference-based user feedback. We demonstrate the utility of the framework through three use cases in the context of a manual skill training task with haptic feedback. First, we demonstrate how the characterized trade-off can be used to design a sample AAN training protocol and evaluate the group level efficacy of the proposed AAN protocol relative to a baseline assistance protocol. Second, we demonstrate that individual-level comparisons of the trade-offs characterized before and after the training session enable fair evaluation of training progress under different assistance levels, providing insights even when users cannot perform the task without assistance. Third, we show that the characterized trade-offs also enable fair performance comparisons across users, since they capture each user's best possible performance at all feasible assistance levels.

title: Tactile-Guided Exploration and Positioning for High-Precision Robotic Peg-in-Hole Tasks
authors: Yuzhe Huang, Wanlin Li, Ziyuan Jiao
publish: IEEE/ASME Transactions on Mechatronics
abstract: Robotic peg-in-hole tasks are fundamental to many industrial applications but remain challenging due to the need for precise component alignment. Vision-based systems enable effective object detection, but their pose estimation accuracy often degrades in the presence of hardware noise and environmental disturbances. To address this, we present <underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">Tac</u>tile-guided <underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">E</u>xploration and <underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">P</u>ositioning (TacEP), a framework that integrates visual and tactile sensing for high-precision positioning of the target receptacle in robotic peg-in-hole tasks. TacEP first estimates the receptacle’s pose from a monocular camera. To compensate for errors during camera estimation, the system reactively adjusts the pose of a high-resolution Vision-Based Tactile Sensor through real-time exploration, enabling finer positioning of the receptacle. We evaluated TacEP on a physical robotic system across peg-in-hole tasks with five daily connectors, achieving an 81.0% success rate with mean position and orientation errors of 0.37 mm and 0.48<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$^{\circ }$</tex-math></inline-formula>, respectively. Compared to the vision-only baseline, TacEP achieved a 97.7% reduction in position error and an 89.5% reduction in orientation error. These results demonstrate that the proposed framework significantly improves insertion reliability and accuracy, highlighting the potential of tactile sensing for high-precision robotic peg-in-hole tasks.

title: Joint Optimization of Routing and Scheduling in Cross-Domain Deterministic Networks
authors: Xiaolong Wang, Haipeng Yao, Lin Zhu, Wenji He, Wei Zhang, Mohsen Guizani
publish: IEEE Transactions on Network and Service Management
abstract: Industrial Internet applications require networks to guarantee deterministic end-to-end latency and zero packet loss at both the data link and network layers. Traditional best-effort communication models in consumer networks are insufficient to meet these stringent demands. To meet these stringent demands, the IEEE 802.1 standards introduce Time-Sensitive Networking (TSN) at the data link layer, while the IETF proposes Deterministic Networking (DetNet) for the network layer. However, enabling seamless cross-domain communication between TSN and DetNet remains a significant challenge. This paper proposes a unified cross-domain network architecture and a time-slot alignment strategy that compensates for synchronization errors between the TSN and DetNet layers. We further develop a Joint Routing and Scheduling algorithm for Deterministic Cross-Domain Transmission (JRS-DCT), which simultaneously addresses routing and scheduling under cross-domain constraints. The algorithm leverages Cycle-Specified Queuing and Forwarding (CSQF) in DetNet and Cycle Queuing and Forwarding (CQF) in TSN to ensure bounded latency and deterministic transmission. Extensive simulations demonstrate that the proposed JRS-DCT algorithm significantly improves the scheduling success rate and effectively reduces network resource utilization compared to two baseline algorithms. These results validate the effectiveness and robustness of the proposed framework in supporting time-sensitive communication across heterogeneous network environments.
