title: Fuzzy-LFS: A Novel Localized Feature Selection with Fuzzy Region Division and Local Neighborhood Rough Set for Classification_supp1-3679190.pdf
authors: Yu Zhou
publish: N/A
abstract: Localized feature selection (LFS) methods divide the whole sample space to determine region-specific subsets for classification which outperform traditional feature selection with a global feature subset for the entire sample space. However, existing LFS algorithms decompose the local regions by impurity level of samples, which can misplace critical samples and impair feature selection results. To tackle this issue, this paper proposes a novel Fuzzy Localized Feature Selection (Fuzzy-LFS) algorithm, which divides local regions around each sample based on Gaussian fuzzy membership to enhance the coherence of region-specific information. Besides, to handle data fuzziness and uncertainty, Fuzzy-LFS establishes a Local Neighborhood Rough Set Model with forward greedy optimization to search the feature subsets. A novel local classifier is subsequently developed, overcoming the limitations of global classifiers unsuited for LFS while mitigating the excessive dependency on impurity level in traditional local classifiers. Specifically, for high-dimensional datasets, we introduce a Localized Feature Relevance Pre-Selection strategy, assigning sample-specific feature subsets according to local relevance to assist in dividing local regions and enhance classification performance. Through comprehensive experiments on 11 low-dimensional and 13 high-dimensional datasets, Fuzzy-LFS achieves superior classification accuracy to state-of-the-art LFS methods, demonstrating its effectiveness.

title: Non-uniform Air Gaps Regulation Method for Hotspot Suppression of Ferrite Cores in 11.1 kW Inductive Power Transfer Systems
authors: Xian Zhang, Yaxin Li, Yize Wei, Fei Xu, Zhixin Chen
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: The local overheating problem of ferrite cores in magnetic coupling structures (MCS) of Inductive Power Transfer (IPT) systems has become a critical challenge limiting high power applications. In this paper, a non-uniform air gap regulation method between ferrite blocks is proposed to reduce hotspot temperatures on the ferrite core. The impacts of four air gap structures (lg1 - lg4) on the hotspot temperature of the ferrite core, system coupling coefficient, and leakage flux characteristics are systematically investigated. Subsequently, a multi-objective optimization design using the Non-Dominated Sorting Genetic Algorithm II (NSGA-II) is conducted to determine an optimal combination of air gaps. To rigorously evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed approach, a comprehensive performance index considering temperature, efficiency, and leakage flux is constructed. To verify the feasibility of the proposed structure, an 11.1 kW/85 kHz experimental test platform is established. Experimental results demonstrate that compared to a uniform air gap of 0.4 mm with equivalent coupling, the optimized structure reduces the hotspot temperature by 8.5% and the thermal gradient by 27.4%. Consequently, the comprehensive performance index of the MCS is enhanced by 32.1%, confirming the method's effectiveness in improving thermal reliability without compromising electrical performance.

title: In Situ Grown MoS₂ on FTO: A Scalable and Biocompatible Platform for Selective Detection of Dopamine
authors: Christeena Thomas, K A Benazeera Beegum, Saranya Sasi, S Sreeja, A S Asha, R Reshmi
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: 2D Molybdenum disulfide ( MoS<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>) has emerged as a pivotal material in nanoscience and nanotechnology due to its remarkable properties and multifaceted applications. The primary obstacles that need to be tackled in applications like electrochemical sensing utilizing 2D MoS<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> are its limited scalability and poor adhesion. Consequently, there is strong motivation to design sensing platforms that balance selectivity, stability, and practical feasibility in real-world sensing applications. This work addresses these critical material challenges while placing equal emphasis on establishing a stable and selective platform for dopamine sensing, which remains a crucial objective in neurochemical diagnostics. The study adopts a facile one-step hydrothermal synthesis approach to grow highly adhesive MoS<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> on the FTO substrate, a first-time report of utilizing such MoS<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>/FTO electrode for stable, selective, and repeatable detection of dopamine. The material has shown very good crystallinity, high surface area, greater electrocatalytic performance, and excellent hemocompatibility and biocompatibility with 98.63% cell viability even after 24 hours. The sensing performance of the MoS<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>/FTO electrode was studied using Cyclic Voltammetry (CV), Differential Pulse Voltammetry (DPV), and Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS). Dopamine molecules were detected in the linear range of 0.5 to 15 μM (R2= 0.98) with a limit of detection (LoD) of 93 nM. The sensitivity of the electrode is observed to be 0.82 μAμM⁻¹mm⁻², and the selectivity study of dopamine in the presence of major interfering species shows that the MoS<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>/FTO electrode is only selective to dopamine. The significance and novelty of this work lie in its comprehensive approach to overcoming key limitations associated with MoS<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>-based electrochemical sensors, particularly issues of scalability and electrode adhesion. By simultaneously focusing on achieving high selectivity and reproducibility for dopamine detection, the study contributes meaningfully to the development of reliable electrochemical platforms for sensing applications. Furthermore, the repeatability, reproducibility, and stability of the fabricated material have been evaluated to ensure reliability and practical applicability.

title: Direct Target Localization for Distributed Hybrid Active-Passive Radars with Direct-path Interference
authors: Qiyu Zhou, Ye Yuan, Wei Yi, Hongbin Li, Maria Sabrina Greco, Fulvio Gini
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: Distributed hybrid active-passive radars (HAPRs) with separately deployed transmitters and receivers face localization performance limitations due to the inevitable presence of direct-path interference (DPI). To improve target localization accuracy and enhance DPI suppression, we develop a direct position estimator tailored for stationary/low-speed targets in distributed HAPRs, which jointly exploits signals from both active transmitters and non-cooperative illuminators of op portunity (IOs). However, the joint processing of active and passive observations, coupled with the unknown signals from non-cooperative IOs, leads to a intractable high-dimensional optimization problem. The expectation-maximization algorithm is employed to address the optimization problem with low complexity, iteratively suppressing DPI and updating the position estimate. To further reduce computational complexity, we use a sequential estimation method that decomposes the high dimensional problem into a sequence of low-dimensional ones. Wealso derive the Cram´ er–Rao Lower Bound (CRLB) to provide an accurate performance benchmark for the proposed estimator. Extensive numerical analysis shows that the proposed direct estimator achieves estimation accuracy closer to the CRLB than estimators used in only active or passive distributed radars.

title: Table of Contents
authors: N/A
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)

title: Robert K. Brayton and the Theory of Nonlinear Circuits [In Memoriam]
authors: Wolfgang Mathis
publish: IEEE Circuits and Systems Magazine
abstract: This article is dedicated to the scientific achievements of Robert K. Brayton in the field of nonlinear circuit theory, who passed away in January 2025. Internationally, he is recognized as an expert and pioneer in the fields of logic synthesis and formal verification as well as circuit simulation, but his groundbreaking work on nonlinear RLC circuits is rarely mentioned. Therefore, following a biographical sketch of Robert K. Brayton’s scientific career, his contributions to nonlinear circuits in the context of the applications that formed the basis of his research will be explained. In particular, we discuss the influence of his research results on the further development of the field of nonlinear circuits. Based on relevant literature, it is shown that the differential geometric interpretation of the dynamics of electrical circuits leads to a physical model for a broader class of physical systems, which is also of interest in control theory and physics.

title: A Novel Finite-Time Stability Criterion for Nonlinear Systems Involving Flexible Delayed Impulses
authors: Shuchen Wu, Xiaodi Li, Shiji Song
publish: IEEE/CAA Journal of Automatica Sinica
abstract: Dear Editor, This letter studies the finite-time stability (FTS) problem of nonlinear impulsive systems with delayed impulses. The system under consideration is subjected to a flexible class of time-varying delays in impulsive signals, allowing them to arbitrarily span one or more impulse instants. Sufficient Lyapunov-based conditions for FTS of such systems are presented, where a novel class of impulse time sequences is established for all admissible uncertainties of delayed impulses. Compared with the existing FTS studies with delayed impulses, our proposed results not only provide greater versatility for impulse delay, but also relax the constraint on the uniformity of impulse intervals. It also shows that delayed impulses have a potential negative impact on system stability by weakening the effect of impulsive control or even converting a stabilizing influence into a destabilizing one. Finally, we give an example with corresponding numerical simulations to validate our theoretical results.

title: Datasets, Metrics, Benchmarks and Future Research in Autonomous Driving: A Review
authors: Yuchen Li, Siyu Teng, Zizhang Wu, Junhui Wang, Mingyu Liu, Zhe Xuanyuan, Long Chen
publish: IEEE/CAA Journal of Automatica Sinica
abstract: Data-driven autonomous driving is a hot topic in academic and industry research due to its impressive performance, flexible mobility, and reduced human intervention. However, the development of this technology relies heavily on large datasets that contain accurately annotated data, obtained through artificial or semi-automated strategies. Consequently, datasets play a crucial role in autonomous driving, and their characteristics significantly impact the effectiveness of algorithms. Currently, there are several diverse datasets available, such as KITTI and CityScape, that cover various tasks. However, researchers often overlook the unique features, similarities, and specificities of these datasets. Furthermore, to the best of our knowledge, there is a lack of survey articles focusing on special metrics and benchmark performance on different datasets in autonomous driving. Therefore, the purpose of this article is to analyze autonomous driving datasets, guide researchers on collecting and utilizing relevant datasets, summarize evaluation strategies, analyze benchmark performance, and provide future research points to enrich the autonomous driving community. We believe that this work will assist researchers in evaluating their data using suitable metrics and offer a fresh perspective on autonomous driving.

title: Adaptive Ai Traffic Signal Control
authors: Ms. T. Bhuvaneswari, M. Anushree, M. Arunkumar, M. Dhivyaa, V. Hariharan
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: The predictability regarding the vehicle flow pattern, congestion pattern, and scalability compared to the fixed timer traffic signals makes the jobs of traffic managers more complex. Currently available traffic solutions based on AI are mostly hardware intensive and based on PCE or are black boxes in terms of logic regarding traffic signal control and do not provide reasons behind other decision-making choices. The increase in cost of operation and absence of clarity pertaining to factors like the above are hindrances in accepting this system. Adaptive AI Traffic Signal Control the proposed project will seek to achieve the implementation of the adaptive software-only realtime traffic flow control system with the advantage of being transparent, flexible, and fully automated. At the structure level of such an adaptive system, the YOLOv8 detection model will be employed for the interpretation of images pertaining to cars, buses, trucks, and motorbikes for up to four lanes when conducting calculations depending on the number of vehicles. Traffic lanes will be set with a relative density level established with the use of special logic for ranking. The clean interface from Flask provides users with access to image uploads from traffic signals with marked detection results, comparison of lane density, and finally the analysis of how AI assigns traffic signal times. The engine of the simulation works through the cyclic display of traffic signals in each lane based on the length of the green time period. The user can follow the system process towards the end in the stages of the procedure from detection up to traffic signals. The experimental results of proposed method achieve High Accuracy <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$92 \%, \text{Reliability} 90\%, \text{Adaptability} 91\%, \text{Efficiency} 90\%$</tex>.

title: Ladder-DenseNet Driven Deep Transfer Learning Framework for Automated Glaucoma Detection Using Fundus Images
authors: A. Thamodharan, R. Pugalenthi
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: The reason of glaucoma being identified early in the world is one of the common causes of irreversible blindness even though once the eye suffers irreversible damage, the eye may not be able to take in any further information and vision becomes blurred. Most of the classical CNN based models are also ineffective in the representation of multi-scale features and the strong gradient flow which leads to poor classification results in real clinical practice. To defeat them, the proposed study will use a Ladder-DenseNet-based deep transfer learning framework in the diagnosis and classification of glaucoma disease by using fundus images. The main strength of the work is the refining of features towards the ladder-like, and the closely connected convolutional layers, which offered the opportunity to provide the information further and reuse the features more efficiently, as well as localize the glaucoma-related features in the structure. The originality of the proposed solution based on the idea is the ability to learn high-level representations and simultaneously reduce the loss of features and improve the stability of the model compared to the classic deep learning setup. The components of the workflow are preprocessing, Ladder-DenseNet based transfer learning, feature improvement, and final classification of the glaucomatous and the normal retina images. It was evaluated on the Kaggle Glaucoma Fundus Image dataset where it is demonstrated that the proposed model works better with an accuracy of 98.3, precision of 97.9, recall of 97.5, F1-score of 97.7, specificity of 98.1, AUC of 99.0 and Cohen kappa of 97.1.

title: VGG16 and ResNet50 for Potato Leaf Disease Prediction
authors: Animesh Srivastava, Sumant Kumar Mohapatra, Parveen Kumar, Bhupender Singh Rawat, Rakesh Pandey, Vikash Sawan
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: Precision potato leaf disease detection is a key element in ensuring food security and minimal loss of food production. Traditional detection using manual processing has the limitation of high cost and labor intensity in particular during the infection stage. In this paper, we have done a comparison analysis transfer learning based performance of two popular deep CNN architectures- VGG16 and ResNet50 to classify potato leaf diseases. The data set was normalized and enhanced and then categorized into healthy, early blight, and late blight. Custom dense layers are added and further trained both models on the augmented dataset to evaluate their performance. Two different architectures are tested in the experiments. Results show that both models can achieve exciting performance results, in particular ResNet50 has the best performance; the average accuracy of ResNet50 is 96%, which is 2% higher than VGG16. With ResNet50, residual learning architecture can alleviate the problem of vanishing gradient and learn features more effectively, thus it can achieve better generalization results and faster convergence speed. The results indicate that transfer learning with deep residual architectures allows for a, robust and scalable model to detect potato diseases. This work is a stepping stone for intelligent, IoTfriendly and agriculture systems for disease detection and yield improvement of crops.

title: Comparative Model Analysis for Early Detection of Breast Cancer Using Clinical Data
authors: Bukke Deekshith Naik, Vasala Hema, Rimmalapudi Ravichandra, Mude Nagarjuna Naik, Sriramkumar R, Lakshmanan M
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: Breast cancer detection is one of the prominent topics in medical research, as timely detection of it may help in obtaining a better treatment outcome and thus a better survival. This paper presents a comparative study of four machine learning algorithms, including Logistic Regression, Decision Trees, K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN), and the Naive Bayes algorithm, for the effective classification of breast cancer. The original data was effectively processed and missing values invalidated, and important features were normalized to obtain a well-trained model. Each of these algorithms is evaluated by means of various metrics of evaluation, including precision, recall, and F measure, which helps to understand the predictive capability of the model for each class of the model. Our experimental analysis shows that Logistic regression gives a superior class performance and also class stability, making it an appropriate algorithm for application in medical diagnosis. The results also affirm the usefulness of machine learning in practical life so that the peculiarities of illness do not permit diagnostic errors, and hospitalizations may be minimized by early intervention. This research leads up to the great mass of information put into form on methods of treatment of cancer detection through data analysis, and a field of development is opened out for future work in hybrid integration and methods of feature selection with better methods of predicting.

title: Multi-Modal Explainable AI Predictions for Dermatological Disease and Allergy Diagnosis
authors: Britney Spierce G, Rajeswari M, Stewart Kirubakaran S
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: Millions of people around the world have suffered from skin diseases, and a lot of morbidity has resulted from delays or incorrect diagnoses. Dermatological diagnosis traditionally is based on subjective visual judgment by experts, which usually faces accessibility issues. This work proposes an AI-assisted framework for multi-class skin disease diagnosis that is enhanced with Explainable Artificial Intelligence for enhancing the trust, interpretability, and clinical usability of the developed model. The proposed system uses three deep learning architectures: ResNet50, DenseNet121, and a hybrid ResNet50 integrated with the Convolutional Block Attention Module (CBAM), to extract highly discriminative visual cues by focusing on relevant lesion areas both spatially and channel-wise. A curated dataset of dermoscopic skin lesion images has been used; several preprocessing and augmentation techniques like flipping, rotation, and color jittering were utilized to enhance robustness against diverse clinical imaging conditions. Weighted Random Sampling and class balanced loss are applied to mitigate challenges offered by class imbalance. Grad-CAM-based heatmaps provide clear visual explanations through highlighting of the disease specific lesion regions, strengthening clinical transparency. Also, it integrates the complete diagnostic workflow into one web interface with FastAPI, providing image upload, symptom-aware evaluation, differential diagnosis, and personalized food recommendations according to the predicted condition. The results affirm that the combination of attention enhancement, feature level interpretability, and calibrated prediction will improve the diagnostic accuracy and clinical trustworthiness of AI algorithms by leaps and bounds. Experimental evaluation has established that the proposed Hybrid ResNet50 + CBAM model achieves the topmost performance with 89 % accuracy, 87 % precision, 86 % recall, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{8 6. 5 \%}$</tex> F1-score, outperforming the baseline models ResNet50 and DenseNet121.

title: AI Driven Predictive Maintenance for Single Phase Induction Motor
authors: Kanthi Raghu Vardhan, Lingappa. J, Pagindla Nithin, Anishetti Ruchitha, Kalagotla Chenchireddy
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: Predictive maintenance has become a crucial approach for monitoring industrial equipment, leveraging Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Learning (ML) to anticipate failures before they occur. This paper presents an AI-driven predictive maintenance system designed for singlephase and three-phase induction motors. The proposed system employs a Random Forest classifier trained on a structured dataset comprising key motor operating parameters such as voltage, current, temperature, vibration, runtime, and motor type. A web-based user interface, developed using Gradio, enables real-time health assessment and provides maintenance scheduling recommendations. The model achieves a perfect classification accuracy of 1.0 on the test dataset, demonstrating its strong predictive capability and reliability. The paper discusses the complete workflow, including dataset preparation, model training, system design, evaluation metrics, and considerations for practical deployment.

title: Energy Consumption Forecasting in Smart Homes: A Scalable Machine Learning Approach
authors: Kintali Nikhilesh, Rohan Paul, S Rajat Pandey, Gouthaman P, T Preethiya
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: The paper presents the methods used for smart home Energy Consumption Forecasting, which utilizes distributed computing with PySpark and Machine Learning algorithms. PySpark distributed computing technologies were utilized to process one million records of energy consumption, drawn from various datasets. The four machine learning algorithms were used, such as Random Forest, XGBoost, Support Vector Machine (SVM), and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM). Random Forest implementation achieved a 0.4319 kWh Mean Absolute Error (MAE) and a 76.48% prediction accuracy and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{R}^{2}=0.7648$</tex>, which shows a production-ready performance. PySpark is applied in distributed feature engineering, MLlib in scalable machine learning, and operational deployment in realtime. The results demonstrate that these techniques can manage a massive amount of data while it can preserve high prediction accuracy, which will provide benefits for Smart Home Applications.

title: Placement Prediction Model Using Neural Prophet and AdaBoost
authors: Nagendra Reddy B, Thota Balaji, M Venkata Pavan Kumar, Anusuri Krishna Veni, K Swetha, K Suresh Babu
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: In today's changing job market, predicting campus placements has become more complex due to several unpredictable global trends. Educational institutions need accurate models that can reflect these changes to better prepare their students for employment. This research proposes a hybrid placement prediction model that combines Neural Prophet and AdaBoost (Adaptive Boosting) algorithms to forecast placement outcomes with improved reliability. Unlike earlier models that considered static or limited global variables such as recession, overhiring, and automation, the proposed model includes three new dynamic factors—AI disruption, cost-cutting, and industry downsizing—which strongly influence hiring trends in the technology sector. Neural Prophet (NP) helps in identifying timebased variations and long-term market shifts, while AdaBoost strengthens the classification accuracy by reducing errors through adaptive learning. Experimental results on historical placement data demonstrate that the hybrid NP and AdaBoost model achieves a prediction accuracy of 95.9%. The findings show that dynamic industrial factors have a measurable impact on student employability, and integrating them into prediction systems significantly improves performance. This work contributes a reliable and adaptable framework for academic institutions to forecast placement probabilities and make informed strategic decisions for student career readiness.

title: Golang Scheduler
authors: Egor Levchuk, Denis Pushkar, Denis Vasin
publish: 2026 ElCon Conference of Young Researchers in Computing &amp; Processing, and Information Security (ElCon-CP)
abstract: Golang-Scheduler is a conceptual study of a hybrid, priority-aware scheduler designed for Go’s goroutine model. We first diagnose weaknesses of the default Go scheduler when serving heterogeneous and latency-sensitive workloads—issues that manifest as elevated tail latency, steal contention, and suboptimal handling of mixed CPU/IO tasks. Based on this analysis, we propose a compact, runtime-compatible design that augments per-P run queues with short-lived priority buckets, applies priority-aware work-stealing, and uses cooperative yielding together with adaptive backoff to mitigate contention. Rather than delivering a full runtime patch, we present a precise architectural specification and implementation principles, and we evaluate the idea with discrete-event simulations and a lightweight user-space prototype. Our evaluation indicates meaningful benefits in target scenarios—up to 35% reduction in 99th-percentile latency and up to 20% throughput improvement under mixed CPU/IO loads - while preserving Go semantics. We conclude with sensitivity analysis, deployment considerations, and recommended next steps for practical adoption.

title: Intelligent Assessment System Enforcing Structured Data Integrity for Real Time E Learning
authors: Mohammed Faisal, K. S. Shashikala, Nabil Khan, Nihal L, Mohammed Faizan Ajani, Yukthi S Mohan
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: The Intelligent Assessment System (IAS) is a powerful, full stack web platform designed for the total automation of alternative assessments such as quizzes and to eliminate workloads. When a teacher enters unstructured PDF or DOCX course material through a secure web interface the regulated data pipeline starts instantly creating a unique, shareable quiz link. Crucially, the core uses of Pydantic Schemas is to ensure complete data integrity and system stability which is actually a common problem in LLM integration. This procedure actually resolves the LLM data structuring issue prior to persistence by rigorously requiring a verifiable JSON output from the Native OpenAI API. For effective, real time relational scoring and permanent submission for record keeping this consistently structured assessment data is passed through a SQLAlchemy Data Pipeline. The output is a full, event-driven score report that includes feedback and explanations for each quiz question. It is sent right away via SMTP. The IAS sets up a very reliable and scalable system for making verifiable educational data assets which greatly helps modern data-driven educational systems.

title: PCAD: A Raspberry Pi Zero–Based Portable Cybersecurity Platform with On-Device K-Means Modeling of Wi-Fi Environments
authors: Ali Mucedidi, Mohammed Al-Hubaishi, Mirna Tout
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: Portable, low-power platforms for cybersecurity are valuable in classrooms, field audits, and authorized research. We present the Portable Cybersecurity Analysis Device (PCAD), a self-contained Raspberry Pi Zero 2 W system with an integrated LCD and hardware buttons that unifies wireless AP management, network reconnaissance, device enumeration, traffic capture, and offline peer-to-peer messaging behind a menu-driven ondevice interface. Beyond classical tooling, PCAD now includes a lightweight K-Means clustering pipeline that summarizes local Wi-Fi scans (e.g., network count, average/max RSSI) and partitions observations into signal-quality clusters to characterize radio environments directly on device. In field trials, the system remained interactive while performing essential tasks; under controlled stress loads, UI latency increased but remained usable, host discovery slowed modestly, and service enumeration accuracy degraded slightly while capture rate and CPU utilization rose as expected on the constrained platform. Collectively, these results show that a tightly integrated, ethics-aligned embedded platform can support both hands-on cybersecurity workflows and ondevice ML analytics for environment assessment without external terminals or cloud inference. We release a modular design that supports reproducibility and extension to intrusion-detection experiments on higher-capacity boards.

title: Teachers’ and Students’ Perceptions of the Role of AI Tutors in Engineering Study Programs
authors: Egor A. Chebotarev, Sergey O. Shaposhnikov
publish: 2026 ElCon Conference of Young Researchers in Computing &amp; Processing, and Information Security (ElCon-CP)
abstract: The paper discusses the role of AI tutors in improving the quality of engineering education. It analyzes the results of student and faculty surveys on the role of AI tutors in teaching and learning. A conclusion is drawn regarding the desired roles and functions of AI tutors in engineering education.

title: On Decimations of M-Sequences Giving Few Valued Cross-Correlations
authors: Serdar Boztaş, Ferruh Özbudak, Elif Kurtaran Özbudak, Eda Tekin
publish: 2025 11th International Workshop on Signal Design and Its Applications in Communications (IWSDA)
abstract: In this paper, we report some experimental results on decimations <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$d$</tex> which yield three-valued cross-correlations for <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$m$</tex> - sequences over odd prime fields <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$p$</tex>. First, we performed a search for decimations different from the ones mentioned by Helleseth in his 2014 survey [6] of open problems on crosscorrelation. We have performed extensive computations but have not found any cases that are inequivalent to those stated as Open Problem 2 in [6]. In this context, we conjecture the form of the decimations that result in these three-valued crosscorrelations and leave the proof as an open problem. We also report computational results on a class of sequences related to Open Problem 8 from Helleseth and Li's [7] 2024 paper. Specifically, we investigate the case <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$n \equiv 2(\bmod 4)$</tex>, which was explicitly excluded from the original problem. Based on our findings, we propose a new conjecture on the correlation distribution for this particular family, thus formulating a new, related open problem.

title: Smart AI Multimodal Architecture for Automated Emergency Detection and Alerts
authors: T G Manjunath, Zainab Imtiyaz, Bokka Sowjanya, Varshini K S, Sharanya Bengoor
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: The increasing demand for intelligent and proactive emergency response systems exposes the limitations of conventional surveillance setups that rely on manual monitoring and are prone to delayed or missed distress detection. This study presents a multimodal AI-based emergency alert system that integrates audio and video analysis for real-time distress recognition. The audio module processes incoming signals through a structured pipeline involving audio pre-processing with Librosa for resampling and normalization, feature extraction using YAMNet embeddings pretrained on Google AudioSet, and binary scream classification through a lightweight feedforward neural network optimized with Adam. Out of 699 total samples, 511 non-scream and 152 scream samples were correctly identified, achieving an overall classification accuracy of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{9 5 \%}$</tex>. The video module detects dynamic “Help Me” hand gestures through frame preprocessing, Mediapipe landmark extraction, and sequential modeling with Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks, achieving a classification accuracy of approximately 80 % while operating under varying lighting conditions, motion blur, and camera angles. Outputs from both modules are fused in an integration and alert layer using Twilio, ensuring rapid, scalable, and reliable emergency detection in high-risk environments.

title: DSA-LoRA: A Seamless Content-Style LoRA Fusion for Adversarial Sample Generation
authors: Gangtao Han, Xinhai Zhong, Lingling Li, Song Wang
publish: 2025 International Conference on Cyber Resilience and Endogenous Safety &amp; Security (CRESS)
abstract: Synthetic images generated by diffusion models pose cybersecurity risks through misinformation and fraud. To tackle these risks, personalized image generation, which involves synthesizing a new image with the content of a source image and the style of a target image, provides sufficient attack samples that are able to strengthen the anti-counterfeiting capacity of the detection models through adversarial training. Current personalized image generation research typically fuses the content and style extracted from sample images to produce high-quality synthesized images. However, such a fusion is likely to generate incoherent visual results, leading to a severe lack of fidelity. In this paper, we propose DSA-LoRA, a diffusion-step-aware low-rank adaptations (LoRA) integration method, that merges trained content and style LoRAs to generate coherent visual images. By analyzing the diffusion process, we find that the early steps are primarily responsible for the subject reconstruction, while the later ones steadily move towards enhancing the visual appearance of the generated images. This finding motivates us to design a piecewise integration strategy to enable a seamless transition from content reconstruction to style enhancement in personalized image generation. To effectively fuse the information contained in the original content and style LoRAs, Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) is employed in this paper to identify the suitable fusion positions for LoRAs. The experimental results demonstrate that DSA-LoRA generates coherent and natural results, outperforming state-ofthe-art methods in terms of content and style fidelity.

title: Mixed Reality Golf Putting: a Comparative Analysis of Golf Putting Performance in Real, Virtual, and Augmented Virtuality Environments_supp1-3679107.pdf
authors: Ke Li
publish: N/A
abstract: Although immersive technologies hold enormous potential for enhancing sports training, existing systems encounter notable limitations. While fully immersive virtual reality (VR) systems can address constraints of physical training setups such as limited space or equipment, full VR environments often lack rich, realistic, and multisensory feedback often required for effective sports training. To address this gap, this work investigates skill-based sport training, particularly, golf putting, across the reality–virtuality continuum. We implemented a mixed reality (MR) putting simulator that combines real equipment (putter and golf ball) with a virtual training environment. Three training conditions were realized: (i) real-world putting, constrained by limited physical space; (ii) a VR putting, in which a real putter and ball are used but represented virtually within the virtual environment (VE); (iii) an augmented virtuality (AV) putting, which reveals the real putter, ball, and users' limbs through a localized video-passthrough window, aiming to preserve users' sense of embodiment and presence while enabling simulation of an unlimited putting area. We conducted a pilot within-subjects experiment with 36 participants and found that the AV condition improved immediate putting performance, reduced task load, reduced perceived motion sickness, and increased user preference compared to the VR condition, while user's sense of presence remained comparable to VR training. These findings indicate that AV offers a pragmatic “sweet spot” on the virtuality continuum, balancing the trade-off between sensory fidelity and training flexibility, offering valuable design insights for immersive sports training systems.

title: Comparing Spatial AR to Passthrough Headset AR in a Real Virtual Heritage Experience
authors: Anna Logetskaia, Paul Hine, Francisco Macia, Narcis Pares
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: A novel projective device, the AR Magic Lantern (ARML), has allowed Spatial Augmented Reality (SAR) to be deployed as a portable AR experience comparable to those on other technologies such as smartphones and headsets. In this study we compare user experience in a real virtual heritage application between two technologies that are growing in feasibility and popularity: SAR (using the ARML) and passthrough headset-AR (using the Meta Quest 3). Given that managers and curators of heritage sites want their visitors to attain a good awareness of the physical site, AR should provide a good blend between the physical and the augmented, without technology getting in the way. The results obtained seem to indicate that SAR provides higher awareness of the physical site than headset-AR. Moreover, the SAR seems to spontaneously spark experience exchange between the user and other visitors, while headset-AR tends to isolate users. Finally, headset-AR seems to put users in a task-oriented mindset while SAR seems to better contextualize the experience and its content within the physical site.

title: Arbitrary-Scale Point Cloud Upsampling with Saliency-Aware Implicit Surface Guidance
authors: Yanzhe Liu, Rong Chen, Yushi Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: Despite significant progress in point cloud upsampling, most existing methods rely heavily on supervised training with paired data, which are often difficult to acquire. Moreover, the inherent lack of explicit connectivity in point clouds makes it difficult to achieve both continuous and uniform densification while accurately recovering fine geometric structures. To address these problems, we propose an upsampling model that treats this task as saliency-aware implicit surface sampling, enabling self-supervised and fine-grained point densification. Central to our idea is correlating implicit surface reconstruction with salient point identification, and carrying out sampling on the saliency-aware surface representation. Motivated by this, we introduce a salient point detector along with a corresponding implicit surface-based interpolator, and a geometry filter, upon which we develop a three-stage architecture consisting of a pre-trained saliency guidance block, a saliency-aware enhancer, and an upsampler. The guidance block captures meaningful shape patterns to prevent detail loss and incomplete recovery, while the enhancer facilitates detail enhancement in complex salient regions. These components are integrated with the upsampler to generate dense results that accurately retain both global shape and meticulous structures. In comparison with state-of-the-art methods, our model significantly improves the upsampling quality. Extensive experiments conducted on various datasets, comprising both synthetic and real-world captured shapes, demonstrate the flexibility and availability of our method in processing the point clouds across different scales and distributions.

title: RSATalker: Realistic Socially-Aware Talking Head Generation for Multi-Turn Conversation
authors: Peng Chen, Xiaobao Wei, Yi Yang, Naiming Yao, Hui Chen, Feng Tian
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: Talking head generation is increasingly important in virtual reality (VR), especially for social scenarios involving multi-turn conversation. Existing approaches face notable limitations: mesh-based 3D methods can model dual-person dialogue but lack realistic textures, large-model-based 2D methods produce natural appearances but incur prohibitive computational costs. Recently, 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS)-based methods achieve efficient and realistic rendering but remain speaker-only and ignore social relationships. We introduce RSATalker, the first framework that leverages 3DGS for realistic and socially-aware talking head generation, with support for multi-turn conversation. Our method first drives mesh-based 3D facial motion from speech, then binds 3D Gaussians to mesh facets to render high-fidelity 2D avatar videos. To capture interpersonal dynamics, we propose a socially-aware module that encodes social relationships, including blood and non-blood as well as equal and unequal, into high-level embeddings through a learnable query mechanism. We design a three-stage training paradigm and construct the RSATalker dataset with speech-mesh-image triplets annotated with social relationships. Our method supports applications such as VR telepresence, social VR, and embodied conversational agents. The socially-aware conditioning can also be extended to other human motion generation tasks. Extensive experiments demonstrate that RSATalker achieves state-of-the-art performance in both realism and social awareness. The code and dataset will be released.

title: Diffusion-Based Dynamic Contract for Federated AI Agent Construction in Mobile Metaverses_supp1-3665640.pdf
authors: Zhang Yang
publish: N/A
abstract: Mobile metaverses are envisioned as a transformative digital ecosystem that delivers immersive, intelligent, and ubiquitous services through mobile devices. Driven by Large Language Models (LLMs) and Vision-Language Models (VLMs), Artificial Intelligence (AI) agents hold the potential to empower the creation, maintenance, and evolution of mobile metaverses, enabling seamless human-machine interaction and dynamic service adaptation. Currently, AI agents are primarily built upon cloud-based LLMs and VLMs. However, several challenges hinder their efficient deployment, including high service latency and a risk of sensitive data leakage during perception and processing. In this paper, we develop an edge-cloud collaboration-based federated AI agent construction framework in mobile metaverses. Specifically, Edge Servers (ESs), as agent infrastructures, first create agent modules in a distributed manner. The cloud server then integrates these modules into AI agents and deploys them at the edge, thereby enabling low-latency AI agent services for users. Considering that ESs may exhibit dynamic levels of willingness to participate in federated AI agent construction, we design a two-period dynamic contract model to continuously incentivize ESs to participate in agent module creation, effectively addressing the dynamic information asymmetry between the cloud server and ESs. Furthermore, we propose an Enhanced Diffusion Model-based Soft Actor-Critic (EDMSAC) algorithm to effectively generate optimal dynamic contracts. In the algorithm, we apply dynamic structured pruning to DM-based actor networks to enhance denoising efficiency and policy learning performance. Simulation results demonstrate that the EDMSAC algorithm outperforms the DMSAC algorithm by up to 23% in optimal dynamic contract generation.

title: The Last Human Job: The Work of Connecting in a Disconnected World—Allison Pugh (Princeton, NJ, USA: Princeton University Press, 2024, 384 pp.)
authors: Rachelle Linner
publish: IEEE Technology and Society Magazine
abstract: Presents reviews for the following list of books, (The Last Human Job: The Work of Connecting in a Disconnected World—Allison Pugh (Princeton, NJ, USA: Princeton University Press, 2024, 384 pp.).

title: Simple Line Lengths for Multiline Thru-Reflect-Line Calibration
authors: Moitreya Adhikary, Jooeun Lee, Florian Bergmann, Bryan T. Bosworth, Anna Osella, Jerome Cheron, Tomasz Karpisz, Nicholas R. Jungwirth, Rob D. Jones, Arkadiusz Lewandowski, Ben F. Jamroz, Jeffrey A. Jargon, James C. Booth, Angela Stelson, Christian J. Long, Nathan D. Orloff
publish: IEEE Transactions on Microwave Theory and Techniques
abstract: Multiline thru-reflect-line (mTRL) is a calibration algorithm for vector network analyzers (VNAs) that uses S-parameter measurements of transmission lines with different lengths. While the conventional thru-reflect-line algorithm chooses the line lengths based on phase relationships, choosing the line lengths in mTRL is not as straightforward because the thru is not always the common line. This work tested a simple algorithm to iteratively compute all line lengths with a recursive fraction, only assuming that the longest and the shortest line lengths are known. A cursory theoretical analysis showed that certain recursive fractions should outperform others in terms of calibration error, S-parameter uncertainty, kit compactness, and resilience to the removal of any two lines from the kit. To test this analysis, we fabricated and measured 51 different mTRL calibration kits and benchmarked them against a kit whose normalized uncertainty was optimized by brute force Monte Carlo simulations with a supercomputer. After analyzing the kits, we performed a comparative analysis to determine which recursive fraction kits most closely matched the behavior of the optimized kit. We then developed a figure of merit to capture the strengths and weaknesses of each kit, revealing that the user should choose a recursive fraction within the range of 53/128 (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\approx 0.41$</tex-math> </inline-formula>)–37/64 (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\approx 0.58$</tex-math> </inline-formula>) to design line lengths for mTRL. The proposed algorithm offers a simple and practical solution for designing high-performance mTRL line kits, enhancing measurement accuracy and robustness for high-frequency measurement applications.

title: Network Representative Model for MV Distribution Grid by a Combination of Network Reduction and Time Aggregation
authors: Md Sazal Miah, Rakibuzzaman Shah, Nima Amjady, Razieh Rastgoo, B M Ruhul Amin, Md. Rabiul Islam, Kashem M Muttaqi, S.M. Muyeen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industry Applications
abstract: Planning of large-scale electric power systems presents significant computational challenges due to the extensive number of network components and time intervals. The primary objective of network reduction is to maintain fidelity to the original network's electrical behavior while minimizing computational complexity and simulation time. However, conventional network reduction methods may not fully capture the temporal dynamics and interactions present in the original network. Furthermore, their effectiveness for long-term planning horizons may be questionable. To bridge these gaps, this paper proposes a novel spatial and temporal system reduction approach using three-step time series and temporal aggregation. The first step performs time series aggregation to obtain a single representative time series from all data sources. In the second step, a diffusion-based clustering model is proposed for the first time, which adaptively adjusts the diffusion process based on temporal smoothness to enhance time series embeddings. In the third step, time series disaggregation is carried out using the average participation factors of all involved time series for representative time intervals. Performance assessments are conducted using a real-world 1759-bus system, where the accuracy of the proposed spatial and temporal system reduction method is evaluated under varying operating conditions.

title: ExGes: Expressive Human Motion Retrieval and Modulation for Audio-Driven Gesture Synthesis
authors: Xukun Zhou, Fengxin Li, Ming Chen, Yan Zhou, Pengfei Wan, Yeying Jin, Hongyuan Zhang, Hongyan Liu, Zhaoxin Fan, Jun He, Xuelong Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: Audio-driven human gesture synthesis is a crucial task with broad applications in virtual avatars, human-computer interaction, and creative content generation. Despite notable progress, existing methods often produce coarse gestures, lack expressiveness, and fail to fully align with audio semantics. To address these challenges, we propose ExGes, a novel retrieval-enhanced diffusion framework with three key designs: (1) a Motion Base Construction, which builds a gesture library from the training dataset; (2) a Motion Retrieval Module, employing contrastive learning and momentum distillation for retrieving fine-grained reference poses; and (3) a Precise Control Module, integrating partial masking and stochastic masking to enable flexible and fine-grained control. Experimental evaluations on BEAT2 demonstrate that ExGes reduces Fréchet Gesture Distance by 4.55%and improves motion diversity by 5.3% over EMAGE, with user studies revealing a 71.3% preference for its naturalness and semantic relevance. Code and project page with the supplementary videos are available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://zxk19981227.github.io/ExGes/</uri>.

title:
KMoPSO
                    <sub>TE</sub>
                    : Advancing Self-Driving Networks with a Knowledge-Driven Multi-Objective Particle Swarm Optimization Algorithm for Traffic Engineering
authors: Hong Zhao, Ling Tang, Li-Guang Xie, Jian-Yu Li, Jing Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Evolutionary Computation
abstract: As communication networks become increasingly complex and expansive, Internet Service Providers (ISPs) face significant challenges in sustaining network efficiency and responsiveness. Traditional traffic engineering approaches, such as local search and linear programming, often struggle to swiftly adapt to changing network conditions and can be computationally intensive for large-scale networks. Therefore, this paper proposes the Knowledge-driven Multi-objective Particle Swarm Optimization algorithm for Traffic Engineering (KMoPSO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">TE</sub>), an innovative solution that addresses the limitations of current approaches by simultaneously considering multiple network performance objectives. The KMOPSO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">TE</sub> offers three key benefits. First, it integrates segment routing with equal-cost multi-path (ECMP) into a multi-objective optimization framework for dynamic routing control. Second, it employs an adaptive particle encoding strategy that efficiently determines routing paths aligned with network topologies. Third, a dynamic knowledge-driven update strategy intelligently adjusts link weights based on network attributes, distinguishing it from static optimization methods. These features collectively enhance network performance and adaptability. KMoPSO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">TE</sub> is designed to maintain excellent performance in larger network sizes and has undergone comprehensive evaluations using real-world network topologies from the Internet Topology Zoo dataset. The experimental results indicate that when compared with state-of-the-art algorithms, KMoPSO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">TE</sub> achieves an average reduction of 30.58% in Maximum Link Utilization, thereby showcasing its enhancements in network performance, adaptability, and generalization capabilities within large-scale network environments.

title: Ultra High Definition Video Quality Assessment Based on Adaptively Spatial Fusion and Temporal Selection
authors: Zhijie Liang, Wei Sheng, Haoran Ma, Fei Zhou, Guoping Qiu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: Ultra high definition video quality assessment (UHD VQA) is challenging due to the high spatio-temporal dimensions of UHD content. In this work, we propose a spatio-temporal framework based on feature fusion and selection to facilitate UHD VOA. First, we introduce a spatially adaptive fusion net (SAFNet) that integrates all patch-wise features into a compact frame-wise representation via local aggregation and global interaction. During the aggregation and the interaction, the contribution of each patch-wise feature is initially estimated via visual saliency-derived weights and then adaptively refined by contextual adjustments. Second, we design a temporally adaptive selection net (TASNet) to reduces temporal redundancy. It retains a small number of temporal positions through thresholding a dynamically estimated importance vector, which captures both representativeness and inter-frame variation. Finally, extensive experiments conducted on 4 datasets with various 4K videos demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method in comparison with some traditional and state-of-the-art VQA methods.

title: Structural Time-Admissibility as a Safety Interlock in Networked Systems
authors: Hyunkeun Shin
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Legacy message-centric communication standards define success by delivery completion. In time-critical operations, this delivery-centric definition allows information to arrive after its operational validity has expired, consuming resources and influencing decisions despite being obsolete. This paper identifies this condition as Structural Time Failure (STF), a structural mismatch between communication success and time-admissible operational validity. To address STF without modifying existing standards, we introduce the Reaction Window (RW) as a human-defined temporal authority and propose a pre-transmission time-admissible overlay architecture that excludes time-invalid messages before they enter the delivery process. Unlike conventional delay or congestion mitigation techniques, the proposed mechanism enforces temporal admissibility as a structural condition independent of network capacity. Discrete-event simulation results demonstrate that delivery-centric systems inevitably experience STF under load, whereas the proposed architecture preserves time-valid delivery for critical traffic by preventing obsolete information from entering transmission, queueing, and actuation paths. This paper does not propose a new protocol nor optimize network performance, but analyzes and enforces temporal admissibility as a structural safety condition. This work establishes a conceptual architecture for temporal admissibility enforcement as a structural safety interlock, rather than a protocol optimization.
