title: Adaptive and Lightweight DDoS Defense in SDN-Enabled Edge–IoT Networks Using Unsupervised and Reinforcement Learning
authors: Ananth Kumar R, Ramachandra Reddy, Bharath Kumar S
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: The high rate of growth in the Internet of Things (IoT) devices within the edge computing settings has grown the vulnerability of the networks to Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) attacks exponentially and necessitates efficient, adaptive, and defensive solutions. In this work, a novel SDN-Edge-IoT-DDoS detection and mitigation system is suggested which makes use of an unsupervised-powered anomaly detection model along with a reinforcement learning-based countermeasures in mitigation strategies. In contrast to the traditional methods based on a fixed threshold or signature-like detection, the suggested solution constantly hears traffic patterns at the Software-Defined Networking (SDN) controller and changes mitigation policies dynamically with the current network behavior and predicts potential attack vectors before they cause a substantial disruption. The framework uses a lightweight feature extraction that is adapted to resourceconstrained IoT devices and thus it has minimal overhead. The results of simulation prove that the methodology positively influences detection accuracy, false positives and resource amount usage, creating a self-learning, context-aware, and predictive security paradigm of SDN-enabled edge-IoT networks. This represents a novel integration of predictive Machine learning (ML)-based mitigation being integrated into an SDN-edge-IoT ecosystem.

title: A Fast Decoding Algorithm for Generalized Reed-Solomon Codes and Alternant Codes
authors: Nianqi Tang, Yunghsiang S. Han, Danyang Pei, Chao Chen
publish: 2025 11th International Workshop on Signal Design and Its Applications in Communications (IWSDA)
abstract: In this paper, it is shown that the syndromes of generalized Reed-Solomon (GRS) codes and alternant codes can be characterized in terms of inverse fast Fourier transform. Then, a fast decoding algorithm is proposed, which has a computational complexity of O(n log (n-k)+(n-k) log<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>(n-k)) for all (n, k) GRS codes and (n, k) alternant codes. Particularly, this provides a new decoding method for Goppa codes, which is an important subclass of alternant codes. When decoding the binary Goppa code with a length of 8192 and a correction capability of 128, the new algorithm is nearly 10 times faster than traditional methods. The decoding algorithm is suitable for the McEliece cryptosystem, which is a candidate for post-quantum cryptography techniques.

title: Quantum Artificial Intelligence: From Quantum Neural Networks to Self-Programming Architectures [Feature]
authors: Samuel Yen-Chi Chen
publish: IEEE Circuits and Systems Magazine
abstract: Quantum Artificial Intelligence (QAI) represents the convergence of quantum computing and machine learning, aiming to harness the computational power of quantum mechanics to enhance the efficiency, scalability, and generalization capability of intelligent systems. This article provides a tutorial on the foundations, models, and emerging directions of QAI. We first review the core concepts of quantum information and hybrid quantum–classical computing, followed by a detailed discussion of variational quantum circuits (VQCs)—the fundamental building blocks of quantum neural networks (QNNs). Building upon these foundations, we introduce representative QAI architectures, including Quantum Convolutional Neural Networks (QCNNs) for pattern recognition, Quantum Long Short-Term Memory (QLSTM) for sequential modeling, and Quantum Reinforcement Learning (QRL) for decision making and control. Advanced topics such as Quantum Architecture Search (QAS), Quantum Fast Weight Programmers (QFWP), and the Quantum-Train (QT) framework are presented to illustrate how classical and quantum models can dynamically co-design and reprogram one another. Furthermore, we discuss federated and distributed quantum learning, encoding complexity, and training challenges such as barren plateaus and hardware constraints. The article concludes with a forward-looking outlook on scalable QAI systems, autonomous quantum agents, and the long-term vision of self-programming, distributed intelligence. By bridging quantum theory, learning algorithms, and system architectures, this work aims to offer both foundational understanding and strategic insights for the next generation of hybrid quantum-classical intelligence.

title: Automatic Rain-Sensing Wiper
authors: Akshat Jain, Hemalatha S, G Deepa
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: Today, in the automotive industry, the important factor is to ensure the safety of people travelling in cars. For a long time, car wipers work on a manual switching. Recent incidents have shown that faulty wipers and improper manual speed adjustments have contributed to accidents, their speed not being adjusted manually have caused accidents. In this paper, we propose an automatic rain sensing wiper system which starts operating when the rain starts and stops when there is no rain. The automatic wiper controller is intended to assist and provide more comfort to the drivers by minimizing distractions. This wiper system in a car detects rainfall and gets activated automatically. When the sensor detects raindrops, it senses the intensity of rain and changes the wiper speed accordingly so that the required rotation rate is achieved. This eliminates the requirement of car wipers needing to be controlled manually. The design of automated rain wiper involves the following components: an Arduino Uno, 2A Digital Rain Drop Sensors Module with Converter, Servo Motor and an External Power Supply. Rain drop sensor detects the rain and transfers the command to the Servomotor which is then processed by the Arduino. The rain drop sensor is installed outside the car and on the side of the windshield and connected to the servo motor which is connected to the Wiper Blade. The proposed wiper system is cost effective and is designed to ensure energy efficient using Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) to control the servo motor speed. This wiper system controls the movement of wipers with a response time below 400 ms.

title: A Novel Optimizer Based Deep Neural Network Model for IoMT
authors: Buditi Sriveni, Naveen Kumar Navuri, Korra Srinivas, Tirumala Paruchuri, Jaganathan Muthusamy
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: Intelligent diagnostics and real-time monitoring in the field of Internet of Things (IoMT) is changing modern healthcare, which also raises the risk of cyber threats, which makes important to get a strong intrusion detection systems (IDS). The current research used the MSCAD dataset to show the efficiency of eight popular optimizer for training the deep neural network model, by considering the real IoMT network conditions. Analyzing different performance metrices shows that the DNN optimized with Adam performs the best as compared to others, achieving a precision of 0.97753. This level of accuracy shows just how important it is to choose the right optimizer when building effective deep learning-based intrusion detection systems for medical networks. The proposed DNN-Adam method provides useful information for creating secure and successful cybersecurity solutions for limited resources, real-time healthcare systems by enhancing detection accuracy, reliability and flexibility in the IoMT environment.

title: Spatiotemporal Forecasting of Renewable Energy Using Hybrid Networks in Tamil Nadu
authors: Jinu Sophia J, Harshni P, Helina Sergius D
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: Tamil Nadu's prime source of generating renewable energy primarily derives from sustainable solar and wind resources, but the presence of climate-related variability and extreme weather events still disrupts traditional methods of power generation and significantly complicates the execution of gridrelated duties for the regulating body. In this research, a unique hybridized deep-learning architecture, utilising both ConvLSTM and a Transformer that relies on Time-Series, is proposed as an innovative solution to this challenge and to provide greater accuracy for spatiotemporal power forecast modelling. ConvLSTM effectively models both “short-term” and “spatial” characteristics, as demonstrated by recent studies concerning the accurate forecasting of electricity flow and loads, respectively, at several geographically separated sites. Current academic literature on this subject reinforces the need for a predictive technology that has capabilities to be utilized in a variety of geographical regions as well as offshore applications and services regarding wind generation infrastructure. By synthesizing the advantages of both architectures, this unique hybridized mode design is expected to produce consistent and reliable results that can be employed to improve smart grid management and provide support for decision making. Ultimately, we hope this model will support Tamil Nadu's energy resource development efforts.

title: A Meta-Model Based Framework for Dynamic Entity Management in Big Data Systems
authors: Roopam Dhanraj Choudhari, Gaurav Khodwe
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: The proliferation of heterogeneous data types in modern big data systems presents significant challenges for traditional data management approaches. This paper proposes a meta-model-based framework that enables dynamic entity classification, validation, and management without requiring custom implementations for each data type. The framework addresses key big data challenges including schema evolution, data variety, and scalable validation. Present the architectural design, implementation considerations, and theoretical analysis demonstrating potential for significant improvements in development efficiency and system flexibility. Through architectural analysis and comparison with traditional approaches, show that the framework can theoretically reduce development overhead by up to 80% while enabling rapid deployment of new entity types. The framework's applicability is demonstrated through detailed design analysis and projected performance characteristics based on established metamodeling principles.

title: Improved Channel Estimation Scheme for STAR-RIS-Assisted Communication Systems
authors: Shibsankar Das, Adrish Banerjee
publish: 2025 11th International Workshop on Signal Design and Its Applications in Communications (IWSDA)
abstract: Simultaneously transmitting and reflecting reconfigurable intelligent surface (STAR-RIS) is a new paradigm in which the impinging signals are divided into transmitted and reflected signals to facilitate full-space network coverage. In this paper, we study a novel extended least-square (ELS) channel estimation design for a STAR-RIS-aided uplink communication system. We propose an ELS scheme to efficiently estimate the channel vectors under the time-switching (TS) and energysplitting (ES) protocols for the STAR-RIS-assisted system. We show that the mean square error (MSE) of the proposed ELS channel estimation scheme is always lower than the MSE of the existing LS scheme. The effectiveness of our proposed channel estimation approach is validated through numerical results and theoretical analysis.

title: Identification of Cyber-Physical Security Boundary for Rotating Bearings under Attacks Using PINNs
authors: Ziyue Jia, Yiying Tao, Hanqi Zhou, Siqi Meng, Yuheng Li, Qingao Yuan, Yang Liu
publish: 2025 International Conference on Cyber Resilience and Endogenous Safety &amp; Security (CRESS)
abstract: Malicious attacks pose a significant threat to the operational safety of rotating machinery by inducing speed fluctuations that modify load conditions, intensify stress, and accelerate fatigue damage. To bridge the gap between cyber-layer disturbances and their physical consequences, this paper proposes a FEA–PPgNN framework that shifts protection from the cyber domain to the physical domain. By coupling speed fluctuations with stress and fatigue behavior, a cyber–physical security boundary is defined to represent the bearing’s intrinsic safe operating limit and enable physical-domain defense against cyber attacks. A transient finite-element analysis (FEA) model is developed to generate speed–stress datasets, followed by a data-driven neural network that predicts equivalent stress under arbitrary speeds. The stress outputs are subsequently fed into a physics-informed neural network (PINN) to estimate fatigue life under embedded physical constraints. The cyber–physical security boundary is quantitatively derived through the proposed FEA–PPgNN framework, which integrates stress prediction and fatigue evaluation to identify the physical threshold where mechanical vulnerability emerges and the transition from safe operation to cyber-induced failure occurs. Computational experiments are conducted on a generator rotating bearing, and the results demonstrate that the proposed method can effectively identify the mechanical safety boundary under cyber-induced disturbances.

title: A Lightweight Approach to Multipath Traffic Splitting Against Website Fingerprinting
authors: Zirui Zhang, Jilong Yang, Weichen Li, Jincheng Deng, Chuanrong Liu, Lina Wang, Jin Zhao
publish: 2025 International Conference on Cyber Resilience and Endogenous Safety &amp; Security (CRESS)
abstract: Website Fingerprinting (WFP) attacks pose a significant threat to user privacy in anonymous communication networks such as Tor by exploiting encrypted traffic metadata to infer users’ browsing behavior. State-of-the-art deep learning-based classifiers can achieve high accuracy when targeting undefended traffic. Existing defense mechanisms face a fundamental trade-off: while traffic padding and shaping provide strong protection, they incur substantial bandwidth and latency overhead; in contrast, multipath-based approaches enhance privacy by distributing traffic across multiple paths, though often at the cost of increased complexity.We present LW-MTS, a lightweight multipath traffic splitting defense that processes only a configurable fraction of packets via sampling-based splitting. Our simulation-based evaluation on standard datasets and attacks shows that LW-MTS reduces attack accuracy under our primary concatenate scenario, provides strong protection in single-path settings, and maintains residual protection against adaptive attackers. The defense requires only a small number of concurrent circuits and adds low latency derived from empirical circuit latencies, offering a practical operating point that balances privacy protection with operational efficiency.

title: Digital Correction of Spatial–Spectral Aberrations in a Mobile Hyperspectral System
authors: Daniil Iu. Gonoshilkin, Margarita S. Shatkovskaya, Nikita M. Sokolov, Dmitry R. Sharivzyanov
publish: 2026 ElCon Conference of Young Researchers in Computing &amp; Processing, and Information Security (ElCon-CP)
abstract: This paper describes a calibration workflow for a mobile LED-based hyperspectral scanning system operating in the 400–1000 nm range. We show that an iterative strategy alternating geometric correction and lateral chromatic aberration alignment yields the lowest residual distortion and stable interband registration. A reproducible approach based on polynomial transforms and a printed grid target is proposed for integration into mobile devices.

title: Blockchain-Based Secure and Composable Model for Semi-Honest Privacy-Preserving Computation
authors: Longyang Yi, Jian Liu, Zhiguo Wan
publish: 2025 International Conference on Cyber Resilience and Endogenous Safety &amp; Security (CRESS)
abstract: Privacy-preserving computation enables multiple parties to jointly compute a function while keeping their inputs private. Protocols designed for the semi-honest model achieve high efficiency by assuming participants will correctly follow the protocol’s cryptographic steps. However, this security assumption is confined to the protocol’s internal execution, creating a crucial accountability gap. It offers no inherent method to prove that the inputs and function used in the computation actually align with what was externally agreed upon. In this paper, we introduce a novel framework that enhances privacy-preserving computation with public verifiability and accountability, while maintaining composability. Our framework leverages a blockchain as an immutable trust anchor to register cryptographic commitments of both participant inputs and the function’s specification. We then employ a zero-knowledge proof protocol to verify that the privacy-preserving computation is performed correctly using the committed data and function logic. The security of our model is formally proven to guarantee both input privacy and computational integrity, while our performance evaluation shows its practical scalability.

title: Blockchain-Based Distributed Identity Supervision and Revocation
authors: Qiang Gao, Zhiqi Liu, Youqi Li, Cong Zuo, Licheng Wang
publish: 2025 International Conference on Cyber Resilience and Endogenous Safety &amp; Security (CRESS)
abstract: Decentralized Identity (DID) systems have gained wide attention due to their advantages in autonomy, interoperability, and openness. However, existing solutions still have significant shortcomings in the adaptability of malicious detection, the granularity of identity revocation, and process collaboration. This paper proposes an autonomous identity supervision and revocation scheme based on a rewritable blockchain: by introducing the XGBoost model and extracting on-chain multi-dimensional features, high detection accuracy is maintained even in extreme scenarios; meanwhile, three types of fine-grained scenarios are designed, covering voluntary deactivation by users and system-triggered malicious revocation; efficient transaction updates are achieved via chameleon hash, and compliance is ensured by combining the PBFT consensus mechanism; finally, a full-process collaborative system of "detection-revocation-evidence storage" is constructed. Experimental results show that the scheme maintains low latency in DID registration, identity verification, and identity revocation processes, operates stably, and effectively addresses the technical gaps in existing solutions.

title: Optimizing Industrial Automation: A Multi-Algorithm Machine Learning Approach for Predicting Robotics Adoption Impact
authors: D. Sungeetha
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: Implementation of industrial robotics is a decision-making process that is very critical to manufacturing organizations that should thoroughly evaluate various interdependent variables such as productivity, cost savings, workforce displacement and training needs. Hence a 14 different machine learning models on a variety of performance indices and the best model was LightGBM that demonstrated almost perfect training performance <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(R^{2}=0.0001)$</tex> and cross-validation <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(R^{2}=-0.1797)$</tex>. The model includes in-depth feature analysis via correlation studies and feature rankings, which show that robots adopted, cost saving, and job displaced are the three factors that most significantly contribute to productivity savings. This approach will utilize a comparative analysis of algorithms based on classical regression methods to the latest ensemble techniques and neural networks through systematic comparison in terms of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{R}^{\mathbf{2}}$</tex> scores, RMSE, and MAE. The comparisons of the residual analysis and the prediction and the actual values indicate that the model is reliable enough to be deployed in the real world. The framework will offer manufacturing decision-makers an information-based planning instrument on strategic robotics investments decision-making based on technological progress and the workforce and financial optimization. The study adds significance in the domain as it has developed a repeatable methodology of multi-objective optimization of industrial automation

title: Soft-DDQN-Based Frequency Selection Strategy in Time-Slotted Channel Hopping Networks
authors: Siming Pang, Xianhua Niu, Shiyang Zhou, Bing Liu, Bosen Zeng, Pengxu Chen
publish: 2025 11th International Workshop on Signal Design and Its Applications in Communications (IWSDA)
abstract: Time-Slotted Channel Hopping (TSCH) in IEEE 802.15.4e improves anti-jamming via precise time-frequency scheduling, but most reinforcement learning (RL)-based methods only consider Packet Delivery Ratio (PDR), neglecting switching delays and thus reducing performance. This paper proposes a Soft-DDQN-based frequency selection strategy for TSCH networks, consisting of a framework and an algorithm. The framework models channel dynamics with a PDR-matrix-based environment and designs a hybrid reward function combining instantaneous quality, trend incentives, and hopping penalties. The algorithm applies Soft Double DQN with soft target updates and double Q -value estimation, enabling channel selection per time slot to avoid low-quality links, trained via experience replay and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\epsilon$</tex>-greedy exploration. These enhancements mitigate Q-value overestimation, reduce policy oscillation, and improve training stability by 43.08% over Double-DQN.

title: Analysis and Application of a Discrete-Time Neurodynamic Approach for Fast Constrained $l_{1}$-Norm Minimization
authors: Youshen Xia, Jun Wang, Changyin Sun
publish: IEEE/CAA Journal of Automatica Sinica
abstract: Discrete-time neurodynamic approaches (also called recurrent neural networks (RNNs)) are easily implemented on software and simulated on digital circuits. First, this paper proposes two modified discrete-time RNNs for quickly dealing with constrained <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$l_{1}$</tex>-norm minimization problems. Next, the two modified discrete-time RNNs are proven to be globally convergent to an optimal solution under a large step size. Finally, we apply the obtained results for image recovery. Two convergent discrete-time RNN based algorithms for non-blind image restoration are presented. Due to having a low complexity, the two discrete-time RNNs are more computationally efficient than the existing discrete-time RNN for image restoration. Computed results with application examples show that the two discrete-time RNN-based algorithms are indeed superior to the existing discrete-time RNN-based algorithms with regards to computation time.

title: Sleep Stage Specificity to Window Length Variations: A Decision Fusion Strategy for Enhanced Scoring
authors: Zhaowen Wang, Dongdong Zhou, Qi Xu, Fengyu Cong, Mohammad Al-Sa'd, Jenni Raitoharju
publish: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
abstract: Sleep stage scoring is a fundamental component of sleep medicine, enabling a comprehensive assessment of sleep architecture and quality. While the standard 30-second (30s) epoch defined by the American Academy of Sleep Medicine represents the clinical gold standard, most automatic sleep stage scoring algorithms process these fixed segments in isolation. This approach may hinder the detection of transient arousal events, sleep spindles, K-complexes, and other phasic sleep characteristics that occur on finer timescales, thus necessitating analysis at sub-epoch resolution. To leverage complementary information across temporal scales, we propose a Multi-scale Decision Fusion Sleep Network (MDFSleepNet). Our systematic analysis across window lengths (1-30 seconds) reveals significant stage-specific temporal preferences: N1 and N3 stages achieve higher accuracy with 30s windows capturing comprehensive context, while N2 stage classification benefits markedly from shorter windows (1-2 seconds) optimized for transient micro-structure detection. REM stage preferences exhibit dataset variability. Motivated by these findings, MDFSleepNet integrates these complementary scales through a dual-stream architecture combining multi-scale segmentation, scale-specific feature learning, and cross-scale fusion. Evaluated on ISRUC-S1 and ISRUC-S3 (by fusing 5s and 30s windows), MDFSleepNet achieves state-of-the-art accuracies of 83.5% and 84.8% (Cohen's Kappa: 0.786, 0.804). On Sleep-EDF-20 (fusing 15s and 30s windows), it reaches 90.9% accuracy (Cohen's Kappa: 0.875), demonstrating robust performance through complementary multi-scale fusion. The source code for this study is publicly available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/wzw999/MDFSleepNet</uri>.

title: A Knowledge-Guided Graph Reasoning Framework for Multi-Modal Detection of Mining-Induced Land Degradation
authors: Fuwen Hu, Xuefei Wu
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: The unprecedented explosion of multi-modal geospatial data offers a transformative opportunity for environmental surveillance. However, a formidable challenge remains in the automated assessment of localized geohazards(e.g., mining-induced land degradation). This difficulty stems from a fundamental limitation of existing remote sensing paradigms: their inability to perceive the concealed relational veins—the complex network of spatial, semantic, and functional linkages that connect disparate land parcels and govern their true interpretation. To overcome this challenge, we propose Venagnosis, a novel knowledge-guided graph reasoning framework that enables a paradigm shift from context-agnostic classification to context-aware relational reasoning. The Venagnosis framework is realized through three synergistic innovations. First, a Synergistic Parcel Characterization (SPC) module employs deep learning models, including a topography-focused CNN and MLP-based enhancers, to distill high-fidelity, information-dense embeddings from raw DEM, multispectral, and morphological data. Second, we construct a knowledge-guided, multi-relational graph that explicitly models the "relational veins" by encoding domain knowledge into its topology through spatial, semantic, and functional corridor edge schemas. Third, at the framework's core is the Progressive Fusion Graph Network (PF-GNN), an advanced graph attention architecture featuring adaptive input enhancement, hierarchical dynamic gating for information flow control, and a stateful cross-layer fusion mechanism for comprehensive reasoning. Comprehensive evaluations on a large-scale, national dataset of China demonstrate the state-of-the-art performance of Venagnosis. The framework achieves an F1-score of 0.8602 and an AUPRC of 0.9056, significantly outperforming competitive baselines. Rigorous ablation studies empirically validate the indispensable contribution of each architectural component. Furthermore, cross-regional generalization experiments on two distinct provincial datasets—from Henan (53,963 parcels) and Shanxi (24,833 parcels)—validate the framework's robustness and practical utility, successfully identifying unrecorded potential hazards. This work establishes a powerful, generalizable, and interpretable new paradigm for the intelligent assessment of geohazards from multi-source geospatial data.

title: Generation and Alignment: Bridging Modality Gap for Visible-Infrared Person Re-Identification
authors: Yi Xu, He Huang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia
abstract: Most existing approaches for visible-infrared person re-identification (VI-ReID) attempt to learn shared features from visible and infrared images by either using a unified deep learning model or projecting the two modalities into a common intermediate space before feature extraction. However, due to the substantial modality gap, these methods often struggle to extract effective and distinctive shared features directly or may lose valuable identity-related information during the projection process. To address these issues, we propose a novel Tri-modal Generation and Alignment Swin (TGAS) network based on the Swin Transformer V2 framework, aiming to achieve distinctive features learning and effective cross-modality alignment. Specifically, we design a Hybrid Modality Generation (HMG) module, which consists of two key components: 1) a lightweight generation network that creates stylistically balanced hybrid modality features from pairs of infrared and visible features for per identity, and 2) a modality aligner that contains an adversarial loss to align different modalities via modality compensation embeddings at the low-level feature extraction stage, as well as an identity alignment loss to align identity distributions at the high-level feature extraction stage. Additionally, we introduce a Component Token Detection (CTD) module that enhances feature discriminability while mitigating the noise introduced by the generation process. Extensive experiments demonstrate that the proposed method achieves superior or, at the very least, comparable performance to state-of-the-art methods on the SYSU-MM01, RegDB, and LLCM datasets.

title: PEEM: Prompt Engineering Evaluation Metrics for Interpretable Joint Evaluation of Prompts and Responses in LLMs
authors: Minki Hong, Eunsoo Lee, Sohyun Park, Jihie Kim
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Prompt design is a primary control interface for large language models (LLMs), yet standard evaluations largely reduce performance to answer correctness, obscuring why a prompt succeeds or fails and providing little actionable guidance. We propose PEEM (Prompt Engineering Evaluation Metrics), a unified framework for joint and interpretable evaluation of both prompts and responses. PEEM defines a structured rubric with 9 axes: 3 prompt criteria (clarity/structure, linguistic quality, fairness) and 6 response criteria (accuracy, coherence, relevance, objectivity, clarity, conciseness)—and uses an LLM-based evaluator to output (i) scalar scores on a 1–5 Likert scale and (ii) criterion-specific natural-language rationales grounded in the rubric. Across 7 benchmarks and 5 task models, PEEM’s accuracy axis strongly aligns with conventional accuracy while preserving model rankings (aggregate Spearman ρ ≈ 0.97, Pearson r ≈ 0.94, p < 0.001). A multi-evaluator study with four models shows consistent relative judgments (pairwise ρ = 0.68–0.85), supporting evaluator-agnostic deployment. Beyond alignment, PEEM captures complementary linguistic failure modes and remains informative under prompt perturbations: prompt-quality trends track downstream accuracy under iterative rewrites, semantic adversarial manipulations induce clear score degradation, and meaning-preserving paraphrases yield high stability (robustness rate ≈ 76.7–80.6%). Finally, using only PEEM scores and rationales as feedback, a zero-shot prompt rewriting loop improves downstream accuracy by up to 11.7 points, outperforming supervised and RL-based prompt-optimization baselines. Overall, PEEM provides a reproducible, criterion-driven protocol that links prompt formulation to response behavior and enables systematic diagnosis and optimization of LLM interactions.

title: Scalable Collocated Multi-User VR through Virtual Environment User Spatial Coherence
authors: Yuqi Zhou, Shuqi Liao, Yufeng Chen, Sonia Fahmy, Voicu Popescu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: This paper presents an approach to alleviating the server–client communication bottleneck in collocated multi-user virtual reality (VR). Instead of transmitting unique packets to each client, the system multicasts the same packets to all users, combining packetization, visibility-aware prioritization, and lightweight acknowledgments to ensure efficiency and fairness. To enable rapid scene completion, the environment is partitioned into independent fixed-size packets that can be decoded immediately upon arrival, and transmission order is guided by precomputed visibility footprints. Scalability is achieved through repeated multicast until compact bitmap acknowledgments confirm reception, keeping communication cost mostly independent of the number of clients. A controlled study with 23 participants in groups of 7 and 16 showed that this method reconstructs environments several times faster and with fewer missing parts than a conventional unicast TCP-based approach. These findings demonstrate that multicast transmission over commodity Wi-Fi can support large collocated VR groups.

title: Can't Nobody Stop Me! Non-Euclidean Portal Reset for Continuous Walking in Virtual Reality
authors: Ho Jung Lee, Taewoo Jo, Sulim Chun, In-Kwon Lee
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: Redirected walking (RDW) allows people to explore large virtual environments while walking within a smaller physical space. When physical space is limited, explicit resets such as turn-in-place are unavoidable. Previous studies have reduced the frequency of resets by adjusting user paths. However, resets remain necessary under severe spatial constraints. The frequent use of turn-in-place resets interrupts locomotion and can degrade task performance and user experience. We present Non-Euclidean Portal Reset (NEPR), a reset technique that enables continuous experiences without pausing the user's walking. When a collision risk is detected, NEPR opens a virtual portal leading to a short, non-Euclidean corridor. Traversing the corridor repositions and reorients the user. The exit returns the user near the point of interest target in the primary world, maintaining flow. To evaluate the effectiveness of NEPR, we conducted user experiments comparing (1) the conventional turn-in-place reset, (2) NEPR, and (3) a hybrid method combining both approaches. Our results demonstrate that NEPR and the combined technique significantly improve the user experience and task performance compared to the traditional method. Overall, NEPR reframes resets as seamless transitions rather than interruptions, enhancing the practicality of RDW.

title: Advances and Challenges for Magnetic Planarization and Integration – An Overview
authors: Yue Liu, Hongfei Wu, C. Q. Jiang, Xufu Ren, Shaoliang An, Teng Long
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Industrial Electronics Society
abstract: Efficiency, power density, and switching frequency are the three key drivers for advancing high-performance power supplies. However, the reliance on conventional wire-wound magnetic components has significantly hindered the development of this field. This paper provides a comprehensive overview of magnetic planarization and integration technologies, which are increasingly adopted in modern power electronic converters to enhance efficiency and power density. It elaborates on the methodologies for planarizing magnetic components, including the impacts and implementations of transformer and inductor integration. Additionally, printed circuit board (PCB) winding strategies are discussed, focusing on current sharing techniques, fractional turn structures, and integrated network design. The effects of air gaps during planarization are also analyzed in detail. This overview aims to help power electronics engineers and researchers gain a clear understanding of recent progress in magnetic planarization and integration technologies and the prevailing challenges in this field.

title: Spectral Efficiency-Aware Codebook Design for Task-Oriented Semantic Communications
authors: Anbang Zhang, Shuaishuai Guo, Chenyuan Feng, Shuai Liu, Hongyang Du, Geyong Min
publish: IEEE Transactions on Wireless Communications
abstract: Digital task-oriented semantic communication (ToSC) aims to transmit only task-relevant information, significantly reducing communication overhead. Existing ToSC methods typically rely on learned codebooks to encode semantic features and map them to constellation symbols. However, these codebooks are often sparsely activated, resulting in low spectral efficiency and underutilization of channel capacity. This highlights a key challenge: how to design a codebook that not only supports task-specific inference but also approaches the theoretical limits of channel capacity. To address this challenge, we construct a spectral efficiency-aware codebook design framework that explicitly incorporates the codebook activation probability into the optimization process. Beyond maximizing task performance, we introduce the Wasserstein (WS) distance as a regularization metric to minimize the gap between the learned activation distribution and the optimal channel input distribution. Furthermore, we reinterpret WS theory from a generative perspective to align with the semantic nature of ToSC. Combining the above two aspects, we propose a WS-based adaptive hybrid distribution scheme, termed WS-DC, which learns compact, task-driven and channel-aware latent representations. Experimental results demonstrate that WS-DC not only outperforms existing approaches in inference accuracy but also significantly improves codebook efficiency, offering a promising direction toward capacity-approaching semantic communication systems.

title: An Efficient Wind Farm Condition Monitoring Method Balancing Complexity and Performance
authors: Xinjian Bai, Yongqian Liu, Amir R. Nejad, Shuang Han, Zijian Kang, Yuanchi Ma
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Informatics
abstract: The predictive maintenance based on condition monitoring (CM) and early fault warning is critical for ensuring the safe operation of wind turbines and reducing wind farm maintenance costs. However, current CM and fault detection methods focus on individual wind turbines rather than the entire wind farm. Furthermore, the growing complexity of models leads to higher computational requirements, which complicates edge deployment. To effectively address the challenges of the tradeoff between model scale and monitoring performance, we propose a clustering and lightweight wind turbine CM method. First, a clustering method incorporating temporal features and data correlations is proposed to group wind turbines and identify central representatives. Second, a lightweight neural circuit policy-driven spiking neural network is developed. The normal behavior models are trained on the central wind turbines and generalized across wind turbines in the same cluster. Finally, we quantitatively compare the proposed method with three baseline models using both healthy and faulty wind turbines to validate its excellent performance in CM at a wind farm comprising 80 wind turbines. The results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves CM and early fault detection for the entire wind farm using only three models. Additionally, the proposed method maintains remarkable stability and accuracy, despite being one-tenth the size or smaller compared to baseline models. The average root mean square error decreases by 9.68% during the healthy operation. The average warning time is 35.31 h before the failure occurrence while minimizing false alarms.

title: Ai-Powered Disease Detection and Root Cause Analysis in Healthcare
authors: M Parameswari, V Mohanraj, D Ramya, S Saranya, S Ravikumar
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: The conventional approach to healthcare diagnosis is based on manual assessment of medical symptoms and images which may be time-consuming and subject to human error. The proposed project presents an AI-Based Root Cause Analysis System which combines Natural Language Processing (NLP) with Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) to automated disease prediction and analysis. It takes three kinds of inputs such as text mode, voice input, and medical pictures as inputs to predict diseases, find root causes, and provide recovery tips. The AI integration will guarantee early diagnosis, better accuracy, and quicker healthcare decisionmaking processes. The project will serve the UN Sustainable Development Goal (SDG) 3 - Good Health and Well-being, through the provision of accessible and smart diagnostic solutions.

title: Near-Field Communication With Massive Movable Antennas: A Functional Perspective
authors: Shicong Liu, Xianghao Yu, Jie Xu, Rui Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Wireless Communications
abstract: The advent of massive multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) technology has provided new opportunities for capacity improvement via strategic antenna deployment, especially when the near-field effect is pronounced due to antenna proliferation. In this paper, we investigate the optimal antenna placement for maximizing the achievable rate of a point-to-point near-field channel, where the transmitter is deployed with massive movable antennas. First, we propose a novel design framework to explore the relationship between antenna positions and achievable data rate. By introducing the continuous antenna position function (APF) and antenna density function (ADF), we reformulate the antenna position design problem from the discrete to the continuous domain, which maximizes the achievable rate functional with respect to ADF. Leveraging functional analysis and variational methods, we derive the optimal ADF condition and propose a gradient-based algorithm for numerical solutions under general channel conditions. Furthermore, for the near-field line-of-sight (LoS) scenario, we present a closed-form solution for the optimal ADF, revealing the critical role of edge antenna density in enhancing the achievable rate. Finally, we propose a flexible antenna array-based deployment method that ensures practical implementation while mitigating mutual coupling issues. Simulation results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed framework, with uniform circular arrays emerging as a promising geometry for balancing performance and deployment feasibility in near-field communications.

title: Optimal Power Scheduling for a Distributed Warehouse Network: Convex Optimization and Comparative Analysis of Classical Gradient Techniques
authors: Saksham Gupta, Nishant Kumar
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: The paper explores the cost-effective power scheduling of deferrable electric forklift charging loads across a network of logistics warehouses in the Kanpur-Lucknow corridor. The central challenge is to minimize the total monthly electricity cost, which is determined by three conflicting factors: punitive demand charges, volumetric energy charges, and a quadratic penalty associated with battery degradation that occurs during high charging rates. The original objective function contains a non-differentiable maximum operator to represent peak demand, which is reformulated into a differentiable Convex Quadratic Program(CQP) using an epigraph-based approach. The resulting large-scale constrained optimization problem, consisting of several hundred variables and linear constraints, is solved within the robust framework of the Augmented Lagrangian Method(ALM). Three classical optimization techniques- Steepest Descent Method(SDM), Conjugate Gradient Method(CGM), and a Quasi-Newton method-are implemented and compared as unconstrained inner-loop solvers. Numerical analysis across three heterogeneous operational scenarios confirms that while all methods converge to the same global optimum, the CGM achieves substantially faster convergence, making it the most effective classical approach for computationally efficient, realtime industrial demand-side management applications.

title: Integer-Valued Complete Complementary Codes Based on Hadamard-Type Matrices Over Finite Fields
authors: Tetsuya Kojima
publish: 2025 11th International Workshop on Signal Design and Its Applications in Communications (IWSDA)
abstract: It is important to find sequences that have autocorrelation with a single sharp peak as well as no crosscorrelation. Such sequences play important roles in many applications such as wireless communications, radar systems, and so on. Complete complementary code (CCC) is a collection of sets of binary or complex-valued sequences with ideal autoand cross-correlation properties, which enables interference-free transmissions of multiple users and avoid multipath fading. In previous studies, CDMA communication systems and information hiding technologies based on CCCs have been proposed. In this paper, we show that it is possible to generate integer-valued CCCs based on Hadamard-type matrices on finite fields. We also discuss the condition to generate such CCCs under the previously proposed generation methods.

title: Semantic and Clustering-Driven Aggregation for Effective Federated Learning on Heterogeneous Data
authors: Bhavana S N, Suma L, M S Bhargavi
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: Federated Learning (FL) offers a decentralized framework where multiple clients collaboratively train a shared model without transferring raw data, thereby safeguarding privacy. Despite its promise, FL often faces challenges due to non-identical and non-independent (non-IID) client data, leading to unstable training and reduced global accuracy. To mitigate these issues, this research investigates clustering and semantics-driven aggregation methods that improve training stability and convergence under heterogeneous conditions. In this context, three representative aggregation methods Federated Averaging (FedAvg), Clustered Federated Learning (FedCluster), and Semantic-Aware Federated Learning (FedSem) are analyzed by employing a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) as a unified architecture for feature extraction and classification. Experiments are performed on EMNIST ByClass for handwritten character recognition and CIFAR-10 for object classification. While FedAvg is efficient under balanced data settings, FedCluster improves stability through client grouping based on similarity, and FedSem enhances convergence using semantic regularization. A modular experimental framework explores the effects of varying client counts, communication rounds, and dataset types. Results show that FedCluster and FedSem outperform FedAvg under heterogeneous conditions, with FedSem achieving the most stable accuracy across scenarios. The study emphasizes the importance of adaptive aggregation mechanisms to enhance model robustness in real-world heterogeneous FL deployments.

title: Transformer-Tuned Deep Learning Architecture for Real-Time Medical Diagnosis Using Federated Learning
authors: N. Shree Makesh, M. Naveenkumar, J. Kavitha, K. Dev Prasad, P. Badrinath
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: Rapid growth of medical data along with the need for instantaneous diagnostic intelligence has led to the requirement for AI solutions that are both secure and scalable. <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$A$</tex> Transformer-Tuned Federated Deep Learning (TF2DF) framework, which allows the privacy-preserving diagnosis with high accuracy across different healthcare systems. While centralized models are at risk of data leakage and latency, TF2DF has federated transformer modules that learn together on the distributed medical datasets without the need of sharing the raw data. The model consists of multi-head self-attention for capturing complex dependencies and an adaptive federated optimizer for the efficient handling of non-uniform data sources. The model has been tested on the MIMIC-III, ChestX-ray14, and COVIDx datasets, and its performance is better than the traditional CNN and LSTM systems in terms of accuracy, precision, and F1-score. Performance evaluation in terms of latency has shown the feasibility of real-time diagnostic. The architecture is compliant with HIPAA and GDPR, thus it supports ethical and scalable AI deployment. There are plans for the future to include explainable AI (XAI) and cross-modal fusion to offer better interpretability and clinical decision support.

title: Privacy-Aware Hybrid Federated–Homomorphic Learning Framework for Secure Medical Healthcare Diagnostics
authors: Ratna Raju Mukiri, Gudapati S Satyanarayana, S. Amarnath Babu
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: The government and business have been closely watching healthcare industry improvements related to the IoT because of the potential they hold to improve patient diagnosis and research. Privacy and security are major issues with healthcare data due to its sensitive nature, especially when user data needs to be sent to data centres for data mining. A privacy-aware healthcare architecture based on federated learning is proposed in this paper to address these difficulties. It allows various entities to work together to train a global Privacy Aware Healthcare model without disclosing personal information. An innovative deep learning architecture called Enhanced Ghost BiNet, which combines GhostNet with the BiGRU is used by the framework to safely train local models. The model is fine-tuned using the CAh algorithm after data is cleaned and features are extracted using cuckoos. The suggested FL-CAh-HE technique is highly effective, as shown experimentally by its 99.05% detection precision. In essence, our research shows that in privacy-aware healthcare settings, federated learning, deep learning, and advanced optimisation methods may work together to improve detection accuracy, safeguard patient privacy, and guarantee secure information exchange.

title: Diffuse++: Semantic-Aware Diffusion Attacks with Progressive Prompting
authors: Xiang Wu, Fengpeng Xiong, Boya Zhang, Gangtao Han
publish: 2025 International Conference on Cyber Resilience and Endogenous Safety &amp; Security (CRESS)
abstract: Deep Neural Networks (DNNs) have achieved exceptional performance across a wide range of applications, including image classification and medical imaging. However, their vulnerability to adversarial attacks, which can cause misclassifications through imperceptible input perturbations. While black-box adversarial attacks better mirror real-world scenarios, they often suffer from low transferability and high detectability due to noisy perturbations. To address these issues, we introduce Diffuse++, a novel framework that leverages the power of diffusion models to generate highly transferable and visually imperceptible adversarial examples. Diffuse++ incorporates two key innovations to enhance attack efficacy and stealth: (1) Progressive Prompting (PP), which incrementally injects adversarial cues into the diffusion process, enabling controlled, gradual modifications that preserve the global structure and coherence of the images; and (2) Semantic Attention Masking (SAM), which intelligently focuses perturbations on foreground regions while stabilizing background areas, ensuring minimal perceptual artifacts and greater stealth. Extensive experimental evaluations across multiple datasets and target models demonstrate that Diffuse++ significantly outperforms state-of-the-art attack methods, yielding higher fooling rates and lower perturbation magnitudes while maintaining superior visual fidelity.

title: Lexicographic Unequal Error Protection Codes
authors: Tomohiko Saito, Yoshifumi Ukita, Toshiyasu Matsushima
publish: 2025 11th International Workshop on Signal Design and Its Applications in Communications (IWSDA)
abstract: Conway and Sloane proposed lexicographic codes, which are defined by a greedy algorithm. Lexicographic codes are also deeply related to combinatorial game theory, making them particularly interesting from a theoretical perspective. On the other hand, unequal error protection (UEP) codes were proposed by Masnick et al. In this study, we demonstrate that the greedy algorithm used to define lexicographic codes can also be applied to the construction of UEP codes, and we explicitly construct UEP codes for several parameter sets. We refer to these constructed codes as lexicographic UEP codes. Furthermore, we compare the code sizes of the lexicographic UEP codes with the linear programming bounds for UEP codes, demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed approach.

title: Comparison of Hybrid Sentiment Classification Models Using Feature-Based Methods and Transformer Architectures
authors: C. Maddilety, Saya Kavya Sri, Shaik Javeriya, Talari Amrutha
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: The use of Sentiment Classification in Natural Language Processing (NLP) is a significant area of NLP because it allows us to automatically extract user expressed opinions about their experience with products or services they have purchased/used from numerous digital platforms (social media, product review sites, movie review sites, etc.) to help us better understand what people think about our brand. As there is an abundance of new data being generated, the need for accurate sentiment classification has grown exponentially and has been incorporated into many applications such as recommendation systems, customer feedback analysis, and opinion mining. Many different types of machine learning and deep learning techniques have been developed over the years to classify sentiments in text, each having varying degrees of success. Techniques have ranged from basic feature based models utilizing TF-IDF to represent terms, to advanced transformer architectures that can capture contextualized semantic information. This research will perform a complete comparative evaluation of the effectiveness of five commonly utilized sentiment classification models (Logistic Regression, SVM, BiLSTM, DistilBERT, RoBERTa). Each model will be evaluated using the same IMDb benchmark dataset to provide a consistent and fair experimental environment. The comparison will focus on classification effectiveness, while also providing insight to the differences between classical machine learning methods, sequential deep learning models, and transformer architectures. The experimental results clearly show that transformer based models were able to significantly outperform the traditional models in both semantic understanding and classification accuracy. Of the models tested, RoBERTA was the best performing model, achieving an accuracy of 95% to confirm the ability of contextualized representation for sentiment classification.

title: AI Integrated Milk Products Spoilage Detection using Colorimetric and Electrochemical Sensing Techniques
authors: N. Nanthini, A T Samyugtha, S Srinithi, V S Swetha, S. Krishna Prabha
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: Quality monitoring of dairy products such as milk and ghee is essential to ensure consumer safety, extend shelf life, and maintain supply chain efficiency. This study proposes a costeffective, real-time spoilage detection system that combines multiple sensors, embedded processing, and machine learning for accurate quality assessment. The setup uses a color sensor, pH probe, and conductivity electrodes to track physicochemical changes linked to microbial growth, while a NodeMCU microcontroller processes the collected data and classifies samples into categories like fresh, marginal, or spoiled using a K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) model. The framework is further enhanced to analyze ghee by incorporating additional datasets during the training phase, enabling accurate detection across different dairy products. Real-time feedback is provided on-site and can be remotely accessed through cloud-based platforms. This solution offers a scalable and user-friendly approach to dairy quality monitoring, reducing wastage and supporting safe, data-driven supply chain operations.

title: Automated Wheat Leaf Disease Classification Using Deep Learning Algorithms: A Study
authors: R Sivakumar
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: Wheat is essential to the world's food supply; nevertheless, its availability is severely limited by numerous illnesses that adversely affect its nutritional value and yield. The manual diagnosis of these illnesses is laborious, subjective, and susceptible to errors, necessitating the development of automated, intelligent solution. This study introduces an automated approach for the detection and categorisation of wheat foliage illnesses utilising deep learning (DL) methods. The proposed method employs convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and transfer learning models like DenseNet to analyze wheat leaf images collected under diverse environmental conditions. Feature extraction is performed through deep layers of CNNs, capturing morphological and texture-based characteristics of infected tissues. The models are trained and validated on benchmark datasets, with all performance metrics. Among all tested architectures, DenseNet121 achieved the highest classification accuracy of 98%, maintaining strong generalization across unseen samples. The proposed system offers valuable potential for precision agriculture, enabling early disease detection, reducing pesticide overuse, and supporting sustainable wheat production in real-world farm environments.

title: On Some Classes of $p$-Ary Linear Codes with Most $p+1$-Weights Derived from Weil Sums
authors: Mrinal Kanti Bose, Udaya Parampalli, Abhay Kumar Singh
publish: 2025 11th International Workshop on Signal Design and Its Applications in Communications (IWSDA)
abstract: Let <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$p$</tex> be an odd prime and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathcal{N}$</tex> be a positive integer such that <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$p$</tex> is a primitive root modulo <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathcal{N}$</tex>. Define <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$q=p^{\phi(\mathcal{N})}$</tex>. In [5], Cheng and Gao provided a novel method to evaluate the exponential sum given by \begin{equation*} S_{\mathcal{N}}(a, b)=\sum_{x \in \mathbb{F}_{q}^{*}} \chi\left(a x^{\frac{q-1}{\mathcal{N}}}+b x\right) \end{equation*} where <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathcal{N}=2,4, \ell^{m}$</tex> or <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2 \ell^{m}$</tex> with <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\ell$</tex> be an odd prime distinct from <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$p$</tex>. For <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$p=3$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$q=3^{\phi\left(\ell^{m}\right)}$</tex>, they investigated the linear code <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathcal{C}_{D}$</tex> by choosing the defining set <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$D=\left\{x \in \mathbb{F}_{q}^{*}: \operatorname{Tr}\left(x^{\frac{q-1}{e^{m}}}\right)=0\right\}$</tex>, and constructed a family of two-weight ternary linear codes with completely determined weight distribution. Very recently, Cheng and Sheng [6] considered a more general defining set <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$D$</tex> for an arbitrary odd prime <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$p$</tex> with <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$D=\left\{x \in \mathbb{F}_{q}^{*}: \operatorname{Tr}\left(x^{\frac{q-1}{2 \ell^{m}}}+\beta x\right)=\alpha\right\}$</tex>, where <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\alpha \in \mathbb{F}_{p}$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\beta \in \mathbb{F}_{q}$</tex>. They constructed some families of two-weight linear codes and computed their weight distribution when <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\alpha \in \mathbb{F}_{p}$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\beta=0$</tex>. For <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\beta \neq 0$</tex>, they determined all possible weights of codewords in <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathcal{C}_{D}$</tex> and demonstrated that it has at most <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$p+1$</tex> distinct nonzero weights. Motivated by the above works, in this paper, for any odd prime <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$p$</tex> we investigate the linear code <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathcal{C}_{D}$</tex> for the defining set <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$D=\left\{x \in \mathbb{F}_{q}^{*}: \operatorname{Tr}\left(x^{\frac{q-1}{e^{m}}}\right)=u, \operatorname{Tr}(x)=0\right\}$</tex>, where <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$u \in \mathbb{F}_{p}$</tex> and determine all possible weights of codewords in <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathcal{C}_{D}$</tex>, demonstrating that it has at most <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$p+1$</tex> distinct nonzero weights. Additionally, we determine the minimum distance of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathcal{C}_{D}^{\perp}$</tex> and prove that <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathcal{C}_{D}^{\perp}$</tex> is optimal under a certain condition with respect to the Sphere packing bound.

title: Sorting Algorithm Performance Under Varying Data Conditions
authors: Kustov Maxim
publish: 2026 ElCon Conference of Young Researchers in Computing &amp; Processing, and Information Security (ElCon-CP)
abstract: In this work, I present my findings from comparing how well different sorting algorithms perform when working with big datasets. The research focuses on three well-known methods: Quicksort, Merge Sort, and Heapsort. All of them were coded in C language and tested under identical conditions. My experiments used computer-generated integer arrays of different sizes, and I tested them with various starting arrangements - completely random order, already sorted, sorted backwards and datasets where only a few unique values repeat many times. The practical outcome of this study is a set of guidelines that can help programmers pick the right algorithm depending on what kind of data they have, how much memory their system has, and what reliability guarantees they need. The most interesting result from my tests was that Quicksort handled one million randomly arranged elements roughly 15% faster than Merge Sort and about 35% faster than Heapsort on my test computer.

title: IoT Based Smart Energy Meter
authors: Thoshita Febbi B, Vini Jasmi V, Arulraj Simon Prabu
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: The IoT-based Smart Energy Meter is designed to revolutionize the traditional energy monitoring approach and offers real-time, intelligent analysis of electricity consumption. Using an ESP32 microcontroller with a built-in Wi-Fi connection, it takes data from current and voltage sensors. This data is sent to the cloud, with the help of Blynk application to monitor it remotely using a dashboard. A key feature is an abnormal usage detection algorithm which alerts users to sudden load variations, indicating possible wastage or faults. Furthermore, machine learning methods are incorporated to forecast monthly bills and track high consumption appliances. This low-cost solution puts the power in the hands of the user, providing actionable insights, encouraging energy-efficient behavior and helping to create sustainable smart homes by closing the gap between data collection and intelligent energy management.

title: Effects of Bilayer Conduction on Hysteresis Characteristics of Si/SiGe Heterostructure Field-Effect Transistors at Cryogenic Temperatures
authors: Hsiang-Shun Kao, Yu-Jui Wu, Yi-Pei Lin, Muskan Sangal, Chia-You Liu, Wei-Hsiang Kao, Jiun-Yun Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Electron Devices
abstract: We investigate the bilayer conduction in undoped Si/SiGe heterostructure field-effect transistors (FETs) and its effects on hysteresis characteristics at temperatures from 300 to 1.8 K. We found that both the transconductance and first-plateau current of the devices slightly change with the channel length due to the large series resistance at cryogenic temperatures. By modulating the maximum gate bias, we find that the threshold voltage of the devices for forward sweeps stays constant, while for backward sweeps, it increases with the maximum gate bias with two distinct regimes, which can be explained by the presence of the bilayer conduction in the Si cap and quantum well (QW) layers. We also characterize forward-sweep and backward-sweep subthreshold swing (SS) at different temperatures. Both forward-sweep and backward-sweep SS saturate at deep cryogenic temperatures due to finite band-tail states. The backward-sweep SS is better at temperatures between 200 and 70 K due to the effective screening of the defects at the oxide interface by electrons tunneling from the buried Si QW.

title: Letting Go of Your Real Body: Noisy Electrical Stimulation Facilitates Body Schema Transformation in Virtual Reality_supp1-3679128.pdf
authors: Maki Ogawa
publish: N/A
abstract: The use of beyond-real interactions (BRI) expands the possibilities of virtual reality (VR) experiences by enabling novel capabilities, such as extending the range of manipulation or employing unusual body configurations. However, such interactions require users to adapt to novel sensorimotor mappings, which can lead to decreased performance and a poorer quality of user experience. Recently, proprioceptive suppression with noisy tendon electrical stimulation (n-TES) has been proposed to promote adaptation to BRI, though its effects have been reported to be limited. This study investigated the effects of proprioceptive suppression via n-TES on the adaptation process to BRI and the consequent changes in body schema, which have remained unexplored in prior research. Using a between-subjects design with 24 participants, we examined the effects of n-TES on a reaching task with Go-Go interaction, which extends virtual hands to enable users to reach farther than physically possible. Body schema changes were measured using a pointing task without visual feedback of the hand after adaptation to the Go-Go interaction. The results revealed that n-TES significantly altered the adaptation process by facilitating early learning and also enhanced body schema changes and aftereffects of adaptation under certain conditions. However, parameter fitting of the learning curves also suggested that n-TES might lead to a deterioration in the final level of performance achieved through adaptation. Our findings provide initial evidence that proprioceptive suppression by n-TES can contribute to facilitating sensorimotor adaptation within VR applications, while its practical utility may require adjusting stimulation to prevent potential side effects in the later stages of adaptation, specifically in trajectory smoothness.

title: Compact Broadband MIMO Antenna Design and Test for the Brazilian TV 3.0
authors: Marcelo Bender Perotoni, Tarcísio Cordaro, Marcos Stefanelli Vieira, Edson Tafeli C. dos Santos, Yuri Pontes Maciel, Cristiano Akamine
publish: IEEE Transactions on Broadcasting
abstract: This article describes the challenge of a Multiple-Input Multiple-Output (MIMO) array for Brazilian Digital TV 3.0. The chosen Advanced Television Systems Committee (ATSC) 3.0 system is the first worldwide to employ <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$2\times 2$</tex-math> </inline-formula> MIMO antennas, which, in view of the unpredictable indoor environment and long wavelengths, is an engineering challenge. The design, focused on mass indoor environments, is oriented towards a compact, low-cost, and planar radiant system. Since the required bandwidth spans the Very High Frequency (VHF) and Ultra High Frequency (UHF) ranges, miniaturization is the first compromise rather than the standard reflection-loss parameter. Aprototype is constructed and tested inside a building room using as reception a test signal broadcast at the center frequency of 177.1428571MHz, and additionally with four different carriers (177.143MHZ, 277.143MHZ, 587.143MHz and 695.142MHZ), within an anechoic chamber. The proposed compact planar antenna covers the 174–698MHz band with envelope correlation coefficients below 0.1 and achieves realized gains of nearby 0dB. Tests in broadcast and anechoic environments confirmed its broadband performance and suitability for low-cost indoor MIMO reception in Brazil’s TV 3.0 system.
