title: Dual Adversarial Perturbations for Zero-Shot Learning
authors: Lei Wang, Shiming Chen, Guo-Sen Xie, Chaojian Yu, Xinhua You, Qinmu Peng, Xinge You
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: In Zero-Shot Learning (ZSL), embedding-based methods learn a visual–semantic mapping that leverages the attribute knowledge of seen classes to predict the attributes of unseen classes, enabling knowledge transfer from seen to unseen classes. However, distributional discrepancies between seen and unseen classes introduce an inherent domain shift, and inter-class variations cause the same attribute to be expressed differently across categories. As a result, models trained on seen classes often struggle to accurately recognize attributes in unseen classes, limiting their generalization ability. To address these challenges, we propose DAPZSL, a dual adversarial perturbation framework that enhances the robustness of visual–semantic mappings through Feature-Level Adversarial Perturbation (FAP) and improves the model’s generalization ability via Weight-Level Adversarial Perturbation (WAP). Specifically, FAP generates semantically perturbed adversarial samples at the feature level, and incorporating these samples during training encourages the model to learn more robust visual–semantic mappings that are resilient to semantic variations, which improves attribute recognition on unseen classes. Meanwhile, WAP introduces adversarial perturbations into the model’s weight space, promoting a flatter loss landscape that alleviates overfitting to seen classes and enhances generalization. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmark datasets—including AWA2, SUN, and CUB—demonstrate that DAPZSL significantly outperforms existing ZSL models.

title: Program-Verifiable Evaluation for Temporal QA: Metrics for Evidence Validity
authors: Beomseok Kim, Hoansuk Choi, Namhyun Yoo, Jinhong Yang
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Time-sensitive question answering (QA) requires that the cited evidence be temporally valid at query time. However, existing evaluations focus on answer correctness while overlooking evidence timeliness. We presented a program-verifiable evaluation framework that explicitly decouples answer correctness from temporal validity. Our oracle-answer diagnostic fixes answers (Acc=1.0) while measuring evidence validity, revealing that even perfect answers cite temporally invalid documents 11.8% of the time (TMR=0.118), yielding RAS=0.903 under distance-based decay. We introduced time-consistency metrics (RAS, RAR, TMR) and evaluated on TimeFact-CEO (synthetic, N=4,793) and Wikidata-CEO (semi-structured, N=1,493). A lightweight lexico-temporal reranker improves the temporal validity rate (RAR) from 48.2% to 88.2% (+40.0pp) under an oracle-answer evaluation. Error analysis showed that the retrieval coverage (76.3% at K=3)was the primary bottleneck.We demonstrate the framework in a CEO-tenure setting where validity intervals are explicit and uniquely verifiable. Applying it to other domains is feasible when comparable interval metadata are available, and we treat cross-domain validation in future work.

title: Directed Graph Learning Via Smooth Signals and Directional Flow
authors: Nelitha Kulasiri, Nainapal Kaur, Milo Schlittgen-Li, William Gillanders, Jing Guo
publish: IEEE Transactions on Signal and Information Processing over Networks
abstract: Graph Signal Processing (GSP) offers a structured way to model and analyze complex data networks. However, a consistent challenge in real world applications is that the underlying graph topology is often unknown. While most existing graph learning methods focus on undirected graphs, specific relationships in network data, such as diffusion, weather data and social networks, sometimes necessitate a directed graph model. In this work, we propose a novel method for learning directed graph structures from observed signals by leveraging both signal smoothness and directional flow. We introduce a Dirichlet energy formulation specifically tailored to directed graphs, favoring smoothness and downwards flow of signals over the graph. We then develop the Perseus Measure to quantify how much the learned graphs obey the directional flow constraint. In addition, we present a novel random graph algorithm that builds a signal matrix alongside the graph, and we derive certain properties about the resulting Hierarchy Graphs. Experiments on both synthetic and real-world datasets show that our directed graph learning approach effectively captures directional relationships.

title: A 3.2mW 2.35-dB NF Noise-Cancelling Balun-LNA Using Current-Reuse-Enhanced Local-Feedback for Improved Power Efficiency
authors: Seungyeon Kim, Donggu Lee, Sukju Yun, Kuduck Kwon
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems II: Express Briefs
abstract: In this article, a 0.1–1.2 GHz broadband balun low-noise amplifier (LNA) achieving low-power operation and balanced output loads using a current-reuse-enhanced local-feedback architecture is presented. The proposed topology employs a current-reuse inverter-based common-source (CS) stage to increase the effective transconductance without additional DC current, thereby strengthening the loop gain of the local-feedback common-gate (CG) stage and relaxing the intrinsic CG gm requirement for wideband input matching. As a result, an improved gain–noise–power trade-off is achieved while maintaining balanced differential output. Fabricated in a 65-nm CMOS process, the proposed balun-LNA achieves a minimum noise figure of 2.35 dB, a maximum voltage gain of 28.9 dB, and S11 < -10 dB over a 1.2 GHz bandwidth. The measured peak IIP3 and OIP3 are –5.6 dBm and 23.3 dBm, respectively. The LNA consumes 3.2 mA from a 1 V supply and occupies an active area of 0.027 mm².

title: Investigating the Effects of Egocentric FPV vs. Exocentric TPV Perspectives and Collaboration Modes on Sense of Agency in VR Teleoperation
authors: Zhonghao Zhu, Kang Yueh, Songyue Yang, Fanlu Zeng, Yue Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: With the advancement of virtual reality (VR), human-robot interaction within remote collaboration contexts has emerged as a pivotal research area. However, the nuanced interplay between the user's perspective and the robot's level of automation, and its subsequent impact on the operator's sense of agency (SoA), remains underexplored. This study investigates how perspective and collaboration mode—differentiated by the robot's level of automation—affect the SoA in VR-based human-robot collaboration. We implemented a 2 (perspective: first-person view[FPV] vs. third-person view[TPV]) × 3 (collaboration mode: Direct Remote Control, Supervisory Cooperation, Spectator Mode) experimental design. SoA was assessed via a multimodal approach, measuring both the explicit judgement of agency (JoA) and the implicit feeling of agency (FoA). The results revealed a dissociation between these two components: explicit JoA was dictated by the collaboration mode, with active modes yielding a higher explicit JoA than the passive Spectator Mode. In contrast, implicit FoA exhibited a significant interaction effect. Under FPV, the influence of collaboration mode was pronounced, where Direct Remote Control elicited the strongest FoA; however, under TPV, the differences among collaboration modes disappeared. The relationship between cybersickness and SoA was also explored. These findings suggest that SoA is not a unitary concept but a multifaceted experience, co-shaped by the user's perspective and their role as either a decision-maker or an executor. Furthermore, we investigate the relationship between cybersickness and SoA. Our study offers novel insights for the design of VR-based human-robot systems.

title: Latency Uncertainty-aware User Allocation in Mobile Edge Computing
authors: Ruikun Luo, Peng Jin, Qiang He, Feifei Chen, Song Wu, Hai Jin, Yun Yang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Services Computing
abstract: Mobile edge computing (MEC) is an emerging distributed paradigm where edge servers are deployed near base stations or access points to support low-latency services. The edge user allocation (EUA) problem, which aims to minimize system cost while meeting constraints like data transmission latency, has become a critical challenge for service providers. Existing studies typically focus on static MEC scenarios, assuming predictable latency between users and edge servers. However, network congestion introduces latency uncertainty, which, if not addressed, increases the risk of allocation failures or excessive service latency. This paper addresses the latency uncertainty-aware edge user allocation (uEUA) problem. We model uEUA as an integer programming problem and apply chance-constrained programming to convert uncertain latency into probabilistic constraints, which we then transform into deterministic conditions using Chebyshev's inequality. We propose two methods to solve the problem: BD-uEUA, an exact approach based on Benders decomposition, and LR-uEUA, an approximate method based on linear relaxation. Extensive experiments on a real-world dataset show that BD-uEUA reduces system costs by 19.70% compared to the state-of-the-art method, while LR-uEUA achieves a 3.05% reduction with just 0.09% of the system overhead.

title: An Equivalent Circuit-Based Method for Antenna Efficiency Measurement in a Reverberation Chamber
authors: Yanwei Ye, Xiang Zhou, Chengjie Li, Zhicheng Xue, Ruxin Zheng
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: This letter proposes a novel method for measuring antenna efficiency in a reverberation chamber based on antenna equivalent circuit theory. By measuring the absorption cross sections of the antenna under matched and open-circuit load conditions, a theoretical relationship between the antenna efficiency and the equivalent circuit parameters is established, from which the efficiency of the antenna under test is determined. Compared to the existing methods for antenna efficiency measurement in a reverberation chamber, the proposed method eliminates the need for a reference antenna with known efficiency and the determination of the time constant of the reverberation chamber. Consequently, the proposed method significantly reduces both the hardware requirements and the data processing complexity.

title: Blockchain-Backed Router Logs for Security Forensics with Machine Learning Anomaly Detection and IoT Integration
authors: P. Libini, Devalla Srisatya Durgalasya, S. Thrishalya, Shinu M Rajagopal
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: Cyberattacks continue to evolve in scale and sophistication, with adversaries increasingly targeting network logs to remove their traces or manipulate digital evidence. Even though network devices regularly generate a large number of logs, standard logging infrastructures are still susceptible to unauthorized changes and single-point failures; thus, they cannot be trusted fully for forensic analysis. This study presents a comprehensive forensic logging framework that merges blockchain-supported immutability, machine-learning-driven anomaly detection, and IoT device trust scoring to overcome this problem. The entire setup acts as a digital black box for contemporary networks. Blockchain guarantees that logs cannot be altered once they are recorded, while machine learning models spot abnormal behaviors and attacks in real-time, and IoT devices are continually assigned trust scores similar to credit ratings to authenticate the trustworthiness of their contributions. The proposed system generates log artifacts, real-time threat alerts, and automated forensic documentation, which can be used in legal cases. The experimental evaluation reveals that the combination of decentralized storage with predictive analytics substantially reduces the chances of log tampering and lowers detection latency compared to conventional centralized logging systems. Earlier research has focused on blockchain-based logging or machine learning anomaly detection separately. By combining permanence, smart detection, and device trust evaluation, the proposed system achieves better resistance, reliability, and readiness for forensics. The output points to tangible enhancements in preserving log integrity, attack detection accuracy, and investigative efficiency; thus, this architecture is a significant leap forward compared to existing methods of secure network logging.

title: A2-DBiLNet: An Attention Auto-Encoder Based Deep BiLSTM for Fault Diagnosis in Rotating Machines Using Vibration Signals
authors: Uttam Popat Waghmode, Shankar Popat Waghmode, Aasha Mahesh Chavan, Dhanashri Maruti Dhawale, Vrishali M Patil, Rajashree Sambhaji More
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: Rotating machinery is an essential component across modern industries that facilitates fundamental operations from manufacturing to infrastructure. For sustainable productivity and minimizing downtime of machines, maintaining the equipment's reliability is crucial. However, the traditional fault diagnosis methods often face shortfalls in the form of high-noise data, limited feature extraction, and poor adaptability to dynamic conditions, resulting in delayed or inaccurate detection. Therefore, to mitigate these limitations, this research proposes an Attention Autoencoder based Deep Bidirectional Long Short Term Memory Network (A2-DBiLNet) for the fault diagnosis in rotating machinery. The BiLSTM is effective at capturing long-periodical correlations in the vibration signal, thus allowing faults to be accurately identified. Moreover, the deep structure provides enhanced representation, and the auto-encoder compresses the dimensionality by preserving only discriminative features, which leads to greater learning efficiency. In addition, the Squeeze-and-Excitation (SE) attention mechanism dynamically boosts the prioritization of features with low computation cost. Collectively, these strategies form a robust diagnostic framework that guarantees reliable predictive maintenance within the dynamic industrial settings. For performance evaluation with 90% of training using the MaFaulDa dataset, the proposed method reported the high accuracy of 98.01%, specificity of 98.64%, and sensitivity of 97.69%, significantly outperforming other existing approaches.

title: Deterministic Model for Assessing the Probability of Cyber-Attack Scenarios Against Artificial Intelligence Systems
authors: Mikhail D. Tatarchuk, Alexandr V. Dushkin
publish: 2026 ElCon Conference of Young Researchers in Computing &amp; Processing, and Information Security (ElCon-CP)
abstract: The security of artificial intelligence systems requires new risk assessment methods. Traditional probabilistic approaches are often inapplicable due to the lack of representative data on emerging threats. This study aims to develop a deterministic algorithm for assessing the likelihood of attacks on AI systems. The method is based on assessing the maturity level of protective measures for each tactic from a defined list. The proposed approach allows for establishing a direct relationship between the quality of countermeasures and the likelihood of an attack’s success. The algorithm demonstrates practical value for identifying vulnerabilities and planning security enhancements. The results of this work can be used to create effective artificial intelligence security systems.

title: MediAI: An Intelligent Healthcare System for Disease Prediction and Patient Support
authors: K. Uday Kumar Reddy, Fahimuddin Shaik, Y.Y. Mani Sankar Reddy, C. Dharma Teja Varma, S. Hammad Ali Khan
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: The detailed nature of disease diagnosis and a sharp increase of medical needs require automated systems that can help both patients and doctors. Medical AI aims to address this challenge by providing predictions for serious issues including kidney disorders, liver cancer, respiratory illness, malaria, heart issues and heart problems. The system makes use of deep learning and machine learning techniques to provide accurate predictions. Convolutional neural networks can be used for disease detection with excellent accuracy in detecting malaria and respiratory diseases. Random Forest Classifiers are used to predict heart, kidney and liver related conditions with reliability and precision for visual clinical data. Advanced processing methods like digital sampling are used for working with imbalanced datasets and exploratory data analysis ensures superior input data. Healthcare doctors are able to make predictions through immediate tracking and interaction using Flask based user interface. The success of disease detection are shown by results that show an overall prediction accuracy of 98.9%. The system may improve patient outcomes, less errors in treatment and improve making choices in medical settings by combining several models of prediction and real time support features. Through the use of advanced algorithms, data preparation and easy user interfaces the method shows how intelligent systems could transform patient care and identify illnesses.

title: Hybrid Deep Learning and Gradient Boosting Framework for Early Detection of Pancreatic Cancer
authors: P Kisshore Raja, P Pradeepa, SA Priyadarsini
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: Pancreatic cancer remain one of the most lethal malignancies because of late detection and lack of reliable screening. This study propose a hybrid deep learning and gradient boosting framework for early detection from medical imaging data. The method integrates convolutional neural networks (CNN), VGG16 with transfer learning, and DenseNet architectures together with CatBoost trained on radiomic features. Medical scans, including CT, MRI and ultrasound, are preprocessed through normalization, contrast enhancement, and augmentation to improve model robustness. Deep features are extracted using CNN variants, while radiomic descriptors such as intensity and texture are passed to CatBoost for boosting-based classification. The outcomes of each model are compared using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, F1-score and AUC to assess diagnostic efficiency. Our experimental evaluation show that DenseNet achieved the best detection accuracy, while CatBoost demonstrated improved interpretability by handling tabular features. The framework also incorporate explainability through Grad-CAM visualization and segmentation support to highlight suspicious pancreatic regions. This hybrid design thus enable both high performance and interpretability, making it more suitable for clinical adoption. The proposed approach can be extended for large multi-institutional datasets in future to validate its reliability and generalization.

title: DHR-LLM: A Dynamic Heterogeneous Redundancy Framework for Resilient Large Language Model Inference
authors: Ping Chen, Zhuyang Yu, Shuting Zhang
publish: 2025 International Conference on Cyber Resilience and Endogenous Safety &amp; Security (CRESS)
abstract: We introduce Dynamic Heterogeneous Redundancy (DHR), an inference-time framework that improves the safety and security of large language model (LLM) systems by orchestrating a pool of heterogeneous models through dynamic trust adjustment. Unlike static single-model pipelines, DHR samples models according to confidence scores, aggregates predictions via majority voting, and updates each model’s confidence multiplicatively based on semantic agreement, thereby suppressing unreliable or compromised behaviors. We validate DHR on two representative tasks including SST-2 sentiment classification and BoolQ true/false question answering under both clean and adversarial settings. Even when partial models are compromised, DHR achieves strong resistance against attacks while maintaining competitive performance on clean inputs. Selection-frequency statistics show that attacked models are rapidly down-weighted and rarely sampled, demonstrating on-the-fly isolation without retraining or model internals. DHR is model-agnostic, and converts architectural/data diversity into a practical resilience mechanism for trustworthy LLM inference.

title: Simplified Geometric Inverse Kinematic Approach for Collaborative Robot
authors: Ivan Yu. Kholodilin, Yuilya S. Kuznetsova, Kristina M. Saranceva, Ksenia A. Egorova, Veronika A. Filimonova, Leonid A. Rzhavtsev
publish: 2026 ElCon Conference of Young Researchers in Computing &amp; Processing, and Information Security (ElCon-CP)
abstract: We present an object-sorting process using a collaborative robot, validated in both simulation and real-world environments. At the core of the system is a geometry-based inverse kinematics (IK) method developed for pick-and-place applications common in sorting tasks. Unlike conventional IK formulations that rely on matrix algebra and numerical solvers, our approach uses only elementary geometry and trigonometry, making it transparent, fast to compute, and accessible to learners with high-school-level math. Calculations are performed with Python that supports widely adopted open-source libraries. Similar code can be used with the simulator and the real robot, which allows rapid transfer from simulation to hardware. Experiments are performed with various sorting scenarios that demonstrates robust operation of the proposed IK approach. The full codebase, experiment scripts, and comprehensive documentation are publicly available. Together, these contributions provide a practical platform for education, prototyping, and research on industrially relevant sorting tasks. The paper code and supporting materials are available online: https://github.com/kholodilinivan/Cobot_IK.

title: Hyperspectral Imaging for Physiological Indices: Saving Bands, Maintaining Accuracy
authors: Margarita S. Shatkovskaya, Daniil Iu. Gonoshilkin, Nikita M. Sokolov, Dmitry R. Sharivzyanov
publish: 2026 ElCon Conference of Young Researchers in Computing &amp; Processing, and Information Security (ElCon-CP)
abstract: Problem: Clinical hyperspectral imaging (HSI) often relies on many wavelengths and heavy post-processing, which limits portability and speed.Method: We propose an index-specific band selection and robust ratio formulation for StO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>, THI, TWI and NIR-contrast. Each index is computed from two carefully placed spectral windows (around HbO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>/Hb or H<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>O features); each window is estimated by averaging a small set of narrow channels. The workflow includes white/black calibration, cube normalization, artifact suppression for maps, thresholds and combined ROIs.Validation: On ex-vivo pork/chicken the method separates fat, muscle, cartilage and skin; on in-vivo hand with short compression it reveals distal drops in StO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>/THI and NIR-contrast with near-stable TWI. Contribution. A band-reduction strategy per spectral range without sacrificing diagnostic content, plus tooling for tuning and reporting—paving the way to compact LED-based imagers.

title: Neurocharge: Neural-Network-Assisted Impedance Matching for Bidirectional Wireless Ev Charging
authors: S Anupriya, K. Malarkodi
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: Bi-directional wireless power transfer (WPT) of electric vehicles (EVs) has considerable issues with the impedance mismatch of coils misalignment, different distances, and load dynamic conditions. These problems decrease the power transfer efficiency and cause oscillations of voltages/current which restrict the performance of the system. The paper suggests NeuroCharge, which is a neural-networkaided impedance matching system allowing real-time adaptive control of the bidirectional EV charging. The model uses artificial neural network (ANN) to predict the best switching frequency in inverter and duty ratio using coil position datasets, misalignment offsets datasets, and electrical datasets. These forecasts are incorporated into a PI/PID control loop, with the stable work being ensured, as well as providing the maximum power transfer efficiency. Simulation experiments show that NeuroCharge is more efficient (up to 15 percent), with less voltage noise (more than 50 percent), and fast response times (less than 7 ms in total). The hybrid ANN + PI/PID model lies between manual adjustment and automatic control that is considered a scalable and robust model of dynamic charging conditions. NeuroCharge is the new generation of intelligent wireless EV charging, and it has a possible application on multicoil, multi-vehicle and dynamic real-time adaptive charging infrastructures. NeuroCharge allows cheap, clean, and efficient wireless EV charging through enhanced energy reliability and minimized wastage. Its adaptive control contributes to sustainable power consumption in the electric mobility. The infrastructure is enhanced in clean-energy and encourages transportation that is greener.

title: Simulated Channel State Information in Indoor Environments for Respiration Monitoring Using Sionna
authors: James Rhodes, Lawrence Ong, Duy T. Ngo
publish: 2025 11th International Workshop on Signal Design and Its Applications in Communications (IWSDA)
abstract: Channel State Information (CSI) has seen increasing utility in passive sensing applications such as respiration monitoring, fall detection, speed estimation, and gesture recognition. Unfortunately, datasets are scarce, and researchers are forced to repeat efforts to create their own ad hoc datasets. We present a simulation framework built on top of Nvidia's Sionna ray tracing framework, to automatically generate indoor environments and simulate the CSI measured from transmitter to receiver. This framework is purpose-built for researching respiration monitoring passively using CSI. The framework allows the placement of multiple people within the room with configurable respiratory rates and chest motion. All results are made deterministic, allowing exact repeatability, which is vital when comparing and evaluating the performance of sensing applications. This framework is applied to respiration monitoring, but it is easily extended into arbitrary motion. In this work, we apply the proposed simulation framework to validate four state-of-the-art CSI-based respiration monitoring techniques. We use it to compare their accuracy when the CSI measurements are subject to common noise sources and environmental interference from motion.

title: Silver Nano Particles Device Based Lightweight Cryptographic System Toward IoT Edge Security
authors: Hiroki Tabata, Yuma Yamashita, Yusuke Nakaoka, Oradee Srikimkaew, Yuki Usami, Shunsuke Araki, Hirofumi Tanaka
publish: 2025 11th International Workshop on Signal Design and Its Applications in Communications (IWSDA)
abstract: This study introduces a lightweight cryptographic system leveraging atomic switch based <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">${Ag}/{Ag}_{2} S$</tex> nanoparticle devices to enhance data security in resource-constrained IoT edge environments. Conventional cryptographic algorithms often require significant computational power and resources, making them unsuitable for such settings. To address this limitation, we propose a hardware-based encryption scheme that exploits the charge accumulation behavior and nonlinear electrical characteristics of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">${Ag}/{Ag}_{2} S$</tex> devices. Analog electrocardiogram (ECG) signals were directly encrypted through voltage input manipulation, while signal decryption was achieved using an Echo State Network model trained to reconstruct input signals from output voltage responses. The encrypted signals exhibited a randomness successfully passing 12 out of 15 statistical tests defined by the NIST SP 800-22 suite. Furthermore, original ECG signals were accurately recovered with a reconstruction rate of 91.0%. These resultss underscore the promise of nanomaterial-based physical encryption as a viable and energy-efficient solution for secure data handling in future IoT edge applications.

title: AI-Based Food Detection System with Freshness Analysis, Nutrition Estimation, and Personalized Diet Planning Using Full Stack and ML
authors: M. Mahendran, D. Pujith Abhinay, A. Pannaga Sai, K. Narendra
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: This research presents an intelligent nutrition-analysis and diet-recommendation system developed using a MERN-based backend and a React-based frontend. The system supports secure user registration, profile management, and personalized health data storage through MongoDB and Mongoose. It integrates several AI-driven modules, including food detection, food classification, nutritional information extraction, and image-based freshness assessment. A custom diet-generation engine uses user attributes—such as age, height, weight, gender, and medical conditions—to produce personalized dietary guidance. The frontend provides an adaptive, component-oriented interface with real-time previews and streamlined API-based processing, while the backend coordinates user management, data validation, and structured responses through secured REST endpoints. Together, these components demonstrate an efficient and scalable architecture for digital nutrition assessment and personalized diet planning suited for practical healthcare applications.

title: GrievanceGrid: AI System for Multilingual Civic Complaint Resolution
authors: R. Suganya, Sneha, Tanya Shukla, Lekhya Tallam, Janiya Maria Tomy
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: An automated platform powered by artificial intelligence aims to simplify citizen complaints handling efficiently and inclusively. Users can submit reports about problems like trash pickup, plumbing leaks, and cracked roads through simple text inputs, which are then analyzed by an advanced machine learning algorithm for efficient dispatching of requests to relevant departments. Users can input their grievances in various languages globally; these submissions are subsequently translated into English before being processed, thereby improving inclusivity among different linguistic communities. Crafted with ReactJS as its backbone and adorned by Tailwind CSS for styling, this user-friendly interface provides an elegant, responsive browsing environment featuring intuitive visual feedback mechanisms alongside a helpful virtual chat function. The backend infrastructure is driven by FastAPI for processing tasks, safeguarded through JWT token verification along with implementing role-dependent authorization mechanisms. The evaluation outcomes indicate remarkable precision in categorization, successful preservation of linguistic integrity, and substantial endorsement by users. The infrastructure can be expanded and modified to accommodate new civic technology initiatives in diverse linguistic environments and those utilizing mobile technologies exclusively.

title: Crafting Physical Adversarial Examples by Combining Differentiable and Physically Based Renders
authors: Yuqiu Liu, Huanqian Yan, Xiaopei Zhu, Xiaolin Hu, Liang Tang, Hang Su, Chen Lv
publish: IEEE/CAA Journal of Automatica Sinica
abstract: Recently, we have witnessed progress in hiding road vehicles against object detectors through adversarial camouflage in the digital world. The extension of this technique to the physical world is crucial for testing the robustness of autonomous driving systems. However, existing methods do not show good performances when applied to the physical world. This is partly due to insufficient photorealism in training examples, and lack of proper physical realization methods for camouflage. To generate a robust adversarial camouflage suitable for real vehicles, we propose a novel method called physical adversarial vehicle camouflage (PAV-Camou). We propose to adjust the mapping from the coordinates in the 2D map to those of corresponding 3D model. This process is critical for mitigating texture distortion and ensuring the camouflage's effectiveness when applied in the real world. Then we combine two renderers with different characteristics to obtain adversarial examples that are photorealistic and closely mimic real-world lighting and texture properties. The method ensures that the generated textures remain effective under diverse environmental conditions. Our adversarial camouflage can be optimized and printed in the form of 2D patterns, allowing for direct application on real vehicles. Extensive experiments demonstrated that our proposed method achieved good performance in both the digital world and the physical world.

title: A Deep Learning-Based Multi-Layer Anomaly Detection Framework for Mitigating Emerging IoT Cyber Threats
authors: G. Rekha, T. Sudha, Manjunath K N, N. Gayathri, S. Sathishkumar, Priyadharshini T. R
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: The expanding adoption of Internet of Things (IoT) technologies across critical and consumer infrastructures has introduced complex security challenges arising from heterogeneous devices, massive traffic volumes, and continuously evolving cyber threats. Conventional intrusion detection systems and static machine learning approaches are often inadequate for identifying sophisticated and previously unseen attacks due to limited feature representation and poor adaptability to dynamic traffic behaviors. To address these challenges, this work proposes a deep learning-driven multi-layer anomaly detection framework that distributes intelligence across device, edge, and cloud layers to achieve both efficient and accurate threat mitigation in IoT environments. Lightweight models deployed at the device layer perform rapid preliminary screening, while hybrid CNN-LSTM/GRU architectures at the edge layer capture spatial and temporal traffic dependencies for contextual analysis. For complex and ambiguous attack patterns, a high-capacity ConvLSTM model augmented with an attention mechanism operates at the cloud layer to extract long-term temporal correlations and contextual relevance. A weighted fusion strategy integrates anomaly scores from all layers, enabling refined decision-making and reducing false alarms. The framework was extensively evaluated using contemporary IoT security datasets, including TON_IoT, IoT-23, and N-BaIoT. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed multi-layer fusion model achieves an overall detection accuracy of 99.1%, with precision, recall, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{F 1}$</tex>-score values of 98.8%, 98.5%, and 98.6%, respectively, outperforming traditional machine learning classifiers and single-layer deep learning models by a margin of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{4}-\mathbf{1 2} \boldsymbol{\%}$</tex>. Furthermore, the hierarchical design significantly reduces false positive rates and computational overhead, making the framework suitable for real-time and large-scale IoT deployments. These findings highlight the effectiveness of distributed spatial-temporal learning and cross-layer fusion in strengthening the resilience of IoT ecosystems against emerging and zero-day cyber threats.

title: Development of an optical Diffraction System for Detecting Defects on the Surface of a Railway Wheel During Movement
authors: Kseniya G. Arinushkina, Pavel I. Tsomaev
publish: 2026 ElCon Conference of Young Researchers in Computing &amp; Processing, and Information Security (ElCon-CP)
abstract: An optical diffraction system has been developed to control the deviation of the parameters of railway wheels from the standard, which eliminates the shortcomings in the systems used. What is new in the developed system is the arrangement of emitting laser diodes and systems for recording reflected diffraction images based on a CCD matrix. A new technique is proposed for processing recorded optical images in laser radiation reflected from the surface of the wheel. To improve the efficiency of image filtering, it uses artificial intelligence (in terms of filtering, rotations, reflections, shifts, thinning, restoring gaps according to the reference, recalculating coordinates and calculating wheel parameters). This increases the reliability of the control results, which is confirmed by experimental control measurements of the rolling surface of the wheels and their condition using a stationary visual examination and a manual metrological instrument.

title: Multimodal Smart Wheelchair Control Using Voice and Head Gestures: an Embedded Human-Computer Interaction Approach
authors: Koutarapu Padma, Mahender Reddy Bobbala, Mehveen Mehdi Khatoon, Dayakar Kondamudi, Mallareddy Adudhodla, Pavan Kumar Reddy Manellore
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: The Voice and Accelerometer Controlled Smart Wheelchair, developed using embedded systems and powered by the Arduino Uno, presents an innovative and cost-effective solution in the domain of assistive technology. Designed to enhance the independence of individuals with physical disabilities, the system leverages a multimodal interface comprising voice commands and head-gesture recognition to enable intuitive, hands-free mobility. A voice recognition module interprets verbal commands such as “forward,” “backward,” “left,” and “right,” while a 3 -axis accelerometer detects head tilt angles, allowing users with speech impairments to control movement through gestures. This sensor fusion approach ensures robust interaction, improving accessibility, user satisfaction, and operational safety. By integrating real-time input processing and motor control through the Arduino Uno, the system offers a practical and scalable application of human-computer interaction (HCI) principles. Its affordability, reliability, and ease of deployment make it suitable for both personal and institutional healthcare environments. This work demonstrates the potential of multimodal, user-centered HCI systems to address real-world mobility challenges and enhance quality of life for users with impairments.

title: Double Skew Cyclic Codes Over $\mathbb{Z}_{4}+u \mathbb{Z}_{4}$
authors: Saumya Shah, Amit Sharma
publish: 2025 11th International Workshop on Signal Design and Its Applications in Communications (IWSDA)
abstract: This paper investigates double skew cyclic codes of length <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\alpha+\beta$</tex> (denoted by <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\alpha, \beta)$</tex>) over <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbb{Z}_{4}+u \mathbb{Z}_{4}$</tex>, where <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$u^{2}=1$</tex>, under the automorphism <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\theta(u)=u+2$</tex>. We determine the generators of these codes for arbitrary lengths and their minimal generating sets when both <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\alpha$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\beta$</tex> are odd. Also, a Gray map is defined to obtain linear codes over <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbb{Z}_{4}$</tex>. Several new codes are presented as examples resulting from this construction. In addition, we study the duality properties of such codes and explicitly determine their duals for a particular case when <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\alpha$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\beta$</tex> are both even.

title: AIR-VISTA: A Hybrid Fourier-TCN-GLU Framework with Real-Time Dashboard for PM2.5 Pollution Forecasting and Environmental Insights
authors: Deepshika Anandaraman, Gayathri Ganesh, Mukhila Rajamani, S. Pavithra
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: Air pollution remains a critical concern for both public health and environmental sustainability, with PM2.5 (particulate matter <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\leq 2.5 \mu \mathrm{m}$</tex>) posing the highest risk due to its ability to penetrate deep into the human respiratory and circulatory systems. Most air quality forecasting systems suffer from limitations including static modeling, coarse temporal resolution, not using modern sequence learning methods and lack of live feed interactive visualization. In response to these limitations, the goal of this paper is to present an air quality forecasting system that utilizes a novel hybrid deep learning framework, called AIR-VISTA, that provides real-time and web-based interactive visualization of predicted PM2.5 in urban areas. The systems central core is powered by a Fourier-TCN-GLU framework that includes (a) Fast Fourier Transform to denoise signals, (b) Temporal Convolutional Networks (TCN) to capture long-range dependencies and (c) Gated Linear Units (GLU) provide gating that selectively uses different feature representation. This hybrid model offers improved robustness and predictive accuracy over traditional RNN and LSTM-based models. The system is trained and validated using historical PM2.5 and meteorological data sourced from public APIs and government datasets. Designed with Dash and Flask, the AIR-VISTA dashboard offers users real-time visualizations of current and projected air quality, confidence intervals, and environmental correlations, such as temperature and humidity. The system has features to select city level, trend comparisons, and archiving data, with the intent to facilitate citizen engagement and inform policy. The platform also has scalable backend support to support data updates through APIs and model retraining. This project has the potential to bridge cutting-edge AI theory and practice with environmental informatics by providing a transparent, scalable, and actionable framework for mitigating exposure to air pollution and making informed decisions.

title: LMI Optimization Based Multirate Steady-State Kalman Filter Design
authors: Hiroshi Okajima
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: This paper presents an LMI-based design framework for multirate steady-state Kalman filters in systems with sensors operating at different sampling rates. The multirate system is formulated as a periodic time-varying system, where the Kalman gains converge to periodic steady-state values that repeat every frame period. Cyclic reformulation transforms this into a time-invariant problem; however, the resulting measurement noise covariance becomes semidefinite rather than positive definite, preventing direct application of standard Riccati equation methods. I address this through a dual LQR formulation with LMI optimization that naturally handles semidefinite covariances. The framework enables multi-objective design, supporting pole placement for guaranteed convergence rates and l2-induced norm constraints for balancing average and worst-case performance. Numerical validation using an automotive navigation system with GPS and wheel speed sensors, including Monte Carlo simulation with 500 independent noise realizations, demonstrates that the proposed filter achieves a position RMSE well below the GPS noise level through effective multirate sensor fusion, and that the LMI solution provides valid upper bounds on the estimation error covariance.

title: Offset Gradient Descent Bit-Flipping Decoding for Irregular Parity-Check Codes
authors: Woohong Min, Kyeongcheol Yang
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: A key challenge for decoding irregular parity-check codes under the gradient descent bit-flipping (GDBF) algorithm is that the irregularity in column degrees reduces the accuracy of the reliability measurements obtained through a flipping function (FF). To address the challenge, we introduce a novel approach based on the gradient descent bit-flipping with momentum (GDBF-w/M). We first propose an offset GDBF-w/M (OGDBF-w/M) algorithm for decoding column-irregular codes over the additive white Gaussian noise channel, where the FF includes an offset term depending on column degrees.We then propose a diversity-based offset GDBF-w/M (D-OGDBF-w/M) algorithm which employs multiple OGDBF-w/M decoders with distinct offset values. Furthermore, we extend the proposed algorithms to the binary symmetric channel, although they are initially intended for the additive white Gaussian noise channel. Numerical results demonstrate that they achieve significant improvements in decoding performance for irregular parity-check codes, particularly for column-irregular codes.

title: Enhanced Corrosion Resistance and Aesthetic Properties of Titanium Alloys through Stabilized Anodizing Processes: A Review
authors: Adavayya Sangayya Chikkamath, Vinod Kumar V Meti, I G Siddhalingeshwar, M A Umarfarooq, N R Banapurmath, Ashok M Sajjan, Kiran Shahapurkar, Tabrej Khan, Tamer A. Sebaey, Manikandan Murugaiah, Balbir Singh
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: This review comprehensively examines the advancements in anodizing processes applied to titanium and its alloys, emphasizing the enhancement of corrosion resistance, surface aesthetics, and functional performance. The study critically evaluates the influence of key process parameters – such as electrolyte composition, temperature, voltage and anodizing duration on the formation, morphology, and crystallinity of anodic oxide layers. Conventional anodizing in sulfuric acid electrolytes is compared withemerging green alternatives, namely deep eutectic solvents (DES), which offer environmentally benign, cost-effective and tuneable electrochemical environments. Results from recent studies reveal that the anodic current density, oxide thickness, and surface colouration are strongly dependent on applied voltage and electrolyte temperature, while DES-based anodizing enables precise control over oxide layer structure and porosity. Morphological and structural analysis indicate the formation of both amorphous and crystalline TiO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> phases, whose characteristics can be altered for specific applications. The review further highlights the role of stabilized anodizing in producing durable, chromatically varied, and corrosion resistant coatings suitable for aerospace, biomedical, and defence applications. Finally, potential research directions are proposed to advance sustainable anodizing technologies and deepen understanding of parameter-property relationships in titanium surface engineering.

title: Accuracy of a Multiplexed Piezoresistive Mat Sensor in Dynamic Center of Pressure Estimation During Simulated Chest Compressions
authors: Jonas Rossavik, Brage Bartnes, Adrian B. Bjørnøy, Eveline Stokkeland, Torjus L. Steffensen, Marius Auflem, Charlotte B. Ingul, Martin Steinert
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Pressure-sensitive mats (PSM) made from multiplexed piezoresistive arrays are used in various load scenarios where thin, flexible pressure sensor arrays are needed. However, their performance in estimating center of pressure (CoP) on compliant, organic surfaces like the human body is not deeply quantified in literature. Accurately locating compression position during simulated cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR) is one such case. This information is desirable in training and research scenarios. To assess usability of a piezoresistive PSM, the study evaluated a widely adopted design with a 20mmsensor grid resolution in three simulated CPR scenarios of increasing complexity: a flat surface, a simulated human torso, and a healthy volunteer. CoP was estimated from light calculations. For the flat surface and simulated torso, calculated CoP was compared to CNC coordinates. 882 compressions were performed at 441 grid points. MAEx for the flat surface was 1.48 mm and MAEy was 3.08 mm, giving a MAE of 3.41 mm. For the simulated torso, MAEx = 7.58 mm, MAEy = 3.78 mm, MAE = 8.47 mm. Repeated light compressions on the same site of a healthy volunteer showed a mean standard deviation of 8.02 mm. The PSM was able to determine the center of a dynamic pressure load in both simulated and real torso, though with less precision than on the flat surface. These findings demonstrate the feasibility of using flexible PSMs for CoP estimation on organic surfaces with potential to improve feedback systems in simulated CPR.

title: VistaDepth: Improving Far-Range Depth Estimation With Spectral Modulation and Adaptive Reweighting_supp1-3679288.mp4
authors: Li Zhang
publish: N/A
abstract: Monocular depth estimation infers per-pixel depth from a single RGB image. It remains particularly challenging in far-range regions, where sparse observations and long-tailed depth distributions bias learning toward near-range content. Diffusion models offer a promising alternative to discriminative foundation models by leveraging rich generative priors for zero-shot generalization. However, existing methods still struggle to recover fine distant structures under weak supervision and limited image evidence. To address this, we propose VistaDepth, a diffusion framework for far-range depth estimation. It improves far-range prediction through two complementary mechanisms: Latent Frequency Modulation (LFM), which refines latent features via dynamic, content-aware spectral filtering, and BiasMap, which adaptively reweights the diffusion objective toward distant, structurally informative regions while remaining aligned with denoising. Extensive experiments on five benchmarks show that VistaDepth achieves state-of-the-art performance among diffusion-based methods. Despite training on orders of magnitude less data than discriminative baselines, it remains competitive globally and excels in challenging far-range regions.

title: Quantifying Uncertainty in Spectral Leaf Chlorophyll Content Retrieval: A Propagation Analysis
authors: M. Werfeli, D. Ganeva, J. L. García-Soria, L. Mihai, L. Filchev, E. Roumenina, J. Verrelst, A. Hueni
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Reliable estimation of leaf chlorophyll content (LCC) from spectral measurements requires explicit quantification of measurement and model uncertainties. This study presents an end-to-end uncertainty propagation analysis for leaf-level LCC retrieval using the Automated Radiative Transfer Models Operator (ARTMO). A controlled laboratory dataset of winter wheat leaves was acquired with an ASD FieldSpec 4 spectroradiometer and a CCM-300 chlorophyll meter. Measurement uncertainties from sensor noise, re-clipping effects and reference-panel characterisation were quantified and propagated using the Community Metrology (CoMet) toolkit. Two machine-learning regression algorithms-Gaussian Process Regression (GPR) and Kernel Ridge Regression (KRR)-were trained using principal component analysis for dimensionality reduction, and epistemic uncertainties were estimated analytically (GPR) or by bootstrapping (KRR). Results demonstrate that epistemic uncertainty dominates total uncertainty, while the contribution of measurement uncertainty remains comparatively small (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\approx 0.2\%$</tex-math></inline-formula> for reflectance; <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\approx 5\%$</tex-math></inline-formula> for chlorophyll meter readings). GPR produced smooth and coherent uncertainty estimates, whereas KRR exhibited higher sensitivity to sampling effects. The workflow provides reproducible uncertainty budgets and correlation structures, and is transferable to canopy- or satellite-based retrievals, supporting metrologically consistent uncertainty propagation in forthcoming hyperspectral missions.

title: Recognition of 2025 Transactions and Magazine Paper Reviewers

title: SceneLinker: Compositional 3D Scene Generation via Semantic Scene Graph from RGB Sequences
authors: Seok-Young Kim, Dooyoung Kim, Woojin Cho, Hail Song, Suji Kang, Woontack Woo
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: We introduce SceneLinker, a novel framework that generates compositional 3D scenes via semantic scene graph from RGB sequences. To adaptively experience Mixed Reality (MR) content based on each user's space, it is essential to generate a 3D scene that reflects the real-world layout by compactly capturing the semantic cues of the surroundings. Prior works struggled to fully capture the contextual relationship between objects or mainly focused on synthesizing diverse shapes, making it challenging to generate 3D scenes aligned with object arrangements. We address these challenges by designing a graph network with cross-check feature attention for scene graph prediction and constructing a graph-variational autoencoder (graph-VAE), which consists of a joint shape and layout block for 3D scene generation. Experiments on the 3RScan/3DSSG and SG-FRONT datasets demonstrate that our approach outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both quantitative and qualitative evaluations, even in complex indoor environments and under challenging scene graph constraints. Our work enables users to generate consistent 3D spaces from their physical environments via scene graphs, allowing them to create spatial MR content. Project page is <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://scenelinker2026.github.io</uri>.

title: Fast Human-Robot Handover using a Vision-Based Pipeline
authors: Enrico Turco, Chiara Castellani, Gionata Salvietti, Maria Pozzi
publish: IEEE Robotics and Automation Practice
abstract: In this paper, we present a fast and robust vision-based pipeline for human-to-robot object handovers. The perception module fuses data from two external RGB-D cameras to estimate the 3D pose of the human hand and reconstruct a multi-view point cloud of the object. From this, an axis-aligned 3D bounding box is extracted and used by a geometry-based grasp planner that computes the grasp pose based on the user's hand and object size. The robot executes the grasp in a fast and reactive way using an event-driven velocity controller. The system has been validated in three real-world experiments: a user study that follows the CORSMAL benchmark, an evaluation on additional objects, and a cross-platform deployment on a different robotic arm. The proposed pipeline achieves high grasp success rates, low placement errors, and short execution times, outperforming existing methods.

title: A BiLSTM-Based Framework for Sleep Spindle and K-complex Detection with Enhanced Segment-Level Learning
authors: Tihua Hu, Zhiya Wang, Jia Liu, Tian Yang, Wei Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Biomedical Engineering
abstract: Sleep spindles and K-complexes in electroencephalo gram (EEG) signals are brief yet physiologically essential signatures of the sleeping brain, supporting memory consolidation, arousal regulation, and neurological assessment. However, existing detection methods often face severe class imbalance, temporal inconsistencies, and uncertain event boundaries. We proposed a unified, physiologically informed framework that integrates data preprocessing, multimodal feature extraction, temporal modeling, and event-level refinement. Context-aware negative filtering and segment-level oversampling effectively alleviated severe class imbalance; multimodal feature combination captured both spectral and morphological cues; a Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory (BiLSTM) backbone enhanced by segment level dropout and bidirectional hard example mining improves robustness under imbalance; and a physiologically plausible post-processing enforced temporal coherence on the predicted sequences. Evaluations on the publicly available polysomnog raphy datasets DREAMS and MASS demonstrated consistent 3–10% F1-score improvements over state-of-the-art baselines. The proposed framework attained event-level F1-scores of 0.796 (sleep spindles on DREAMS), 0.938 (K-complexes on DREAMS), 0.882 (sleep spindles on MASS), and 0.903 (K-complexes on MASS), substantially outperforming recent deep-learning-based detectors.

title: SACM: SEEG-Audio Contrastive Matching for Chinese Speech Decoding_supp1-3678858.pdf
authors: Dongrui Wu
publish: N/A
abstract: Objective: Speech disorders such as dysarthria and anarthria can severely impair patients' ability to communicate verbally. Speech decoding brain-computer interfaces (BCIs) offer a potential alternative by directly translating speech intentions into spoken words, serving as speech neuroprostheses. Methods: This paper reports an experimental protocol for Mandarin Chinese speech decoding BCIs and proposes a contrastive learning-based decoding algorithm termed SEEG and Audio Contrastive Matching (SACM). Stereo-electroencephalography (SEEG) and synchronized audio data were collected from ten patients with drug-resistant epilepsy as they performed a word-level reading task. SACM leverages the cross-modal correlation between neural activity and audio signals to decode matched speech segments. Results: The proposed framework achieved accuracies significantly exceeding random matching in both isolated-word and continuous speech decoding tasks, and outperformed SEEG-only baselines across seven backbone architectures in the isolated-word setting. Electrode- wise analysis revealed that a single ventral sensorimotor cortex electrode achieved performance comparable to that of the full electrode array. Our code is publicly available. Significance: To our knowledge, this is the first work on multimodal decoding for tonal speech BCIs.

title: Benchmarking Large Language Models for Automated Code Changes: A Systematic Review
authors: Padamjit Sandhu, Pankaj Khurana, Jimmy Singla
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have been employed more and more to automate code changes (ACC) for a range of development projects, such as enhancements in features, refining and error remediation. Recently, a number of LLM based automate code change paradigms have been created and reviewed. Although LLMs in ACC tasks have been studied in past research, this domain still needs to evaluate uniform procedures, a standardized dataset and reducibility challenges. Therefore, this study provides a systematic analysis of existing literature on LLM based ACC tasks to examine the core data sets, frequently employed assessment metrics and persistent reducibility issues. Findings of this review study revealed that the most frequently used dataset to train an LLM model for code change is Defects4J. Similarly, repair success rate, pass@k and patch accuracy were the main evaluation measured utilized in the articles to evaluate the performance of the developed model. Moreover, this study also examined various reproducibility challenges that can be faced when recreating a similar ACC model. These results open the door to more investigation into how to fully utilized LLMs to transform automated code changes.

title: “MelDetect”: A Lightweight and Interpretable Model for Melanoma Classification from Dermoscopic Images
authors: S. Khaja Khizar, B. Mathiarasi, V. Lokeswara Reddy
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: Accurate and early detection of melanoma is crucial for reducing the skin cancer mortality. So as to provide suitable treatment in advance, such early detection and classification of skin cancer type is mandatory where the emergence of deep learning models are of good scope. This paper proposes one such approach called “MelDetect”- a lightweight and interpretable deep learning model for melanoma detection from dermascopic images. With the implementation of an efficient CNN backbone -EfficientNetV2, the framework is used to classify dermoscopic images into 3 class types like melanoma, Nevus, and other groups like BCC, AKIEC, and so on. Additionally, the MelDetect module is employed with Aquila Optimizer, a nature-inspired metaheuristic algorithm for improving the melanoma classification process. With the use of high-resolution input from ISIC 2019 challenge dataset, the proposed approach eliminates the use of segmentation module for end-to-end learning. The experimental results demonstrate strong classification performance while maintaining a low computational footprint making it suited for real-time or mobile deployment in low-resource clinical environments. The proposed MelDetect model achieves a precision of 0.92, recall of 0.83, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$F 1$</tex>-score of 0.87 in the melanoma category. In analysis of proposed technique with other approaches like CNN+GWO, ANFIS+FFNN, SNC_Net, and so on,the proposed classification technique demonstrates superior performance with 95% of accuracy, precision of 94%, and 93% F1-score. This approach overall acts as a clinical decision-support tool for assisting dermatologists in melanoma screening from dermoscopic images.

title: Smart Secure Event Management and Recommendation System Using Machine Learning Algorithm
authors: I. Vasudevan, P. Saiteja, K.Deepesh Kumar
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: The extensive application of machine learning is being employed to improve user experiences, applicable to event management systems in universities and clubs. The Existed methods are limited to simple event lists and it lacks personalized sorting and does not meet the requirement of individual user. This approach was made with the help of K-Means clustering and content-based filtering to explore user interests and history of previous events. It comprises secure <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\log$</tex>-in, automatic registration, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{E}$</tex> mail notification, and a dashboard. The real user activity was based on predefined filters, but the system gives more realistic content based on previous activity, and it reduces the manual effect. Tests indicate that it provides more relevant output and the event experience is seamless, achieving an accuracy of 96.8%.

title: PSO-based Illumination Normalization for Face Recognition
authors: Amanuel Zinabu Siraj, Abdullah Mohan
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: Face recognition system performance is greatly affected by light fluctuations on the face across different perspectives. These variations must be minimized to improve recognition accuracy. This work presents a new normalization of an adaptive illumination method for facial recognition from different angles of light. The goal is to create a strong approach that compensates for uncontrolled light illuminations on the face. Normalization is applied to selected DCT coefficients at low frequencies, chosen in an adaptable manner based on their alteration due to lighting. A non-linear modifier is applied to these coefficients, also derived from the image's illumination variation. The method is adaptive, selecting only significant coefficients for modification, reducing computation in contrast to other advanced techniques. K-nearest neighbor (KNN) classifier is used for classification, with parameter tuning via particle swarm optimization (PSO). The approach achieves 100% accuracy on CMU PIE, 2.41% error on YALE (with 3 training images), and 0.4 % error on AR using only illumination images. Results show the proposed method significantly outperforms existing illumination normalization techniques.

title: Lightweight AI-Based Battery Health Prediction for Electric Vehicles Using Temporal Attention and Multi-Feature Data
authors: Chinmay Parikh, Rajakumar K, Arjun Sreedhar, Shashwat Chaudhary, Sai Bhargav Kasetty
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: The rapid adoption of EVs has made lithium-ion batteries the cornerstone of eco-friendly transportation. However, the highly accurate estimation of the SOH and RUL is crucial to their lifespan and performance. The use of state-of-the-art conventional methods based on deep learning and physics-based models has been restricted to real-time BMS because they either require intricate calculations or are not interpretable, respectively. This study describes the creation of a lightweight temporal degradation attention network that takes into account multiple features from the NASA battery degradation dataset in order to predict SOH and RUL in an effective and comprehensible manner. It has a Random Forest regression layer for prediction and a temporal attention encoder for identifying critical cycles in degradation. Temperature statistics, voltage range, capacity fade, and discharge and charge times are important features. High predictive performances are shown by <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{R 2} \boldsymbol{=} \mathbf{0. 9 9 8}$</tex> for both SOH and RUL, with MAEs of 0.006 and 7.13 for each, according to experimental evaluation using Group K-Fold cross-validation. The accuracy, interpretability, and computational efficiency of the suggested model are all balanced. It is therefore appropriate for embedded applications in EV BMS. Generally speaking, the TDAN + Random Forest hybrid framework allows for real-time, explicable battery health prediction by bridging the gap between data-intensive deep learning and lightweight machine learning.

title: Challenges in Designing Music Recommendation Systems
authors: Nikita S. Churilov, Iana S. Petrova
publish: 2026 ElCon Conference of Young Researchers in Computing &amp; Processing, and Information Security (ElCon-CP)
abstract: High-quality music recommendations play a crucial role in user retention and the monetization of streaming services. This paper examines the typical challenges in designing recommendation systems and the approaches to address them. It identifies the key tasks that arise at launch, including choosing an initial architecture, collecting data, and handling cold start and data sparsity, and during ongoing operation, including storing large volumes of data, shifts in user preferences, and long-term behavior. Industry-used solutions were analyzed regarding implementation complexity, adaptability to user context, and the quality of the recommendations. The study highlights solutions that offer the best overall balance, delivering high recommendation quality with moderate resource costs. Finally, practical guidance on selecting strategies was provided to overcome common challenges in developing music recommendation systems, and integrating user mood analysis into the recommendation architecture was proposed.

title: Are There Manufacturer Differences in Hard-Drive Reliability?_supp1-3679404.pdf
authors: Christoph Siemroth
publish: N/A
abstract: Based on the Backblaze hard disk drive (HDD) dataset, we analyze whether the four major HDD manufacturers represented in the dataset—HGST, Seagate, Toshiba, Western Digital (WD)—show differences in short- to medium-term HDD failure rates. Using two different duration regression models, we find—holding constant drive age, capacity, form-factor, and drive temperature—that Toshiba's failure rate is slightly above Seagate's. HGST HDD failure rates are the lowest, about 41% of Seagate's. WD HDD failure rates are significantly above HGST's, but still only about 52% of Seagate's. We also document the effects of age, capacity, temperature and drive location on failure rates.

title: A Dual-Mode Online BISR Architecture for Interconnect and Memory Repair in Chiplet-Based Systems
authors: Donghyun Han, Sooryeong Lee, Jongho Park, Dayoung Kim, Seungtae Kim, Youngkwang Lee, Sungho Kang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Reliability
abstract: Chiplet-based systems provide a scalable platform for heterogeneous integration, but permanent faults in interconnects and memory cells challenge their reliability. While standards like Universal Chiplet Interconnect Express (UCIe) employ single-domain system Error Correction Code (ECC) simplify architecture, they often limit fault isolation and recovery. This paper proposes a dual-mode Online Built-In Self-Repair (OBISR) architecture for unified detection and repair of both interconnect and memory faults during post-bond testing and in-field operation. Unlike conventional methods that merely expand ECC capacity, the OBISR targets the root causes of persistent errors by repurposing existing Content-Addressable Memory (CAM) and pathfinding logic. A hierarchical CAM structure classifies faults using spatial and recurrence patterns of ECC metadata to distinguish permanent defects from transients. Memory faults are masked via logical redirection, while interconnect faults are rerouted, both operating outside the datapath to ensure zero performance degradation. Evaluations demonstrate that the OBISR improves system-level resilience by 235.49% and reduces hardware area by 16.69% compared to conventional schemes. Its integrated dual-domain and dual-mode capabilities offer a robust, scalable solution for next-generation chiplet reliability.

title: Following the Herd or the Bellwether: Peer Effects in Firms' AI Adoption
authors: Siyu Shao, Jianjun Yang, Ling Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Engineering Management
abstract: Imitation and competition among peer firms serve as critical drivers of technology adoption and diffusion. Drawing on competitive dynamics and institutional theory, this study investigates the heterogeneous influence of peer firms on a focal firm's AI adoption by distinguishing between the peer group (the set of industry peers) and the peer leader (the most advanced AI adopter among industry peers). We use a panel dataset of publicly listed Chinese firms from 2012 to 2023 and estimate fixed-effects regression models to test the proposed hypotheses. The results show that the AI adoption levels of the peer group and peer leader positively influence the focal firm's AI adoption level, with the influence of the peer group being stronger. Industry digital maturity enhances the impact of the peer group while it weakens the effect of the peer leader. Chief Information Officer (CIO) presence strengthens the influence of the peer group and the peer leader, with the influence of the peer leader being more pronounced. In addition, firms with a high market position tend to imitate the peer leader, whereas firms in middle and low positions are more likely to follow the peer group. Multiple robustness checks confirm the validity of these findings. This study deepens the understanding of AI adoption antecedents and provides a more fine-grained perspective on peer effects, offering actionable insights for policymakers and engineering managers.

title: Quantum Intuition XR: Tangible Quantum Mechanics Using Interactive XR Experience
authors: Jamie Ngoc Dinh, Marven Wong, Matthew Brooks, Charles Tahan, Myungin Lee
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: Understanding quantum mechanics is inherently challenging due to its counterintuitive principles. Quantum Intuition XR is an interactive, extended reality (XR) experience designed to make quantum concepts tangible. Our system visualizes core principles of quantum computing, including qubits, superposition, entanglement, and measurement, through immersive interaction. Using a Mixed Reality headset, users engage with floating qubits, manipulate their states via controllers, and observe entanglement dynamics through real-time audiovisual feedback. A key feature of our implementation is the mathematically accurate and dynamic representation of qubits, both individually and while interacting with each other. The visualization of the qubit states evolve—rotate, shrink, grow, entangle—depending on their actual quantum states, which depend on variables such as proximity to other qubits and user interaction. Technical validation, along with preliminary demonstrations and feedback from quantum specialists, indicate that the system accurately represents quantum phenomena, suggesting strong potential to educate and enhance quantum intuition for non-expert audiences. A user study with non-experts further supports the system's accessibility and effectiveness in facilitating intuitive understanding of key quantum concepts. This approach bridges abstract quantum mechanics with embodied learning, offering an intuitive and accessible way for users to explore quantum phenomena. Future work will focus on expanding multi-user interactions and refining the fidelity of quantum state visualizations.

title: DreamLifting : A Plug-In Module Lifting MV Diffusion Models for 3D Asset Generation
authors: Ze-Xin Yin, Jiaxiong Qiu, Liu Liu, Xinjie Wang, Wei Sui, Zhizhong Su, Jian Yang, Jin Xie
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: The labor- and experience-intensive creation of 3D assets with physically based rendering (PBR) materials demands an autonomous 3D asset creation pipeline. However, most existing 3D generation methods focus on geometry modeling, either baking textures into simple vertex colors or leaving texture synthesis to post-processing with image diffusion models. To achieve end-to-end PBR-ready 3D asset generation, we present Lightweight Gaussian Asset Adapter (LGAA), a novel framework that unifies the modeling of geometry and PBR materials by exploiting multi-view (MV) diffusion priors from a novel perspective. The LGAA features a modular design with three components. Specifically, the LGAA Wrapper reuses and adapts network layers from MV diffusion models, which encapsulate knowledge acquired from billions of images, enabling better convergence in a data-efficient manner. To incorporate multiple diffusion priors for geometry and PBR synthesis, the LGAA Switcher aligns multiple LGAA Wrapper layers encapsulating different knowledge. Then, a tamed variational autoencoder (VAE), termed LGAA Decoder, is designed to predict 2D Gaussian Splatting (2DGS) with PBR channels. Finally, we introduce a dedicated post-processing procedure to effectively extract high-quality, relightable mesh assets from the resulting 2DGS. Extensive quantitative and qualitative experiments demonstrate the superior performance of LGAA with both text- and image-conditioned MV diffusion models. Additionally, the modular design enables flexible incorporation of multiple diffusion priors, and the knowledge-preserving scheme effectively preseves the 2D priors learned on massive image dataset, which leads to data efficient finetuning to lift the MV diffuison models for 3D generation with merely 69k multi-view instances. Our code, pre-trained weights, and the dataset used will be publicly available via our project page: https://zx-yin.github.io/dreamlifting/.

authors: Hang Yu, Qian Liu, Witold Pedrycz, Jie Lu

title: Investigation on Mechanism of Water Solubility in Mineral Oil: Measurement and Modeling_supp1-3679659.docx
authors: Nan Liu
publish: N/A
abstract: Water solubility in insulation oils is a critical parameter determining the lifespan of oil-immersed transformers, as it influences the aging rate, dielectric strength, etc. Despite extensive research, the lack of a quantitative model has left the underlying mechanisms poorly understood. In this paper, we proposed a quantitative model, requiring only the molecular structure as input, to analyze the water solubility. Furthermore, the effects of molecular structure and temperature were discussed in detail. First, experimental data reveal that the water saturation (Sw) of mineral oil increases exponentially with temperature. Our model successfully reproduces the results with an average deviation of 10.14%. Further investigation revealed that the electrostatic potential distributions (ESPs) of mineral oil molecules are unable to alleviate the electrostatic misfit of water molecules. Consequently, water molecules tend to self-aggregate rather than disperse in mineral oil, resulting in the high hydrophobicity. Based on the model, we quantified the changes in excess properties that are responsible for the temperature dependence of water solubility, and revealed the main reason for increased water solubility in mineral oil at higher temperatures. Finally, the vapor-liquid equilibrium curves were calculated and used to develop an empirical model. If the relative humidity of the air inside the transformer can be accurately measured, the water content in mineral oil can be quickly estimated. Additionally, utilizing the temperature mapping relationship from the empirical model, the water distribution in a transformer was determined through finite element simulation.

title: Corporate Governance Drivers and Mitigation Strategies for Internal Control Failure: Evidence and Early-Warning Screening via L2-Regularized Logistic Regression
