title: A 76-Gb/s DSP-DAC-Based Discrete Multitone Wireline Transmitter in 5-nm FinFET
authors: Seoyoung Jang, Marcel Kossel, Jaewon Lee, Matthias Brändli, Thomas Morf, Yujin Choi, Donggeon Kim, Gayoung Kang, Yoonji Choi, Pier-Andrea Francese, Gain Kim
publish: IEEE Journal of Solid-State Circuits
abstract: This article presents a 76-Gb/s digital-to-analog converter (DAC)-based discrete multitone (DMT) wireline transmitter (TX) fabricated in 5-nm FinFET. The TX employs a 16-way parallel multi-path delay feedback (MDF) inverse fast Fourier transform (IFFT) processor for area-efficient implementation. The TX digital signal processor (DSP) includes on-chip bit/power-loading and cyclic-prefix (CP) insertion logic and supports 4- to 256-QAM modulation formats across 31 orthogonal subchannels. The time-domain DMT samples generated by the TX DSP are converted into analog waveform using a source-series termination (SST)-based DAC. The prototype is demonstrated over a channel with 9.7-dB insertion loss (IL) at Nyquist, achieving a bit error rate (BER) of 2.1E-4 with a total power consumption of 144 mW from 0.735-V digital and 0.725-V analog supplies, resulting in an energy efficiency of 1.89 pJ/b. The proposed DMT TX improves bandwidth efficiency and signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) over conventional pulse-amplitude modulation (PAM)-based transmitters by employing frequency-domain modulation and equalization. This work is the first demonstration of a DAC-based DMT TX operating at >76 Gb/s fabricated in advanced CMOS technology.

title: Active power regulation of wind turbine based on deep reinforcement learning guided active disturbance rejection control
authors: Xuehan Li, Peng Chen, Wei Wang, Guorui Ren, Fang Fang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Sustainable Energy
abstract: Enhancing the active power regulation ability of wind turbine is a crucial approach to improving the flexibility of new power systems. This paper proposes a coordinated optimization strategy for reinforcement learning (RL) guided error-based active disturbance rejection controller (EADRC). Firstly, a well-established EADRC framework is employed, in which an extended state observer estimates and compensates for system disturbances in real time, thereby enhancing the system's disturbance rejection capability and adaptability. Secondly, a Markovian decision process is integrated into the EADRC, allowing RL to adaptively tuning EADRC parameters in real time. This innovative integration of data-driven learning with disturbance rejection control enables the system to achieve optimal active power responses across diverse operating scenarios. Finally, simulations on a 5 MW wind turbine show that this proposed strategy outperforms conventional control methods in both active power tracking accuracy and system dynamic response, significantly enhancing the robustness of wind turbine control. This provides effective technical support for large-scale wind power integration in new power systems.

title: AMHF-Net: A Multispectral and Hyperspectral Image Fusion Network for Arbitrary-band Hyperspectral Images
authors: Shunyao Wang, Qi Xie, Qian Zhao, Deyu Meng
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Hyperspectral and multispectral image fusion serves as an effective approach to obtain high-resolution hyperspectral images at low cost. Current deep learning based hyperspectral image fusion methods often focus on fusing images with a fixed number of spectral bands, making them difficult to adapt to remote sensing images with varying spectral bands. To address this limitation, we propose a novel network framework named Arbitrary-band Multispectral and Hyperspectral images Fusion Network (AMHF-Net). Specifically, our method propose an Implicit Spectral Basis Generation block (ISBG) to learn continuous spectral basis vectors that are adaptively sampled according to the wavelength of different bands, thereby generating appropriate spectral basis matrices. We also design an Arbitrary-band Feature Extraction block (AFE) to effectively extract features from hyperspectral images with arbitrary numbers of spectral bands. Furthermore, we develop a Spectral Information Compensation block (SIC) to enhance spectral information acquisition. Extensive experimental results demonstrate that our approach not only achieves comparable performance with state-of-the-art methods with fixed number of bands, but also exhibits superior adaptability to process data with arbitrary numbers and positions of spectral bands.

title: Satellite-Enabled Edge Intelligence for Climate-Resilient Emergency Mobility: A Survey of On-Orbit Edge Computing
authors: Ping Zong, Zhonghong Ou, Ran An, Qingnan Zhao, Guoxin Zhang, Kaiwen Xue, Zijing Tian, Shuai Lyu, Yiran Shen, Sihao He, Yifan Zhu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: Climate change is increasing the frequency and intensity of extreme weather events, which can rapidly disrupt road capacity, public transit operations, and logistics corridors. Reliable emergency mobility and low-carbon recovery therefore require timely, trustworthy, and spatially comprehensive situational awareness as well as resilient decision-making under intermittent connectivity. Earth-observation satellites and emerging LEO constellations provide global coverage for hazard monitoring (e.g., floods, wildfires, landslides) and infrastructure assessment, yet the resulting data volumes and limited contact windows make purely ground-centric processing increasingly insufficient for real-time transportation response. This paper surveys on-orbit edge computing (OEC) as a spaceborne edge layer that can close the sensing–analysis–action loop for climate-resilient transportation systems. We organize the literature into three evolutionary stages: 1) single-satellite on-orbit processing for event triggering and data triage, 2) satellite–ground collaboration for split inference, task offloading, and end-to-end orchestration, and 3) inter-satellite distributed computing for constellation-scale learning and inference under dynamic topology. Beyond summarizing methods, datasets, and platforms, we discuss how these OEC capabilities can be integrated with transportation digital twins and emergency operations to enable faster disruption detection, evacuation planning support, and bandwidth- and carbon-aware information delivery. Finally, we outline open challenges and future directions, including robust benchmarking under climate stressors, trustworthy autonomy, and LLM-assisted decision support across satellite–ground workflows.

title: Deep Learning-Assisted Design of Doherty Power Amplifiers Using Discretized Topology
authors: Mingyu Liu, Yongwen Yin, Yanzhou Qu, Longhao Li, Leyan Wan, Min Xiong, Changjiang Xu, Yijie Tang, Chuan Li, Fei You, Songbai He
publish: IEEE Microwave and Wireless Technology Letters
abstract: This letter proposes an automated Doherty power amplifier (DPA) design methodology integrating theoretical S-parameter derivation with multiport artificial intelligence (AI) modeling. Specifically, this approach formulates DPA load-modulation requirements into explicit S-parameter targets, establishes a convolutional neural network (CNN) surrogate model for high-precision mapping, and optimizes the discretized topology to match these constraints. This strategy extends the scope of AI-assisted design, generalizing automated synthesis capabilities to complex load-modulated architectures. Afabricated 2.5–2.7 GHz DPA validates this methodology, demonstrating a saturation drain efficiency (DE) of 74%–81% at 43 dBm output power and a DE of 62%–71% at 6-dB output back-off (OBO). With digital predistortion (DPD), the design achieves an adjacent channel leakage ratio (ACLR) of −48.3 dBc under a 40-MHz 256-QAM signal with 6.3-dB peak-to-average power ratio (PAPR).

title: A Stochastic Differential Equation Framework for Modeling Queue Length Dynamics Inspired by Self-Similarity
authors: Shakib Mustavee, Shaurya Agarwal, Arvind Singh
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: This article proposes a stochastic differential equation (SDE) to model the temporal evolution of queue length dynamics at signalized intersections. Motivated by the observed quasiperiodicity, long-range dependence, and self-similar characteristics of queue length time series, the proposed model incorporates these three features into the SDE through: (i) a periodic mean-reversion term to capture quasiperiodicity, (ii) a multiplicative noise term, and (iii) a fractional Brownian motion (fBm) to capture self-similarity and long-range dependence. Model parameters are estimated directly from data using a particle-filter–based likelihood framework. Using the extracted parameters, the model is applied to one-step-ahead prediction of queue lengths at four signalized intersections along a traffic corridor in Orlando, Florida. The proposed gray-box approach achieves error metrics that are better than those obtained with an LSTM-based predictor, despite using substantially fewer training samples. In addition to prediction accuracy, the model reproduces key statistical properties of the observed data, including a <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$1/f$</tex-math> </inline-formula>-type power spectral density and closely matching probability distributions. The results demonstrate that the proposed SDE and particle-filtering framework provides a data-efficient, interpretable approach for modeling and predicting queue dynamics at signalized intersections.

title: RayFlex: Inducing Weight Perception through Raycast Pseudo-Haptics in Virtual Reality
authors: Yushi Wei, Sen Zhang, Rongkai Shi, Simon Fong, Pan Hui, Hai-Ning Liang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: Weight perception is essential for delivering compelling and realistic object interaction in VR systems. While existing pseudo-haptic techniques have enabled users to perceive virtual object weight without physical actuation, their application has primarily been limited to near-field, direct hand interaction. As VR systems continue to advance in fidelity and versatility, interactions with objects beyond arm's reach are becoming increasingly common. However, how weight perception can be introduced into such remote interactions, and whether it can enhance user immersion and experience, remains underexplored. To bridge this gap, we present RayFlex, a pseudo-haptic technique that conveys object weight in raycasting-based interaction through visual displacement and dynamic ray deformation. The technique was evaluated in two user studies, which examined its effectiveness in supporting weight discrimination and its impact on user experience across different interaction contexts. Results indicate that RayFlex leads to significant improvements in perceived realism, presence, and satisfaction, while maintaining usability. From the results, we derived two implications and an application guideline that can help design future VR systems.

title: Graph Neural Network-Based Adaptive Data Imputation and Outlier Detection for Streaming IoT Time Series
authors: Shivakumar. E, Moorthy Agoramoorthy, D. Sathyanarayanan, Michael Raj TF, Samson Ebenezar Uthirapathy
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: The proliferation of Internet of Things (IoT) ecosystems has led to an explosion of streaming time series data from a multitude of sensors. This data is often incomplete due to sensor failures or transmission errors, and can contain anomalous readings due to noise or malicious activity. Traditional data imputation and outlier detection methods are often ill-suited for the dynamic and non-stationary nature of streaming IoT data, and fail to capture the complex spatial and temporal correlations between interconnected sensors. To address these challenges, this paper introduces a novel framework that leverages Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) for adaptive online learning to perform real-time data imputation and outlier detection. Our approach models the IoT sensor network as a dynamic graph, where GNNs are used to learn the intricate dependencies between sensors and infer missing values and identify anomalies by considering both the local neighborhood and the global network structure. A key innovation of this work is the development of an adaptive learning mechanism that continuously updates the model to handle non-stationary data streams and concept drift, enabling it to maintain high performance over extended periods without the need for periodic retraining. Extensive experiments on real-world streaming IoT datasets demonstrate that our GNN-based framework significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in both imputation accuracy and anomaly detection, while maintaining low latency suitable for edge deployment.

title: IntelliSweep: The Design and Testing of an Adaptive Intelligent Cleaning System for Wet and Uneven Outdoor Surfaces
authors: Gisha Thankachi M S, Anand Prem P K
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: Cleaning outdoor yards is especially difficult during the rainy season since wet leaves and other trash tend to stick to muddy and uneven ground. For older people or those with disabilities, the job is considerably harder, and the current interior cleaning technologies don't work well in these kinds of outdoor settings. An intelligent cleaning system is suggested to solve this problem. It would employ an algorithmic control mechanism to find, move, and pick up rubbish from wet ground. The device uses infrared sensors to identify rubbish and an STM microcontroller to regulate it in real time. The DC motor powers the suction unit and the sweeping arm. The stepper motors allow the equipment move smoothly over difficult ground. We set up a rough surface in the lab with debris to check how well the system functioned. The results showed that the strategy worked well to get rid of trash, therefore it is a good and practical way to maintain yard clean.

title: DHR-Based Threat Detection Method For ADAS
authors: Qingyu Shen, Wenbo Hu, Shuguang Ding, Gangtao Han, Dalong Zhang
publish: 2025 International Conference on Cyber Resilience and Endogenous Safety &amp; Security (CRESS)
abstract: Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS) have seen tremendous development in recent years due to their highly intelligent perception and decision-making technologies. However, as ADAS technology becomes more widespread, its security threats are increasingly exposed. Traditional threat detection methods rely heavily on human expertise and struggle to address unknown threats. Therefore, this paper proposes an ADAS threat detection method based on the Dynamic Heterogeneous Redundancy architecture derived from endogenous safety and security principles. Through comprehensive testing across multiple heterogeneous ADAS devices, the method achieves threat discovery without relying on any prior knowledge. Experiments demonstrate that the proposed method significantly enhances threat detection efficiency.

title: Learning-Based RIS-Assisted Secure Beamforming Design: A User-Scalable GNN Approach
authors: Natasha Francis, Peng Cheng, William D. Lukito, Yijie Gao
publish: 2025 11th International Workshop on Signal Design and Its Applications in Communications (IWSDA)
abstract: Secure beamforming design in reconfigurable intelligent surface (RIS)-assisted communication systems plays a critical role in overall system performance. However, optimizing beamforming becomes significantly more challenging in practical scenarios with unknown eavesdropper instantaneous channel state information (CSI) and a time-varying number of users. To address this, we formulate an ergodic secrecy rate (ESR) maximization problem that jointly optimizes active beamforming and RIS phase shifts under dynamic user configurations. Specifically, we propose a graph neural network-based framework, termed RIS-oriented message passing neural network (RMPNN), where both users and the RIS are modeled as graph nodes to effectively capture the characteristics of cascaded channels. Through multi-layer message passing, RMPNN learns to generate optimized beamforming matrices while ensuring scalability with respect to the number of users. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves robust ESR performance with low computational overhead and strong generalization across diverse user distributions without requiring retraining.

title: State Estimation With Model Uncertainty Using Structure Variational Bayesian and Transfer Learning
authors: Shuang Gao, Xiaoli Luan, Biao Huang, Shunyi Zhao, Fei Liu
publish: IEEE/CAA Journal of Automatica Sinica
abstract: This paper proposes a novel approach to address parameter uncertainties for state estimation in Markovian jump linear systems by leveraging transfer learning. Assume that the source domain model is available and reliable, and the target domain model has significant model parameter uncertainties. To enhance estimation performance in the target domain, the proposed method transfers model knowledge from the source domain and adjusts it using a tuning factor before incorporating it into the target domain estimator. More specifically, this approach involves transferring the modified probability density functions of state prediction from the source domain to the target domain and determining the tuning factor via structure variational Bayesian inference using measurements in the target domain. Using numerical examples and a 1-DOF torsion system, we showcase the competitiveness of the proposed state estimator compared to the existing robust state estimation methods when dealing with parameter uncertainties. The results highlight its capability to improve estimation accuracy in practical scenarios, showcasing its potential for real-world applications.

title: Modularized Graph Convolutional Network
authors: Tiantian He, Zhixuan Duan, Xin Luo
publish: IEEE/CAA Journal of Automatica Sinica
abstract: Dear Editor, This letter presents a novel graph neural network, namely modularized graph convolution network (MGCN), to address the underexplored issue in graph convolution networks (GCNs), wherein the weights for neighbor aggregation are fixed, leading to the limited capability of capturing diverse relationships among nodes for representation learning. Conventional GCNs always learn node representations in the graph according to the weights computed from the graph Laplacian, consequently overlooking the similarity and group cohesiveness of node features. Motivated by this observation, the proposed MGCN is designed to learn expressive node representations by considering the following two aspects. First, we propose a novel Laplacian operator to generate weights for feature aggregation that can adaptively capture the feature similarity among nodes. Second, based on modularity optimization [1], we design a novel auxiliary loss that enables the weights for aggregating features to capture the cluster structure of the graph. The proposed MGCN, therefore, can learn expressive representations for each node in the graph by leveraging the modularized messages, i.e., predominately aggregating the features of neighbors in the same cluster. The proposed MGCN has been tested with several well-established datasets and compared with strong GCN-based models. The experimental results demonstrate that MGCN is more effective in various downstream tasks.

title: Designing and Optimizing a Sustainable Inland Waterways Transport System for Iron Ore Export
authors: Dhatri, Balaraj Sharma Aribam, Nishant Kumar
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: The Jal Vikas Marg Project (JVMP) was launched as the part of the development of the National Waterway-1 (NW1) program in India, aims to convert the Ganga-BhagirathiHooghly River system into modern and sustainable inland transport system. This paper conducts research on a 100 km pilot area found between Tribeni and Haldia of Hooghly River Basin, which was a very important route in transportation of iron ore and bulk cargo. This is aimed at maximizing the operations of the barge, in terms of speed, fuel consumption, and emissions, which is economically feasible, and environmentally sustainable. In the research, the classical methods of optimization, which are Golden Section Search, Newton Method, and Steepest Descent are used to minimize the total voyage cost and CO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> emitted under the constraints of navigation, such as river draught, vessel capacity, and maximum service time. This model combines the hydrological and operational parameters, incorporating trade-offs of both energy use and traveling time.

title: Backward Fano Decoding for PAC Codes
authors: Po-Cheng Kung, Chung-Hsuan Wang, Chi-chao Chao
publish: 2025 11th International Workshop on Signal Design and Its Applications in Communications (IWSDA)
abstract: In this paper, we propose a backward Fano decoding algorithm for polarization-adjusted convolutional (PAC) codes. The corresponding path metric is derived, and some techniques for performance enhancement and decoding complexity reduction are introduced. Good codes for multiple code rates and lengths are then constructed. Our backward Fano decoding eliminates the integration of successive cancellation, which allows for construction of codes with better weight distributions. Simulation results show that the proposed backward Fano decoding can achieve better performance with comparable decoding complexity than forward Fano decoding at high signal-to-noise ratios.

title: Comprehensive Security for Smart City Blockchain Infrastructures through Advanced Consortium- Based Transaction Verification
authors: Nikita M. Okhlopkov, Maxim O. Kalinin
publish: 2026 ElCon Conference of Young Researchers in Computing &amp; Processing, and Information Security (ElCon-CP)
abstract: The deployment of blockchain technology within smart city critical infrastructure, such as smart grids, intelligent transportation, and automated utility management, demands robust security and reliable data integrity. This paper presents a solution to key vulnerabilities in blockchain systems, including majority attacks and consensus manipulation, which threaten the stability of data-driven systems. There is introduced a consortium-based verification method that ensures transactional integrity through a dynamic reputation mechanism and threshold signature scheme, providing fault tolerance suitable for distributed control systems. Experimental study has confirmed that the proposed solution enhances security for critical IoT applications while maintaining the low-latency and low-power operational requirements essential for the blockchain-enabled smart city automation and control systems.

title: A Two-Stage Anomaly Detection Framework for 5G Core Networks Based on Multi-Source Data
authors: Xinming Wang, Xingxing Liao, Jie Yang, Runhan Feng, Jingchen Xu
publish: 2025 International Conference on Cyber Resilience and Endogenous Safety &amp; Security (CRESS)
abstract: With the cloud-native transformation of 5G core networks, the complexity of network operations and maintenance (O&M) has increased significantly, accompanied by a growing number of targeted attacks. Traditional anomaly detection methods based on single data sources or specific anomaly types are no longer sufficient to cope with the diversity of abnormal scenarios. To address this challenge, this paper proposes a two-stage anomaly detection framework that leverages multi-source data fusion to enable accurate anomaly identification and root cause analysis in 5G core networks. The framework first collects heterogeneous data sources—including core network function logs, network traffic, and pod monitoring metrics—and constructs comprehensive features through preprocessing and temporal alignment. In the first stage, an XGBoost-based model integrates these multi-source features to detect overall anomalous network states. In the second stage, a weighted fusion of three single-source models is employed to perform fine-grained root cause analysis, identifying 43 specific anomaly types. These anomalies result from combinations of different network functions and various anomaly categories, including both internal failures of network functions and anomalies induced by external attacks. Experimental results demonstrate that the fused multi-source anomaly detection model achieves an F1-score of 0.9812, significantly outperforming single-source models. Furthermore, the root cause analysis stage achieves an F1-score of 0.9723 after weighted fusion, representing a 5.6% improvement in precision over the best-performing single-source model. This study provides a robust and effective approach to anomaly detection and O&M enhancement for 5G core networks.

title: STGNI: Spatio-Temporal Graph Node Importance for Software-Defined Networking Traffic Prediction
authors: Yanxin Jia, Long Xu, Hengsheng Zhang, Kailai Peng, Hui Zuo, Lihu Pan, Junhua Kuang
publish: 2025 International Conference on Cyber Resilience and Endogenous Safety &amp; Security (CRESS)
abstract: Network traffic prediction enables SDN controllers to optimize traffic engineering and load balancing, thereby mitigating congestion and faults while improving resource utilization. While most studies focus on spatio-temporal correlations in traffic data, in practical networks link capacities differ, resulting in unequal influences among nodes. We define this unequal contribution as node importance. To model this phenomenon, we propose STGNI (Spatio-Temporal Graph Node Importance for Software-Defined Networking Traffic Prediction), a model comprising three core modules: DNM (Data Normalization Module), NIM (Node Importance Module), and STDM (Spatio-Temporal Dependency Module). Experiments on the Abilene and GEANT datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of STGNI, outperforming eight baseline methods across three evaluation metrics and multiple prediction horizons (1–5 steps).

title: Efficient Widening Operations for Static Analysis of Bit-Vector Programs
authors: Guangsheng Fan, Dengping Wei
publish: 2025 International Conference on Cyber Resilience and Endogenous Safety &amp; Security (CRESS)
abstract: Static analysis of bit-vector programs is crucial for detecting bugs such as integer overflow, yet it remains challenging due to their reliance on machine integer semantics and the intricate interplay between arithmetic and bitwise operations. To tackle this difficulty, the recently proposed swb abstract domain combines bit-level and word-level abstractions. Its bit-level component generalizes the tnum abstraction—originally used in the Linux eBPF verifier—into a signedness-aware, standard abstract domain. However, the current widening operator employed in this bit-level abstraction is overly conservative, often resulting in substantial analysis overhead.In this paper, we propose a significantly more efficient widening operator for tnum and the bit-level abstraction of swb, inspired by the characteristics of bit-vector operations. Unlike existing widening operations, our design can maintain stable bits while accelerating the iteration of other higher bits to achieve convergence. Experiments on the SV-COMP benchmarks show that the generalized tnum abstraction equipped with our new widening operator achieves a speedup of up to 23.51× in analysis time over the original approach. When integrated into the full swb domain, it still accelerates analysis by up to 1.93×. Crucially, in both settings, the optimized abstract domain validates all programs that the original domain could verify, demonstrating that our optimization incurs no loss in practice verification capability.

title: Detection and Classification of Lung and Colon Cancer Cells Using an Optimized Hybrid Deep Learning Model
authors: R. Raja, A. Selvakumar
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: The ability to correctly recognize cancer cells in histopathological images is critical to the early diagnosis and better patient outcomes, especially in lung and colon cancer, which is one of the primary causes of death in the entire world. In this paper, a hybrid deep learning model is proposed in the form of the best optimization to detect and classify lung and colon cancer cells. The suggested framework will combine the local feature extraction of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and the global attention model based on Transformers to learn contextual dependencies. Further, to optimize hyperparameters to improve the model performance and generalization, optimization algorithms like Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) and the Genetic Algorithm (GA) are used. The model is trained and tested on publicly available datasets of histopathology and makes a significant improvement in terms of accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score against the more traditional deep learning architecture. The experimental results emphasize the strength and versatility of the hybrid model of multi-cancer image analysis. This paper reveals the opportunities of optimized hybrid architectures as a reliable and scalable solution to AI-assisted cancer diagnosis to the field of precision medicine development. The suggested model will help reduce premature deaths associated with non-communicable diseases with the aim of ensuring that the world leads the fight against early mortality by aiming at early cancer diagnosis as stipulated in the United Nations Sustainable Development Goal 3 (Good Health and Well-Being). This study will enhance equitable and accessible healthcare by enhancing the accuracy of diagnoses and lowering clinical workloads in line with SDG 3 goals.

title: Communication Optimization Techniques for Distributed Synchronous Data Parallel Training
authors: Elvira A. Vlasova, Polina K. Nemytykh
publish: 2026 ElCon Conference of Young Researchers in Computing &amp; Processing, and Information Security (ElCon-CP)
abstract: In recent years, deep learning technology has been developing rapidly, especially driven by large-scale datasets, and distributed deep learning systems have been widely used in academia and industry. Aiming at the problems in distributed synchronous data parallel training algorithms and communication interval selection methods, this paper focuses on the study of distributed synchronous data parallel training algorithms with low communication overhead and high statistical efficiency, and communication interval selection methods with low additional overhead. training algorithm with low communication overhead and high statistical efficiency, and communication interval selection method with low additional overhead. The training algorithm and tuning strategy for communication optimization are proposed, and a prototype system is implemented. Experimental results show the effectiveness of the above communication optimization techniques, specifically, the training algorithm combining the skip communication strategy and the correction technique reduces the total training time by 88.9% compared to the SSGD training algorithm, and the adaptive communication interval strategy reduces the additional overhead by three orders of magnitude compared to the existing communication interval selection strategy.

title: Elysian: Building Emotionally Responsive Non-Playable Characters using Integration of FSM and Generative AI for Real-Time Dialogues in Visual Games_supp1-3669660.pdf
authors: Sachin Patil
publish: N/A
abstract: Elysian is a modular architecture designed to generate emotionally expressive and contextually coherent dialogue for non-playable characters (NPCs) in interactive games. As the first contribution in a larger research program, this paper focuses on the system's dialogue generation module, which leverages large language models (LLMs) under strict runtime and hardware constraints. We investigate two practical approaches for enabling emotion-aware dialogue on consumer-grade devices: parameter-efficient LoRA fine-tuning and structured prompt orchestration. Targeting deployment on 6 GB VRAM hardware with sub-5-second response latency, we evaluate whether small, 4-bit-quantized 1B-parameter models can reliably produce emotionally aligned NPC dialogue. Across 1,000 generated samples, LoRA fine-tuning yields substantial performance gains in lexical alignment (BLEU: 0.0587 vs. 0.0006), semantic similarity (BERTScore: 0.8645 vs. 0.2712), and emotion-tag accuracy (100% vs. 0%) compared to prompting alone. Meanwhile, prompt orchestration demonstrates strong structural reliability, achieving 78% valid JSON generation for branching narrative formats and reaching 100% accuracy using a lightweight two-pass correction scheme. This work establishes the technical foundation for future research on multimodal NPC behavior, including integration with speech synthesis, behavioral animation, and visually grounded interaction.

title: Reinforcement Learning-Enhanced Model Predictive Control to Improve Energy Efficiency of Water Pumping Stations
authors: Wenyuan Bai, Jiatong Wang, Kashem M. Muttaqi, Danny Sutanto, Raad Raad, Allan Gilkes
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industry Applications
abstract: Water pumping stations (WPS) are among the most energy-intensive components in water supply systems, and their operational costs are increasingly sensitive to fluctuations in electricity prices. Conventional control approaches, such as manual scheduling and standard model predictive control (MPC), often rely on short prediction horizons. This limitation prevents them from exploiting seasonal variations in electricity prices and restricts long-term operational optimization. This study proposes a hierarchical control architecture that combines the long-term planning capability of reinforcement learning (RL) with the short-term optimization of MPC. In the upper layer, a distributional RL agent learns cross-seasonal cost-minimization strategies from historical operational and environmental data. In the lower layer, a physics-informed MPC (PI-MPC) translates these strategies into real-time pump operations that are safe, efficient, and compliant with physical system constraints. The proposed framework was evaluated using one year of operational data from an Australian WPS. Results show that the method reduced the unit pumping cost to $13.9/ML, compared with $37.8/ML for manual operation and $29.4/ML for a baseline MPC. Savings were driven by seasonal load shifting aligned with electricity price trends and improved pump efficiency via variable frequency drive (VFD) control, resulting in a 38% gain in energy performance.

title: A Compact Energy-Efficient Unified Micro-Robotic Processor With Flexible Visual Perception
authors: Shuyuan Zhang, Yujin Wang, Xizhu Chen, Huazhong Yang, Yongpan Liu, Hongyang Jia
publish: IEEE Journal of Solid-State Circuits
abstract: This work presents a programmable processor for both neural-network (NN)-based inference and localization in micro-robotic visual perception (VP). While specialized accelerators for the two applications demonstrate substantial energy efficiency and throughput gains, the built-in system with separate cores suffers from a mismatch across diverse robotic tasks, making it difficult to harness energy- and area-efficiency gains at the system level, which are the two critical metrics for intelligent computing platforms on micro and even more miniaturized robots, such as robot insects. To overcome this limitation, we employ a unified VP architecture that features a reconfigurable spatial array to perform matrix multiplication and decomposition, along with further fusion of different key operators from circuit level to architecture level, including a hybrid precision dataflow and a customized ISA. This leads to a unified processor for programmable computation across the micro-robotic VP domain, enabling: 1) efficient acceleration for both localization and NN inference; 2) flexible mapping of diverse tasks to the accelerator. Fabricated in 28 nm, the prototype achieves a peak area efficiency of 62.2 fps/mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> and a peak energy efficiency of 116 uJ/frame when performing visual navigation under the energy-efficient mode at 0.6 V, with less than 0.3% accuracy loss compared to full-precision localization running on a commercial CPU. Validated across four typical micro-robotic applications, the prototype demonstrates <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$8.6\times $</tex-math> </inline-formula> system-level area efficiency compared to a combination of renowned NN inference and localization accelerators.

title: PIC-MCC Simulation of Hydrogen Generation by Electron Stripping of Negative Hydrogen Ions Using Solid Carbon Foils
authors: Jie-Qing Fan, Yao Li, Jun-Jie Shi, Jian-Hong Hao, Fang Zhang, Qiang Zhao, Bi-Xi Xue, Zhi-Wei Dong
publish: IEEE Transactions on Plasma Science
abstract: Neutral beams are considered a competitive technology for space junk disposal. To prepare neutral beams using solid targets, in this study, a particle-in-cell with Monte Carlo collisions (PIC-MCCs) calculation model is constructed to simulate the neutralization of negative hydrogen ions using gaseous carbon target with solid-equivalent density instead of solid carbon foils. The transmission of negative hydrogen ions through the gaseous carbon target is simulated, and three main collision reactions are considered: single-electron stripping of negative hydrogen ions to form hydrogen atoms, further stripping of hydrogen atoms to form protons, and double-electron stripping of negative hydrogen ions to directly form protons. In addition, changes in particle composition (negative hydrogen ions, hydrogen atoms, and protons) during beam transmission are analyzed as a function of target thickness. The PIC-MCC simulation results are consistent with theoretical calculations and experimental data, verifying the feasibility of using gaseous carbon instead of solid carbon foils to simulate anion neutralization. On this basis, the model was used to calculate the neutralization efficiency and electron-stripping efficiency of negative hydrogen ions at different energies. The results indicate that the electron-stripping efficiency increases progressively with increasing target thickness Meanwhile, the neutralization efficiency increases rapidly to a maximum value and then decreases gradually. The maximum neutralization efficiency for hydrogen ions is approximately 55% and remains unaffected by the negative hydrogen ion energy. Nevertheless, higher beam energy requires greater optimal target thickness. To achieve high neutralization efficiency, the required target thickness for an 800-MeV beam is approximately in the range of 30–<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$68~\mu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>g/cm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>.

title: GTEA: A Game-Theoretic Evolutionary Algorithm for Solving Vehicle Routing Problem With Time Windows Under Uncertain Travel Times
authors: Hao Jiang, Junhao Wang, Bowen Song, Xiaoshu Xiang, Jinliang Ding, Xingyi Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: The Vehicle Routing Problem with Time Windows under Uncertain Travel Times (VRPTW-UT) is a challenging and practically significant combinatorial optimization problem. Although evolutionary algorithms (EAs) have shown potential in solving VRPTW-UT, they often struggle to balance robustness and convergence. Conventional EA approaches evaluate solutions across multiple disturbance scenes and discard those that become infeasible under any scenario. This often leads to the premature elimination of solutions that are only infeasible in a limited number of scenes, hindering the ability to effectively explore the trade-off between robustness and convergence. To address this issue, this paper proposes a Game-Theoretic Evolutionary Algorithm (GTEA) that models the search process as a game between two adversarial components: a perturbation generation part that constructs high-impact uncertainty scenes, and a robustness enhancement part that improves solutions under those critical conditions. This antagonistic process forces the population to evolve toward solutions that possess both high robustness and convergence, so that GTEA can efficiently produce solutions with high robustness and convergence. Extensive experiments on four benchmark datasets demonstrate that GTEA outperforms five state-of-the-art algorithms designed for VRPTW-UT, achieving superior convergence and robustness.

title: Anti-Reconnaissance Routing in Non-Cooperative Networks via Reinforcement Learning
authors: Danqi Li, Jilong Yang, Weichen Li, Jincheng Deng, Chuanrong Liu, Lina Wang, Jin Zhao
publish: 2025 International Conference on Cyber Resilience and Endogenous Safety &amp; Security (CRESS)
abstract: In response to tightly regulated, adversarial communication environments, we propose RAIN-Net (Resilient Anti-reconnaissance Intent-driven Networking), an intent-driven, reinforcement-learning framework for autonomous anti-reconnaissance routing. RAIN-Net first uses an Intent-to-Objectives (I2O) agent to translate natural-language user requirements into reproducible multi-objective weights and constraints. It then performs conditional candidate generation over a cross-regional cloud-node resource pool and applies compliance masks to define the feasible set. A PPO-based path-selection agent (PPORP) subsequently executes three-stage decisions (Guard–Mid–Exit) within a masked-softmax legal action space, producing distinct primary and backup routes. At runtime, a sub-network manager and node-risk monitors enable risk-aware switching and path regeneration. In simulation, RAIN-Net satisfies heterogeneous QoS demands and, relative to the public Internet and Tor baselines, substantially lowers detection risk while maintaining competitive latency, bandwidth, and packet-loss performance, demonstrating improved anti-reconnaissance resilience and service stability.

title: Environmental Monitoring with Wireless Sensor Networks Using a Hybrid Bidirectional ARIMA-ANN Method
authors: Md Yousuf Ahmed, Zameer Ahamad B, Jayalakshmi V, C. Sathiyamoorthy, D. Suganthi, T. Raja
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: In the field of environmental monitoring, WSN have recently become a major technological player. Due to developments in low-power circuits and micro-sensor technologies, data can be collected and analysed efficiently, leading to more precise monitoring. Incorporating state-of-the-art data processing methods as feature extraction, model training, and preprocessing, this study suggests a WSN-based strategy. To minimise inconsistencies caused by different measurement units, we employ normalisation. To ensure that the most relevant variables are chosen, we use feature selection based on correlation. Feature selection is the initial and most important phase in training unified ARIMA-ANN models, which are utilised by the suggested strategy. This strategy outperforms cutting-edge AI models like ARIMA and Artificial Neural Networks (ANN). Results from experiments show that the model is effective in environmental monitoring, with an improvement in accuracy of 95.43 percent. By using advanced preprocessing and feature selection approaches, the proposed WSN-based method considerably boosts monitoring capabilities, enabling more precise and trustworthy data-driven decision-making. More efficient and precise real-time assessments of environmental conditions are possible thanks to these results, which highlight the potential of WSN in environmental monitoring.

title: Neural Network Training Methodology for Semantic Segmentation of Maps in Navigation Tasks
authors: Vladimir Kovalev, Anton Salamakhin, Ekaterina Ilatovskaya, Anastasia Fedorkova, Denis Filatov
publish: 2026 ElCon Conference of Young Researchers in Computing &amp; Processing, and Information Security (ElCon-CP)
abstract: This paper presents a neural network training methodology for semantic segmentation of terrain maps, aimed at improving the accuracy of recognizing structural surface features such as buildings, roads, and natural objects. The proposed approach utilizes specialized convolutional neural network architectures and adaptive training strategies to achieve predictable segmentation quality depending on dataset characteristics. The developed method enables the generation of probabilistic class maps, enhancing the reliability of localization for autonomous systems in GPS-denied environments. The experimental results confirm the effectiveness of the proposed approach and demonstrate its potential for visual navigation and terrain analysis applications.

title: Vision Transformer-Based Wheat Health Mapping Using Geospatial Data for Precision Agriculture
authors: Jagan M A, Kapilsuriya K, Manochitra S
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: The number of cases of wheat yield loss by diseases, nutrient deficiencies is still a large problem in high farming operations as early signs of these conditions are often missed because of the time lag related to the traditional system of scouting of the fields. Manual inspection is too laborious and cannot be used to provide round-the-clock and extensive coverage of crops. In order to overcome this shortcoming, the current study will suggest an automated wheat health monitoring system that combines Vision Transformers (ViT), Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM), and geospatial visualization by using the QGIS. This system uses a fine-tuned ViT model to obtain global spatial features of high-resolution wheat image to make high accuracy health classification, and LSTM networks track the development of temporally consecutive image sequences to make temporal changes in crop condition. The joint spatio-temporal learning technique allows one to identify healthy crops, diseased areas, and regions with nutrient deficiency at an early age with certainty. QGIS is used to convert model outputs into georeferenced wheat health maps that can be used to provide intuitive decision-support methods of precision agriculture. The proposed framework will decrease the reliance on manual monitoring, increase the ability to intervene in the early stages, and provides the possibility to monitor the resources effectively, which in turn will lead to high yield wheat production and sustainable crop production methods.

title: Ensemble Learning-Based Malicious Node Identification in MANETs: A Robust Approach for Attack Detection and Mitigation
authors: C.Chandra Prabha
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: MANETs are susceptible to very high rates of security attacks due to their decentralized and dynamic nature. The most challenging is detecting anti-jamming and rogue nodes, which compromise network quality and communication reliability. This research proposes an ensemble learning framework for detecting malicious nodes in MANETs. The target approach is an ensemble of heterogeneous learning methods that rely heavily on the discovery of malicious actions and observations over extended periods. The ensemble system, to achieve best possible malicious node detection accuracy and reliability, leverages diversity and complementarily among individual learning methods. Streaming communication anomaly, node activity, and network traffic activity are fed into the ensemble system. These methods, such as bagging, boosting, and stacking, are used in ensemble learning to combine the decisions of multiple models and produce a final prediction. Experimental results on real-world MANET data sets on detection accuracy and against false negatives. False positives demonstrate that the proposed ensemble learning method is better than individual learning algorithms. Experiments validate the effectiveness and practicability of an ensemble learning-based method for detecting and preventing malicious nodes in MANETs, thereby improving the network's dependability and security.

title: Information Security Technical Means Characteristics Assessment Methodic
authors: Andrey Ladynin, Nina Shmeleva
publish: 2026 ElCon Conference of Young Researchers in Computing &amp; Processing, and Information Security (ElCon-CP)
abstract: Technical information security encompasses devices wide range required for installation in organizations. Consequently, technical devices must undergo certification to confirm that configuration meets industry standards requirements. Certified devices may be approved for production and delivery, but the manufacturer retains rights for independent determine which device characteristics are accessible to the buyer. Consequently, situations arise where important data is concealed from the consumer. The paper proposes technical vibro-acoustic information security device required parameters assessment methodic, based on static mechanical method for evaluating technical equipment based on conformity principle. Methodic is aimed at decision-making support, considering organizations’ information security tools.

title: Personalized VR Learning in Engineering: Predicting Outcomes with Machine Learning
authors: Cheick Ould Nema, Mohammed Al-Hubaishi
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: Virtual Reality (VR) is becoming a transformative technology in engineering education, yet predicting learning effectiveness remains challenging due to contextual variability across settings, disciplines, and learner populations. This paper presents a machine learning-based predictive framework that achieves 88.81% accuracy in forecasting VR learning outcomes using multi-dimensional feature engineering. By analyzing 712 studies from a systematic review, we engineered features spanning study design, VR implementation characteristics, engineering domains, temporal factors, and scale indices. Six machine learning algorithms were evaluated: Logistic Regression, Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, XGBoost, LightGBM, and Support Vector Machine. Logistic Regression achieved the best performance (90.91% recall, 0.930 ROC-AUC), consistent with prior educational data mining literature showing that regularized linear models often generalize well on tabular metadata. SHAP analysis revealed that sample size, recent implementation (post-2020), and presence of control groups are the strongest predictors of VR effectiveness. This framework provides educators with datadriven guidance for VR curriculum design, though predictions should be interpreted cautiously given the metadata-only feature set and potential publication bias in the training corpus.

title: Minimizing Eye Dryness in Gaming: Optimizing Environment and Display Settings
authors: Vijender Thakur, Nishant Kumar
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: We model eye dryness during extended gaming with a four-variable objective (Illumination I, Relative Humidity RH, Viewing Distance d, Break Duration B) multiplied by hardware and content multipliers (panel size/power, sync mismatch <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\boldsymbol{\Delta}$</tex>, and game's genre factor A). The constants are calibrated via constrained least squares on 5-6 anchor scenarios and then held fixed across all experiments. We compare Steepest Descent, Newton method, QuasiNewton (BFGS), and Nonlinear Conjugate Gradient (PolakRibiere+, PR+) under realistic bounds. Cases isolate illumination mismatch, dry winter with seat constraints, humidification, adjustable geometry, and an all-controls desktop with VRR. PR+ and BFGS give the best practical trade-off (few steps, low memory for PR+), while all methods converge to the same constrained optima. Results quantify how much each control (humidity, distance, breaks) compensates for harsh hardware/content conditions.

title: Identifying DDoS Attack Patterns Through Time Series Analysis
authors: Ganesh Kumar K, Chandramukhi K, Ganika S S, Mirdula V
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: With the goal to protect the network's resources against various threats, network security is vital. Every system associated with the network possesses vulnerabilities. These permit unlicensed users to attempt to go on with a certain level of intended use to access and use the system resources. One of the most significant threats to security for real-life scenarios is the increased potential for Distributed Denial-of-Service (DDoS) attacks. DDoS concept is used in order to make attempts more intense and effective. This focuses on DDoS Attack prediction and visualization techniques using Matplotlib functions of Python and its libraries to create graphs of various parameters that helps us to analyze the given “CSV” file of which stores the packets captured in the real time network.

title: Optimal Portfolio Allocation for Risk-Averse Investors in Fintech Robo-Advisory Platforms
authors: Nilesh Acharya, Nishant Kumar
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: This is a comprehensive study of a real-time portfolio optimization model created for Indian fintech robo advisory platforms. This is built on the Markowitz meanvariance framework to minimize portfolio risk for desired target return. We implement and evaluate five classical optimization techniques/methods, they are as follows: Newton's Method, Quasi-Newton (BFGS), Steepest Descent, Conjugate Gradient and interior point (log barrier) method. These are under realistic investment conditions including no short-selling, SEBI's 10% exposure cap per asset and a full-investment condition. The model uses daily historical data from NSE-listed securities data with three investor profiles like conservative, balanced and aggressive which are constructed to reflect risk preferences in the Indian retail fintech ecosystem. The findings of the experiment shows that the Conjugate Gradient method gives the best trade-off for real-time mobile deployment by achieving a portfolio variance of 0.00473 with low memory usage and making it ideal for lightweight robo advisory platforms.

title: Mitigation of DDoS Attacks in Software-Defined Networks Using Machine Learning
authors: Abdullah Al Kanj, Elena Fedorchenko
publish: 2026 ElCon Conference of Young Researchers in Computing &amp; Processing, and Information Security (ElCon-CP)
abstract: This paper presents a comprehensive approach to mitigating Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) attacks within Software-Defined Networking (SDN) environments using machine learning (ML) techniques. The proposed system integrates the Mininet emulator, the Ryu controller, and a Random Forest classifier to achieve real-time detection and mitigation of DDoS attacks. A custom dataset comprising both legitimate and attack traffic was generated in an emulated SDN topology. Experimental results show that the proposed method achieves 100% detection accuracy with efficient mitigation time and low CPU utilization. The study demonstrates the potential of ML-enhanced SDN architectures in improving network resilience against evolving cyber threats.

title: Development of a Database Structure for Constructing a Mathematical Model of a River Section Using Vector Geotaxons
authors: Andrey K. Chikin, Vladimir V. Alexeev
publish: 2026 ElCon Conference of Young Researchers in Computing &amp; Processing, and Information Security (ElCon-CP)
abstract: The paper considers a system for constructing a digital twin of a river section in order to calculate geochemical properties. To complete the task, the source object is divided into geographic information units - geotaxons. The junctions of geotaxons and environmental load points are defined as nodes. A set of relationships between nodes and geotaxons forms a database, the structure of which is presented in the work.

title: Context-Aware Strategy Classification for EV Charging
authors: Mohammed Al-Hubaishi, Abdulkareem Al-Hubaishi, Nor Khutaba
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: This paper presents a deployable home energy management approach that controls residential electric-vehicle (EV) charging by classifying operational strategy from contextual signals. A lightweight Home Energy Management System (HEMS) predicts one of four actionable classes-(0) defer, (1) opportunistic charge, (2) capped urgent charge, (3) high-renewable fast charge with limited vehicle-to-home-using engineered temporal, user, and joint photovoltaic (PV)-wind features. Employing an IEEE DataPort dataset that combines high/low-urgency EV sessions with 24-hour joint PV-wind probability distributions, nine standard classifiers are benchmarked within leakage-safe pipelines, and the classifier output is embedded into a rules-based HEMS scheduler that enforces time-of-use tariffs, carbon intensity, feeder headroom, connector limits, and user energy requirements. Random Forest provides the best operational baseline (80: 20 split: accuracy 86.8%, balanced accuracy 86.8%, macro-F1 86.9%, MCC 82.1%) and remains robust across split ratios and renewable thresholds. Feature ablation shows that renewable-context features materially improve classification performance (Random Forest macro-F1 drop <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\approx 5.1$</tex> percentage points when removed). The proposed taxonomy and mapping deliver an interpretable, edge-deployable primitive that improves renewable utilization and preserves mobility constraints, providing a practical pathway for scaling residential EV integration with distributed renewables.

title: ThreatClassifier: Real-Time Cyber Attack Classification Using Hybrid ML Models
authors: Phanindra Kumar Reddy Poli, Fahimuddin Shaik, Guru Raghavendra Kamboji, Gayathri Kondaveeti, Guru Mahendra Kalluri, Mahesh Bollu
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: Real-time threat detection and classification is the core requirement for the cybersecurity discipline since the threat of advanced cyberattacks has increased multiple with the rapid expansion of digital technologies. Classic intrusion prevention and detection commonly utilize signature-based or single-model solutions that are unable to combat growing network traffic, new attack patterns and zero-day attack. This paper suggests ThreatClassifier, an online cyberattack classifier using hybrid machine learning models in a bid to reverse these downsides. The classifier combines the strengths of supervised learning models like Random Forest, XG-Boost, and Logistic Regression for maximum flexibility as well as accuracy in identifying multiple attack patterns. For efficient model optimization, feature engineering and dimension reduction are utilized, and ensemble learning methods provide immunity against spurious positives. With preventive security actionable insights, the intended system is aimed at detecting attacks like hacking, phishing, Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS), and malware infection in real-time. Also included in the system is a real-time visualization dashboard and user-friendly interface to allow security analysts to monitor threats, discover anomalies, and make timely decisions. Experimental evaluations on benchmark cybersecurity datasets demonstrate that the hybrid model achieves greater accuracy, faster classification, and improved cost- effectiveness compared to conventional models. By combining predictive potential and usability, ThreatClassifier increases proactive defenseso that organizations can remain strong against emerging cyber threats.

title: Multiprotocol Internet of Things Networks - A Mini Review on BoT-IoT Dataset and Ton-IoT Dataset
authors: Vishal Arun Pawar, Vaishali Bhagawat Patil
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: Recently, in the Internet of Things (IoT), developments have led to a significant surge in the number and variety of interconnected smart devices. These devices use different protocols like MQTT, CoAP, Zigbee, Wi-Fi, etc., which ensure robust connectivity and automation. However, the combination of multiple protocols also introduces significant complexity and expands the potential attack surface to the networks. Thus, to protect these networks from sophisticated security threats, ensuring secure connectivity is essential. Comprehensive Datasets (DS) like BoT-IoT as well as ToN-IoT, play a key role in enhancing the MultiProtocol IoT networks' security by enabling the detection along with mitigation of complex cyberattacks for protecting these networks. Critically analysing the structure, content, and applicability of these DSs in preventing real-world cyber threats across heterogeneous IoT environments is this review's key goal. The significance of this study is to create better security against these attacks by employing BoT-IoT along with ToN-IoT DSs. Drawing upon the recent studies, this review highlights how BoT-IoT, as well as ToN-IoT, facilitate the development of adaptive, effective cybersecurity solutions. Ultimately, this review concludes that the continuous DS evolution and integrated security frameworks remain essential for robust IoT protection.

title: TS-Morph: A Generative Covert Timing Channel over QUIC via Multi-Head Time Series Prediction
authors: Zeshun Qiu, Weiwei Liu
publish: 2025 International Conference on Cyber Resilience and Endogenous Safety &amp; Security (CRESS)
abstract: The proliferation of QUIC as a foundational protocol for the modern web has introduced complex and non-stationary traffic dynamics, presenting both a challenge and an unprecedented opportunity for covert communication. Traditional covert timing channels based on simple statistical perturbations fail to capture these temporal characteristics, making them vulnerable to detection by statistical and machine learning–based methods. In this paper, we propose TS-Morph, an end-to-end generative framework for covert timing channels over QUIC. Unlike conventional perturbation approaches, TS-Morph synthesizes entire, statistically plausible timing trajectories through a process of controlled morphing. Its core is a multi-head time series prediction model built upon a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture, where each expert generates a distinct but realistic morph of QUIC timing sequences. Secret data are embedded by dynamically selecting among these experts to form the outgoing packet stream. Inspired by Quantization Index Modulation (QIM), we design a dual-objective loss function that simultaneously minimizes the distributional divergence between generated and real traffic while maximizing the separation between the morphed trajectories from different experts. Experiments on large-scale QUIC datasets demonstrate that TS-Morph achieves strong concealment and robustness, reducing detector accuracy to near-random levels. By shifting the paradigm from perturbation to generative synthesis and controlled morphing, TS-Morph establishes a new benchmark in creating stealthy, reliable, and high-fidelity covert timing channels for modern encrypted web traffic.

title: Proactive Self-Healing via Time-Series Forecasting of Network Metrics for SD-IIoT-Edge
authors: Daniel Singo Ongor, Mohammed Al-Hubaishi
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: The rapid expansion of the Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT) and edge computing has brought new capabilities to industrial systems, but it has also introduced serious challenges for network security, reliability, and anomaly detection. This paper presents a practical machine learning framework for detecting anomalies in software-defined IIoT-edge networks, with a particular focus on turning raw port statistics into useful, high-level features. An enhanced feature engineering pipeline is designed that converts low-level network statistics into 27 informative features capturing temporal behavior, traffic patterns, and imbalanced flows. Ten widely used machine learning models are evaluated under two train-test split settings (80-20 and 60-40) using a real-world IIoT network dataset of 479,846 port statistics records. The experiments show that ensemble-based models—especially LightGBM, Random Forest, XGBoost, and Gradient Boosting—consistently deliver outstanding performance, achieving 100 A detailed analysis of the models is provided using confusion matrices, ROC curves, feature correlation heatmaps, and comparative rankings. These results not only highlight which methods are most effective, but also offer concrete guidance for deploying self-healing and autonomic mechanisms in software-defined IIoT-edge networks. Overall, the proposed framework advances the state of the art in network anomaly detection by combining careful feature engineering with high-performing yet computationally efficient models suitable for real-time deployment at the network edge.

title: Robust Adaptive Beamforming via Block Sparse Bayesian Learning and Covariance Matrix Reconstruction in the Presence of Gain-Phase Errors
authors: Xuanrui Zhang, Yuexian Wang, Chuang Han, Ling Wang, Chintha Tellambura, Mérouane Debbah
publish: IEEE Wireless Communications Letters
abstract: This letter addresses the problem of robust adaptive beamforming for linear arrays impaired by gain-phase errors. We propose a joint calibration and estimation method based on block sparse Bayesian learning, in which all unknown parameters—including gain-phase errors and those required for covariance matrix reconstruction—are incorporated into a unified Bayesian inference framework. By reparameterizing the steering vector with gain-phase perturbations, the error structure is decoupled from the overcomplete dictionary, resulting in a fully deterministic dictionary that requires no further learning. An expectation–maximization procedure is employed to iteratively estimate these parameters. The estimates are then used to reconstruct the interference-plus-noise covariance matrix. Based on the minimum variance distortionless response principle, a novel robust adaptive beamformer is developed for partially calibrated arrays. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed beamformer significantly enhances output performance and exhibits strong robustness under gain-phase error mismatches, achieving an output SINR improvement of up to 3 dB over state-of-the-art methods in low SNR scenarios.

title: Digital Twin-Assisted Large AI Task-Aware Edge Offloading and Resource Allocation for Low-Altitude Wireless Sensor Networks
authors: Bintao Hu, Hengyan Liu, Haotong Cao, Shijing Yuan, Xiaoli Chu, Shugong Xu
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Areas in Sensors
abstract: Emerging vehicle-to-everything (V2X) applications, especially for smart transportation, require high-accuracy sensing and low-latency communications and computation; however, existing V2X architectures that deal with sensing, communication, and computation separately are ill-equipped to meet these coupled requirements. In this paper, we propose a framework that combines integrated sensing and communications (ISAC) and digital twins (DTs) with low-altitude edge intelligence for V2X, and formulate an optimisation problem to minimise the total service delay (i.e., the transmission delay plus the task computation delay) of all vehicular users by jointly optimising task offloading decisions, communication and computation resource allocation, and the association between unmannedaerial vehicles (UAVs) and vehicular users. To solve this problem under fast varying mobility and high-dimensional coupled constraints, we propose a task-aware multi-agent resource allocation optimisation (TM-RAO) algorithm, which enables scalable cooperative decision-making, including UAV-vehicular user association, and communication and computation resource allocation. Simulation results show substantial reductions in total service delay over the traditional deep reinforcement learning benchmark, especially in dense, dynamic low-altitude edge intelligent V2X scenarios.

title: Robust Quantum Walk Search on Complete Multipartite Graph with Multiple Marked Vertices
authors: Ningxiang Chen, Meng Li, Xiaoming Sun
publish: IEEE Transactions on Quantum Engineering
abstract: Quantum walks are a potent technique for building quantum algorithms. This paper examines the quantum walk search algorithm on complete multipartite graphs with multiple marked vertices, which has not been explored before. We employ the coined quantum walk model and achieve quadratic speedup with a constant probability of finding a marked vertex in two specific cases of complete multipartite graphs. Furthermore, we investigate the robust quantum walk of two cases and demonstrate that even with an unknown number of marked vertices, it is still possible to achieve a quadratic speedup compared to classical algorithms and the success probability oscillates within a small range close to 1. To establish this conclusion, we employ approximation and reduction methods to conduct theoretical analysis and proofs, and provide an intuitive demonstration through numerical simulations. This work addresses the overcooking problem in quantum walk search algorithms on some complete multipartite graphs and may offer insights for robust quantum search on broader graph structures. Finally, we design a circuit for the algorithm that future researchers can use to conduct experiments.

title: Small Language Models for Legislative Summarization: An Empirical Evaluation of Performance and Suitability
authors: Miguel Medina-Ramírez, Cristian Estupiñán-Ojeda, Victoria Torres-RodríGuez, Elena Sánchez-Nielsen, Cayetano Guerra-Artal, Mario Hernández-Tejera
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Parliamentary institutions generate extensive, domain-specific legislative documents, including normative texts and parliamentary debate transcripts. These documents differ in content and linguistic complexity, making automatic summarization essential for producing coherent summaries aligned with institutional standards. While large language models (LLMs) achieve high summarization quality, their computational requirements limit deployment in parliamentary and public-sector environments. In contrast, small language models (SLMs) offer a more resource-efficient alternative, but their capabilities and performance relative to LLMs, extractive methods, and other SLMs remain underexplored. In this work, we present the first comprehensive evaluation of SLMs for legislative summarization, assessing their effectiveness across document types and languages. We use two complementary datasets: EUR-Lex-Sum, a multilingual corpus of normative texts covering six European languages, and ParcanDeb-Sum, a Spanish dataset of parliamentary debate records aligned with expert-written summaries. Summary quality is evaluated through a three-tier framework combining automatic metrics (ROUGEand BERTScore), LLM-based qualitative assessment, and expert-guided evaluation formalizing parliamentary debate summarization criteria. Our results show that: (1) instruction-tuned SLMs consistently outperform extractive baselines and, in several settings, rival LLMs with seven to eight billion parameters; (2) performance differs by document type, with fine-tuning being critical for debate transcripts, whereas instruction-tuning alone suffices for normative texts; and (3) for normative texts, SLMs establish a new benchmark for multilingual performance, while for parliamentary debates, fine-tuned SLMs achieve performance comparable to domain experts. These findings provide empirical evidence that high-quality legislative summarization can be achieved with SLMs, offering actionable guidance for selecting models that balance performance with computational constraints.

title: Rethinking Skeleton-based Action Recognition from Action-class Prediction Distribution Perspective
authors: Yingying Jiao, Haipeng Chen, Yingda Lyu, Yuheng Yang, Shuang Wu, Zhenguang Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing
abstract: Action recognition has long been a fundamental and compelling problem in the field of computer vision. However, one aspect that has been overlooked so far is that current action recognition approaches often produce an unfavourable multi-peaked distribution when identifying the action class of a given motion sequence, which is ambiguous and hard to learn for neural networks. Moreover, current methods heavily rely on neural networks to extract action features for differentiating actions, lacking theoretical constraints ensuring that action-specific features are selectively extracted and ambiguous features common to multiple actions are effectively reduced. These shortcomings culminate in inadequate action recognition accuracy. Motivated by this, in this paper we seek to tackle the problem from three aspects: (1) We try to eliminate ambiguity by enforcing a smooth single-peaked distribution instead of a multi-peaked one for action-class prediction. (2) We theoretically analyze the lower bound of the label prediction log-likelihood and derive a training objective, which focuses on the extraction of action-specific features and the reduction of ambiguous features. (3) We further advocate feeding the model with richer information, including positive information like body-part structures and negative information like masked inputs. Empirically, our approach sets the new state-of-the-art performance on five large-scale benchmarks. Our code is released at https://github.com/ActionR-Group/DPM to facilitate future research and is available in the supplementary material.

title: Front cover
