title: Addressing Long-Tailed Spatial and Category Imbalances in Citywide Incident Prediction
authors: Bhumika Bhumika, Debasis Das
publish: IEEE Transactions on Big Data
abstract: Citywide incidents such as crimes, accidents, and public safety threats contribute to substantial societal disruption and economic loss. Accurate prediction of such incidents can significantly aid city administrators in proactive response planning. Existing approaches model the incident prediction as a spatio-temporal task, but often neglect the inter-region spatial long-tailed distribution of incidents. This uneven distribution introduces spatial bias in learning which causes models to overfit regions with frequent incidents (head regions) while underfit the regions with occasional incidents (tail regions). Furthermore, model learning is hindered by intra-region category imbalance, where certain incident types (e.g., theft) dominate over rarer categories (e.g., robbery) within the same region. To address inter and intra region challenges, we propose an approach named SLIP (Spatial Long-tail Incident Prediction). Specifically, for inter-region skewness, SLIP adopts a multi-expert design comprising a common feature extraction backbone followed by three expert branches. In addition, to mitigate the intra-region category imbalance, we utilizes a variant of focal loss, particularly for positive-negative imbalance. SLIP outperforms spatio-temporal state-of-the-art methods by 2-11% in Macro F1, 4-11% in Micro F1, and 1-11% in Severity Weighted F1 across Los Angeles and Chicago cities for the urban crime dataset. Additionally, we incorporate fairness metrics into the evaluation and present a comprehensive comparison of spatio-temporal incident prediction.

title: TriFusNet: A Triple-Fusion Convolutional Network for Multivariate Time Series Classification
authors: Wenhan Liu, Shurong Pan, Sheng Chang, Qijun Huang, Nan Jiang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Knowledge and Data Engineering
abstract: Multivariate time series classification (MTSC) plays a critical role in a wide range of real-world applications, such as healthcare, finance, and industrial monitoring. This paper proposes a triple-fusion network (TriFusNet), a novel convolutional network designed to address the challenges of MTSC. TriFusNet employs a specialized architecture that captures both variable-specific features and features shared across variables through the parallel use of standard, depth-wise, and shared-kernel convolutions. A hierarchical triple-fusion strategy is introduced to enhance representation learning across three stages: input-level fusion transforms raw variables, intermediate-level fusion integrates heterogeneous features, and output-level fusion improves decision robustness. Extensive experiments on 26 benchmark datasets show that TriFusNet outperforms 15 competitive baselines, achieving the best average rank (4.3077) with a Win/Draw/Loss of 5/4/17. The effectiveness of its architectural design and parameter settings is empirically validated, and a qualitative theoretical discussion is conducted to support the proposed fusion strategy. These results highlight TriFusNet's strong potential for real-world applications involving complex and high-dimensional time series data.

title: STEP-SAFE: Smart TinyML and Few-Shot Enabled Pedestrian and Cyclist Safety Framework for Intelligent Transportation System
authors: Maira Khalid, Ahmed Raza Mohsin, Jehad Ali, Houbing Herbert Song, Byeong-Hee Roh
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: Ensuring the safety of pedestrians and cyclists referred to as vulnerable road users (VRUs) is a central challenge in intelligent transportation systems (ITS), especially in urban environments where visual occlusion, behavioral unpredictability, and data scarcity pose significant barriers to real-time detection. This paper introduces STEP-SAFE, a novel few-shot learning framework optimized for TinyML platforms that enables accurate and efficient VRU recognition and alert generation with minimal supervision and extremely low computational overhead. The proposed model utilizes a prototype based meta learning architecture in combination with a lightweight convolutional encoder to support rapid generalization from as few as one to five labeled samples per class. To simulate embedded deployment, we integrate quantization aware training and sparsity based pruning, reducing the model size to under 300 KB and enabling inference latency below 64 milliseconds on micro-controller class hardware. Additionally, STEP-SAFE incorporates a dynamic risk aware alert mechanism that converts classification confidence into real-time safety decisions. The model is evaluated across multiple few-shot configurations (1-shot to 10-shot, 3-way to 5-way) and benchmarked against state-of-the-art methods. Moreover, results show that STEP-SAFE achieves up to 85.2% accuracy and 0.85mAP while requiring significantly fewer computational resources and support samples. Confusion matrix and episodic performance analyses confirm its robustness in distinguishing visually similar and occlusion prone VRU behaviors. By combining compact model design, few-shot adaptability, and interpretable alert logic, STEP-SAFE represents a deployable and intelligent solution for future ready ITS safety infrastructure.

title: Mirage Detector: A Reliable Multiuser Preamble Detector for 6G-NTN
authors: Ali Georganaki, Jorge Querol, Thang X. Vu, Symeon Chatzinotas
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Communications Society
abstract: Accessing non-terrestrial networks (NTN) via existing 4G/5G handheld devices without positioning information presents significant challenges in achieving precise synchronization between a gNodeB and hundreds of users dispersed across an NTN cell. While terrestrial communication standards limit the relative delay between the center and edge of coverage to 200 km, multilayered NTN can introduce user-specific delay variation spanning thousands of kilometers. To address this challenge, we reeingeer the receiver’s Random Access (RA) preamble detector to achieve robust detection and Transmission Arrival (TA) estimation of scattered users with unknown but deterministic differential delays. The proposed Physical layer algorithm, termed Mirage detector, aligns with current 3GPP NTN standardization efforts and is forward/backward-compatible with future/earlier releases. Mirage detector leverages a dynamic Striding mechanism for multiple hypothesis testing, making it resilient to high false alarm rates caused by signals arriving beyond their nominal duration. Mirage also efficiently supports multiuser detection, enabling rapid resynchronization during frequent handovers—approximately twice per minute per Connected UE—while minimizing computational load of the transceivers. Extensive simulations results validate the advantage of the proposed Mirage detector that enables reliable multilayer connectivity for hundreds of legacy devices within the access occasion periodicity, without requiring hardware upgrades or any self-localization capability.

title: Long-term Adaptation in VR: Retention of Altered Sensorimotor Contingencies through Redirected Walking
authors: Niklas Hypki, Taravat Anvari, Frank Steinicke, Markus Lappe
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: Redirected walking (RDW) alters the relationship between physical locomotion and visual feedback to guide users along virtual paths that vary from their real-world trajectories. Over time these changes can lead to adaptation of the user and produce novel sensorimotor settings that users might retain across sessions and apply directly once they enter virtual reality (VR). Although short-term adaptation to altered sensorimotor contingencies is well established, it remains unclear whether such adaptation is retained across multiple days. We investigated long-term retention of RDW adaptation by repeatedly exposing ten participants to a fixed rightward curvature gain of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\pi$</tex-math></inline-formula>/30 across nine sessions over two weeks. Each session consisted of 200 walking repetitions with gain applied. Adaptation was assessed before and after each session using blind walking and a pointing task. Moreover, perceptual detection thresholds for curvature gains were measured before the first session (Baseline), a day after the ninth session (Final), and once in-between before the 5th adaptation session. Results showed clear retention of adapted locomotor behavior: during blind walking at the beginning of the sessions, when instructed to walk straight, participants consistently exhibited curved trajectories, which indicates that newly acquired sensorimotor contingencies can be retained over days and are immediately available upon re-entering VR. At the same time, pointing accuracy remained stable throughout the experiment and detection thresholds showed no consistent changes across sessions. In summary, our study provides evidence that adaptation to altered sensorimotor contingencies in VR can be retained across multiple sessions and days and can be available as soon as the user enters VR. This may be useful for many scenarios in which users repeatedly use VR tools over a long period of time. The complete data set, all supplemental materials and the preprint of the manuscript are available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://osf.io/z973w</uri>.

title: Online Network Slice Deployment across Multiple Domains under Trust Constraints
authors: Julien Ali El Amine, Nour El Houda Nouar, Olivier Brun
publish: IEEE Transactions on Network and Service Management
abstract: Network slicing across multiple administrative domains raises two coupled challenges: enforcing slice-specific trust constraints while enabling fast online admission and placement decisions. This paper considers a multi-domain infrastructure where each slice request specifies a VNF chain, resource demands, and a set of (un)trusted operators, and formulates the problem as a Node–Link (NL) integer program to obtain an optimal benchmark, before proposing a Path–Link (PL) formulation that pre-generates trust and order-compliant candidate paths to enable real-time operation. To mitigate congestion, resource prices are made dynamic using a Kleinrock congestion function, which inflates marginal costs as utilization approaches capacity, steering traffic away from hotspots. Extensive simulations across different congestion levels and slice types show that: (i) PL closely tracks NL with negligible gaps at low load and moderate gaps otherwise, (ii) dynamic pricing significantly reduces blocking under scarce resources, and (iii) PL reduces computation time by about 3×–6× compared to NL, remaining within a few seconds even at high load. These results demonstrate that the proposed PL and dynamic pricing framework achieves near-optimal performance with practical runtime for online multi-domain slicing under trust constraints.

title: A (111)-Silicon Diaphragm With Dual X-Cross Microstructure for Resolving the Sensitivity-Linearity Trade-Off in High-Performance Industrial Pressure Sensor
authors: Xiaopeng Chen, Sijia Ling, Jin Zhang, Zhengyin Yu, Jiawen Yin, Qinghui Jin
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Electronics
abstract: The inherent trade-off between sensitivity and linearity consistently limits the advancement of piezoresistive pressure sensors and their applications in precision industrial systems. This article presents a novel piezoresistive pressure sensor utilizing the purpose-designed “minimally invasive surgery” technique based on (111)-silicon, designed as a dual X-Cross microstructured diaphragm featuring an embedded cavity. This ultra-miniature (430 <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\boldsymbol{\times}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> 450 <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\boldsymbol{\upmu}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>m), ultra-thin (2.5/10 <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\boldsymbol{\upmu}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>m) composite diaphragm enhances sensitivity and linearity simultaneously by stress field regulation and deflection suppression. The sensor features a tiny size of 0.6 <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\boldsymbol{\times}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> 0.8 mm, a sensitivity of 0.242 mV/kPa/3.3 V within the 0–1500 kPa, and a full-scale output of 363.7 mV, demonstrating an overall performance exceeding 413% compared with previous works. A thermistor is incorporated into the sensor to provide temperature compensation. Additionally, a sensor on-chip electronic subsystem was further developed for the proposed sensor as functions of sensor driving, signal acquisition, temperature compensation, and I<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>C digital signal output. In conclusion, this innovative design and fabrication strategy offers a novel approach for advancing the development of future pressure sensors. This work also provides a comprehensive solution for pressure measurement in the enhancement of industrial systems and processes.

title: Online Fault Detection and Diagnosis Methodology for Photovoltaic Systems Using I–V Characteristic Analysis
authors: Jhih-Hao Lin, Yuan-Kang Wu, Gia-Huy Le
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industry Applications
abstract: This paper introduces an advanced online fault detection and diagnosis (FDD) methodology for photovoltaic (PV) systems based on I–V characteristic analysis. While traditional I–V analysis is effective in evaluating PV system performance, it is limited by its reliance on offline detection, which can disrupt normal power generation. The proposed methodology addresses this limitation by employing an innovative online approach that utilizes real-time data from the monitoring system. Despite certain constraints, this approach processes the captured data to generate corrected parameters, estimated values, and reference values through data preprocessing. This enables the rapid identification of various faults, including open-circuit faults (OCFs), short-circuit faults (SCFs), shading faults (SFs), and hybrid faults involving SCFs and SFs. Additionally, it allows for the assessment of shading degree, number of short-circuit modules, and degradation degree. The method overcomes the limitations of offline I–V analysis and offers advantages such as real-time operation, low cost, and rapid fault detection. To verify the effectiveness, accuracy, and generalization capability of the proposed method, a comprehensive validation framework was conducted, including theoretical derivation, MATLAB/Simulink simulations, real fault experiments, and topology extension tests. The simulation and experimental results demonstrate that, for irradiance levels of 200 W/m² and above, the proposed FDD framework can reliably and accurately identify all predefined fault categories. This approach represents a substantial improvement in PV system maintenance and reliability, enabling more efficient and timely fault detection.

title: IntentionAR: An Intention-Driven Camera-Projector System for AR Assembly Guidance
authors: Xin Cao, Wenbo Shi, Mingyu Ma, Shanhao Yang, Kang Xie, Xibin Song, Fan Zhong, Hai-Ning Liang, Xueying Qin
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: Augmented reality (AR) assembly guidance systems can help users quickly master the assembly process of unfamiliar objects. However, it is very difficult for practical use without a thorough understanding of users' intentions. Furthermore, existing approaches still struggle to handle complex hand-part interactions and nonlinear assembly steps, and achieving intuitive and real-time guidance remains challenging. To address these issues, we propose an intention-driven camera-projector AR assembly guidance system (IntentionAR) that integrates online user intention inference with a finite-state assembly machine. The intention module recognizes interaction actions and infers the target object, enabling feedback before assembly begins, while the finite state machine (FSM) manages step progression. Using a camera-projector device, the system highlights candidate parts and, based on inferred intention, flags correct/incorrect selections to provide real-time guidance. As the assembly progresses, the models used for spatial registration and visualization switch dynamically to accommodate the nonlinear workflow. Experiments and user studies show that the system delivers a more robust AR interaction experience and improves assembly efficiency.

title: SurfaceXR: Fusing Smartwatch IMUs and Egocentric Hand Pose for Seamless Surface Interactions_supp1-3679136.mp4
authors: Vasco Xu
publish: N/A
abstract: Mid-air gestures in Extended Reality (XR) often lead to fatigue, discomfort and imprecision, limiting their suitability for extended use. Surface-based interactions offer a compelling alternative, providing improved accuracy, speed, and comfort. However, current egocentric vision-based methods struggle with reliable surface inputs due to challenges in hand tracking and surface plane estimation from oblique and occluded viewing angles. To this extent, we introduce SurfaceXR, a novel sensor fusion approach that combines headset based hand tracking with micro-vibration data sampled from commodity smartwatch IMUs to enable precise and robust inputs on everyday surfaces. Our system is designed with flexibility in mind — it can function using only hand tracking, only IMU sensing, or optimally with both modalities combined, and remains robust even without explicit surface calibration. Our key insight is that these modalities are complementary — hand tracking provides 3D positional data of hand joints, whereas IMUs supply high-frequency wrist/hand motion data. Our user study across 21 participants validates SurfaceXR's effectiveness in augmenting surface touch tracking and 8 class hand-surface gesture recognition, demonstrating significant improvements over single-modality approaches. Enabled by SurfaceXR, we demonstrate a series of interactive apps for both AR and VR, ranging from on-surface sketching, text entry and gesture-based navigation.

title: Bovine Serum Albumin-Coated Magnetic Nanoparticles: Synthesis and Characterization
authors: V. Jedinak, M. Kubovcikova, M. Koneracka, J. Kovac, A. Jurikova, I. Antal, P. Pillarova, I. Khmara, V. Girman, V. Zavisova
publish: IEEE Transactions on Magnetics
abstract: In this study, superparamagnetic iron oxide nanoparticles (IONPs) were synthesized, stabilized, and functionalized with bovine serum albumin (BSA) to enhance their stability and biocompatibility. From a series of BSA-modified samples with varying loading ratios, two were selected for detailed characterization and compared with the unmodified sample. The magnetization curves exhibit negligible hysteresis, with near-zero coercivity and remanent magnetization at room temperature. The magnetic core size (7.5 ± 0.3 nm), obtained by fitting the magnetization curve with the Langevin function, was in good agreement with the mean core size determined by TEM (6.0 ± 0.1 nm). The amount of BSA bound to the IONPs was quantified using two complementary methods—thermogravimetric analysis and magnetic measurements—producing consistent results. These measurements revealed that a similar amount of BSA was bound to the nanoparticles in both selected samples, indicating a saturation. Furthermore, zero-field-cooled/field-cooled (ZFC/FC) measurements showed that the average blocking temperature of the modified nanoparticles is comparable to that of the unmodified ones. This implies that BSA encapsulated small clusters of nanoparticles rather than individual particles, thereby forming larger stabilized objects.

publish: Proceedings of the 2025 6th International Conference on Computer Science and Management Technology

title: Design and Application of a Thematic Exhibition Hall for Cultural Tourism Industry Integration and Innovation Using Virtual Reality Technology
authors: Ruohan Wang, Quan Zhou

title: An Endogenous Security Defense Strategy for Adversarial Robustness in UAV Target Recognition
authors: Yuezhong Zhang, Shaoxun Liu, Jingfeng Zhang, Shiyu Wang, Yongshang Li, Peng Fu, Wei Qing, Zhen Zhang
publish: 2025 International Conference on Cyber Resilience and Endogenous Safety &amp; Security (CRESS)
abstract: Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs), particularly rotorcraft, are extensively utilized in applications such as inspection and security surveillance, where the YOLO series algorithms have demonstrated excellent performance in real-time target detection. However, vision-based target recognition systems are vulnerable to adversarial example attacks. When confronted with carefully crafted adversarial examples, traditional single YOLO models suffer a drastic decline in recognition rates, posing significant security risks. This paper constructs a model library comprising diverse target detection algorithms, specifically incorporating non-YOLO series algorithms to enhance heterogeneity and reduce the impact of adversarial example transferability. This constitutes a key strategy for improving the robustness of UAV ground target recognition under adversarial scenarios. The Dynamic role in this multi-model fusion, enhancing the overall system security and resilience by introducing dynamism and diversity. Furthermore, a dynamic voting and arbitration mechanism synthesizes the outputs of the multiple algorithms to effectively filter out interference from adversarial examples. Experiments on public datasets demonstrate that the proposed method maintains a high recognition rate on clean images and exhibits significantly superior robustness against typical adversarial attacks, such as FGSM and PGD, compared to single-model approaches and other existing defense methods.

title: MATRIX: Mixed-Basis Architecture Using Towering Field Representations for Extendable GrøStl State Blocks
authors: Yuta Kodera, Yasuyuki Nogami
publish: 2025 11th International Workshop on Signal Design and Its Applications in Communications (IWSDA)
abstract: This paper introduces a novel matrix representation for the Grøstl hash function using a Mersenne prime and a tower of fields. Our approach focuses on the structure of a tower of fields, and we propose to regard it as a matrix to reduce the circuit size by conducting the round operations without lookup tables. Especially, we adopt a mixed basis architecture using a Gauss normal basis and a polynomial basis to build a tower of fields. This approach allows a simple modular arithmetic and optimal table-less round operation that inherits the basic principle of the Grøstl design.

title: Adaptive Trust Aware Interfaces for Human AI Collaboration: Evaluating Calibration Approach
authors: Snehal Rathi, Saanchi Itkelwar, Naman Chordia
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: Trust miscalibration when users either undertrust accurate recommendations or over-trust flawed outputssubstantially decreases the effectiveness of human-AI collaboration. While there exist various ways to measure trust, it is rare to compare adaptive interface strategies in a systematic manner. We investigate five different trust calibration approaches in a synthetic benchmark (Trust-Bench) that spans three trust states across three domains. These include a noadaptation baseline, rule-based heuristics, two variants of reinforcement learning (RL) trained with Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO), and a hybrid approach. Rule-based approaches performed the best among the five tested, and all adaptive strategies outperformed the baseline. Despite extensive training, the RL approaches did not learn effective conditional adaptations-using persuasive interfaces for under-trust users inconsistently, and failing to activate cautious interfaces in overtrust scenarios despite explicit incentives for doing so. Detailed analysis identifies weak reward signal strength for adaptation and difficulty in learning multi-condition logic as primary failure modes. Results show that, while deterministic approaches provide reliable trust calibration, algorithmic limitations are essential in guiding further development.

title: Advancements in Machine Learning for Epileptic Seizure Detection from EEG in the Last Decade: A Comprehensive Review
authors: Anusree R, Jerrin Thomas Panachakel, Ancy S. A., Chirutha Kottantharayil
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: A chronic illness, epilepsy is characterized by recurrent seizures brought on by aberrant electrical signals generated by damaged brain cells. An abrupt, uncontrollable spike in electrical activity in a brain cell that alters behavior, movements, emotions, and consciousness levels is called a seizure. For management and intervention to be effective, epileptic seizures must be detected accurately and promptly. Since electroencephalogram (EEG) signals give a clear picture of brain activity, they are commonly used to identify seizures. With the introduction of new algorithms and the growing availability of EEG datasets, recent advances in machine learning have increased the precision and effectiveness of seizure detection. With a focus on important methods from 2014 to 2024, this study examines current machinelearning approaches for epileptic seizure detection utilizing EEG data. We assess methods focusing on feature extraction, model selection, and validation techniques, comparing performance across single-channel and multi-channel EEG systems.

title: Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning for Coordinated Routing and Traffic Engineering in Distributed Data Center Systems
authors: Selvamani Ramasamy
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: Modern data center systems support a wide range of latency sensitive applications, while their routing and traffic engineering mechanisms remain dependent on static or heuristic driven strategies. Traditional approaches such as equal cost path routing and rule-based traffic engineering often fail to respond effectively to rapid fluctuations in workload intensity, leading to queue accumulation, uneven link utilization, and unpredictable latency under high demand. These limitations highlight the need for routing models that can interpret dynamic traffic behavior and adjust decisions in coordinated manner. This study introduces a learning-based routing framework that integrates a Transformer encoder with an Actor-Critic Multi Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) architecture. The Transformer encoder extracts temporal dependencies from workload sequences derived from Google Cluster traces, providing each routing agent with a richer state representation. The multi agent framework enables every data center node to act as an autonomous decision maker while still coordinating actions through shared environment feedback. The agents learn routing policies that minimize latency and reduce congestion by anticipating traffic variations. Experimental evaluation demonstrates that the framework maintains consistently low latency between <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1. 8 2 m s}$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{2. 2 1 m s}$</tex>, achieves a packet delivery ratio of 99.21%, sustains effective goodput of 98.74%, and distributes link utilization more evenly across the network fabric. The policy converges quickly and shows stable decision behavior with minimal oscillation. These findings indicate that combining Transformer based representations with coordinated reinforcement learning provides a promising direction for improving routing efficiency in modern data center environments. The results pave the way for valuable insights into adaptive traffic engineering and intelligent network control.

title: CT-RAG: A Deep Retrieval-Augmented Generation Framework for Automated Cyber Threat Intelligence and Severity Assessment
authors: K S Chandrakala, T Murali Mohan, Praveena Mallampalli, T V Satyasheela
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: The rapid escalation of cyberattacks needs autonomous models capable of high-fidelity threat identification, assessment of severity, and incident-level reasoning. Conventional analytical pipelines depend on static scoring mechanisms or shallow models, struggle to extract the contextual, semantic, and behavioral complexity inherent in modern threat landscapes. To overcome these limitations, this work proposes CT-RAG, a fully deep-learning–driven Cyber Threat Retrieval-Augmented Generation framework trained and evaluated on the CTI-HAL human-annotated cyber-threat intelligence dataset. CTI-HAL provides rich natural-language threat narratives, Indicators of Compromise (IoCs), behavioral descriptors, and MITRE ATT&CK tactic-technique mappings, enabling CT-RAG to learn deep contextual representations reflective of real analyst workflows. CT-RAG introduces four core innovations (1) a Deep Hybrid Threat Classifier (D-HTC) that fuses transformer-based text encoders, CNN-based behavior extractors, and BiLSTM contextual embeddings into a unified deep fusion layer for high-accuracy attack classification (2) an Adaptive Deep Risk Prediction Network that generates context-aware risk scores using IoC embeddings, severity cues, and ATT&CK tactic-weighted representations (3) a Threat Retrieval-Augmented Summarizer, where a transformer decoder generates enriched, analyst-style threat summaries by integrating retrieved vectors from external CTI repositories and (4) a Rule-Augmented Severity Engine that refines severity outputs using expert-designed TTP rules to ensure explainable and operationally interpretable assessments. Experimental results on the CTI-HAL severity-balanced subset demonstrate that CT-RAG significantly outperforms standalone deep-learning baselines in attack classification accuracy, risk estimation reliability, and intelligence-quality summarization.

title: An Iot-Based Smart Glove for Real-Time Tremor Detection and Suppression in Parkinson's Patients
authors: Gautham Sai, Hrishikesh S, Gunisha Kaur, Gowrinandana R Kaimal, Aswathy K Nair, Prajitha S
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: Parkinson's disease is a brain disorder and a progressive neurological disorder that causes unintended or uncontrollable movements, such as shaking, stiffness, and difficulty with balance and coordination, thus affecting the patient's ability to perform daily tasks. The current treatment options can be very expensive, invasive, as well inaccessible in a lot of regions. To overcome this issue, our project proposes a cost-effective, non-invasive and wearable anti-tremor glove that detects and suppresses tremors in real time. This glove has an MPU6050 motion sensor to monitor hand movement and uses an Arduino Nano, which helps process the data with the help of a thresholdbased algorithm. It also uses vibration motors on each finger to deliver counter vibrations to help reduce the tremors. Our system uses a Bluetooth module that enables real-time data transmission to a custom-designed mobile application that visualises tremor intensity and gives live charts. The glove's lightweight design, affordability, and comfort make it ideal for everyday use and can be integrated for home-based care. Observations show effective tremor suppression without restricting voluntary motion, highlighting its potential as an accessible, assistive portable device.

title: Fairer Noiseless Broadcast Source Coding
authors: Serdar Boztaş
publish: 2025 11th International Workshop on Signal Design and Its Applications in Communications (IWSDA)
abstract: We present a noiseless source coding problem in a broadcast environment and supply an improved solution to this problem. A transmitter wishes to transmit a binary random vector <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$X_{1}^{n}=\left(X_{1}, \ldots, X_{n}\right)$</tex> to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$n$</tex> receivers/users over a noiseless channel, where user <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$k$</tex> is only interested in the random variable <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$X_{k}$</tex>. A source encoding is a binary sequence \begin{equation*} f=\left(f_{1}\left(X_{1}^{n}\right), \ldots, f_{j}\left(X_{1}^{n}\right), \ldots\right) \end{equation*} which is chosen by the transmitter. The time at which the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$k^{\text {th }}$</tex> receiver determines <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$X_{k}$</tex> (with zero error probability) is denoted <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$[\ell(f, k)]$</tex>. Define the performance measure \begin{equation*} L(n)=\min _{f} \max _{k} \mathbb{E}[\ell(f, k)], \end{equation*} where the minimization is over all possible encodings, and aim to approach this minimization by practical schemes. We present a new and fairer scheme for this problem.

title: Software Development Using Transformer-Based LLMs
authors: Petr V. Gorshkov
publish: 2026 ElCon Conference of Young Researchers in Computing &amp; Processing, and Information Security (ElCon-CP)
abstract: Increasing competition in software development is accelerating the pace of delivery. At the same time, it remains crucial to maintain software quality and prevent budget overruns. One potential solution to the issues of low quality and excessive costs is the use of transformer-based large language models (LLMs). In recent years, an increasing number of development teams have sought to adopt artificial intelligence for code generation, with the intention of partially or potentially fully replacing junior-level development tasks. This study includes a comprehensive literature review and extensive testing of different prompt engineering approaches for LLMs. The results identify which software development tasks can be effectively delegated to transformer-based models, how to formulate prompts to achieve the desired outcomes, and what level of task decomposition ensures acceptable code quality.

title: Parkinson's Disease Diagnosis Using Spatio -Temporal Gait Feature Extraction
authors: Mythili N, Janani B
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: Parkinson's Disease is a progressive neurological disorder that affects millions of people worldwide. Early detection is crucial for effective management. This work outlines the development of a mobile and web-based application for non-invasive detection and monitoring of Parkinson's Disease using deep learning models, specifically CNN-LSTM networks, based on gait video analysis. The system is designed to provide a fast, accurate and convenient diagnostic solution that can be accessed from any device with an internet connection. Unlike traditional clinical assessments that rely heavily on subjective observations or costly imaging techniques, the application uses patients' walking patterns to spot early signs of Parkinson's. To ensure user data security, the system includes features like encrypted storage and secure authentication. For evaluation, metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall and F1-score have been calculated, showing the high potential of the proposed system. This work significantly improves healthcare accessibility, offering an effective and non-invasive tool for early detection and monitoring of Parkinson's Disease, especially in remote or underserved areas.

title: Leveraging Generative AI for Cognitive Skill Enhancement in Low-Performing Programming Learners
authors: R Kumaran, A. Mohan
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: This study investigates the efficacy of Generative Artificial Intelligence (GenAI) in enhancing cognitive programming skills specifically problem decomposition, algorithmic thinking, and debuggingamong low-performing novice learners. We developed and deployed a structured pedagogical intervention, the Generative AI-Driven Cognitive Scaffolding (GAIDCS) framework, which integrates a Large Language Model (LLM) tutor into a 12-week introductory Python course. The framework employs prompt engineering to guide the AI in providing Socratic questioning, incremental code generation, and misconceptiontargeted feedback, rather than direct solutions. Participants <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\mathbf{N}=\mathbf{1 2 0})$</tex> were divided into an experimental group (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$n=60$</tex>) using the GAIDCS framework and a control group (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{n}=60$</tex>) using traditional teaching aids. Cognitive skill proficiency was measured via a validated pre-test and post-test instrument. Additionally, learning traces (prompt history, code versions) were analyzed for metacognitive engagement. Results demonstrated a statistically significant improvement in the experimental group. Post-test scores for problem decomposition showed an 82.3 % accuracy rate (vs. 58.7 % control), algorithmic logic scored <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{7 8. 9 \%}$</tex> (vs. <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5 5. 1 \%}$</tex> control), and debugging efficiency improved to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{7 6. 5 \%}$</tex> (vs. <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5 1. 2 \%}$</tex> control). A paired samples t-test revealed a large effect size (Cohen's <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{d}=1.45, \mathrm{p}<0.001$</tex>). Furthermore, analysis of learning traces indicated a 65 % increase in metacognitive prompts (“explain your approach”) issued by learners in the later weeks, correlating with higher post-test gains <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(r=0.71)$</tex>. These findings suggest that strategically scaffolded GenAI interactions can significantly enhance core cognitive programming skills by promoting active reasoning and mitigating overreliance. The study posits that the key to efficacy lies not in the AI's capability to generate code, but in its structured role as a reflective partner that externalizes and guides the problem-solving process for struggling learners.

title: The Life Cycle of Electronics Research Topics: Navigating a Complex Ecosystem [Reflections and Historical Perspectives]
authors: Yongfu Li, Franco Maloberti
publish: IEEE Circuits and Systems Magazine
abstract: The pervasive integration of electronic devices into modern life has generated unprecedented interest in electronics research, fundamentally altering the natural trajectory and lifecycle of research. This article examines the genesis, evolution, and decline of electronics research topics through a life-cycle model, analysing critical influencing factors, including funding dynamics, quality metrics, peer-review systems, and industrial-military interests. Using the historical evolution of electrical filters as a case study, we illustrate how research topics progress from initial stimulus to massive investigation and eventually to maturity or decline. We discuss the strategic use of buzzwords, geographic concentration of research efforts, and the tension between academic freedom and external pressures. The analysis reveals that while funding and institutional support are vital, excessive restrictions, metric-driven evaluation, and confidentiality can prematurely extinguish promising research directions. We conclude with practical guidance for researchers seeking to choose sustainable topics while preserving the curiosity-driven spirit essential for genuine innovation, emphasizing that long-term success correlates more closely with intellectual freedom and happiness than with quantitative measures of productivity.

title: Multi-Modal Brain Tumor Segmentation via Multi-Category Interaction and Graph Co-Reasoning
authors: Baoyao Yang, Dongzhe Li, Chong Yin, Fei Lyu, Xiaochen He
publish: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia
abstract: Numerous multi-modal tumor segmentation techniques have been developed to identify affected regions in brain images, thereby enhancing the intelligence of diagnosis. Nevertheless, the morphological and semantic knowledge of brain tumors in multi-modal images remains incompletely explored, and spatial relations between multiple categories are still under-researched. The limited exploration of multi-modal images leads to an irrational distribution of tumor areas in the predictive results. To address this issue, this work proposes a Graph Co-reasoning Network (GCoN), in which the morphological and semantic information between regions and contours interact to guide the graph reasoning, facilitating the recognition of tumor areas. The GCoN also incorporates a Multi-category Interaction Module (MIM), which models the dependency between multiple categories, thus enabling more accurate subregion localization of brain tumors. In addition, we derive a unified graph re-projection, which re-projects the results of intra- and inter-Graph Attention Network (GAT) into the prediction space through a unified assignment matrix. Experimental results demonstrate that our method outperforms the current state-of-the-art methods, achieving 87.54%, 87.76% and 90.02% average Dice scores on the BraTS2019, BraTS2020, and BraTS2021 datasets, respectively.

title: Image Classification and Explainability Analysis Using GPU-based CUDA-Q Hybrid Quantum Neural Networks
authors: Sieun Kim, Inwhee Joe
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Explainable artificial intelligence (XAI) remains underexplored in quantum machine learning (QML) due to limited quantum computational resources and the lack of practical interpretability techniques. In this study, we implement the Local Interpretable Model-Agnostic Explanations (LIME) algorithm within the CUDA-Q framework and propose an interpretable QML analysis method using GPU-accelerated quantum simulation. All experiments are conducted on a GPU-based simulator rather than real quantum hardware, enabling stable large-scale sampling required by LIME and ensuring reproducible results. We design a binary classification model based on a single-qubit variational circuit composed of parameterized RY and RX rotations, and the model is trained with gradient-based optimization. The performance is evaluated on the MNIST, KMNIST, Fashion-MNIST, and CIFAR-10 datasets using training and test accuracy as metrics. By applying LIME, the classification outcomes of the quantum model are visualized and interpreted in a human-understandable form, demonstrating that meaningful interpretability can be achieved even under limited qubit counts and constrained computational environments. Furthermore, we analyze the trade-off between simulator performance and model accuracy, confirming that GPU-based CUDA-Q simulation provides a scalable and practical research environment for QML. This work offers a concrete pathway toward transparent and interpretable quantum machine learning.

title: A Dispersion-Invariant Reconfigurable Repetition Rate Manipulation Technique Based on Intensity Modulation and the Temporal Talbot Effect_supp1-3679386.pdf
authors: Yamei Zhang
publish: N/A
abstract: Flexible control of the repetition rate of optical pulse sequences is a critical requirement in modern optoelectronic systems. Traditional repetition rate manipulation (RRM) methods based on the temporal Talbot effect are constrained to integer control factors and require dynamic adjustment of dispersion parameters, which limits their feasibility. To overcome these limitations, we propose a dispersion-invariant reconfigurable RRM technique based on the temporal Talbot effect. The RRM factor in the proposed method is reconfigurable and supports RRM by a family of N/2 factors, where N is a positive integer. In this method, the reconfigurability is achieved by applying a periodic electrical signal to an intensity modulator synchronized with the input pulse sequence. This electrical signal directly defines the RRM factor. Importantly, the required dispersion remains fixed regardless of the RRM factor and depends on the initial repetition period of the input optical pulse sequence. This method overcomes the inherent limitations of the conventional temporal Talbot method. As a proof of concept, we demonstrate RRM with configurable factors ranging from 1/2 to 17/2. Both theoretical analysis and experimental results validate the capability of the proposed scheme to realize N/2 RRM using a straightforward system architecture. Furthermore, we conduct an in-depth study on the impact of high-order dispersion on the stability of the repetition-rate-multiplied pulse sequences. This progress not only expands the practicality of the temporal Talbot effect but also provides a promising solution for applications such as ultrafast signal processing.

title: On the Euclidean and Hermitian Hulls of a Class of Twisted Generalized Elliptic Curve Codes
authors: Xiaofeng Liu, Jun Zhang, Fang-Wei Fu
publish: 2025 11th International Workshop on Signal Design and Its Applications in Communications (IWSDA)
abstract: Motivated by the researches of Euclidean and Hermitian hulls of generalized Reed-Solomon (TGRS) codes, generalized elliptic curve codes (GECCs), algebraic geometry (AG) codes and the related twisted codes, we study the Euclidean and Hermitian hulls of a class of twisted generalized elliptic curve codes (TGECCs) in this paper.

title: Minimizing Demand Forecasting Error for a Wholesale Produce Distributor Using Classical Optimization
authors: Beladiya Nikunj, Nishant Kumar
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: The issue of predicting demand at exact accuracy in the wholesale fresh produce distribution business demands to strike a balance between the adversarial stockout and spoil risks. This paper addresses a real-world optimization problem faced by a produce wholesaler, Prime Crop Suppliers, formulating a model to minimize demand forecasting error utilizing classical optimization techniques. The formulation is a Data Fitting Problem formulated in the Least Squares method in which the goal is to reduce the total squared error between a linear regression model and past sales. The resultant objective function is unconstrained, convex and quadratic in nature and is thus best solved by iterative classical optimization methods like Conjugate Gradient and Quasi-Newton methods, which lie at the heart of the course. This model uses several predictor variables price, marketing spending, and seasonality to explain the dynamics of complicated demand of perishable goods. It gives three realistic test cases which represent the various categories of products (staple, seasonal and special products), which will have different demand characteristics. This model offers a pragmatic yet mathematically sound model that could be solved through the gradient-based optimization methods discussed in this course, thus providing a direct way to a better inventory management, less wastage and increased profitability.

title: A Semi-Supervised Hybrid Deep Learning Model with Mobile Net- GCN for Interpretable Diabetic Retinopathy Detection
authors: P. Renuka, S. Fahimuddin, Shaik Mahammed Saif, Simhadri Bathula, Shivani Mallela, Chandrika Padamatinti
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: DR causes significant impairment of the vision of patients with diabetes, largely due to late diagnosis and therapy. In a bid to solve this serious problem, the latest research has proposed more sophisticated diagnostic systems developed by using the strong potential of DenseNet as a feature extractor. The dataset to be utilized in the research was obtained on Kaggle and it comprised 506 training images and 217 validation images which were divided into five different stages of DR; namely, Mild, Moderate, No_DR, Proliferative_DR and Severe. The stages are critical to the evolution of DR and are plotted on the following scales; Mild (0), Moderate (1), No DR (2), Proliferative DR (3), and Severe (4). Medical imaging is complicated, so DenseNet is especially suitable to this task. Its distinctive structure, which maximizes gradient flow and facilitates feature reuse, is most suited to large-scale medical image datasets, including retinal scans. The model performance will be evaluated on the basis of the weight learnt during the training process by testing them on a special validation set to ensure an exemplary level of accuracy and precision, in the prediction. Not only will this improve the rate of the diagnostic accuracy, but it will also assist in the earlier diagnosis of diabetic retinopathy which will enable the health care practitioners to provide effective treatment in a timely manner. After all, this system also promises to help improve patient outcomes substantially, with the faster and more accurate diagnosis of DR possible.

title: A Two-Stage Queue-Aware Prioritized ACB Scheme for Massive Machine-Type Communications
authors: Yuxuan Wen, Liu Yang, Chaoyuan Bai, Heng Liu, Ping Wang, Xinhang Tan
publish: 2025 11th International Workshop on Signal Design and Its Applications in Communications (IWSDA)
abstract: The rapid growth of the Internet of Things (IoT) has posed significant challenges for massive Machine-Type Communications (mMTC), including access collisions and signaling congestion. Traditional Access Class Barring (ACB) schemes often overlook device heterogeneity and dynamic queue states, limiting their effectiveness. This paper proposes a queue-aware prioritized ACB algorithm (QPAC-PSACB) that models device access using a two-dimensional Markov chain. Devices with fuller queues are assigned higher priority through differentiated ACB factors. A two-stage ACB mechanism reclaims unused preambles to improve resource utilization. An optimization problem is formulated to maximize packet success rate by deriving optimal parameters from the Markov chain's steady-state distribution. Simulation results show that QPAC-PSACB outperforms conventional methods in packet success rate, failure rate, and access delay, effectively alleviating congestion in buffer-limited largescale IoT networks.

title: A Multi-Stage High-Dimensional Learning Pipeline for Fraud Detection on Online Payments
authors: Duggirala Sarath Babu, Koganti Gayathri
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: Presently, digital payments are becoming more complex due to increased online fraud. Many people worldwide rely mainly on online and digital payments. Detecting online payment fraud is becoming increasingly complex, resulting in significant losses for users. In this paper, we design a Multi-Stage High-Dimensional Learning Pipeline (MS-HDLP) model to detect online fraud payments and handle complex situations accurately. We can identify these fraudulent payments based on online transactions, payment behavior, and payment mode. Pre-processing is dealt with by the dynamic frontier-smoothing layer, which includes noise filtering, normalization, and advanced class-balancing strategies (SMOTE, ADASYN, and cluster-based re-sampling) to address severe class imbalance in fraud datasets. In the second stage, a hybrid feature selection method, comprising mutual information and variance thresholds, as well as embedded model selectors, eliminates redundant features and retains useful discriminative features. The last stage uses a balanced ensemble learning framework comprising optimized Gradient Boosting, Balanced Random Forest, and cost-sensitive SVM classifiers to enhance robustness against mini-class misclassification. Experiment results on benchmark online payment datasets show that the proposed MS-HDLP outperforms traditional detection methods across accuracy, recall, specificity, precision, and F1-score.

title: Multi-Metric Fusion Based Quantitative Analysis Method for Comprehensive Cyber Resilience
authors: Gangtao Han, Fengpeng Xiong, Zhiqing Tang, Gaofeng Pan, Changhao Du, Dalong Zhang
publish: 2025 International Conference on Cyber Resilience and Endogenous Safety &amp; Security (CRESS)
abstract: The growing complexity of cybersecurity systems and the escalating severity of security threats have rendered the concept of traditional absolute security impractical. As an alternative paradigm, cyber resilience has emerged as a critical issue which focus on ensuring business continuity and system availability. At the meantime, researches on quantitative evaluation of cyber resilience also attract worldwide attention. This paper proposes a quantitative multi-metric fusion method for comprehensive cyber resilience evaluation, which integrates multiple key performance metrics to provide a comprehensive evaluation. Specifically, multiple metrics are measured separately under various perturbations, and then the entropy weight method is used to determine the weight of each metric. Additionally, a revised 2-additive fuzzy measures method is proposed to figure out the interactions among different metrics. The resilience value of each metric is quantified by the area under the curve (AUC) method. Finally, fuzzy integration is applied to synthesize these values into a comprehensive cyber resilience score. The proposed method is executed on a Web server system to analyze its comprehensive cyber resilience across distinct perturbation scenarios. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method not only accurately captures the comprehensive cyber resilience across several individual dimensions, but also effectively reveals the complex effects of multi-dimensional interactions on the comprehensive cyber resilience.

title: SLMark: Robust Watermarking Against Image Editing via Simulation-Based Noise Layer and Low-Frequency Embedding
authors: Mingyang Li, Yichao Tang, Yuliang Xue, Sheng Li, Zhenxing Qian, Xinpeng Zhang, Ji He, Hongda Li
publish: 2025 International Conference on Cyber Resilience and Endogenous Safety &amp; Security (CRESS)
abstract: The rapid advancement of generative artificial intelligence (AI) enables efficient image editing but poses new challenges to image copyright protection. Existing watermarking methods struggle against AI-generated content (AIGC) editing, as traditional methods rely on hand-crafted features and deep learning-based approaches cannot effectively simulate AIGC editing distortions. To address this issue, we propose SLMark, a robust watermarking scheme against image editing. Our framework incorporates a Simulation-based noise layer trained on real AIGC editing outputs to fit editing distortions, and embeds watermarks in the stable Low-frequency domain obtained via Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) to enhance robustness. Extensive experiments demonstrate that SLMark achieves superior performance against both conventional signal processing and AIGC editing attacks while maintaining higher image quality.

title: AgriVisionNet: Dual-Stream Hybrid Transformer-Gated CNN Framework for Fine-Grained Rice Leaf Disease Recognition and Severity Assessment
authors: M. Rajeswari, R. Jeena, G.Mary Valantina, P Sangeetha, P. Muneeshwari
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: Rice leaf diseases are on the rise and this threat to food security of the world is especially a major concern in agricultural based economies that ensure crop management sustainability with accurate and prompt diagnosis. The paper is a proposal of AgriVisionNet, a new Dual-Stream Hybrid Transformer-Gated Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) that is aimed at fine-grained detection and severity evaluation of rice leaf diseases. The suggested framework combines a transformer encoder to capture the long range spatial dependencies with a gated convolutional stream that lays stress on the local texture and lesion specific images. It uses feature fusion attention module that combines both contextual and visual information and optimizes disease feature discriminative representation. The model was trained and tested on an augmented version of the RiceLeaf-2025 dataset with a classification accuracy of 98.72, which was better than baseline CNN and transformer-based architectures. The results of the experiments prove that AgriVisionNet offers a high level of performance in the changing lighting and background conditions in addition to the ability to determine the disease severity with high accuracy. This mixed-method solution creates a channel of AI-assisted intelligent agriculture, which allows monitoring in real time, early detection of disease, and predicting crop yield optimally.

title: Comparison of GPU, FPGA, and VPU Hardware Accelerators for Edge AI Tasks in Cyber-Physical Systems
authors: Alexander A. Stetsenko, Polina E. Tsareva
publish: 2026 ElCon Conference of Young Researchers in Computing &amp; Processing, and Information Security (ElCon-CP)
abstract: Modern Cyber-Physical Systems, such as autonomous vehicles and industrial robots, require real-time execution of AI models directly at the network edge. Cloud processing introduces critical latency and standard CPUs are insufficient for these computational demands, making on-device “Edge AI” essential. This paper comparatively analyzes the primary hardware accelerators: Graphics Processing Units, Field-Programmable Gate Arrays, and dedicated Neural Processing Units. We evaluate these architectures against key CPS metrics: performance-per-watt, inference latency, development complexity, and reconfigurability. The analysis shows GPUs offer high throughput but consume significant power. FPGAs provide superior flexibility and low latency for custom algorithms but require significant development effort. VPUs deliver exceptional energy efficiency for common neural networks but lack flexibility. The findings conclude that no single solution is optimal. The selection is a critical engineering trade-off determined by the CPS application’s specific constraints on power, cost, and development time.

title: Unmasking Deepfakes: An AI Approach with Audio-Visual Analysis
authors: Kailasam Selvaraj, M. Madhumitha, T. Priya Roshini, S. Nithish, R.M. Jaya Priyangaa
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: The rapid advancement of generative models has enabled the generation of unrealistically convincing manipulated audio-visual content, commonly referred to as DeepFakes. This type of content poses a serious threat to digital trust, security, biometric authentication systems, and the authenticity of digital media. In order to address these concerns, this work proposes a robust DeepFake detection system that jointly takes into consideration both input visual and audio signals. Different from methods that rely exclusively on either facial appearance or speech patterns, the system integrates deep spatial feature extraction using a ResNet architecture with temporal audio modeling through Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks. This framework extracts from the visual modality facial texture inconsistencies, micro-expressions, and blending artifacts, while from the audio modality, it models speech features, pitch variations, and lip-audio synchronization patterns. In contrast, the presented approach integrates high-level abstractions from these two modalities to identify the very subtle incongruities that usually arise in manipulated content. Extensive experiments over benchmark datasets demonstrate that the proposed hybrid framework significantly outperforms unimodal techniques in terms of accuracy, compression resistance, and cross-dataset generalizability. Moreover, the model provides reliable performance under low-resolution and noisy conditions, which makes it suitable for practical applications with resource-limited deployments.

title: SafeLinkX: Hybrid Dsrc-5G/6G Optimization for Safety-Critical Vehicular Communication
authors: J. Surendiran, K. Malarkodi
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: The fast development of autonomous vehicles requires low-latency and high throughput communications systems that are reliable to guarantee safety as well as efficient information transfer. The conventional vehicular communication approaches (DSRC and cellular 5G/6G networks) are limited in their nature: DSRC allows ultra-low latency to send safety-related messages, but with limited bandwidth, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5 G} / \mathbf{6 G}$</tex> offers high throughput at the expense of higher latency and unreliability in congested traffic conditions. A solution to these issues is proposed in this research project, which is a hybrid DSRC-5G/6G car-to-car communication system called SafeLinkX that uses deep reinforcement learning (DRL) to select the dynamic channel. It uses realistic vehicle motion traces of the SUMO and LuST databases in combination with a network simulation of NS-3/Veins to produce the DRL agent. SafeLinkX is smart enough to give safety-related messages priority using DSRC whilst delivering highthroughput cooperative and infotainment data over <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5 G} / \mathbf{6 G}$</tex> connections. The performance measurements reveal that the response time, packet delivery ratio, throughput, and the accuracy of decisions are vastly better than the response time, packet delivery ratio, throughput, and accuracy of decisions in case of DSRC-only, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5 G} / \mathbf{6 G}$</tex>-only, and the tools that do not start. The findings confirm SafeLinkX as a strong, flexible and scalable solution to a heterogeneous vehicular environment, and assures safety-first communications usage without the cost of bandwidth use. The current work adds a new DRL-based solution to hybrid V2X communication, between the needs of autonomous driving and the future-generation networking technology.

title: Phase-Derived Temporal Fingerprints for Cross-Domain Detection of Cyber-Physical Attacks in Power Networks
authors: Mohammed Al-Khayyat Member, Mohammed Al-Hubaishi
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: Detecting cyber-physical attacks on power networks is challenging due to the subtle and coordinated nature of anomalies across multiple phase measurements. This paper introduces Phase-Derived Temporal Fingerprints (PDTF), a concise and physically grounded feature set that captures per-phase statistics, short-lag temporal derivatives, and inter-phase relationships within sliding windows. A hybrid detection framework is integrated, combining a multiclass gradient-boosting classifier with calibrated one-vs-rest detectors tailored for prioritized attack types. Evaluation is conducted using rigorous cross-domain validation across stratified, temporal, and device-based splits, alongside a systematic leakage mitigation process that removes capture metadata to ensure genuine generalization. On leakage-mitigated splits, the proposed approach achieves a 153.4 % relative improvement in macro-F1 score (from <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.086 \pm 0.031$</tex> to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.218 \pm 0.074$</tex>) compared to the baseline. Complementary ablation studies, permutation importance analysis, and correlation analyses confirm the physical interpretability of the selected features and the effectiveness of metadata removal in producing robust detection. All code and artifacts supporting these findings are publicly archived to facilitate reproducibility and further research.

title: Multi-Device Context-Aware Indoor Localization Using Advanced Feature Engineering and Ensemble Learning Techniques
authors: Ahmet Berat Güçlü, Mohammed Al-Hubaishi
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: Because WiFi networks are ubiquitous, and WiFienabled devices are becoming cheap and widespread, WiFi-based indoor localization is promising. We propose a device-free WiFi fingerprinting approach based on ensemble learning, which combines rich feature engineering with robust tree-based and gradient boosting models, and this approach is presented in this paper. First, we extract a variety of statistical, frequency-domain, context-aware, and device-free features from raw RSSI readings, and then we train and evaluate multiple ensemble models under different train-test splits to improve generalization. On a realworld WiFi dataset, our best model achieves a classification accuracy of 97.74%, which is approximately <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$15-20 {\%}$</tex> higher than traditional WiFi fingerprinting baselines, moreover, the proposed method is robust to different devices and environmental conditions. The experimental results indicate that the proposed device-free WiFi fingerprinting approach can achieve accurate and efficient indoor localization, and is feasible for practical deployment, therefore, the proposed approach is a viable solution.
