title: Adaptive Bayesian Optimization for Online Bandit Model Partitioning and Resource Allocation in Split Federated Learning_supp1-3674795.pdf
authors: Jia Yan
publish: N/A
abstract: Federated learning (FL) has been recognized as a promising paradigm to support distributed AI model training among wireless devices (WDs) under the coordination of an edge server (ES) without sharing local datasets. To alleviate computation burden of resource-limited WDs, model partitioning that leverages computing capability at the ES is further integrated into FL, yielding the split (S) FL framework. In this paper, we study online bandit model partitioning and resource allocation for SFL over dynamic wireless networks, aiming to minimize overall energy-latency cost (ELC). Unlike prior works focusing on offline static or online gradient-based model splitting and resource allocation, we consider a practical setting where the analytical expression of ELC function is unavailable, and instead only the function values at queried points are revealed. To tackle such a challenging mixed-integer non-linear programming problem in the online bandit context, novel Bayesian optimization (BO)-based approaches are put forth by relying on a Gaussian process (GP)-based surrogate model to actively select the model splitting points and resource allocation decisions per round via the low-complexity acquisition. Besides incorporating training model-specific structural information in the kernel design of the GP surrogate, an ensemble of GP models with data-adaptive weights is further leveraged to capture system dynamics. To cope with the challenging combinatorial nature and strong coupling over mixed action space during acquisition, an efficient alternating optimization approach is proposed building upon a novel contextual local search method. Numerical tests demonstrate that the proposed BO-based approaches outperform the contemporary baselines under various practical SFL settings.

title: AI-Driven Interference Mitigation Between PMCW and OFDM Systems for IoT Spectrum Coexistence
authors: Heekwon Yoon, Yonghee Lee, Jeong-Hoon Park, Seongwook Lee
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: We propose an artificial intelligence (AI)-based interference mitigation method to enable the coexistence of phase-modulated continuous wave (PMCW) and orthogonal frequency-division multiplexing (OFDM) systems in spectrum-sharing internet of things (IoT) environments. Existing mitigation techniques struggle to address the complex mutual interference arising from the structural mismatches of heterogeneous systems in dense IoT deployments. To overcome this challenge, we first formulate a signal model that accounts for PMCW–OFDM coexistence and characterizes bidirectional interference between the two systems. Subsequently, we propose a U-Net-based deep learning model that first applies time-domain thresholding as a pre-processing step. The model uses complex-valued inputs and an atrous spatial pyramid pooling bottleneck to capture multi-scale contextual features in the latent space, where ambiguous structures caused by interference can be distinguished from true target components. The proposed model is trained across various vehicular scenarios to enhance robustness under varying interference conditions. Simulation results for both systems under diverse vehicular scenarios demonstrate that the proposed method achieves signal-to-interference-plus-noise ratio improvements of up to 14.47 dB and masked peak signal-to-noise ratio improvements of up to 22.19 dB across various signal-to-noise ratios, compared to conventional baselines such as denoising convolutional neural network (DnCNN) and denoising diffusion probabilistic model (DDPM). In addition, the proposed method reduces latency by 12.5% and 58.7% compared to DnCNN and DDPM, respectively, indicating improved computational efficiency. These results highlight its potential for reliable sensing and offer spatial awareness to facilitate coexistence strategies in large-scale IoT networks.

title: Building Intuition in Amplifier Design Using Load-Pull Based Multi-Objective Visualization
authors: Stefan Stroessner, Scott McCall, Jacob Kravits, Zoya Popović
publish: IEEE Journal of Microwaves
abstract: This paper presents an efficient method for analyzing trade-offs during various stages of a power amplifier (PA) design. The method is based on multi-objective analysis and gives the designer multiple solutions that meet predefined requirements and provide a compromise among the desired objectives. The design approach takes simulation data and presents it in an interactive format that significantly reduces time needed for trade-off decisions and builds designer intuition. The Load-Pull Extraction (LPE) method starts from simulated source- and load-pull data swept over many parameters. This is extended to a Selective Trade-Off (STO) method to be used at different stages of a PA design for specific goals, such as sensitivity analysis and multi-stage amplifiers. To validate the LPE approach, a wideband (1.9-3.6) GHz single-stage hybrid PA using a 6-W packaged GaN HEMT is designed, with the load quality factor and with harmonic termination impedances included in the trade-off analysis. This PA demonstrates a fractional bandwidth of 61.8 $\%$ and exhibits a peak power-added efficiency of 58 $\%$, large signal gain of 10.4 dB, and output power of 38.9 dBm (7.7 W). The LPE method is then extended to an STO approach example, validated on the design of an interstage network for a multistage K-band MMIC amplifier designed in a 120-nm GaN-on-SiC process. Possible further extensions of this approach include additional trade-off parameters such as transistor size selection, various process variations, as well as specific frequency dependence profile.

title: Vanadium dioxide based reconfigurable Janus metasurface for terahertz full space wavefront manipulation
authors: Weiyu Luo, Fangrong Hu, Mo Yang, An Su, Mingzhu Jiang, Longhui Zhang, Zhongpeng Gao, Ying Chen
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Quantum Electronics
abstract: Full space beam control can achieve precise orientation of signal energy in three-dimensional space, and has important application in radar systems and wireless communications. Here, we present a reconfigurable Janus metasurface based on the phase change material vanadium dioxide (VO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>), realizing bidirectional transmission and reflection at different temperatures. The meta-atom is composed of a three-layer metal-VO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> composite structure and two dielectric layers. When the VO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> is in the insulating state, the metasurface operates in transmission mode, enabling linear polarization conversion and independent beam control for terahertz (THz) waves incident from both forward and backward directions. When the VO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> is in the metallic state, the metasurface switches to reflection mode, where linear polarization conversion and phase control of reflected waves are realized through the transmission-phase principle. Furthermore, by combining transmission-phase and geometric-phase mechanisms, decoupled control of circular polarization is achieved. By arrangement of these meta-atoms, we further designed a four-channel device capable of dynamically switching between the metalens and vortex beam generator. Meanwhile, a six-channel imaging device is constructed to produce distinct letter patterns under different operating modes. The results demonstrate that the proposed metasurface device achieves versatile multi-channel multiplexing. This work provides a new strategy for enhancing the multiplexing freedom of metasurface-based wavefront manipulation, with promising applications in THz rader, imaging, communications, and related fields.

title: Adaptive Multimodal Servoing Control for Unmanned Aerial Manipulator Perching
authors: Yitian Zhang, Bo Cai, Dongyang Li, Ye Li
publish: IEEE/CAA Journal of Automatica Sinica
abstract: Dear Editor, This letter presents an adaptive control framework for unmanned aerial manipulator (UAM) perching, integrating image segmentation-based tracking with multimodal nonlinear model predictive control (NMPC). A hybrid control strategy with depth derivative prediction and pitch feedforward compensation effectively resolves control parameter conflicts between pole grasping and suction-based perching on planar surfaces. An autonomous switching mechanism, leveraging detection confidence, spatial fusion, and pixel-level feature enhancement, ensures accurate target pose estimation under dynamic occlusions. Moreover, a multimodal NMPC framework with phased weight adaptation optimizes energy efficiency, trajectory tracking precision, and contact compliance across distinct flight phases.

title: Construction of Codes Over $\mathbb{F}_{p^{3}}$ from Cyclic Codes Over $\mathscr{M}_{3}\left(\mathbb{F}_{p}+u \mathbb{F}_{p}+u^{2} \mathbb{F}_{p}\right)$
authors: Shikha Patel, Soham Ravikant Joshi, Om Prakash
publish: 2025 11th International Workshop on Signal Design and Its Applications in Communications (IWSDA)
abstract: Let <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbb{F}_{p}$</tex> be a finite field with <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$p$</tex> elements and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbb{F}_{p}[u] /\left\langle u^{3}\right\rangle=\mathbb{F}_{p}+u \mathbb{F}_{p}+u^{2} \mathbb{F}_{p}, u^{3}=0$</tex> be a finite chain ring. This paper presents the construction of cyclic codes over <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbb{F}_{p^{3}}$</tex> for certain primes <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(p \leq 100)$</tex> from a noncommutative non-chain ring <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathscr{R}=\mathscr{M}_{3}\left(\mathbb{F}_{p}[u] /\left\langle u^{3}\right\rangle\right)$</tex> of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$3 \times 3$</tex> matrices. First, we show that the structure of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathscr{R}$</tex> is isomorphic to (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbb{F}_{p^{3}}+e \mathbb{F}_{p^{3}}+e^{2} \mathbb{F}_{p^{3}}+u\left(\mathbb{F}_{p^{3}}+e \mathbb{F}_{p^{3}}+\right. \left.e^{2} \mathbb{F}_{p^{3}}\right)+u^{2}\left(\mathbb{F}_{p^{3}}+e \mathbb{F}_{p^{3}}+e^{2} \mathbb{F}_{p^{3}}\right)$</tex> where <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$e^{3}=1, u^{3}=0, u^{i} e^{j}=e^{j} u^{i}$</tex> for <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$i \in\{1,2\}$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$j \in\{1,2,3\}$</tex>. Then we provide the form of ideals of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathscr{R}$</tex> and derive that cyclic codes can be written as the direct sum of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathscr{R}$</tex>-submodules of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\frac{\mathscr{R}[x]}{\left\langle x^{n}-1\right\rangle}$</tex>. Further, we find the formula for the cardinality of cyclic codes over <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathscr{R}$</tex>. Later, in the module isometry for <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathscr{R}$</tex>, we define the Gray map, which takes derived cyclic codes over <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathscr{R}$</tex> to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbb{F}_{p^{3}}$</tex>. In addition, we provide non-trivial examples of linear codes over <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbb{F}_{p^{3}}$</tex> with good parameters which support our derived results.

title: An Efficient Deep Learning Approach for Video Content Authenticity Verification
authors: Ashwini S, Nikitha K S, Savitha S K
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: The increasing sophistication of Deepfake generation methods presents significant challenges for distinguishing authentic videos from synthetically manipulated content, raising critical concerns across social, security, and legal spheres. To address this, a robust deepfake detection framework is proposed that integrates frame-level extraction, Multi-task Cascaded Convolutional Neural Networks (MTCNN) for accurate facial localization, and data augmentation to enhance generalization and reduce overfitting. Leveraging transfer learning, the system employs a fine-tuned VGG16 Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for high-level spatial feature extraction. Trained and evaluated on the extensive Deepfake Detection Challenge (DFDC) dataset, the model achieves an accuracy of 97.24%, effectively distinguishing between genuine and forged content even under challenging conditions such as low resolution and compression artifacts. By processing videos frame-by-frame and applying a majority-voting mechanism for final classification, the framework demonstrates strong robustness and adaptability across diverse video qualities and durations. These results underscore the system's potential for real-world deployment in applications such as digital forensics, social media verification, and cybersecurity.

title: A Deep Residual Recurrent Learning Approach for the Identification of Learning Disability Disorders
authors: Tadi.Durga Devi, S. Gopalakrishnan, S. Ravi Chand
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: The early and successful discovery of the learning disability disorder (dyslexia, dysgraphia, and dyscalculia) will become the crucial opportunity to prevent the later challenges in the academic life of the students and to increase the chances of offering the students in question the required assistance in the proper way. Specific intervention facilitated by proper identification would lead to better learning and less psychological barrier. These disadvantages make them not valid and their extensive application to real education impossible. To overcome these challenges, the paper proposes a new Hybrid ResNet-LSTM diagnostic model on the early diagnosis of LD using academic, behavioral, and text-based student information. The model uses ResNet to learn higher-level semantic and structural patterns and LSTM to learn long-term contextual and sequential dependencies resulting in a more rich and discriminative representation. The hybrid fusion method combines the two feature spaces in a composite manner and ensures that it has a greater learning capacity in comparison with the conventional independent models. Empirical testing indicates that the proposed system has a high level of performance improvement with 95 percent accuracy, 94 percent precision, 96 percent recall, 95 percent F1-score, 96 percent sensitivity, 97 percent specificity, and 97 percent AUC, which is 5-15 percent higher in all the performance parameters compared to the existing deep learning models.

title: An Intelligent IoT-Based Multi-Sensor Framework for Algae Detection and Water Quality Monitoring
authors: Abdul Ahthar N, Aloysius manuel M, Anacovid A, Anas Javith A, T.V Narmadha
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: The water quality degradation resulting from the spread of algae constitutes a serious danger to human health, aquatic organisms, and water-dependent industries. Traditional water monitoring methods are often marred by manual procedures that are time-consuming and provide no real-time feedback, thus making it difficult to identify algae blooms early. This project presents an innovative and automated system for real-time water quality monitoring and algae tracking. Multiple sensors, including TDS, turbidity, DS18B20 waterproof temperature, and DHT11 temperature and humidity sensors, are attached to an Arduino Nano (ATmega328P) to acquire and process crucial water and environmental metrics. Information is relayed via an ESP8266 Wi-Fi module to the ThingSpeak platform, which provides remote viewing, real-time notification, and logging. Powered by a 12 V source regulated through buck converters and controlled using a slide switch (SDP-T), the system ensures effective operation. Its ingenuity lies in being cost-effective and scalable, thus presenting a viable solution to household water storage, aquaculture, water treatment, and environmental monitoring to avert harmful algae blooms.

title: Real-Time Emotion Detection in Spoken English Using Multimodal Deep Learning Networks
authors: M Jayaranjan, Madhavaiah U, S. Aruna, Bhuvaneswari Pagidipati, Vimochana. M, Sreela B
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: Emotion recognition from spoken English is essential for conceptualizing a better human-computer interaction. This allows an interface to respond with emotional intelligence in the real-time interaction of virtual assistants, elearning software, or online mental health diagnostics. Standard unimodal systems traditionally use only an audio or only text modality and have looked at the contextual limitations and the problem of lacking accuracy in real-time dynamic conditions. To tackle these issues, EmotiCrossFormer, a new cross-modal transformer-based multimodal deep-learning approach integrating audio and text modalities, is introduced. The idea is to improve real-time emotion recognition in terms of its accuracy and comprehension of a context by utilizing the complementary facets of acoustic and semantic features. Basically, the proposed framework utilizes a CNN + Transformer for audio modelling and SBERT for text encoding, followed by a cross-attention-based fusion mechanism that helps recognize the inter-modal emotional dependencies. In TESS dataset, EmotiCrossFormer demonstrated a staggering 98% accuracy rate, which is a strong testimony to its ability in characterization and showcasing more eloquently than traditional models that have recorded <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$86 {\%}, 89 {\%}$</tex>, and 91% for a pure audio CNN-LSTM, text-only SBERT, and late-fusion Bimodal LSTM, respectively. Therefore, research affirm the superiority of cross-modal integration for subtle emotion recognition-an important factor distinguishing human perceptions. The winds up asserting the applicability of EmotiCrossFormer as a strong and real-time solution for implementation in emotionally intelligent AI systems.

title: Interpretable AI Enabled Solutions for Crop Selections, Yield Estimation and Rainfall Analysis in Precision Agriculture
authors: P. Phanindra Kumar Reddy, K Sasidhar Reddy, C Chaithra Vardhan Reddy, P Vandana, G Madhusudhan, N Chandrakala
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: The rapid growth of the world's population and the growing need for food security have made modern farming very difficult. Traditional farming methods often don't work well with changes in the environment, low yields and changes in rainfall. In order to solve these limitations this study focuses on the creation of usable artificial intelligence driven solutions for crop selection, yield prediction and rainfall analysis thus enabling precision agriculture. The goal is to give farmers accurate and simple data-driven insights so that they can make important decisions about crop management that are both economical and clear. The suggested method combines a number of machine learning and deep learning models such as Decision Tree, Random Forest, AdaBoost, XGBoost, Support Vector Machine, Gradient Boosting Machine, k-Nearest Neighbors, Artificial Neural Network, Recurrent Neural Network, Long Short-Term Memory and Convolutional Neural Network. To make predictions easier and build trust among those involved clear techniques are used. The framework's test outcomes show that it has a very high prediction accuracy of 98.9% which makes agricultural forecasting much more accurate. The study finds that the combination of advanced algorithms and understanding not only improves predictive performance but also makes it possible for farming practices that are both smart and economical and can adjust to future agricultural needs.

title: Enhancing Result Verification of Convolutional Neural Network Predictions
authors: Yitong Sun, Lingling Wang, Yuxiang Wang, Xiaozhou Wang
publish: 2025 International Conference on Cyber Resilience and Endogenous Safety &amp; Security (CRESS)
abstract: The widespread deployment of verifiable convolutional neural network (CNN) predictions has raised significant security concerns. Recent approaches focus on providing integrity verification, ensuring that each intermediate computation within the neural network is correctly executed. Although these techniques provide correctness guarantees, they typically incur substantial computational and communication overheads. These bottlenecks make them impractical in settings where the majority of provers are honest, or where constrained provers and verifiers require minimal overhead. To address these issues, we present a lightweight framework for enhancing Result Verification of Convolutional Neural Network predictions (rvCNN). Specifically, we implement verification for the output results of the convolution layer, ReLU activation layer, and average pooling layers, respectively. To this end, we employ Freivalds’ algorithm to verify convolutional outputs, and leverage the im2col algorithm combined with vector commitments to verify ReLU and pooling layers. Experimental results show that the proposed rvCNN is up to two orders of magnitude speedup compared to prior integrity verification approaches.

title: Comparative Analysis of Sequential and Ensemble Prediction Models for Enhanced Stock Price Forecasting
authors: Nandhini S, Saranya G, Kumaran K
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: The ability to predict the stock market accurately is important to both effective investment decision-making and reduction of risk during periods of uncertainty. Financial time series data is highly dynamic and nonlinear, which presents challenges to traditional statistical direct forecasting methods to capture market behavior. This paper presents a combined hybrid-and-boosted stock market trend forecast framework based on LSTMs and GRUs aided by Extreme Gradient Boosting to increase forecast accuracy as a mitigation to overfitting. The proposed method consists of a multi-layer hybrid LSTM-GRU framework to extract sequential relationships from historical price data, trading volume data, and trading volatility indicator data then an XGBoost regression to account for nonlinear relationships for predictions. The results of experiments conducted on Indian stock indices (Nifty 50, Sensex and Nifty Bank) as well as globally (Apple and Microsoft) validated the accuracy of the combined LSTM-GRU hybrid predictive forecasting models with XGBoost, outperforming all competitors, especially ARIMA baseline model, and both standalone LSTM and GRU models. The results show accuracies of 90 percent while simultaneously reporting low levels of RMSE, MAE, and MAPE across all participating models tested.

title: Machine Learning Framework for Early Detection and Prediction of Sleep Disorders Using Multimodal Health Data
authors: A. Ramesh Babu, Pandurangaraju. B, D. Deepika, K. Hemanth Kumar, S.M. Mahammad Mubarak, A.C.Guru Balaji
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: A growing number of sleep disorders like apnea and insomnia has increased and they have been connected to major health problems like overweight, mental health disorders and heart diseases. Although accurate, traditional methods of detection are often time consuming, costly and require specific medical knowledge which prevents their broad adoption. This work offers a machine learning-based method for predicting sleep disorders and delivering immediate feedback through an interactive system driven by the need for a cost-effective and effective solution. To get sleep related datasets ready for model training the suggested framework uses a variety of preprocessing methods such as data cleaning, feature engineering, normalization and correlation analysis. Several classification models were used and compared the highest accuracy of 95% was obtained by Random Forest, followed by Logistic Regression (91%), Quadratic Discriminant Analysis (93%) and Gradient Boosting (94.6%). Users can now enter their own sleep data and receive predictions and preventive recommendations because of the integration of the most accurate model into an interactive web application. With a simple interface for users and powerful machine learning techniques this study provides a flexible and effective solution for early identification and treatment of sleep disorders which will enhance the quality of life.

title: Analysis of Organ Donation Willingness in India: a Counterfactuals Approach
authors: Rajneel Wagh, Atharva Rajpurkar, Pooja Makhijani, Nabanita Mandal
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: Organ donation continues to face challenges due to the limited willingness of individuals to register as donors, despite increasing demand. This paper presents a novel machine learning-based framework that predicts and analyzes individual willingness to donate organs by leveraging counterfactual explanations. The proposed system utilizes survey data containing demographic, socioeconomic, and awareness-related features to train a Random Forest classifier, achieving an accuracy of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{8 2 \%}$</tex>. What sets this work apart is the integration of Diverse Counterfactual Explanations (DiCE), enabling the system to ask: “What minimal changes in personal attributes could shift a person's stance from unwilling to willing?” Counterfactuals are generated for negative predictions, with configurations optimized to promote diversity and actionable insights. Analytical functions are used to identify and rank the most influential features contributing to changes in donation willingness, offering both basic and advanced interpretations.

title: Recommendations for Using Blockchain Technology to Protect the Personal Data of Web Service Users
authors: Dmitry E. Tomchuk
publish: 2026 ElCon Conference of Young Researchers in Computing &amp; Processing, and Information Security (ElCon-CP)
abstract: This paper addresses the problem of ensuring transparency and controllability of personal data processing in web services under centralized architectures. An analytical model based on the use of blockchain as a distributed ledger for recording data processing events is proposed, separating personal data storage from verifiable consent and operation logging. The results demonstrate that the proposed approach enables user-controlled data modification and deletion through cryptographic mechanisms while ensuring service accountability and regulatory compliance.

title: Towards Understanding Masked Distillation
authors: Tuo Chen, Jie Gui
publish: 2025 International Conference on Cyber Resilience and Endogenous Safety &amp; Security (CRESS)
abstract: Masked Image Modeling (MIM) is a prominent pre-training paradigm in computer vision, learning representations by masking and predicting image patches. A recent variant, Masked Distillation (MD), leverages high-level semantic features for supervision. Although recent works have demonstrated its effectiveness, the underlying mechanisms remain unclear. Our investigation reveals that MD inherits beneficial inductive biases from MIM, which we believe contribute to its performance. We further find that MD mitigates multiple forms of overfitting in pre-trained models, including attention homogenization and representation folding. We also analyze the nuances of its architecture and decision-making process. Our findings challenge previous conceptions of MD and establish a more powerful baseline.

title: How Much Is Too Much? Comfort Envelopes for Distortions in Virtual Reality Interaction
authors: Hayeon Kim, In-Kwon Lee
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: Virtual reality (VR) is compelling precisely because it transcends physical realism, allowing reach to be stretched, motion rescaled, and objects relocated. While these distortions expand interaction possibilities, they lack principled limits: How far can they go before agency, ownership, or presence collapse? We present a dual-axis framework that decomposes distortions into metric deviations (continuous remappings akin to stretching) and topological breaks (discontinuous jumps akin to cutting), both applied to the same hand–target relation. To operationalize this framework, we introduce the Single-Interaction Dual-Axis (SIDA) protocol, combining a standardized task with psychophysical estimation of Just-Acceptable Distortion (JAD) and Breaks in Presence (BiP) under stable or jittered mappings. In a study with N = 52, results show that metric deviations are significantly more tolerable than topological breaks, predictability expands tolerance by approximately 35%, and combined distortions interact contractively, constraining the total budget. Our contributions include (i) a unified model for commensurate comparison, (ii) a reproducible protocol for threshold estimation, and (iii) the comfort envelope, a multi-dimensional budget of acceptable distortion that guides trade-offs between agency and presence in VR design.

title: RISAT: Rotation Interpolation with Smooth Axis Transitions
authors: Val Vec, Sašo Tomažič
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: We propose RISAT, a lightweight and efficient algorithm for interpolating sequences of orientations. RISAT operates on rotation vectors, which are first transformed into a local coordinate frame. The method then applies cubic Hermite interpolation to each component of the local rotation vectors, yielding smooth trajectories with continuous first derivatives. Despite its conceptual simplicity, RISAT matches the accuracy of state-of-the-art rotation interpolation techniques, while requiring roughly half their computational cost. This makes RISAT particularly suitable for real-time applications, including deployment on embedded devices.

title: EdgeAI-Assisted Intent-Based Dynamic Scheduler Selection Resource Allocation Strategy for V2X
authors: S Ajish, Suresh Chavhan
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: The lack of resources to meet the exponentially growing number of users is one of the major problems, along with shortages of computing resources, security issues, vulnerability, reliability, concerns, and complexity. This paper aims to achieve maximum efficiency in resource allocation. The resources will be allocated to users based on their intent and needs, with the assistance of an ML model and edge computing. Intelligent resource allocation by dynamically changing the scheduler algorithm yields much greater efficiency than traditional methods. The dynamic scenario simulation implemented in NS3, the dynamic allocation of resources through different algorithms for various scenarios, and the verification of the results show that the proposed methodology will increase the efficiency in resource allocation for each user according to the environment, without compromising on parameters such as QoS, SINR, delay, throughput, etc.

title: Development and Experimental Validation of a Custom Acoustic Emission Acquisition System for Structural Health Monitoring
authors: Raul Gaspari Santos, Niederauer Mastelari
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: Early detection of defects in mechanical components is essential for the safety and reliability of service equipment such as automobiles, aircraft, and ships. In this work, we present and experimentally validate an acoustic emission (AE)-based system composed of a dedicated sensor, signal conditioning circuitry, and processing software, featuring a custom electrical, mechanical, and software architecture with a cost-effective implementation. Operating at a sampling frequency of 400 kHz and an average gain of ~50 dB, with mean signal-to-noise ratio ~39 dB, the system successfully detected dislocation-related phenomena in SAE 1020 steel specimens within the elastic region, with characteristic signals concentrated between 140–160 kHz, and additional activity above 110 kHz during the elasto-plastic transition. AE event counting metrics showed direct correlation with microscopic deformation mechanisms, while accumulated counts correlated with the macroscopic stress–strain behavior. These results demonstrate the feasibility of the proposed system for structural health monitoring of critical components.

title: APC-Scheduler: Allocation-Planning-Charging Integrated Scheduling System for Cooperative Automated Valet Parking
authors: Hanyang Zhuang, Wenyue Zhang, Qizhe Xu, Chunxiang Wang, Ming Yang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: The Automated Valet Parking system (AVP), as one of the promising technologies, offers significant benefits in saving maneuver time and parking cost. With the increase number of vehicles using AVP system in large-scale parking lots, the overall efficiency is limited by the selfish decision-making of each individual; therefore, cooperative-AVP (C-AVP) is developed to achieve global optimization by scheduling the vehicles. Existing C-AVP methods focus only on one of the key processes in AVP, such as space allocation, trajectory planning, and electric vehicle(EV) charging. However, these factors interact with each other and are rarely considered in an entire system. Therefore, this paper aims to model the space allocation, trajectory planning, and EV charging problem in a whole framework, named APC-scheduler, and adopt it to improve the overall efficiency for large-scale parking lot with EV. The APC-scheduler comprises two parts: 1) a zone-based parking space allocation module dynamically assigns parking spaces based on real-time conditions using a hierarchical optimization strategy. It contains a charging priority estimation process to determine the EV’s charging requirement urgency; 2) a conflict-based trajectory planning module is developed to reduce trajectory overlaps and vehicle conflicts. It uses spatial-temporal path planner and speed planner to eliminate conflicts. Experiments under reasonable vehicle arrival and departure statistics have been conducted in a large-scale parking lot with 526 parking spaces. The results demonstrate that the proposed method effectively enhances parking efficiency and minimizes conflicts, particularly under high vehicle arrival frequencies and in dense traffic conditions.

title: Three-Dimensional Gravity Inversion via Residual V-Net with Multi-Scale Attentional Feature Fusion
authors: Haobing Yang, Bing Cheng, Qiu Guan, Zhongkun Qiao, Junjie Hou, Zongyu Zhang
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Three-dimensional (3D) gravity inversion remains a fundamentally ill-posed and non-unique problem, where reconstructing subsurface density distributions from two-dimensional (2D) surface gravity observations is severely constrained by depth-dependent sensitivity decay and ambiguity in mass placement. Classical Tikhonov-based inversion algorithms partially alleviate these issues through explicit regularization but often suffer from the skin effect, oversmoothed deep anomalies, and substantial computational cost. Recent deep learning (DL) approaches provide a data-driven alternative; however, conventional convolutional architectures still struggle with semantic inconsistency during multi-scale feature fusion, limited depth-awareness, and extreme class imbalance in geophysical models.This paper presents GravityVNet, a physics-aware deep neural framework tailored for high-fidelity 3D gravity inversion. The architecture is built upon a Residual V-Net backbone enhanced with a 3D Coordinate Attention (CoordAtt) mechanism to encode long-range spatial dependencies and explicitly preserve depth-direction information, which helps compensate for depth-dependent attenuation effects commonly associated with the skin effect. To further improve structural recovery, we introduce Multi-Scale Attentional Feature Fusion (AFF) modules, which adaptively recalibrate skip-connected features and bridge semantic gaps between encoder and decoder pathways. A Deep Supervision training strategy, combined with a Hybrid Loss function integrating Focal Tversky Loss and Total Variation (TV) regularization, enforces boundary sharpness, suppresses artifacts, and strengthens convergence stability. Extensive experiments on large-scale, physics-consistent synthetic datasets generated by SimPEG indicate that GravityVNet achieves competitive performance compared to standard baseline methods. The results indicate that the model has improved in terms of geometric fidelity, quantitative accuracy, and physical consistency with the observed gravity response. In addition, the validation of the measured data in the Vinton Salt Dome also proves the effectiveness of the model in solving composite anomalies and deep structures.

title: A Collaborative Relay Tracking Method Incorporating Larger-Scale Spatial Context for UAVs
authors: Yongxiang He, Zhao Zhang, Jianjun Ma, Peng Leng, Hongwu Guo
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: The problem of uncrewed aerial vehicles (UAVs) collaborative relay tracking is extremely challenging in the case of dense target distribution. This paper proposes a relay tracking method incorporating larger-scale spatial context (LSCR). The target handover problem is converted into a graph similarity computation problem. Specifically, in order to overcome the effect of perspective difference, this paper determines the topological relationship between targets based on the Delaunay triangulation theory, and the target graph structure is constructed. Besides, a target graph representation (TGR) feature is proposed by constructing a graph representation convolutional network (GRCN) that can automatically extract the spatial context information. In addition, this paper proposes a lightweight graph similarity matching model to measure the probability that two targets in different viewpoints are the same source. The experimental results show that the proposed method can accurately accomplish target handover in the case of dense target distribution, and adapt to the effects of differences in UAV viewpoints. Compared with the existing methods, the proposed method does not rely on target localization data and is able to distinguish targets with close positions or the same appearances. It can also meet the real-time requirements on the CPU with a smaller model size and less information transmission.

title: Impact of Freight Task Characteristics on Driving Styles: A DSS-Based Quantitative Analysis
authors: Jun Ma, Yiping Wu, Zheng Zhao, Jian Rong, Yangyang Lu, Jie Ren
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: Driving style (DS) assessment plays an important role in intelligent transportation system (ITS) application, such as driving feedback provision and usage-based insurance. This paper aims to study the impact of the freight tasks (i.e., vehicle Axle, cargo type, task mileage, and freight costs) on the DS. After cleaning and processing truck GPS, Waybill, and Map, the concept of a minimal unit of DS recognition (MUDSR) is proposed and considered as a micro-DS expression unit to quantify the impact of the freight tasks on the DS. In addition, a unified expression framework, termed driving style structure (DSS), is proposed to compare and apply results of DS related studies in different conditions. The impacts of different characteristics of freight tasks on the DS are then analyzed using the DSS. The results obtained demonstrate that the freight tasks lead to an average increase of 1.58% in the proportion of safe DS in the trucks’ DDS. The safety awareness and the concern for monetary losses of truck drivers significantly affect the DSS. In addition, the freight tasks exhibiting higher costs of goods have a greater impact on the improvement of the DSS safety. Based on the results obtained in this study, the relationship between the safe driving attitude and driving performance is discussed. Relevant recommendations are also provided for freight task allocation and long-term and short-term trucker training.

title: Asynchronous TOA-Based Long-Distance Localization With NLOS Error Mitigation
authors: Jizhou Wang, Wen Liu, Zhongliang Deng, Jilong Guo, Heng Kang, Yingjian Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Vehicular Technology
abstract: Non-line-of-sight (NLOS) errors constitute the primary source of inaccuracies in time-of-arrival (TOA) positioning. While recent studies have successfully mitigated NLOS effects in small-scale scenarios, these methods often struggle to maintain effectiveness in long-distance localization due to the exacerbated impact of NLOS errors. This paper proposes an asynchronous TOA-based positioning method capable of achieving accurate positioning in long-distance, severe NLOS environments while accounting for clock errors. A novel abnormal TOA detection method leveraging geometric characteristics is proposed to initially mitigate the influence of NLOS errors. Additionally, an asynchronous TOA positioning algorithm based on semidefinite programming (SDP) is developed, demonstrating robust applicability even under severe NLOS conditions. Experimental results from both simulations using a parameterized error distribution model and ray-tracing simulations in long-distance scenarios confirm that the proposed method achieves high positioning accuracy despite significant NLOS interference.

title: Non-Arrhenius Trapping Effects in GaN HEMTs
authors: Carter Mayfield, Matt Grupen, Dave Frey, Gary Hughes, Dennis Walker, Andrew J. Green, Maher Tahhan, Matt DeJarld, Eduardo M. Chumbes, Nicholas C. Miller
publish: IEEE Transactions on Electron Devices
abstract: This article reports a new physical description of non-Arrhenius behavior observed in drain current transient (DCT) measurements of gallium nitride (GaN) high-electron-mobility transistors (HEMTs). The field-assisted trap tunneling ionization process is identified as the key contributor to the non-Arrhenius behavior in the temperature-dependent DCT measurements. Physics-based simulations using Fermi kinetics transport (FKT) provide the detailed analyses of the measured behaviors. A particular pulse profile is used to characterize the specific traps investigated in this work. DCT measurement data are reported for three different GaN HEMT technologies to provide experimental data in support of fundamental understanding of the non-Arrhenius behavior in GaN HEMTs. DCT measurements and Arrhenius plots are ubiquitously used to analyze defect dynamics in GaN HEMTs. Therefore, the results and conclusions in this work provide the trap characterization community with a clear link between non-Arrhenius behavior and trap tunneling ionization, which occurs near semiconductor interfaces.

title: Dynamic Worlds, Dynamic Humans: Generating Virtual Human-Scene Interaction Motion in Dynamic Scenes_supp1-3679139.mp4
authors: Yin Wang
publish: N/A
abstract: Scenes are continuously undergoing dynamic changes in the real world. However, existing human–scene interaction generation methods typically treat the scene as static, which deviates from reality. Inspired by world models, we introduce Dyn-HSI, the first cognitive architecture for dynamic human-scene interaction, which endows virtual humans with three humanoid components. (1) Vision (human eyes): we equip the virtual human with a Dynamic Scene-Aware Navigation, which continuously perceives changes in the surrounding environment and adaptively predicts the next waypoint. (2) Memory (human brain): we equip the virtual human with a Hierarchical Experience Memory, which stores and updates experiential data accumulated during training. This allows the model to leverage prior knowledge during inference for context-aware motion priming, thereby enhancing both motion quality and generalization. (3) Control (human body): we equip the virtual human with Human–Scene Interaction Diffusion Model, which generates high-fidelity interaction motions conditioned on multimodal inputs. To evaluate performance in dynamic scenes, we extend the existing static human–scene interaction datasets to construct a dynamic benchmark, Dyn-Scenes. We conduct extensive qualitative and quantitative experiments to validate Dyn-HSI, showing that our method consistently outperforms existing approaches and generates high-quality human–scene interaction motions in both static and dynamic settings.

title: Tuning Immersion and Performance with Adaptive Generative Music in VR
authors: Jiayuan Wen, Daniele Giunchi, Pasquale Cascarano, Riccardo Bovo, Eyal Ofek, Anthony Steed
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: Music in virtual environments has long been treated as a temporal evolving element, enhancing atmosphere and game pace but rarely considered as a performance adaptive element. Recent advances in artificial intelligence (AI) and procedural audio make it possible to generate music that adapts in real time to player actions and system state. Yet, despite its potential, the behavioural impact of such adaptive generative soundtracks in head-mounted display-based virtual reality (VR) remains largely unexplored. To address this gap, we introduce a VR archery system that integrates Google MusicFXDJ with Ubiq-Genie to deliver continuous AI-generated adaptive music driven by gameplay events. In a within-subjects experiment (N = 22), participants completed trials with either a stylistically-matched fixed soundtrack or an adaptive soundtrack that escalated tension across four phases as arrows depleted. Measures combined self-reported ratings of presence, focus, stress, and emotional impact, with performance metrics of accuracy and aiming time. Results reveal that adaptive generative music not only heightens immersion and emotional salience but also modulates motor precision in an arousal-dependent inverted-U pattern: moderate musical tension improved accuracy and speed, whereas excessive tension impaired them. These findings establish AI-generated music as a powerful behavioural feedback modality in VR, opening pathways for training, rehabilitation, and next-generation immersive entertainment.

title: Adaptive Eye-Controlled Mouse using Deep Belief and Q-Learning Networks
authors: Rohini M, Santhosh M J, Sakthibalan R
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Intelligent Data Communication Technologies and Internet of Things (IDCIoT)
abstract: With increasing use of traditional input devices such as keyboards and mice, people with motor impairments would face many more difficulties in accessing computers. The proposed system of an Eye-Controlled Mouse Pointer presents a hands-free, vision-based solution for intelligent humancomputer interaction. The system captures real-time eye movements using a standard webcam; Python's OpenCV and Dlib are used for facial landmark detection and tracking of the pupil. The visual features extracted are further enhanced through the use of a Deep Belief Network to ensure gaze and blink recognition that is robust across changing lighting conditions and head orientation. A reinforcement model based on a Deep Q-Network is trained which, through reward adaptation, dynamically learns the most effective cursor movements and click actions. Calibration and integration testing of the hybrid model are facilitated by MATLAB. The novelty in its contribution, this work fuses DQN and DBN to realize adaptive, highly accurate, low-latency mouse control that allows an accessible, cost-efficient alternative to physical input devices.
